File.Name: k629b_k629-028.jpg
Notes: 50060241611 Here is little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly in a flowering plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's probably another female with those dull colors barely visible on its upper wings. This one looks like it's been around a while, definitely not as fresh as the one in this photo a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2020)

File.Name: k629c_k629-015.jpg
Notes: 50059647293 This is the caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The spikes on its back are remarkable, and so is its spiky head, though it's mostly hidden in this photo, look to the left. This caterpillar is still small, maybe 1/2 inch long, see this photo for a mature specimen. I've been having good luck finding these caterpillars this year since I found their secret, see my [Previous] photo. Note that there's some webbing in this photo. I'll try to get a better shot for the Web Wednesdays group later this week. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2020)

File.Name: k629d_k629-011.jpg
Notes: 50059615248 Just to the right of center is the caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a half-eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - see my [Next] photo for a closer shot of the caterpillar. This photo shows how I spotted it - by the exposed vein of the leaf to the left. These caterpillars pick a leaf and eat it leaving the center vein exposed and reinforcing it with webbing, see this photo and this photo. The caterpillar in my photo today is not as small as those, maybe 1/2 inch long, and quite invisible if you don't know it's there. The exposed leaf vein is the key to finding them. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2020)

File.Name: k630a_k630-014.jpg
Notes:
50063616646 This is the caterpillar of a Lycaenid butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group that includes Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. It's on a flowering shrub of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). Many of these caterpillars look similar, but maybe this will become another Brown Elfin butterfly - compare this photo and Alice Abelia's comment. And see this photo of another similar caterpillar on a different plant that is tended by an ant. I actually saw two of these in the same shrub today - it's been a good year for interesting caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2020)

There was no morning gloom today, though it was still breezy from the south and rather cool, in the 70s. I consider that very pleasant for almost July.

File.Name: k630b_k630-035.jpg
Notes: 50063855792 Here are two mating pairs of tiny black beetles on different plants in the woods today. They might even be the same kind of beetle, but who knows. The ones on the left are on a flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae), and the ones on the right are on a flower of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). Procreation is going on all over the place, all the time, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2020)
ScientificName: Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: k630c_k630-046.jpg
Notes: 50063008338 Here is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on a flowering stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). They are NOT the eggs of the Acmon Blue butterflies that I've seen ovipositing on these shrubs lately, see this photo. Those butterflies move around depositing their eggs one by one in different places rather than all together like this. I suspect these are the eggs of true bugs in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, but I won't try to guess beyond that. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2020)

File.Name: k630d_k628-010.jpg
Notes: 50063809057 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) digging a hole into a composite flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) growing on the wild edge of our garden a couple of days ago. Note the frass (caterpillar poop). It was a gloomy morning with a breeze, and I'm glad the photo came out as well as it did. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2020)

File.Name: k701a_k630-011.jpg
Notes:
50066479153 This is another photo of a caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It might look like it's leaving a trail of slime behind like a snail, but that's a cushion of webbing - see this photo of a more mature specimen that shows something similar. The spikes on the caterpillar's back are remarkable, and so is its spiky head, unfortunately hidden under its body to the left. I have a similar shot in this photo, but I got this one a day later, and I hope it shows the webbing better. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2020)

No new photos today. Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. It was foggy when we left and still foggy when we returned a few hours later. The sun did finally come out after 3:00 pm.

File.Name: k701b_k630-006.jpg
Notes: 50067251502 Here are two different caterpillars of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly in the same shrub of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). They have each recently graduated from the "bird turd" stage that I show in this photo. This is how I find them every day, resting on a cushion of webbing. I've never seen them move away, but they are getting larger - so maybe they are nocturnal feeders? I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesdays group, but happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2020)

File.Name: k701c_k629-022.jpg
Notes: 50066399648 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) behind a veil of webbing in its shelter between two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I don't really associate these spiders with webbing at all, though I've seen them in their shelters before, see this photo and this photo. The spider might be a male in the genus Phanias, compare this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2020)

File.Name: k701d_k625-019.jpg
Notes: 50066364613 Here are two shots of tiny spiders on different plants in the woods that sure look like they're cohabiting. In each case I would guess that that's the larger female above, though it's hard to make out their palps. They each seem to have a nest made of webbing - in Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) on the left and Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) on the right. I guess by the markings that these are Mesh Web Weaver spiders in the family Dictynidae. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesdays group, but happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Deerweed; Mesh Web Weaver
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Dictynidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Dictynidae

File.Name: k702a_k702-005.jpg
Notes:
50070640667 Here is a caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - the first mature one I've seen this year. It's spiky head is on the left, but the caterpillar is holding it down so it's hard to see - see this photo. This caterpillar is more mature than the ones I've been showing lately - going back in time, compare this photo, this photo, and this photo. Those photos also give an idea of the range of instars or stages of these caterpillars. The one in my photo today is about one inch long or a bit more, and it will get larger before it's done. It's been a good year for these caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2020)

Today started gloomy, and then the fog winds kicked up as the clouds started to burn off before noon. It wasn't the best time for new photos, but this California Sister caterpillar made my day.
ScientificName: Adelpha bredowii; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; instars
associatedTaxa: Adelpha bredowii; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; instars

File.Name: k702b_k702-007.jpg
Notes: 50070346101 This isn't the face of a dragon! It's the rear end of a remarkable mature caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). See my [Next] photo (here) for the rest of the caterpillar, but I figure this shot is perfect for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2020)

File.Name: k702c_k701-001.jpg
Notes: 50070564212 Here is a Bumble Bee (Bombus) with its proboscis in a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. These large bees and the Carpenter Bees are the only bees heavy enough to pry open the sage flowers that are mostly "closed" when they first open, see this photo. I got this photo in gloomy weather yesterday morning before we went on our shopping trip. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2020)

File.Name: k702d_k627-036.jpg
Notes: 50069735133 This is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a bristly flower cluster of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that's pretty much gone to seed - in fact, it's the same katydid I showed in this photo a few days ago. It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. These nymphs are particularly gorgeous. This photo shows the katydid's rear end, perfect for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2020)

File.Name: k703a_k702-017.jpg
Notes:
50073097003 Here are red, white, and blue flowers for Independence Day. I tried to get new photos of them all today, but there was a breeze, and I like these photos from yesterday and a few days before better. On the left are RED flowers of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae); in the middle are WHITE flowers of White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae); and on the right are BLUE flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae). Native blue flowers are the hardest ones to find at this time of year. I've done this before, try searching my photos for "red white blue flowers". Happy birthday USA! It's a good time to remember our revolutionary heritage. Flowers, not fireworks in someone else's fire-prone mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2020)

It was sunny and warmer today, but there was still that annoying breeze from the south that keeps everything moving. It might turn hot for the weekend - never good for the Fourth of July holiday. There's some concern that since the beaches are closed by Covid-19, certain idiots might bring their (illegal) fireworks up into the mountains and start fires. We'll be watchful.
CommonNames: Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; White Sage; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k703b_k703-016.jpg
Notes: 50073062363 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. They seem to come in all different sizes, from big to small, but they all have reddish eyes and red butt like the glowing ember of a cigar. Despite their unpleasant common and scientific names, I like these flies - they are large and husky, like they could take on anything. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2020)

File.Name: k703c_k630-026.jpg
Notes: 50073569706 I found this little fly (Diptera) working flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it's not "just a fly" like so many of them are. For one thing, I don't think I've ever seen a fly that is so deeply black. For another, its abdomen looks powdered underneath, like a bee in the family Megachilidae, see this photo. One possibility is Eristalis obscura in the Syrphidae family, I wish I could see more of the fly's face. Any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2020)
ScientificName: Diptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Megachilidae; Eristalis obscura; Syrphidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Megachilidae; Eristalis obscura; Syrphidae

File.Name: k703d_k702-013.jpg
Notes: 50073789892 Here is one more shot of the caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. Together with my [Previous] two photos here and here, they make a nice triptych - with head, side, and rear views. The heads of these caterpillars are remarkable, though they usually hold them down like this - at least when I'm trying to get a photo. See this photo for another head shot that's not obscured. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2020)

File.Name: k704a_k704-020.jpg
Notes:
50077454002 Here is another triptych of red, white, and blue flowers for Independence Day - and this time I did manage to get all the photos today from the canyon at home. On the left is a RED flower of Mexican Pink aka Indian Pink (Silene laciniata, Caryophyllaceae); in the middle are WHITE flowers of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae); and on the right are BLUE flowers of Sticky False Gilyflower aka Sticky False Gilia (Allophyllum glutinosum, Polemoniaceae). Native blue flowers are the hardest ones to find at this time of year. I've done this before, try searching my photos for "red white blue flowers". Happy birthday USA! It's a good time to remember our revolutionary heritage. Flowers, not fireworks in someone else's fire-prone mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2020)

It was sunny and almost HOT today, with temps up to about 88∞. There was still that annoying breeze from the south that keeps everything moving. It is expected to be hotter tomorrow - never good for a Fourth of July holiday weekend. There's some concern that since the beaches are closed by Covid-19, certain idiots might bring their (illegal) fireworks up into the mountains and start fires. So far it's been quiet, but we're being watchful.
Locality: illegal) fireworks up into the mountains and start fires.  So far it's been quiet
CommonNames: Mexican Pink; Indian Pink; Wedge-leaved Horkelia; Sticky False Gilyflower; Sticky False Gilia
ScientificName: Silene laciniata; Caryophyllaceae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Allophyllum glutinosum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Silene laciniata; Caryophyllaceae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Allophyllum glutinosum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: k704b_k704-024.jpg
Notes: 50077172481 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) in a new flower of Mexican Pink aka Indian Pink (Silene laciniata, Caryophyllaceae) in the woods today. The bee does have pollen-collecting hairs on its hind legs, so it may be another Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina - but it hardly matters. It's getting pollen from one of the flower's anthers. The flower's species name "laciniata" sounds like "lacerate" - irregularly slashed and jagged as if torn. Freddy Krueger comes to mind, but I'd rather think of Henri Matisse in his later years. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Mexican Pink; Indian Pink; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Silene laciniata; Caryophyllaceae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Silene laciniata; Caryophyllaceae; Ceratina

File.Name: k704c_k704-021.jpg
Notes: 50077150346 This is a little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae) on a stem of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. It's larger than most of these hoppers, but still pretty small. Hah - there are about 3,000 species in North America alone . . . and this is one of them. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2020)

File.Name: k704d_k704-005.jpg
Notes: 50076540303 These aren't my best photos today, but they're probably the most interesting for natural history. I found this newly molted green caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. As I approached it, I noticed something tiny on its head, and managed to get the photo on the right before it flew away. This is a 1:1 640x800 crop from my 6000x4000 pixel original, so please don't judge it too harshly! After the critter disappeared, I got the photo on the left. I'm not sure what the tiny critter is or what it's doing on the caterpillar. Maybe it's a Biting Midge (Ceratopogonidae) fly, see Ian Jacobs' comment below. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2020)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; California Coffeeberry; Biting Midge
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Ceratopogonidae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Ceratopogonidae

File.Name: k705a_k705-051.jpg
Notes:
50080797978 This is a striking black-and-white beetle (Coleoptera) perched on my finger tip in the woods today. It's about the size of my finger nail, so it's about 1/2 inch long. If you look close at its legs, can you see what's missing? (It doesn't seem to be slowing it down any.) Maybe this is a Long-horned Beetle in the family Cerambycidae, but I'm not sure if its antennae are long enough for that group - and look how its antenna segments are different from one another, is that a clue? I don't know much about beetles as a group, and any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2020)

It was downright HOT today, up into the 90s on our upstairs deck in the afternoon. Fortunately, there wasn't much wind, though sundowner winds from the north might kick up later tonight. I wasn't aware of any illegal fireworks in the mountains, though there was apparently quite a show of home-brew pyrotechnics in town. I'm glad they kept it there.

File.Name: k705b_k705-002.jpg
Notes: 50080744273 Here are two shots of a little caterpillar of a Lycaenid butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) that's being tended by an ant on a flowering shrub of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). This is the butterfly group that includes Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks - see this photo for more info on the caterpillar. I've seen something similar recently, compare this photo of another caterpillar on a different plant that is being tended by an ant. The ants are tending the caterpillar, not attacking it. In exchange for the caterpillar's honeydew, the ants will offer protection from parasitic flies and wasps. They are really partners in trade. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2020)

File.Name: k705c_k705-037.jpg
Notes: 50081290851 This is another caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - the same one that I showed in this photo a few days ago. It's still in the same place, and I figure I better get photos of it before it gets tired of my attentions and disappears like these large caterpillars do. I took a liberty with this photo and turned my camera 90 degrees counter-clockwise to use my screen space more efficiently. The caterpillars like this head-down pose. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2020)

File.Name: k705d_k705-021.jpg
Notes: 50081239086 Here are tiny "parasitic wasps" (Hymenoptera) on new seed capsules of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I don't think either of the wasps is actually ovipositing, but they both look like they're about to. I believe these are Chalcidid Wasps in the superfamily Chalcididae, maybe family Eurytomidae. I have several other photos of these wasps from years past, including this one that actually shows its ovipositor. I'm not sure what these wasps are after in the seed capsule - the larvae of another critter that's already in there? (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2020)
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Chalcididae; Eurytomidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Chalcididae; Eurytomidae

File.Name: k706a_k706-014.jpg
Notes:
50085415802 Here is another handsome Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's a male, though I only know that because I saw its blue upper wings as it flew away. However, I notice that this one has a bluish fringe on its wings which may be sign of deeper blue inside. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2020)

It was HOT again today, cooler than yesterday, but still up to about 90∞ on our upstairs deck in the afternoon. The wind never did get too bad last night or today, but it's starting to kick up right now from the north. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and I hope the winds will subside tomorrow.

File.Name: k706b_k706-023.jpg
Notes: 50085377302 Here is a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) with prey by flowers of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. This is the first of these predators that I've seen this year - though I've been making a point of staying in the canyon close to home. Its prey is a tiny wild bee, maybe a Masked Bee in the genus Hylaeus, which is quite hairless. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; California Coffeeberry; Masked Bee
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Hylaeus
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Hylaeus

File.Name: k706c_k706-012.jpg
Notes: 50084510543 This is a handsome nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a dusty leaf of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica, Rosaceae) in the woods today. It looks like it's blowing hot jet fuel from its butt! Those hairs make it look like the nymphs I find earlier in the year that I think are genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae, see this photo, but I think from the colors and time of year that It must be something else. I checked my records and found this photo of the same critter back in 2012 - also in July. I'm not sure what it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2020)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: k706d_k705-045.jpg
Notes: 50085066281 This is another head-on shot of the striking black-and-white beetle (Coleoptera) that I showed in my [Previous] photo (here). It's perched on my finger tips, and it's a good thing I trimmed my nails recently! The beetle is about 1/2 inch long. My first thought was that it's a Long-horned Beetle in the family Cerambycidae, but I'm not sure if its antennae are long enough for that group - and look how its antenna segments are different from one another, is that a clue? Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2020)

File.Name: k707a_k707-008.jpg
Notes:
50088973187 I first noticed one blue wing sticking out of the leaves of this small oak tree - it was quite motionless, and I thought maybe a predator had got it. I started moving in with my camera, and the butterfly came out from hiding, and I got this photo just before it flew away. It's a male Blue butterfly in the Polyommatinae subfamily, but which one? It doesn't have orange on its hind wings, so it's not an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) like this one. The black border of its wings is not wide like the Pacific Azure (Celastrina echo) in this photo. That leaves the Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) on the short list of probable butterflies, and that one actually does look right, compare this photo. I'm not sure about this ID, and it is late for this species which is more of a spring butterfly, see my one photo for the year taken in late May. Whatever it is, it's my first photo of any male Blue butterfly with its wings open all this year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2020)

It was a few degrees cooler today than yesterday with not much wind, very nice. We have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow from 8:00 am to 5:00 pm, and I should be able to post if it doesn't run too much later than that. Hah - now they call it a "Critical Power Outage Alert" as though a maintenance power outage doesn't sound important enough. Among other things, they will be doing some tree work along our road, and I hope they don't take too much.
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Plebejus acmon; Celastrina echo; Glaucopsyche lygdamus
associatedTaxa: Polyommatinae; Plebejus acmon; Celastrina echo; Glaucopsyche lygdamus

File.Name: k707b_k707-033.jpg
Notes: 50088927792 This is another little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) on a seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The green color of its abdomen suggests to me that it has recently molted. These bugs always remind me of shogun-era samurai warriors with their armor! These are small but effective predators, see this photo from yesterday. These are the first ones I've seen this year, but their time is just beginning. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2020)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k707c_k707-036.jpg
Notes: 50088060318 Here are two pairs of different mating insects on different plants in the woods today. What they have in common is that they are all tiny, less than 1/8 inch. Those are tiny "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the left, on a seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), maybe Plant Bugs in the family Miridae. Those are different Planthoppers (Fulgoroidea) on the right, on a stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). I believe they are Cixiid Planthoppers in the family Cixiidae. They have a narrow head, antennae below the eyes, and interesting wings - see this photo for a closer look. It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2020)
CommonNames: Black Sage; Plant Bugs; Planthoppers; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Cixiid Planthoppers
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Miridae; Fulgoroidea; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Cixiidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Miridae; Fulgoroidea; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Cixiidae

File.Name: k707d_k707-012.jpg
Notes: 50088604271 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on an open flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today, probably genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. As I was getting this photo, a Honey Bee came by to visit the open flowers, and it was remarkable how big it looked in my viewfinder. Photobombed by a bee, but I couldn't get a photo I liked - the difference in size between bee and spider was just too much. The Coffeeberry is finishing its run now - there are still open flowers like the one the spider is guarding, but most flowers are done. I'll miss them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2020)

File.Name: k708a_k708-002.jpg
Notes:
50092713792 Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden today. Well, it's most of the butterfly - it's missing one tail and a bird-sized chunk out of one of its hindwings. Note that this one has the cream-colors that these butterflies usually get in summer. The Butterfly Bush has just started flowering in earnest, and the one thing missing has been butterflies. I was happy to spot this one today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2020)

Just two photos today as our power was off all day for scheduled maintenance. The power came back around 5:30 pm, but the internet didn't come back on our DSL router for another hour after that. The power company's tree work looks better than I expected.

File.Name: k708b_k704-007.jpg
Notes: 50091854943 This is the "cushion of webbing" where a caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly has been lounging on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), see this photo for he caterpillar. (As I recall, this is the one on the right in that photo.) It's my impression that the caterpillars make this cushion to have something to hang onto when they are resting. This caterpillar has now left this leaf, but it must be be *somewhere* in the shrub. See my [Next] photo for the butterfly. Spiders aren't the only ones - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2020)

File.Name: k709a_k709-009.jpg
Notes:
50095642716 Here is the latest stage or instar of a caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on a leafy stem of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. This is the same caterpillar I last showed in this photo from two weeks ago, but it looks different now - bigger, but less colorful and less cute. I suppose it happens to all of us. I haven't seen this caterpillar since my last photo, and I suspect it crawled off to molt in a private place. It's about two inches long now, and it might double in size before it's ready to pupate. I'll try to keep an eye on it. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2020)

It was sunny but a bit cooler today and more breezy with wind from the south. It sounds like a hot weekend coming up before it cools down again next week.
ScientificName: instar; Hyalophora euryalus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: instar; Hyalophora euryalus; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: k709b_k709-017.jpg
Notes: 50098464153 Here is another shot of a caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly munching a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This isn't the mature green caterpillar I show in this photo, but the less-mature one in this photo. The spikes on the caterpillar's back are remarkable, and so is its spiky head. Maybe I see a hint of green in this one, a sign that it's getting ready to molt into that more mature form? (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2020)

File.Name: k709c_k709-019.jpg
Notes: 50095024198 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. They seem to come in all different sizes, from big to small, but they all have reddish eyes and red-tipped butt like the glowing ember of a cigar. (Look close!) Despite their unpleasant common and scientific names, I like these flies - they are large and husky, like they could take on anything. My photo shows the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2020)

File.Name: k709d_k707-041.jpg
Notes: 50095814197 This is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a bristly flower cluster of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that's pretty much gone to seed. I don't think it's the same nymph I showed last week in this photo, but it's possible. It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. These nymphs are particularly gorgeous. This photo shows the katydid's rear end, perfect for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2020)

File.Name: k710a_k710-005.jpg
Notes:
50098464153 Here is another shot of a caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly munching a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - but this is the same caterpillar I showed in this photo yesterday that looked quite brown! I guess the caterpillar did molt last night, at least it changed color from brown to green. This photo shows the caterpillar's enormous head, which looks like some sort of mask. It also shows two signs you can use to find these caterpillars in Coast Live Oak trees - a patch of webbing on the leaf that it hangs onto, and a leaf eaten down to the central vein on the right, see this photo and this photo. These caterpillars have been great fun to photograph this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2020)

It was warmer today, up into the high 80s on our upstairs porch. There was that same annoying breeze from the south, not a big wind, but it keeps everything in motion and makes getting my kind of photos difficult. The web program Ventusky has helped me understand this as an eddy wind coming around Point Conception.

File.Name: k710b_k710-014.jpg
Notes: 50098445453 This is another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) munching a succulent flower of native Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. It's the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. These nymphs are particularly gorgeous, and they seem to be rather common right now. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2020)
CommonNames: Katydid; Southern California Dudleya; Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: k710c_k710-018.jpg
Notes: 50099005266 This is a little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) that I always seem to find at this time of year perched on the steel guard rail of the little bridge across our creek. I don't know the proper name of this fly, but I've always called it the "Steel Bridge Fly", see this photo of the same kind of fly on the same bridge rail taken in July 2013. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2020)

File.Name: k710d_k710-036.jpg
Notes: 50098399298 Here are two shots of a little fly (Diptera) working new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it has "V"-shaped antennae like a Soldier Fly in the family Stratiomyidae that remind me of TV rabbit ears. Any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2020)

File.Name: k711a_k711-017.jpg
Notes:
50102806992 Here is another caterpillar of a Lycaenid butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) that's being tended by ants on a flowering branch of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). This is the butterfly group that includes Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks. The ants aren't attacking the caterpillar but tending it in exchange for the sweet honeydew they get by stroking it's back. I'm not sure what butterfly this caterpillar will become, but one possibility is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) which do tend to be quite hairy, see this photo and Alice Abelia's comment. Hah - is that its head or tail to the left?? (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2020)

It was HOT today, into the 90s - and tomorrow might be hotter. Yipes, it was 107∞ down by the river today! We did have the usual annoying breeze in the morning, but that does cool things down a bit with some marine influence - not bad when it gets this hot.
ScientificName: Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Strymon melinus
associatedTaxa: Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Strymon melinus

File.Name: k711b_k711-011.jpg
Notes: 50102732082 There's a lot happening in this photo, so look close. Ants are tending aphids of a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that's done flowering and starting to develop their seeds now. I believe the odd white things are the larvae of a Dusky Lady Beetle (Scymnini, Coccinellidae), probably the genus Scymnus. Those are white waxy outgrowths on their backs. The Mealybug Destroyer (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri) has similar outgrowths, but they look more ragged, and they prefer mealybugs - see the discussion that goes with this page at BugGuide. The ants were pretty excited as the larvae consumed their herd. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2020)
CommonNames: Deerweed; Dusky Lady Beetle; Mealybug Destroyer
ScientificName: Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Scymnini; Coccinellidae; Scymnus; Cryptolaemus montrouzieri
associatedTaxa: Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Scymnini; Coccinellidae; Scymnus; Cryptolaemus montrouzieri

File.Name: k711c_k711-030.jpg
Notes: 50101888178 This is looking into the funnel-like opening of the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods today - but what is it showing?? My first thought was that it's two spiders mating, but the one on top is all shriveled up. My second thought was that they were mating, but now the one is eating the other - it happens, see this photo of different spiders. But no, the spider on top has the slender palps of a female. I think my third thought is right, that it's a female spider just finishing its molt into a larger size. I'll go back tomorrow and see if I can find the cast-off skin or exuviae as it is properly called. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2020)

File.Name: k711d_k711-037.jpg
Notes: 50101868018 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on one of the last flowers of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that's mostly gone to seed at this point. It's probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with those hairy legs and abdomen. Mostly I've been seeing tiny ones, and I believe this is the largest one of these spiders I've seen this year. The babies are growing up! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2020)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Mecaphesa

File.Name: k712a_k712-023.jpg
Notes:
50106723317 Here are two shots of an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today - both on the flowers and in the air. Well, they might be the same butterfly, but maybe not - I didn't get the two photos together, but I did get them in the same plant. The butterfly is definitely a male with those blue upper wings that live up to it's common name. I like the shot in the air - you can see just enough detail. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2020)

It was HOT today, up to 96∞ (at least) in the shade on our upstairs deck this afternoon. It was cooler in the morning with our usual breeze from the south - welcome rather than annoying today - but that died down before noon. It was definitely too hot for insects or insect photographers, but I did my best and found a few (like myself) who lack the good sense to stay in the shade.
Locality: at least) in the shade on our upstairs deck this afternoon.  It was cooler in the morning with our usual breeze from the south - welcome rather than annoying today - but that died down before noon.  It was definitely too hot for insects or insect photographers

File.Name: k712b_k712-014.jpg
Notes: 50106681257 Here is the large caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) munching a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today, see this photo for the rest of the caterpillar. I like those little knobs on its back that look like playdough fire hydrants! These caterpillars have a way of being completely invisible until they catch you by surprise and suddenly jump out at you. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2020)

File.Name: k712c_k712-007.jpg
Notes: 50105834133 This is the second mature caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly that I've found this year - and actually watched turn into a mature green caterpillar, see this photo and this photo. Here it is happily munching leaves of of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - a different small tree from the caterpillar in my [Previous] photo. Its large head is remarkable, like it's wearing a mask. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2020)

File.Name: k712d_k711-043.jpg
Notes: 50106347336 This is another shot of the first mature caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly that I found this year in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I first found it back on July 2 when it was quite green, see this photo. But look how it changed yesterday between morning and afternoon - it's quite yellow now. I've actually seen these yellow caterpillars before, see this photo from a few years ago. I didn't post this photo yesterday hoping that the caterpillar would change even more by today, but instead it disappeared from the one branch where its been since I first found it. I'm thinking it's ready to molt or perhaps to pupate as a chrysalis. I'll keep my eyes out for it. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2020)

File.Name: k713a_k713-024.jpg
Notes:
50110098551 Here is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly showing what it's got on its upper wings. These butterflies are quite plain below, and they can almost disappear when they close their wings. That's how I first found this one, with wings closed. I waited for some time, and it finally flashed it's wings open several times - then it started ovipositing with wings closed again, see my [Previous] photo. I suspect that it opened its wings to startle me and scare me off - those eyes! - and then got on with its business when it didn't work. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2020)

It was still warm today, but in the mid-80s rather than the mid-90s like yesterday. There was the usual breeze from the south in the morning. Tomorrow is expected to be another 10 degrees cooler. I'm not complaining.

File.Name: k713b_k713-027.jpg
Notes: 50110310992 Here is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly ovipositing - laying eggs - on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). The plant is a perennial that is still just starting to come up before it flowers later in the summer. I've seen these butterflies show interest in this plant before, see this photo from last year. It's interesting how striking the butterflies are above - see my [Next] photo - and how plain they are below. That's camouflage - they can fold their wings up and virtually disappear. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2020)

File.Name: k713c_k713-030.jpg
Notes: 50109482503 Waiting for service?? This is another little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for lunch on a new flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). They can wait like this for hours and instantly spring into action when prey does arrive. I also like the stamens of these flowers - they remind me of those wide matches that some restaurants used to give out when I was a kid. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2020)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae

File.Name: k713d_k713-040.jpg
Notes: 50109994966 This is one of the first flower clusters of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the woods at home. It's one of my favorite mid-summer flowering plants - with flowers that actually look like wildflowers! I find some of my favorite summer butterflies on this plant as well, like this one and this one. It has a musty smell that is not as pleasant as some other members of its genus, but it is one of the smells of our summer. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2020)

File.Name: k714a_k714-028.jpg
Notes:
50113289558 Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly rising up over a cluster of new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden today. I like the way its tails stick out below the flowers. Like I said, I'm a sucker for swallowtails - I never can resist getting a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I'm staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. I did have time for a few photos before we left. It was a pleasant day with temps less than 80 degrees. I was expecting morning fog, but it didn't happen.

File.Name: k714b_k714-010.jpg
Notes: 50114038707 This is another shot of the large caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on a branch of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. Coffeeberry is not the same as Ceanothus, but it's in the same plant family, and I guess that's good enough. I don't usually find this caterpillar out in the open like this, so I took the opportunity to measure it with my finger - a bit over two joints of my index finger which comes to a bit over two inches. I've read that they can get up to twice this size, so it's still got some growing to do. Hah, it starting to remind me of Jabba the Hutt! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2020)

File.Name: k714c_k714-004.jpg
Notes: 50113205528 This is another interesting "true bug" - species Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) - on a flower stalk of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) that's mostly gone to seed now. I usually find these beautiful bugs on this plant and Bush Monkey Flower, often inside the flowers of the latter as in this photo. Their time is just about done, and this is the first one I've seen for a while. It's large, and I wonder if it's a female with eggs? (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2020)
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k714d_k713-016.jpg
Notes: 50113982522 Here are two shots of a mating pair of little Beetles (Coleoptera) "doing it" both above and below a flowering stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). I'm pretty sure they are Red-shouldered Leaf Beetles (Saxinis saucia) in the Chrysomelidae group of beetles, at least that is the genus. They are about the size of a ladybug. It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2020)

File.Name: k715a_k715-012.jpg
Notes:
50117593671 Here are two shots of "true bugs" on a plant of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden today. I believe the bugs are the species Cosmopepla uhleri - a native that belongs here, unlike the somewhat similar Bagrada Bug that is invading our area. I only find these bugs on this native plant. The photo on the right shows one of the bugs sucking on a seed capsule of the plant next to a hatchling nymph. The one on the left looks like it might be large with eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2020)

I had things to do in town this morning, but it didn't matter because we were socked in with marine-layer fog driven by fog winds from the south. I got home around noon when the fog was just starting to burn off - and the fog winds were even worse. Temps were in the 60s today, though they creeped into the 70s during the afternoon. Tomorrow sounds like more of the same.

File.Name: k715b_k715-001.jpg
Notes: 50117518866 Here is a dogpile of new caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly under their webbing in a native plant of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica, Scrophulariaceae). They probably come from the egg clutch of a single mama butterfly - see this photo of a mama ovipositing lots of eggs and this photo of eggs hatching. The caterpillars are quite social when they first emerge, and they make these communal nests of webbing to shroud their host plant as they feed - see my [Previous] photo for more info. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesdays group, but happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2020)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; California Figwort; California Bee Plant

File.Name: k715c_k714-009.jpg
Notes: 50116902803 This is webbing on a flowering stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae), but its the webbing of caterpillars and not spiders. You can see a couple of the caterpillars on the web, but most of them are further up along the stem. They are the caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, see this photo for the adult. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and they eat the leaves as they cover them with their shroud-like webbing. They will feed into the summer, and then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. Tents now mean butterflies next year. Spiders aren't the only ones, so happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2020)
CommonNames: Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Variable Checkerspot

File.Name: k715d_k713-033.jpg
Notes: 50117440071 This might be an insect's idea of the entrance to hell! It is looking into the funnel-like opening of the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on the ground a couple of days ago. If you look close, I think you can see at least one leg of the spider sitting at the bottom of its hole - see this photo for what might be down there. It's an impressive web design, so happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2020)

File.Name: k716a_k716-005.jpg
Notes:
50120999716 Here is another handsome nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It looks like it's blowing hot jet fuel from its butt! Those hairs make it look like the nymphs I find earlier in the year that I think are genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae, see this photo, but I think from the colors and time of year that It must be something else. I have another recent photo here, and I checked my records and found this photo of the same critter back in 2012 - also in July. I'm not sure what it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. I reckon its perfect for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2020)

All my photos today happen to be beautiful bug butt shots, that's just how it worked out. Today was much like yesterday with marine-layer fog driven by fog winds from the south spilling over the mountains in the morning. It cleared after noon, but the winds continued, warming up to the mid-70s as the day progressed. Tomorrow sounds like more of the same.
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Danepteryx; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: k716b_k716-017.jpg
Notes: 50120410373 Look close at this dead oak leaf - there's a cricket inside with just it's rear end and legs sticking out! I'm sure it's the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae). I believe they work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but they do it "Ostrich style" in the belief that if they can't see you then you can't see them - leaving certain body parts exposed. Other local crickets do something similar, compare this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2020)

File.Name: k716c_k712-038.jpg
Notes: 50120388998 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. It also provides a way for small critters like this wasp to get a drink without falling in. This photo shows the wasp's impressive "petiolate abdomen" - I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but HBBBT anyway! My photo also shows a drowned beetle in the upper-left. There must have been a hatch that day as there were literally 100s of the drowned beetles floating on the water surface - kind of creepy. Yeah, I got a photo which I suppose I'll post one of these days. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2020)

File.Name: k716d_k712-004.jpg
Notes: 50120935726 This is a rear view of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a cluster of new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. Actually, this is one of the not-so-pale swallowtails. These butterflies look less pale as we move further into summer - they get a cream color, and sometimes I confuse them with Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus) which are downright yellow. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2020)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Buddleja davidii; Scrophulariaceae; P. rutulus
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Buddleja davidii; Scrophulariaceae; P. rutulus

File.Name: k717a_k717-025.jpg
Notes:
50124558492 Here is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) flying into a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. This is the first one of these moths I've managed to photograph this year, and it came out surprising well. It's always a challenge to capture these moths that fly so fast among the summer flowers and (almost) never stop. I've always wondered what insect this flower with such long exserted stamens was designed for, and this might be the one - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2020)

The marine-layer fog spilled over the mountains again this morning, but it soon backed off. Unfortunately the "fog winds" that push the marine layer ashore remained until sometime in the afternoon. I take this as a sign that the marine layer is not so deep today, and it might not spill over the mountains at all tomorrow.
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k717b_k717-018.jpg
Notes: 50123704223 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) blowing a bubble on a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). I assume that bubble is really a drop of nectar. I watched the bee for a while, and it seemed to be working the drop back and forth, manipulating it with its proboscis. And what a proboscis! I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, though my first guess would be genus Lasioglossum - and aren't those short-tongued bees? (Hah, see this photo and this photo at BugGuide.) I first found what I believe is the same kind of bee working a nearby flower of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). That one got away from me, but then I found the one in my photo - it might even be the same bee. As I often say, any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2020)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Lasioglossum; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Lasioglossum; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: k717c_k716-023.jpg
Notes: 50124230991 Here are two different flies getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our summer-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. The fly on the right is a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae), maybe the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa), at least it's similar to that species. The fly on the left is more interesting, and I'm not sure what it is. If pushed to guess, I would say it's some sort of Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, though it's different from the usual brown ones that I find like this one. But that's just a guess, and any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2020)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Sinuous Bee Fly; Stiletto Fly

File.Name: k717d_k711-001.jpg
Notes: 50124205516 You have to look close at these flies to realize that they are not bees or wasps. This is a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) - maybe a Drone Fly (Eristalis tenax) or something similar. This one is visiting new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2020)
CommonNames: Syrphid Fly; Drone Fly; Butterfly Bush
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis tenax; Buddleja davidii; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis tenax; Buddleja davidii; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: k718a_k718-010.jpg
Notes:
50127997332 I found this little nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The plant is done flowering now, and is producing little pea-like fruit. If you look under the bug's chin, you can see that it has got its proboscis into the seed capsule. I don't know what kind of adult this one will become, but it's quite handsome as a nymph. Maybe it's the same kind as this one that I found last year on a different plant? Hah, I just noticed that I also found that one on July 18! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2020)

The marine-layer fog stayed on the ocean-side of the mountains today, but the "fog winds" did come across the mountains to stir things up. I hope the marine layer continues to diminish - maybe enough that we don't get the wind. It was cool today, barely up to 80 degrees.

File.Name: k718b_k718-024.jpg
Notes: 50127156883 Here is a tiny spider with its prey on a shrub of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I guess that the spider is a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, maybe a cribellate species as suggested by it's frizzy webbing, compare this photo and this photo. I think the prey is more interesting. It appears to be a fly (Diptera) as it has halteres and just one pair of wings, but its face looks totally strange! As always, any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2020)
CommonNames: Woolly Blue Curls; Mesh Web Weaver; fly

File.Name: k718c_k718-027.jpg
Notes: 50127139893 Here is another little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) casually standing between two flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The oversize stamens of the flowers go on and on out the top, see this photo from yesterday. These bugs are predators, and I guess this one is trying to figure out where to wait for prey on these odd flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k718d_k718-002.jpg
Notes: 50127674806 This is a tiny Weevil (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) next to some of the damage they do to new foliage of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. For scale, the spines of the oak leaves are about one millimeter long - compare the spines on the leaf to the right with the small fragment of a green leaf at the bottom of the stem to the left. These tiny weevils seem quite aware of intruders - I see them scatter whenever I try to get close. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2020)

File.Name: k719a_k719-029.jpg
Notes:
50131604006 Here is a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family in the woods today - the first flower I've seen this year in our home canyon, though I've seen them higher up in the mountains during a rare excursion. (Since I've gotten such a good start at it, I've decided to see how long I can keep posting only photos from home - I'm doing pretty good so far!) This plant is rated 1B.3 by the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants which I think means: uncommon, locally endemic, and not very endangered. The flowers are highly variable, and I want to photograph every one I see because they are all so different - see this photo. Expect more. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2020)

Today was just about a perfect day - no marine-layer fog, no fog winds, and warm without being too hot. *Sigh* Now there's talk of gusty sundowner winds from the north picking up later tonight and tomorrow. There's always something.

File.Name: k719b_k719-024.jpg
Notes: 50131789507 This is an Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper on a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). The grasshopper looks like a nymph at first with those stubby little wings, but those little stubs are all the wings it will ever get. The color of these grasshoppers is confusing. This one is tan, but others are quite dark - compare this photo. (Hah - and compare this photo at BugGuide by Alice Abelia of a completely black individual!) They just are variable, but they all have those little stubby wings. The deerweed is down to it's last few flowers now, but it still has fresh foliage and ripening seed capsules. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2020)

File.Name: k719c_k719-041.jpg
Notes: 50130971858 Here is a large Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on the sandstone rock we keep in a water tub by the summer-dry creek. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. These flies have antennae like an old-fashioned TV set and wonderful patterned eyes. I think of them as "compost flies" - see this photo of a maggot in our compost bin. They might actually do some good in the compost bin, see the article at Wikipedia, though it's interesting that I actually found this one some distance away from anyone's compost. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2020)

File.Name: k719d_k719-035.jpg
Notes: 50131514716 This is another little nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a ripening berry of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today, compare my [Previous] photo for a different one. This nymph was actually part of a small group, and once I noticed them, I started seeing more in the same shrub. I don't know what kind of adult this one will become, but it's quite handsome as a nymph. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2020)

File.Name: k720a_k720-003.jpg
Notes:
50135800252 I found this little Treehopper (family Membracidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is the Coast Live Oak Treehopper (Telamona vestita), a common name that I found at BugGuide. I like these little insects that look like thorns or bumps on a tree branch - though the mimicry is less effective on green leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2020)

Today started nice, though it was foggy in the early morning - fog from the valley-side not the ocean-side, that disappeared with nary a breeze. According to local automated weather stations, it did get windy last night - I guess I slept through it. A wind from the north has been picking up all afternoon, to 30+ mph so far. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory tonight.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Coast Live Oak Treehopper

File.Name: k720b_k720-015.jpg
Notes: 50134971238 I found this handsome Green Stink Bug (Pentatomidae) on an unripe berry of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods this morning. There are lots of different kinds of green bugs, maybe this one is species Chinavia hilaris? I've seen this kind before on this plant, see this photo from last year. I wonder if this is the adult that goes with the nymphs in this recent photo - compare this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2020)
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Chinavia hilaris
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Chinavia hilaris

File.Name: k720c_k720-025.jpg
Notes: 50135732672 Look close! I was lucky to spot this little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) snuggled into a mostly-done flower cluster of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae). I didn't notice at the time that it's upside down! I can't say why it's guarding the seed capsule above rather than the flower bud below, but I reckon it has its reasons. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k720d_k720-030.jpg
Notes: 50134910563 This is a small wasp getting a drink from the water tub we keep by the summer-dry creek. It's got the kind of petiole-like "double abdomen" that I associate with Potter Wasps aka Mason Wasps (Eumeninae, Vespidea, Hymenoptera) - compare this photo of another similar wasp with its mud nest. I like the dark shadows and bright points of light by its extremities - and that face on its butt! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2020)

File.Name: k721a_k721-007.jpg
Notes:
50139019771 Here is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick on a leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) in the woods today. If you look close, you can see that it's standing on two pairs of hind prolegs on the left with three pairs of true legs on the other end. The caterpillar is cantilevered out from the plant stem and held taut with a line of webbing. Pretty good camouflage - at least it would be if it was green or the leaf was brown. This one appears to be different from the one in my [Previous] photo, but who knows? (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2020)

Today started much like yesterday with early morning fog from the valley-side of the mountains that disappeared with nary a breeze. Then a breeze did start up from the ocean-side of the mountains, no fog but a breeze just as well. That breeze from the ocean kept it relatively cool today, up to about 80 degrees.

File.Name: k721b_k721-012.jpg
Notes: 50139218767 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) - with its shadow. Like all of these caterpillars, it stands on a platform of two pairs of hind prolegs with three pairs of true legs in front, and walks by looping along - caught here in the middle of a loop. This one appears to be different from the one in my [Next] photo, but who knows? (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2020)

File.Name: k721c_k721-025.jpg
Notes: 50139169112 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) trying to find an actual flower inside the green bracts of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). Later in the summer, the flowers stick out more, see this photo, but only the bees can find them at the beginning of their season like this. I'm sure this bee is family Megachilidae with pollen-collecting hairs on its abdomen, maybe genus Megachile. See this photo for a clue about how these bees use the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2020)

File.Name: k721d_k721-022.jpg
Notes: 50138371388 Look close to spot the yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on one of the last flower clusters of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I tried to move around to the other side to get a shot of its face, but I drove it away with my disturbance. I believe these spiders that wait on flowers (probably genus Mecaphesa) are slow color-shifters, and this one is now a good match for the flowers it's waiting on. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2020)

File.Name: k722a_k722-009.jpg
Notes:
50142587691 Here are two shots of an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. Well, they might be the same butterfly, but maybe not - I didn't get the two photos together, but I did get them on adjacent plants. It's definitely a male with those blue upper wings that live up to its common name. It's curious that I was finding mostly females earlier this year - including some that were ovipositing - and now I'm finding mostly males. Something seems wrong here. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2020)

Marine-layer clouds started to spill over the mountains this morning, but then they backed off. Unfortunately the annoying fog winds that push the clouds ashore continued most of the day. It was breezy, but there were also moments of relative calm to get my photos.

File.Name: k722b_k722-001.jpg
Notes: 50142015158 This is a tiny spider perched on its web stretched over a leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure the spider is a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider, though it seems unusually pale and even smaller than usual. The wind was starting to pick up as I got this photo, and I was pleasantly surprised at how it came out. I don't think it would work without the shadow to give a sense of depth. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2020)

File.Name: k722c_k722-030.jpg
Notes: 50142521446 I found these caterpillars with their webbing on a flower head of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) that's going to seed now. I'm sure these are the same web-spinning caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that I show in my [Previous] photo. What's odd is their choice of a host plant. I usually find these caterpillars on "scrophs" - plants that were once placed in the Scrophulariaceae plant family like Climbing Penstemon and Bush Monkey Flower, but here they are on a plant in the mint family. I've seen this before, see this photo and this photo. I guess there's always an exception. These caterpillars are web spinners whatever plant they're on, so happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2020)
ScientificName: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: k722d_k722-048.jpg
Notes: 50142491716 These are caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a native plant of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica, Scrophulariaceae). Compare this photo from a week ago, and you can see that they've grown. They probably all come from the egg clutch of a single mama butterfly - see this photo of a mama ovipositing lots of eggs and this photo of eggs hatching. The caterpillars are quite social when they first emerge, and they make communal nests of webbing to shroud their host plant as they feed. They are web-spinners, and they eat the leaves as they cover them with their shroud-like webbing. They will feed into the summer, and then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. Tents now mean butterflies next year. Usually I find these caterpillars of "scrophs" - plants that were once placed in the Scrophulariaceae plant family Climbing Penstemon and Bush Monkey Flower, but there are exceptions - see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2020)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; California Figwort; California Bee Plant
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: k723a_k723-032.jpg
Notes:
50146301327 We had a foggy morning with some drizzle today, and I found this scene on a spider web in a shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) while there was still some moisture in the air. Those green spheres are unripe berries - look in the drops for reflections too. This looks like an abstract ray tracing demo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2020)

It was foggy all morning, but for some reason there were no fog winds. There was some breeze from the south after noon when the fog started to burn off, but even that wasn't too bad. Temps were in the 60s for most of the day. Nice!

File.Name: k723b_k723-001.jpg
Notes: 50146016471 Here is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) carrying an ant for prey on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. This is the first one of these small (but effective) predators that I've seen since early February. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2020)

File.Name: k723c_k723-030.jpg
Notes: 50146232977 This is the hairy behind of a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on one of the last flower clusters of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is another genus Mecaphesa because of its hairy legs and abdomen. I have another shot of the same spider from a couple of days ago in this photo, and it's been staying in the same place. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2020)

File.Name: k723d_k718-026.jpg
Notes: 50146219872 This is the rear end of a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) standing between two flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. See this photo for the rest of the insect. Hah, it looks like it has a trap door on its butt - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k724a_k724-032.jpg
Notes:
50149703911 Here is a little Mosquito (Culicidae, Diptera) crawling out of its pupal shell in the water tub we keep by our summer-dry creek. At least that's what I think it is, though I can't see an actual proboscis. (Did I err?) It's standing for the first time - never mind that it's standing on water! This is the first adult that I've seen in the water tub, so it might be time to empty it before many more appear. Mosquitoes are flies, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2020)

There was no marine-layer fog today, but we did get the usual annoying "fog winds" - I guess the fog layer was sitting just below the mountain pass above us. It was a nice day, warm enough for shorts but still pleasantly cool, only in the 70s.

File.Name: k724b_k724-038.jpg
Notes: 50149910532 This is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) getting a drink from the sandstone rock in the water tub we keep by the summer-dry creek. I believe this fly is genus Villa in the tribe Villini - and maybe even species Villa lateralis which is said to have clear wings unlike most members of the group. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Villa; Villini; Villa lateralis
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Villa; Villini; Villa lateralis

File.Name: k724c_k719-031.jpg
Notes: 50149111768 This is a tiny humpbacked fly on a new flower of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae), a summer plant that's just starting to flower up here in the mountains. I believe the fly is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it a "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2020)
CommonNames: Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed; Bee Fly

File.Name: k724d_k718-014.jpg
Notes: 50149856902 Here two shots of a tiny fly (Diptera) on a plant of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae) that's just starting to flower. The fly is ovipositing on a flower bud on the left and looking for another bud on the right. My guess would be a that it's a Tephritid Fruit Fly in the family Tephritidae or maybe a Picture-winged Fly in the family Ulidiidae. As always, any help with ID will be appreciated. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed; Tephritid Fruit Fly; Picture-winged Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Hieracium argutum; Asteraceae; Tephritidae; Ulidiidae

File.Name: k725a_k725-028.jpg
Notes:
50153414862 Here is another mature caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly happily munching leaves of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. This is a different caterpillar on a different small tree from any of the others I've found this year. Its large head is remarkable, like it's wearing a mask. I've been finding more of these caterpillars lately than ever before - probably because I've been staying in the canyon and looking closer at what's here. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2020)

There was no morning fog today, but we still had those annoying onshore winds from the south that keep everything in motion. At least they are keeping the temperature down - it was mostly in the 70s today, though it did get up to 80∞ in the afternoon. I'll miss these days when it gets HOT again.

File.Name: k725b_k725-035.jpg
Notes: 50153126926 This is a remarkable Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae). The thistles were all done flowering, but this plant developed a new bud in one of the leaf axils which has just opened. You can see the wasp's "third eye" between its goggle-like real eyes - see this photo for another shot. These wasps seem to show up wherever there are summer flowers. They are not uncommon, but they are so fast and alert that it's hard to get decent photos of them. See this photo to appreciate how deep into the flowers its proboscis goes. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2020)

File.Name: k725c_k725-017.jpg
Notes: 50153314987 This is an interesting Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on a green stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), but I actually found it on native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) right next to it. I saw the grasshopper in the same place yesterday, and it also moved between the two plants. I don't know what kind of grasshopper it is. It's small, less than an inch long. It has skinny legs that stick out to the side. It reminds me of the Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in this photo and this photo, but it's clearly different - it has wing buds for one thing, and it's not so toothpick-like. Great eyes - a clue? I'll look for it again tomorrow. As always, any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2020)
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Morsea californica
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Morsea californica

File.Name: k725d_k725-041.jpg
Notes: 50152499168 Our katydids are growing up! I found this one today on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I suspect it is one of the handsome Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia like the one in this photo as it still has some color along its back. Maybe it's Scudderia mexicana? It has wings that are not mature yet, but they're just a molt away. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2020)
ScientificName: Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: k726a_k726-020.jpg
Notes:
50157516272 Here is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today, the first one I've managed to photograph this year. The thistles were all done flowering, but this plant developed a new bud in one of its leaf axils which has just opened - and the butterflies came!
