This is a male butterfly, and you can see just a hint of the dogface pattern under its forewing - try a search of my photos to see more. I'm still trying to get the perfect shot, but these fine butterflies always close their wings at rest, so it's a challenge to capture their upper wings. This is our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2020)

I did something to my back yesterday, so I took it easy today - pretty much sitting in one place by the thistle flower and letting the butterflies come to me. It was a nice sunny day with no particular breeze in the morning and warmer than its been, in the mid-80s.

File.Name: k726b_k726-037.jpg
Notes: 50157228701 This is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) working a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The thistles were all done flowering, but this plant developed a new bud in one of its leaf axils which has just opened. This is the largest of our grass-skippers. Sometimes I confuse them with Rural Skippers, but the dark antennae tips are a useful clue - compare this photo. I believe this is now their second brood of the year. Their first spring brood was quite sparse this year, though I did manage to get this photo in early May. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2020)

File.Name: k726c_k726-010.jpg
Notes: 50157440942 This is another shot of the large caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on a branch of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today - with my hand for scale. It's about the length of my index finger now, maybe three inches long. I've read that they can get up to four or five inches long, so it's not done yet. It's been missing since I got my last photo on July 14, and I'm glad I found it again today. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2020)

File.Name: k726d_k724-029.jpg
Notes: 50157174561 Here is another shot of the mature caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly in my [Previous] photo, taken the day before when I first found it happily munching leaves of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. This photo shows its remarkable face up close, like it's wearing a mask. Those two black spots on the top/left are just spots - its real eyes are the two rows of three spots each further down on its cheeks. I don't believe they see very well - just well enough to munch those leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2020)

File.Name: k727a_k727-041.jpg
Notes:
50160501493 This is a fresh-looking California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly getting a drink from the sandstone rock in the water tub we keep by the summer-dry creek. Actually, it's probably getting minerals from the rock, compare my [Previous] photo where one of these butterflies is getting minerals from me! They might be the same butterfly, but maybe not - there were several of these in the air today. This photo shows the lovely colors on top of its wings, see my [Previous] photo for what's down under. Hah, this might be a butterfly that came from one of the caterpillars I've been finding lately. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2020)

It was another nice sunny day with some breeze from the south in the morning. It was rather warm when the breeze stopped in the afternoon, in the mid-80s.

File.Name: k727b_k727-029.jpg
Notes: 50160463403 This is a fresh-looking California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly getting a taste of my leg today as I'm sitting by the water tub we keep by the creek. The butterfly was definitely more interested in my leg than the water in the tub, presumable for the minerals in my sweat. This photo shows the lovely colors under its wings, see my [Next] photo for what's on top. Hah, this might be a butterfly that came from one of the caterpillars I've been finding lately. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2020)

File.Name: k727c_k727-021.jpg
Notes: 50161227627 Here is another California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) in the woods today - but its a female rather than a male like the one in this photo from yesterday. The ladies are more plain than the males with no "dogface" pattern, simpler spots on its wings, and a paler yellow color that almost looks greenish when the light is just so. It's still an attractive butterfly, and this is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2020)

File.Name: k727d_k727-017.jpg
Notes: 50161207297 Here is another fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family in the woods today - only the second one I've seen open this year in our home canyon. The flowers are highly variable, and I want to photograph every one I see because they are all so different - see this photo. This photo is surrounded by butterflies in my photostream, and that's appropriate since this is a Mariposa Lily and "mariposa" means butterfly en espaÒol! (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2020)

File.Name: k728a_k728-008.jpg
Notes:
50164186603 Our katydids are growing up! I got this photo today on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but I actually found it nearby standing by it's exuviae or shed skin after a molt - see my [Previous] photo. I guess that means it is teneral, which explains the turquoise color of its front legs. I suspect it is one of the handsome Bush Katydids in the genus Scudderia, like the one in this photo, as it still has some color along its back. Compare this photo of a similar katydid taken a few days ago. Maybe these are Scudderia mexicana? Both have wings that are not mature yet, but they're just a molt or two away. It's interesting that I'm still finding tiny ones that look like they've just hatched. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2020)

It was another nice sunny day with some breeze from the south in the morning. It was rather warm when the breeze stopped in the afternoon, up to almost 90 degrees.
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; exuviae; teneral; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; exuviae; teneral; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: k728b_k728-004.jpg
Notes:
50164142083 This is the exuviae or shed skin of the Katydid (Tettigoniidae) in my [Next] photo. I found the katydid right by it in a small oak tree, so I have no doubt about its ownership - but the katydid moved as I tried to get a photo, so I have to show them in separate shots. It's interesting to think that every insect in the woods produces several of these in the course of growing up - that's a lot of exuviae! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2020)

Grammar note: That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. There's some discussion of this at BugGuide here and here.

File.Name: k728c_k727-001.jpg
Notes: 50164903557 Here are two photos of a gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) growing on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) taken on two successive days. The gall is changing color as it gets ready to fall. I went back today to get a third shot, but the gall fell away when I barely touched it. The galls form on the leaf buds of the tree, and grow to the size of a blueberry - and then they fall away to the ground. I'm confused by the timing of these galls, since I also find them in autumn, see this photo. There might be several different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k728d_k725-023.jpg
Notes: 50164632181 Here is a mating pair of Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) in the plush setting of a flowering shrub of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods a few days ago. What can I say? It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bugs; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k729a_k729-005.jpg
Notes:
50167740078 Here are more aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) in the woods today. If you look close, you can see the actual aphids on the deformed flowers - see this photo for a closer look. The aphids start on the foliage before the flowers open, see this photo from two months ago. By now, they've almost entirely moved inside the flowers - mostly deep inside so they can't be seen, but sometimes they come out like this. The deformed flowers look very different from normal flowers, compare this photo. By now the ungalled flowers are all done, but it's interesting that the galled flowers are still making random flower parts as though they are still trying to flower - like something from a horror movie. This is not beautiful, but very interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I'm tired now after a day out, and I'm keeping my photos and comments simple tonight. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. I did have time for a few photos before we left, like this one. It was warm again today, up to 90 degrees, and it might be even hotter for the next couple of days. Breeze is also in the forecast, a dangerous combination.

File.Name: k729b_k728-032.jpg
Notes: 50168502852 This is some critter's house in a carefully folded leaf of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. The shape looks square from this side and triangular from the other two sides. I don't know who lives inside, but after I noticed one of these folded leaves, I noticed a few more in both the same and different trees of the same kind. I suppose I'll have to open one up, but I hate to disturb the occupant. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2020)

File.Name: k729c_k727-009.jpg
Notes: 50167690793 Here is webbing on the underside of a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm not sure whose webbing it is, but I reckon it counts for a happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2020)

File.Name: k729d_k725-001.jpg
Notes: 50168207446 This is the web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in last year's plant of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). These spiders make a composite web that is both orb web and cobweb, but the haphazard section in hidden in the background. You can see the spider's retreat behind the orb web, I'll make a note. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2020)

File.Name: k730a_k730-003.jpg
Notes:
50171883517 Here is another shot of the same green Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) that I showed in this photo a few days ago. This time it's in the same plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) where I found it last time, but in that photo it had moved to a nearby plant of Deerweed. I still don't know what kind of grasshopper it is, but it seems to like this spot. It's a nymph with small wing buds. It has remarkable eyes. As always, any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2020)

It was HOT today, in the mid-90s. Fortunately, these was little wind - in fact, a little breeze would have felt pretty good. We're under a NWS Heat Advisory until Saturday night, and Friday might be the hottest day. There has been some concern about Sundowner Winds over the mountains, but there's nothing so far.

File.Name: k730b_k730-015.jpg
Notes: 50171604031 This is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's one of my favorite mid-summer flowering plants - with flowers that actually look like wildflowers! The spider is probably another genus Mecaphesa with those hairy legs and abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2020)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: k730c_k730-040.jpg
Notes: 50171588436 Here are a pair of Western Paper Wasps (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. It also provides a way for small critters like this wasp to get a drink without falling in. This photo shows the wasp's impressive "petiolate abdomen" for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2020)

File.Name: k730d_k725-040.jpg
Notes: 50171576331 This is the rear end of a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) - see this photo for the other end of the wasp, taken at the same time. The thistles were all done flowering, but this plant developed a new bud in one of the leaf axils which had just opened when I got this photo. These wasps seem to show up wherever there are summer flowers. They are not uncommon, but they are so fast and alert that it's hard to get decent photos of them. This one's for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2020)

File.Name: k731a_k731-010.jpg
Notes:
50175193732 I found this pair of mating flies (Diptera) of a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today - those are ripening berries of the shrub in the background. The flies are smaller than house flies, and it's interesting how different the male and female are - I don't think I would recognize them as the same kind if I saw them separately. It's also interesting that what I take as the male on the left does not have holoptic eyes that meet along the midline, compare this photo of different flies. HFDF and HIHD! (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2020)

It was HOT today, in the mid-90s again. Fortunately, there has been little wind so far, though we're under both a NWS Heat Advisory and a Wind Advisory tonight. There is a breeze now starting to pick up from the north. The combination of heat, wind, and cars on the road is worrisome.
ScientificName: Diptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; holoptic eyes
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; holoptic eyes

File.Name: k731b_k730-032.jpg
Notes: 50175160932 Here is a little Mosquito (Culicidae, Diptera) standing on the water in the tub we keep by our summer-dry creek right next to floating rafts of mosquito eggs - at least I think that's what they are. One mosquito couldn't possibly be responsible for all those eggs, but maybe it added one raft to the flotilla - and being responsible for just one of those rafts is impressive enough! Yeah, it's past time to empty the tub and start over. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2020)

File.Name: k731c_k729-002.jpg
Notes: 50174892786 I found this handsome fly on a frond of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) in the woods a couple of days ago. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it might be the same kind I show on the left in this photo. This is a handsome fly, and any help with ID will be appreciated. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2020)

File.Name: k731d_k727-005.jpg
Notes: 50175128572 This is a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) sucking on my shorts, probably after minerals from my sweat. Hah, is it trying to eat my shorts?? It looks like it might be biting me, but these flies don't have the mouthparts for that. I suspect this one is tribe Villini, maybe species Dipalta serpentina with those wings that look like camouflage netting, though I can't see the distinctive wing venation in my photo. It sounded buzzy in the air. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2020)

File.Name: k801a_k801-015.jpg
Notes:
50178671682 This fresh-looking California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly landed right next to me as I was tring to photograph something else in this small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. This photo shows the lovely colors under its wings, see this photo for what's on top. Hah, this might be the butterfly that came from one of the caterpillars I've been finding lately. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2020)

It was HOT today, up to 98∞ (at least) in the shade on our upstairs deck this afternoon. Fortunately, the winds did not develop either last night or this afternoon. It was definitely too hot for insects or insect photographers today, but I did my best and found a few (like myself) who lack the good sense to stay in the shade. Our NWS Heat Advisory expires tonight, but it sounds like it will continue to be just as hot for the next two days.
Locality: like myself) who lack the good sense to stay in the shade.   Our NWS Heat Advisory expires tonight

File.Name: k801b_k801-038.jpg
Notes: 50177842463 Here is a tiny "Calochortus Bee" (my name) collecting pollen from the stamens of a summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Hah, "collecting pollen" is a misnomer - this bee is swimming in it! And look how the bee has compacted the pollen into something like plastic or wax on its hind legs. These bees are quite shy, but it's interesting how often I catch them looking right at me like this one is - see this photo and this photo. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae - maybe Perdita californica or P. calochorti. I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. I don't know how they manage. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2020)

File.Name: k801c_k801-026.jpg
Notes: 50178342811 I found this little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) inside a new summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. At first I thought maybe it was just overcome by the beauty of its surroundings, but I poked it - the bee is quite dead with no apparent cause. I'm sure this bee is family Megachilidae with pollen-collecting hairs on its abdomen, maybe genus Megachile. This happens to be the same flower where I found the Ambush Bug in my [Next] photo. I'm not saying that that predator brought down this bee . . . but it's possible. Another mystery is where this bee has been to collect such yellow pollen on its abdomen - see my [Next] photo for the pollen of this plant, which is not yellow. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2020)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Megachilidae; Megachile
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Megachilidae; Megachile

File.Name: k801d_k731-030.jpg
Notes: 50178577387 Here is a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) clinging to the outside of a new summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. I got this photo yesterday, but it's interesting that this is the same flower where I found the dead bee in my [Next] photo today. Now, I'm not saying that this predator brought down that prey . . . but it's possible. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily

File.Name: k802a_k802-014.jpg
Notes:
50182841067 This is a Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly working new new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon at home. The butterfly is probably a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) which is distinctive mostly by having nothing very distinctive about it - plain but handsome. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2020)

Today was hot and muggy. There was a thin layer of haze this morning that seemed to cut the heat a few degrees, but it also seemed to noticeably increase the humidity - up to near 50%. The haze and humidity diminished in the afternoon, and the temperature did get back up into the high-90s. There has been a breeze today, mostly from the south.
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Woodland Skipper
ScientificName: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides
associatedTaxa: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: k802b_k802-016.jpg
Notes: 50182531376 Here is another good-sized caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. This is a different shrub from where I was finding these caterpillars back in June, see this photo. Those caterpillars disappeared and are probably butterflies by now, so I was happy to find this new one somewhere else in the woods today. Hah, just by chance I happened to find a new "bird turd" stage caterpillar in that other shrub that looks like this one. Note that the caterpillar in my photo today has a cushion of webbing like they always seem to have - something to hang on to? (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2020)

File.Name: k802c_k802-034.jpg
Notes: 50182745447 I found this handsome Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae on a stem of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods this morning. I find this a confusing group, but this one might be the Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens), though there are other possibilities. If it is that species, they don't always have that red line across the thorax - in fact, sometimes they look completely brown. Like I said, it's a confusing group - but a handsome bug. (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2020)
CommonNames: Green Stink Bug; Woolly Blue Curls; Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens

File.Name: k802d_k802-042.jpg
Notes: 50181913943 Here are two different summer flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae) showing just how variable the flowers are. I want to photograph every one I find! It's also remarkable when they choose to flower - months after our last real rainstorm. (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2020)

File.Name: k803a_k803-004.jpg
Notes:
50186937177 This fresh-looking Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly landed right next to me as I was trying to photograph something else in this small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning - much like this photo of a different butterfly in the same tree a couple of days ago. No time to think - just get the photo! Note that this swallowtail is not at all pale - they get a cream color in summer, but never so bright yellow as the Western Tiger Swallowtail in this photo. Hah, this might be the butterfly that came from one of the caterpillars I've been finding lately. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2020)

It was hot again today, into the upper 90s again on our upstairs deck but not quite into triple digits. A pretty good breeze has developed from the north this afteroon, to about 30 mph. We're under another NWS Wind Advisory for the night.

File.Name: k803b_k803-042.jpg
Notes: 50186085298 Look close! This is a remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). If you can't see the caterpillar, its head is by the topmost flower on the right - then work backwards. They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they finally transform into the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and has decorated itself with the flower bits of the same flowers. I only found it because it was moving when nothing else was. This is the first one I've seen this year, and I've been looking for them. See this photo from last year of one that's been feeding on Woolly Blue Curls - it looks completely different. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2020)
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: k803c_k803-014.jpg
Notes: 50186854017 Here is another shot of the little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey in a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today - see my [Previous] photo for the whole picture. These flowers have stamens that go on and on out of my photo, but this ambush is planned just for the body of the flower. The only visitor to the flower while I was there was a tiny wild bee on the stamens way off to the right, but the predator is well placed for whatever else comes along looking for nectar rather than pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: k803d_k803-013.jpg
Notes: 50186032428 Here is a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey in a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods. Yes, the stamens of these flowers go on and on - and the only visitor to the flower while I was there was a tiny wild bee on the end of the stamens, a couple on inches away from the planned ambush. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot of the predator. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: k804a_k804-003.jpg
Notes:
50189867758 This is a feisty little Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly on a flower stalk in the mint patch in our garden this morning. I say it's feisty because it was sitting on a perch chasing any other butterflies away - at least it was trying to sit on a perch, but the equally feisty photographer kept getting too close and forcing it to move. I finally got my photo and let it get back to work guarding the mint patch. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2020)

Today was a nice day, warm but not hot, up into the mid-80s in the afternoon. There was some breeze, not bad in the morning but up to 30 mph as evening approaches. The marine layer might return over the mountains tonight.

File.Name: k804b_k804-009.jpg
Notes: 50190621502 Here is another shot of the large caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on a branch of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. More and more I'm thinking of Jabba the Hutt! It's about the length of my index finger up to my knuckle now, maybe four inches or a bit more. I've read that they can get up to five inches long, so it might be getting close to the end. There's been quite a change from the first time I found this caterpillar when it was less than an inch long, see this photo and this photo. Now comes the biggest change from caterpillar to adult moth, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2020)

File.Name: k804c_k804-039.jpg
Notes: 50190559177 I found this adult Katydid (Tettigoniidae) in a small oak tree today, the first adult that I've seen this year. It's a female with a visible ovipositor if you look close. I believe it is one of the Bush Katydids in the genus Scudderia, probably the Mexican Bush Katydid (Scudderia mexicana). I've been following their development from tiny nymphs to larger nymphs to mature nymphs and now to adult. We also have the California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) with plain green nymphs and more leaf-like wings, see this photo. The adults of both kinds seem to be much less common than the nymphs. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2020)
CommonNames: Katydid; Bush Katydids; Mexican Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: k804d_k804-018.jpg
Notes: 50190529107 This isn't the best photo, but it's a gorgeous creature that I want to document. It's a different Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a stem of non-native White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus, Fabaceae) growing along the road in the canyon. I believe it is species Pselliopus spinicollis. It's rather small, less than 1/2 inch long, see this photo for a better view. I usually find a few of these from February to April, so it's interesting to find one now. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis

File.Name: k805a_k805-009.jpg
Notes:
50193888581 Here are the faux "eyes" on the back of a caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly that is stretched out on a new leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. Hah, Julie says it reminds her of Cecil the Seasick Sea Serpent, and the eyes do look like they're drawn on. The caterpillar's real head is pressed down against the leaf - it is always in this pose when I find it, and I've never actually seen its face. It's not huge like the caterpillar in this photo, maybe the size of a child's thumb. I have another recent photo of the same caterpillar here. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2020)

There were no marine-layer clouds coming over the mountains today, but I kept looking up expecting to see the fog coming because it felt like the usual fog winds from the south. It was pleasantly cool today, up to about 80 degrees. Maybe the marine layer will return over the mountains tonight.

File.Name: k805b_k803-027.jpg
Notes: 50194080242 Here are mating "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a flower head of I'm pretty sure this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly working new flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). These skippers have little translucent spots near the tips of their forewings which are visible with the light behind and visible on both wings in my photo.. Maybe they are Plant Bugs in the family Miridae or something similar with four-segmented antennae. I figure that's the smaller male on top because the larger female below needs room for her eggs. It's Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Ochlodes agricola; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Ochlodes agricola; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Miridae

File.Name: k805c_k804-025.jpg
Notes: 50193244658 This is the bowl-shaped web of a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela) in this case. This web is in a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). It consists of three main parts. The main part is "the bowl" of non-sticky webbing. The spider hangs below this and grabs its prey from underneath. Below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is random webbing that supports the bowl and might knock down flying insects into it. The spider is in my photo but hard to see, I'll make a note. Compare the dome-shaped web in my [Previous] photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2020)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Bowl and Doily Spider; Chamise
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; Frontinella communis; Frontinella pyramitela; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: k805d_k801-045.jpg
Notes: 50194016722 This is the dome-shaped web of a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, probably either the Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata) or the Sierra Dome Spider (Neriene litigiosa). This web is in a thicket of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). The webbing also extends upward as a lattice of unorganized strands that support the main web and maybe knock down possible prey. The spider is in my photo but hard to see, I'll make a note. Compare the bowl-shaped web in my [Next] photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2020)
CommonNames: Filmy Dome Spider; Sierra Dome Spider; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Neriene radiata; Neriene litigiosa; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; Neriene radiata; Neriene litigiosa; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: k806a_k806-006.jpg
Notes:
50196659363 Here are a mating pair of Green Stink Bugs in the family Pentatomidae among new flowers of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. They are probably the same Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens) that I recently showed in this photo. I found a nymph that looks completely different in another of the same plants a few feet away, but I wonder if they are the same kind - see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2020)

The marine layer caught up with us last night, and we woke up in a cloud. I found other things to do in the morning, and I managed to get out with my camera when it started to clear up before noon. The fog retreated to the edge of the mountains, but the fog winds remained. Nothing to do but goose up the ISO a notch and take lots of photos to get a few keepers. We'll probably be in for a few days of this typical "June gloom" weather.
CommonNames: Green Stink Bugs; Bird's Beak; Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens

File.Name: k806b_k806-005.jpg
Notes: 50197441897 I found this little nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a flower cluster of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind of adult this one will become, but it's quite handsome as a nymph and will probably look quite different as an adult. Maybe it will become the same kind of adult as in my [Next] photo taken on the same kind of plant? (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2020)

File.Name: k806c_k804-020.jpg
Notes: 50196611813 This is a different Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) about to fly away from me. It's on a leaf of non-native White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus, Fabaceae) growing along the road in the canyon. It's rather small, 1/2 inch long or less. I believe it is species Pselliopus spinicollis, see this photo for another shot of the same one and this photo for a better shot of a different one. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis

File.Name: k806d_k731-006.jpg
Notes: 50196571993 I found this late-stage nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) in a small oak tree a few days ago. I can't explain why it looks like it's been rolling in the dirt because it was head-high in the tree. It looks a little "chunkier" than the nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia) that I show in this photo, and I suspect this is our other common katydid - a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) with plain green nymphs like in this photo. I figure this is a nice shot of the critter's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2020)

File.Name: k807a_k807-018.jpg
Notes:
50201037992 Here is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly showing all its "eyes" on a shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods today. I usually have trouble getting close to these butterflies, but when I do they sometimes freeze and sit quietly with their wings closed - it can be quite startling when they suddenly flash their wings open! I guess that's the point. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2020)

I was expecting marine-layer fog this morning, but it was sunny and clear instead. It was on the cool side, barely getting up to 80∞ in the afternoon, and not much wind. Very nice.

File.Name: k807b_k807-026.jpg
Notes: 50200724741 Here is a caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly munching a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. I've been watching this particular caterpillar for a while, and this is the first time I've seen its face - usually it is hunched over with its face pressed down on a leaf, see this photo from two days ago. In this photo today you can clearly see the two features that distinguish true caterpillars from other look-alikes like the sawfly wasp larva in this photo: it has no more than five pairs of prolegs behind its true legs and a broken ring of eyespots on its face; whereas sawfly larvae have more prolegs and only a single eyespot. I've been fooled more than once. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2020)

File.Name: k807c_k807-002.jpg
Notes: 50200698946 I found this small Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) by the creek this morning. These are attractive flies that are no bother at all except to the aphids and other small critters their larvae prey on. There are many different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2020)

File.Name: k807d_k804-033.jpg
Notes: 50200939717 This is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the Diptera - probably genus Cylindromyia and maybe species Cylindromyia intermedia. It's atypical because it doesn't resemble a big bristly fly like this one. I found it perched for a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The larvae of these flies are predators and parasitoids of other arthropods, often caterpillars. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2020)
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Cylindromyia intermedia; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Cylindromyia intermedia; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k808a_k808-018.jpg
Notes:
50204297076 Here is the caterpillar of an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on an Everlasting plant in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm pretty sure the plant is Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum), but the butterflies seem to use all different everlasting and cudweed plants. These caterpillars change as they grow through different stages or instars, but they are also quite variable - compare this photo and this photo to appreciate how different they can be. It's curious that I was finding lots of these caterpillars back in June, and now here is one more. I've only seen one of the adult butterflies so far back in early June, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2020)

There was no marine-layer fog coming over the mountains today, but there was plenty of gusty "fog wind" - the winds from the ocean-side of the mountains that usually push the clouds ashore. It was also cool today, only in the 70s. There were times when I was ready for more than my usual shorts and Hawaiian shirt.

File.Name: k808b_k808-026.jpg
Notes: 50204235706 This large black Bumble Bee (Bombus) has managed to find a real flower of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. The plants' flowers are normally hidden between green bracts, but somehow the insects find them. This bee does have a yellow face, so maybe it's the Yellow-faced Bumble Bee (Bombus vosnesenskii) - but maybe not, there are other similar bees. I have to admit that bumble bees are not my favorites to photograph - most are so dark and hairy that it's hard to show details. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2020)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Bird's Beak; Yellow-faced Bumble Bee
ScientificName: Bombus; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Bombus vosnesenskii
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Bombus vosnesenskii

File.Name: k808c_k808-008.jpg
Notes: 50204463767 This is a bristly fly (Diptera) on a bristly plant. The plant is the same Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that I show in my [Previous] photo. I suspect the fly is a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) because of its bristles and general gnarliness, but I don't really know. Friday is fly day, which of course means that Saturday is inevitably a good day to find interesting flies! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2020)
ScientificName: Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Tachinidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Tachinidae

File.Name: k808d_k807-012.jpg
Notes: 50204432132 The plants of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) have finally stopped making new flowers and are going about ripening their seeds. The old flowers along the stems do look something like Woolly Bear caterpillars. The calyx lobes of the individual flowers make neat little cages with the seed pods inside, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2020)

File.Name: k809a_k809-009.jpg
Notes:
50208468266 Here is another Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly on new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon at home today. Hah, look just below the dark tip at the end of the butterfly's left antenna - see the spider?? It appears to be a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae), and it seems to have the good sense to get away. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2020)

We had no marine-layer clouds today, but we did have those annoying gusty winds from the south/ocean side of the mountains. The wind makes getting my kind of photos difficult and takes some of the fun out of it.
CommonNames: Umber Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Poanes melane; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Poanes melane; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Thomisidae

File.Name: k809b_k809-027.jpg
Notes: 50207892078 This is a handsome little beetle (Coleoptera) on the hairy edge of a summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I'm pretty sure this critter is a Soft-winged Flower Beetle (Melyridae), maybe genus Collops and species Collops quadriguttatus. This is the first one I've noticed this year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2020)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Soft-winged Flower Beetle
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Melyridae; Collops; Collops quadriguttatus
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Melyridae; Collops; Collops quadriguttatus

File.Name: k809c_k809-013.jpg
Notes: 50208650297 This is a tiny humpbacked fly on new flowers of native Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum, Asteraceae). It's the same woolly plant that the caterpillars of the American Lady butterflies use, see my photo two clicks back. The caterpillars have eaten most of the flowers, but they missed a few. I believe the fly is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it the "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. It seems to have a glob of pollen stuck to its hind leg - like a bee. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2020)

File.Name: k809d_k809-004.jpg
Notes: 50207796998 Here are two shots of a tiny spider on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's less than 1/8 inch long. I guess from its size and markings that it's a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, possibly genus Dictyna. It has the kind of frizzy webbing that I associate with the cribellate members of that group including this genus, see this photo for more info and links. The spider seems to have a layer of webbing across the surface of the leaf which I'll try to get a decent photo of for the Web Wednesdays group later this week. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2020)

File.Name: k810a_k810-005.jpg
Notes:
50212689452 Here is one more shot of the giant caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on a branch of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today - and the answer to a stumper. Look at this photo or this photo of the same caterpillar and it appears to only have two pairs of true legs - but all caterpillars should have three pairs of legs. I think you can see the missing leg in my photo today - buried in the folds of flesh around its head. It's really starting to remind me of Jabba the Hutt! The caterpillar is about five inches long now. It's been missing for a few days, and I thought maybe it had gone off to pupate - not yet, but it won't be long. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2020)

Today started sunny and calm and a few degrees warmer than yesterday, but then the usual annoying wind from the south started up. At least it started later and ended earlier than yesterday, so maybe we're heading in the right direction. We might actually be heading into another heat wave next weekend when a cool breeze from the south will feel great.

File.Name: k810b_k810-017.jpg
Notes: 50212649062 This looks like the work of a terrestrial caddisfly, but I believe it is the stone nest of a Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae, probably genus Dianthidium. The whole nest is about 1/2 inch across, and the stones are tiny. It was located about waist high on a dead stem of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), and made entirely out of small stones that are glued together. I inadvertently broke the stem trying to get close to it, and I'm glad I did - otherwise I wouldn't have seen the exit hole on the side where a new bee got out. (Did I get that right, or is the bee tending it's nest?) The stones probably came from our nearby chipseal road. The glue that's holding it all together is visible in the cracks, and I guess its some sort of wax or resin. I don't find these interesting nests very often, and I don't know that I've seen the bee that's responsible. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2020)

File.Name: k810c_k810-035.jpg
Notes: 50211800938 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a dead leaf in the mint patch of he garden this afternoon. I guess it's genus Phidippus with those reddish colors, possibly a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni), though its back is more orange than red, and it seems smaller than that species. A moment later, it jumped and was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2020)

File.Name: k810d_k810-028.jpg
Notes: 50211765188 This is a neatly packaged flower bud of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). Compare this photo of an open flower and imagine all that summer goodness wrapped up in such a neat package! The flowers have been going since mid-July, and they're still going strong. I want to photograph every one I see because they are all so different. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2020)

File.Name: k811a_k811-007.jpg
Notes:
50216551856 Here is a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly working new flowers in the mint patch of our garden this morning. This butterfly may be humble, but it has its charms - like those soccer ball eyes. The Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterflies in this photo look similar, but they are slightly more ornate. This is the first of these butterflies that I've managed to photograph this year, but they are not a high priority like certain other butterflies that I'm still looking for. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2020)

It was a few degrees warmer today and a bit less breezy from the south - these are no doubt related since the south wind comes from the cool ocean. Now we're working up to another heat wave for next weekend.

File.Name: k811b_k811-008.jpg
Notes: 50215866188 This is a white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly on new flowers in the mint patch of the garden this morning. It's tattered, and you have to look close to spot the remnant of a white fringe on the trailing edge of its hindwings. Without the white fringe, it looks more like a Propertius Duskywing (E. propertius). The similar Funereal Duskywing (E. funeralis) butterfly also has a fringe, but that one is smaller and darker, more black than brown. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2020)
ScientificName: Erynnis tristis; E. propertius; E. funeralis
associatedTaxa: Erynnis tristis; E. propertius; E. funeralis

File.Name: k811c_k811-020.jpg
Notes: 50215836278 This is another caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly in repose on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. It's probably the same caterpillar that I showed in this photo a few days ago, but that one disappeared until I found this one today a few feet away. It's not huge, less than two inches long. If you look close, you can see that it's resting on a bed of webbing. I'll have another photo for the Web Wednesday group tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2020)

File.Name: k811d_k808-030.jpg
Notes: 50215804883 Here is another mating pair of Stink Bugs in the family Pentatomidae among new flowers of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. They are probably the same Western Red-shouldered Stink Bugs (Thyanta pallidovirens) that I recently showed in this photo despite being different colors. They don't seem to mind that they're different, and they don't really stink - they smell like cilantro! It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2020)
CommonNames: Stink Bugs; Bird's Beak; Western Red-shouldered Stink Bugs
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens

File.Name: k812a_k811-015.jpg
Notes:
50220511882 Here is a tiny spider sitting in the center of its web stretched over leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The whole web is only a couple of inches across, and this is just the center of it. I believe the spider is a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata), though it seems unusually pale and even smaller than usual. I hope to get a better shot of this spider's web for next week's Web Wednesdays group, but Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. I did have time for a few photos before we left, but this one is from yesterday. It was warm today, up into the 90s, and that's just the beginning of our weekend heat wave. We're under a NWS Excessive Heat Warning for the weekend - and we actually have a 20% chance of showers tomorrow, a remnant of once-hurricane Elida! Rain seems unlikely, but the humidity might be uncomfortable.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Six-spotted Orb Weaver

File.Name: k812b_k812-003.jpg
Notes: 50220468542 Here is webbing under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It's about the size of a dime, and it appears to be a nest with eggs visible near the bottom. The leaf was originally covering the nest, and I had to prop the leaf up to let some light in by hooking one of its handy spines under a neighboring leaf - visible on the left. I'm not sure if this nest belongs to an insect or a spider, but it is definitely a web - so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2020)

File.Name: k812c_k807-038.jpg
Notes: 50219561128 This is the "cushion of webbing" where a caterpillar of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly has been resting on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), see this photo for a similar caterpillar on its webbing. It's my impression that the caterpillars make this cushion to have something to hang onto when they are resting. This caterpillar has now left this particular leaf, and moved over a few feet, see this photo taken yesterday. Spiders aren't the only ones, so happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2020)

File.Name: k812d_k808-003.jpg
Notes: 50219529008 This is a tiny spider with its prey (an even tinier fly) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods a few days ago. The spider is less than 1/8 inch long, hiding under a thin veil of webbing that runs along the main vein of the leaf. I guess from its size and markings that it's a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, possibly genus Dictyna - see this photo for more of the same spider. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesdays group, but happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2020)

File.Name: k813a_k813-003.jpg
Notes:
50223334963 This caterpillar looks like it's on an amusement park ride! It's munching a large hole in one of the terminal "grown together" leaves of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) like in this photo. I don't know what kind of caterpillar it is, but it has a little horn on its tail that looks like a Sphinx moth (Sphingidae). (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2020)

It was hot and muggy today as tropical weather streamed in, the remnant of once-hurricane Elida. It was one of those days when you start sweating just sitting in the shade. The temperature got up into the mid-90s - and that's with the clouds that must have cut the heat a bit. Fortunately there was little wind today, though a breeze would have felt pretty good. Yipes, our NWS Excessive Heat Warning starts on Friday and runs through Monday.

File.Name: k813b_k813-025.jpg
Notes: 50224161367 This is a goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera in a plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. I've seen a couple of these so far this year, but they are wary and don't like being photographed, and this is the first one I've managed to get. They're not large, something less than 3/4 inch long. They look like they've been assembled out of toothpicks! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2020)
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: k813c_k813-026.jpg
Notes: 50223262358 Here are two shots of a goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera in a plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). The differing color values in my two photos are a white balance thing, the result of changing light today as the sun was in and out of the clouds. See my [Next] photo for the rest of the grasshopper, but these two shots show the grasshopper's rear end. If you look close (especially on the left) you can see that it has little hooks - which I imagine match little sockets on it's partner's rear end. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2020)
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: k813d_k809-001.jpg
Notes: 50224111992 You have to look close to spot this nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding in a spent flower cluster of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. These little crickets like to hide "Ostrich-style" figuring that if it can't see you then you can't see it. It's not the best strategy for hiding, but it makes for great butt shots - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2020)

File.Name: k814a_k814-017.jpg
Notes:
50227533827 Here is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon this morning. This is a female rather than a male like the one in this photo from a few weeks ago. The ladies are more plain than the males with no "dogface" pattern, simpler spots on its wings, and a paler yellow color. Those pointy forewings are distinctive for both male and female. It's still an attractive butterfly, but you have to see a male with its wings open to appreciate why it is the official California State Butterfly - like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2020)

As expected, today was hot and muggy with temps just shy of 100 degrees on our upstairs porch. Yipes, it was 107∞ down by the river just a few miles away. Our NWS Excessive Heat Warning started today and has been extended through Wednesday, and it sounds like the hot weather will last for at least another week. There's more info at the California Weather Blog under the title: "Dangerously intense, prolonged, and humid heatwave for most of California." Now at 7:30 pm, it's still almost 90∞.
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: k814b_k814-019.jpg
Notes: 50226642868 Here is a little Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the canyon this morning. The butterfly is probably a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) which is distinctive mostly by having nothing very distinctive about it - plain but handsome. The small size and orange antennae tips are useful distinguishing marks. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2020)
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Woodland Skipper
ScientificName: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides
associatedTaxa: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: k814c_k810-012.jpg
Notes: 50227466852 This is a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) on the steel guard rail of the little bridge across our creek. It's another "Steel Bridge Fly", see this photo of different Bee Fly on the same bridge rail. I believe this one is tribe Villini, maybe species Dipalta serpentina with those wings that look like camouflage netting, though I'm not sure if the wing venation is right. It's a large fly that sounds buzzy in the air, and it's probably the same kind of fly that was trying to "eat my shorts" in this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2020)

File.Name: k814d_k809-014.jpg
Notes: 50227221416 This is a tiny humpbacked fly on new flowers of native Wright's Cudweed (Pseudognaphalium microcephalum, Asteraceae). I believe the fly is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it the "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. It seems to have a glob of pollen stuck to its hind leg - like a bee. I have another similar shot of the same fly in this photo - look close and you will see that the fly has moved from a front flower to the back one. This one's for the Fly Day Friday group - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2020)

File.Name: k815a_k815-028.jpg
Notes:
50231164492 This is an interesting Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata on a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. It looks like a damselfly, but it perches with wings open like a proper dragonfly. I believe this one is the California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica), a male with those "claspers" at the end of its abdomen. Great eyes! This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2020)

We had a little bit of everything but wind with our weather today! It was hot and muggy, up to 104∞ on our upstairs deck with 30-40% humidity. There were clouds streaming over, and there was thunder and lightning - though the lightning was too far away to see, west of us towards Lompoc. We even had a bit of rain - not enough to measure, but the woods had that wonderful smell of summer rain. I believe all this interesting weather is coming from the remnants of once-hurricane Elida down in Baja. Mostly it was just HOT today, up to 112∞ down by the river just a few miles away. We're in for a week of this hot weather.
CommonNames: Spreadwing Dragonfly; Bitter Gooseberry; California Spreadwing
ScientificName: Lestidae; Odonata; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Archilestes californica
associatedTaxa: Lestidae; Odonata; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Archilestes californica

File.Name: k815b_k815-011.jpg
Notes: 50230905726 Here is another Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the woods this morning. If you compare the Woodland Skipper in this photo from yesterday, see how the antennae are colored differently - dark in this one, orange in the other. These butterflies are common right now on our summer flowers - even when it's too hot for man or beast like today. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2020)
CommonNames: Umber Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: k815c_k815-024.jpg
Notes: 50230852746 Here is a line of tiny insect eggs on a stem of non-native Smilo Grass aka Rice-Millet or Millet Mountain Rice (Stipa miliacea aka Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family. I found this grass growing from inside a shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today - it has a nasty habit of crowding out other natives by starting inside them. This one is ripening its tiny seeds that don't stick in your socks but are easy to transport - see this photo of a shoeful! I figure this is one of the worst weeds in our mountains, one that spreads along trails into the backcountry. I don't know whose eggs these are, but I would guess some kind of true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera). I hope the nymphs eat that grass! (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2020)
CommonNames: Smilo Grass; Rice-Millet; Millet Mountain Rice
ScientificName: Stipa miliacea; Piptatherum miliaceum; Poaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Stipa miliacea; Piptatherum miliaceum; Poaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: k815d_k815-014.jpg
Notes: 50230833081 This is the first adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) that I've seen this year, though I've been seeing lots of nymphs. It's hiding among dead leaves and a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). This one is female with narrow wings and an ovipositor in the rear. It's the males with broad wings (like this) that do the singing later in summer and autumn. I wonder what this lady is doing out so early? (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2020)
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: k816a_k816-009.jpg
Notes:
50235068846 I found this interesting nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I suspect it is species Heza similis, there's some info at BugGuide and at iNaturalist. Both sites only record this species from Arizona, so it's an interesting find here in California. I've seen these different assassins before, see this photo and this photo of nymph and adult from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2020)

It was hot and muggy again today, but we had a break because there was a cooling breeze from the ocean-side of the mountains. It did get into the 90s, but it was only in the upper-80s for much of the day - it made up for it with near 50% relative humidity. We've been under three NWS warnings today - a Red Flag Warning that expired this morning, a Flash Flood Watch that will expire tonight, and an Excessive Heat Warning that goes on through Thursday. It will get hotter before it starts to cool down. We don't have AC at home, but we do have a swamp cooler that helps.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis

File.Name: k816b_k816-005.jpg
Notes: 50235036506 Here is an adult Stilt Bug (Berytidae) on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's a bit overexposed, but I like how you can see its long proboscis folded under its head. These bugs look a bit like skinny Assassin Bugs, see my [Next[ photo, but I believe they only suck plants and not other insects. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2020)

File.Name: k816c_k816-016.jpg
Notes: 50235017201 This is another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper on a plant of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). This grasshopper looks like a nymph at first with those stubby little wings, but those stubs are all the wings it will ever get. The color of these grasshoppers is confusing. This one is tan, but others are quite dark - compare this photo. They just are variable, but they all have those stubby wings. The deerweed is done flowering now and has lost most of its foliage, but it still has ripening seed capsules to munch on. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2020)

File.Name: k816d_k816-025.jpg
Notes: 50234936386 Hah, I don't just photograph small critters! This is a California Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. californicus?) along our road in the woods today. I believe it's a mama deer, in company with last spring's fawn that's losing its spots now, though that photo wasn't so good. Hunting season just started, and the deer have been keeping to themselves lately. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2020)

File.Name: k817a_k817-011.jpg
Notes:
50238885161 Here is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working new flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The butterfly is under a canopy of the long-exserted pistils and stamens of the flowers. I regularly find these little skippers on the large flowers, but they are not the pollinator the flowers are designed for. One possibility is shown in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2020)

It was hot and muggy again today, with temps close to 100∞ and a 40-50% relative humidity. The cooling breeze from the ocean never appeared. Tomorrow is expected to be even hotter.

File.Name: k817b_k817-022.jpg
Notes: 50239074797 Here are a twofer of Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterflies working new flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. Some shrubs are all done flowering, but this one still has lots of buds. I regularly find these little skippers on the large flowers, but they are not the pollinator the flowers are designed for. One possibility is shown in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2020)

File.Name: k817c_k817-024.jpg
Notes: 50238162883 This is a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) with a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) for prey on flower buds of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. If you look close, you can see that another tiny fly (Diptera) is also interested in the bee, maybe a Freeloader Fly (family Milichiidae). (Actually there are two of the flies - the other one is the lump under the bee.) I believe the bee is a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina - but it hardly matters. The bee can't have been captured too long before I got this photo, so the flies are fast. The bees are so hard and shiny, I wonder what the fly can hope to gain? (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; wild bee; Woolly Blue Curls; fly; Freeloader Fly; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Diptera; Milichiidae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Diptera; Milichiidae; Ceratina

File.Name: k817d_k817-003.jpg
Notes: 50238137723 This is another shot of the interesting nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I show in my [Previous] photo. This shot is focused on its interesting symmetrical antennae, particularly striking when you first see the critter. I got this photo today, in the same place it was yesterday - I hope it stays put until it molts to become an adult. I believe this is species Heza similis, see my other photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis

File.Name: k818a_k818-016.jpg
Notes:
50242791916 I found this late-stage nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) in a small tree of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. It was in a shady place, but there was cloud cover at the time to even out the contrasty light - pale but nice. The nymph looks a little "chunkier" than the nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia) that I show in this photo. My first thought was California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), but Alice Abelia suggests the Greater Angle-wing Katydid (Microcentrum rhombifolium). Well, I was close. We have a number of these katydids, compare this photo and this photo - all different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2020)

It was hot and muggy again today, with a high temperature of 102∞ and 30-40% relative humidity. That's too hot for man or beast, and that goes for insects and insect photographers as well. I'm doing my best to get out every day. It's still in the mid-90s as I write this after 6:00 pm. It sounds like we're in for at least one more very hot day before it starts to cool down a few degrees.
CommonNames: Katydid; Arroyo Willow; California Angle-winged Katydid; Greater Angle-wing Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Scudderia; Microcentrum californicum; Microcentrum rhombifolium
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Scudderia; Microcentrum californicum; Microcentrum rhombifolium

File.Name: k818b_k817-039.jpg
Notes: 50242961957 Here are top and side views of two different little Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica) on flower buds of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods yesterday. I'm pretty sure the one on the right is the same one I showed with a wild bee as prey in this photo - it's just a few inches away. These tiny predators have remarkable armor that reminds me of crabs or traditional Japanese armor. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bugs; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: k818c_k817-035.jpg
Notes: 50242916442 Here is another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper on a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). Flowers? Yes, the white flowers are in there, hidden by tight-fitting bracts. This grasshopper has distinctive short wings like a nymph, but they are mostly hidden behind a bract of the plant. I've been getting more photos of these grasshoppers than any other kind - partly because they are relatively common, but also because they are usually up in the plants rather than down on the ground where I would rather not go right now. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2020)

File.Name: k818d_k816-019.jpg
Notes: 50242872777 I believe these are the galls of the Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp (Diplolepis polita) growing on leaves of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica, Rosaceae). They are hard now, but I have another shot of the same galls taken last May when they were red and succulent, this photo. Hah, they look like naval mines - those floating explosive devices with spikes as triggers used to damage ships, at least the way they look in cartoons. These galls are caused by a different genus of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) than the ones that cause the many galls on oak trees, and I believe they occur exclusively on roses. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Leaf Gall Wasp; California Wild Rose; Cynipid Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Diplolepis polita; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Diplolepis polita; Rosa californica; Rosaceae; Cynipidae

File.Name: k819a_k819-022.jpg
Notes:
50246389806 Starting from the left, here is a little Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly working a flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) that has had its stamens clipped by something. Then comes a second skipper butterfly that looks different. If you look close, you can see that it has become prey for the small Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) that is just to the right of its head - proof that these little predators really can bring down prey much larger than they are. Did skipper 1 realize that something was odd about skipper 2? It's also curious that the skippers are different kinds. That's an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) on the right and *maybe* a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) on the left - but I'm not sure about that one on the left. The skipper is plain enough, but it seems too white below and its antennae are too dark on top. Could it be something else? I got this photo in deep shade today, and I like how it came out. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2020)

It was hot and muggy again today, with a high temperature of 100∞ and 35-40% relative humidity. It's smoky today from the wildfires up north - you can see the smoke clearly in the GOES 17 (GOES West) satellite views. We have one more day under our NWS Excessive Heat Warning, but it sounds like there are a few more excessively hot days in the wings.
Locality: GOES West) satellite views.  We have one more day under our NWS Excessive Heat Warning
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Woolly Blue Curls; Pacific Ambush Bug; Umber Skipper; Woodland Skipper
ScientificName: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Phymata pacifica; Poanes melane; Ochlodes sylvanoides
associatedTaxa: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Phymata pacifica; Poanes melane; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: k819b_k819-004.jpg
Notes: 50246337361 I was getting a photo of this interesting webbing on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) today when the spider stuck it's head out! I got the photo but lost the focus on the rest of the web, so here is the spider shot done as an insert - the spider emerged just above and to the right. I believe it's a Jumping Spider (Salticidae), probably genus Phidippus with those reddish colors, possibly a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). I figure the webbing is a nest and not just a retreat. I've probably seen these before and didn't give them a thought. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2020)
ScientificName: Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Salticidae; Phidippus; Phidippus johnsoni
associatedTaxa: Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Salticidae; Phidippus; Phidippus johnsoni

File.Name: k819c_k819-010.jpg
Notes: 50245664048 This photo shows some spider engineering - the outer structural supports of the sheet part of the web of a fairly large Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, the kind of web that I show in this photo. This web is anchored (in part) to a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) from which many supports extend. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2020)

File.Name: k819d_k817-033.jpg
Notes: 50245640913 This is a new summer flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods. Most of the flowers are done now for the year, but a few are still going like this one - a good trick with the 100∞ degree days we've been having lately. I like the stamens of these flowers - they remind me of those wide matches that some restaurants used to give out when I was a kid. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2020)

File.Name: k820a_k820-036.jpg
Notes:
50250057722 I found this remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) picking out a path along a leafy branch of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. The narrow leaves give scale - mostly less than 3/4 inch long. The caterpillars have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, white buckwheat flowers in this case. They are almost impossible to see when they're on their flowers, but this one stood out in a different place. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2020)

It was hot and muggy again today, but cooler than it's been. It still got up to 96∞ with uncomfortable 40-50% humidity. It's been quite smoky from the wildfires up north - you can see the smoke clearly in the GOES 17 (GOES West) weather satellite views like this one from this morning. QuÈ l·stima.
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; QuÈ l·stima
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; QuÈ l·stima

File.Name: k820b_k820-019.jpg
Notes: 50249178458 Here is another late-stage nymph of an interesting Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I suspect it is species Heza similis, though it's an unusual find here in California. I've seen this species before, see this photo from a few days ago in a different oak tree for more info. Today's photo shows the predator's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis

File.Name: k820c_k815-008.jpg
Notes: 50249160723 This is another Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. I have another shot of the same butterfly in this photo. Today's photo shows its beautiful butterfly butt, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2020)
CommonNames: Umber Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: k820d_k816-023.jpg
Notes: 50249139853 This is the deer fawn that was with its mom in this photo from a few days ago. I believe it's a California Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. californicus) along our road in the woods. It must be last spring's fawn that's losing its spots now, though you can still see a few if you look close. Hunting season has started, and the deer have been keeping to themselves lately. This is a butt shot and it is Thursday, so HBDBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2020)

File.Name: k821a_k821-005.jpg
Notes:
50253503632 I've been trying for some time to get a photo of the Lorquin's Admiral (Limenitis lorquini) butterflies that guard the willow trees down by the creek, but they've been too fast for me. So today, one landed on my leg to take a taste, and I got the photo looking straight down! They look similar to the California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterflies that are more common in our woods, but the Admiral's orange wing tips go right to the edge of its forewings, while the Sister has a dark border on the trailing edge - see this photo for a comparison.
