They seem to spend most of their time chasing away the Sisters. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2020)

It was hot and muggy again today, but a degree or two cooler than it's been. It still got up to 94∞ with uncomfortable 40% humidity. It did feel a little cooler overall, but it's still smoky. Our NWS Excessive Heat Warning has been downgraded to a Heat Advisory that will expire later tonight . . . to be replaced by a Flash Flood Watch tomorrow night as we have a 20% chance of thunderstorms from the remnant of Hurricane Genevieve. It's always something.

File.Name: k821b_k821-024.jpg
Notes: 50252626268 Here is a mighty Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) cradling it's prey between it's two front legs on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. It's cradling it's prey, but there's nothing tender about it - look close! The fly looks something like the species Stenopogon californiae in this photo, except the long hairs on its face are black - but maybe it's the same genus? (Hah, someone else at BugGuide says the same thing!) This is really two flies for one, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2020)
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Stenopogon californiae
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Stenopogon californiae

File.Name: k821c_k820-006.jpg
Notes: 50252592763 Here is a little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) on red succulent flowers in the garden. I believe the fly is the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa) or kin in the Villini tribe. The flowers are impressive, maybe the Propeller Plant (Crassula perfoliata var. falcata, aka Crassula falcata, Crassulaceae) which is named for its foliage. I didn't notice at the time, but look just to the right of the fly's wing - a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2020)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Sinuous Bee Fly; Propeller Plant; Crab Spider
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Hemipenthes sinuosa; Villini; Crassula perfoliata; falcata; Crassula falcata; Crassulaceae; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Hemipenthes sinuosa; Villini; Crassula perfoliata; falcata; Crassula falcata; Crassulaceae; Thomisidae

File.Name: k821d_k820-001.jpg
Notes: 50253409767 I believe this is a Blue Bottle Fly aka Blue Blowfly in the family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera on a leaf of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. There are many different kinds - including some natives - so I won't try to guess beyond that. These flies are rather attractive as long as they're walking over foliage and not your potato salad. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2020)
CommonNames: Blue Bottle Fly; Blue Blowfly; Bitter Gooseberry

File.Name: k822a_k822-014.jpg
Notes:
50256212578 Here is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) walking across a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. It's the first one I've seen since I photographed a nymph in this photo a month ago - and that was the first I've seen since February. There's also something odd about this photo in that the assassin's abdomen seems very swollen like it's full of eggs, but it also has the green colors I associate with being teneral - just emerged from a molt. So - is it full of eggs or just gas?? (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2020)

It was definitely cooler today, up to 90∞ but mostly in the low-80s. The humidity made up for it, 50-60% for most of the day and up to 70% at one point. There was a breeze from the south to push some of the smoke aside. We're under a NWS Flash Flood Watch tonight and again tomorrow as remnants of former Hurricane Genevieve stream over. So far it's been cloudy with no rain or rumble of distant thunder. I hope it stays like that - lightning makes me nervous now with all the wildfire activity up north.
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; teneral
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; teneral

File.Name: k822b_k822-002.jpg
Notes: 50256848941 This is another late-stage nymph of an interesting Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I suspect it is species Heza similis, though it's an unusual find here in California. This is in the same tree as the one I showed a couple of days ago in this photo but moved several feet over, so I'm not sure if it's the same one or not. Those odd antennae and thick forelegs seem distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heza similis

File.Name: k822c_k820-027.jpg
Notes: 50256171283 Here is another Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey in a still-flowering shrub of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae). These small predators really can bring down prey much larger than they are, see this photo from a few days ago where one has managed to ambush a skipper butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2020)
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: k822d_k821-006.jpg
Notes: 50256138583 I found this small Araneus spider in an old flower head of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek yesterday. It's tending a small web, touching the sensing line with its extended front leg. It's about 1/2 inch long, too large to be one of our tiny spiders like Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis - see this photo. I suspect it's a juvenile of one of the larger species like Araneus andrewsi with its dark colors and bristly legs. I went back to the same place on the next day, and it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2020)
ScientificName: Araneus; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; Araneus andrewsi
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: k823a_k823-003.jpg
Notes:
50261271457 Here is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly looking at me from its perch in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods this morning. This photo is a good match for my shot of a similar Lorquin's Admiral, compare this photo from a couple of days ago. The oak is its host plant, the food for its caterpillars. I've found several of the caterpillars in different trees this year, for example in this photo, but I always seem to lose them when they go to pupate. I'll keep trying to get one. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2020)

It was mostly cloudy today as remnants of former Hurricane Genevieve stream over us. There has been no rain yet, but it was definitely cooler - up to 80∞ at one point but mostly in the 70s. The humidity has been in the 70-80% range today, really unusual for us! There was a breeze from the south, which has reduced the smoke in the air.

File.Name: k823b_k823-022.jpg
Notes: 50260381738 This is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) visiting a new flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The flowers have just started to appear, see my [Previous] photo. The markings on the fly's abdomen suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis, but there are many different kinds of these small syrphids. I like how the fly's wings are reflecting the colors of the flower. Today was mostly cloudy with tropical weather - good for subtle color. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k823c_k823-027.jpg
Notes: 50260324698 Here are three new wildflowers that have just started flowering in the woods at home. It's interesting that they are all composite flowers in the Asteraceae plant family. On the left is Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) - a male staminate plant of this shrub that has separate male and female forms. In the middle is California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia), with small but pretty flowers that might continue until the new year. And on the right is Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) with flowers that look like little cones with those imbricated bracts. Remember that this is our summer-dry season - it's been months since our last real rainstorm, but somehow new flowers keep appearing. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2020)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; California Aster; Sawtooth Goldenbush
ScientificName: Asteraceae; Baccharis pilularis; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Hazardia squarrosa
associatedTaxa: Asteraceae; Baccharis pilularis; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Hazardia squarrosa

File.Name: k823d_k823-016.jpg
Notes: 50260292268 These are tricolored berries of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. The berries start green, then turn yellow, then red, and finally black when they are ripe. Birds love the ripe ones, and they usually wait for them. I see Bushtits, Wrentits, and newly arriving Warblers taking the berries at home. I've never actually tried eating the ripe berries, as this plant is related to Cascara Sagrada (Frangula purshiana) which has a strong laxative effect. It's a good way to spread seeds though! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2020)

File.Name: k824a_k824-007.jpg
Notes:
50264839068 I believe this is a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly working a new composite flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The shrubs have just started to flower, and they are a favorite of many insects. These butterflies are distinctive mostly by having nothing very distinctive about them - plain but handsome. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2020)

I guess our weather excitement is over for now - there was no rain, no lightning, no clouds, and no great heat today, though there was some wind and high humidity - it was mostly in the low 80s with 60-70% humidity and some wind from the south that felt like fog winds. It sounds like this will be our pattern for a while.

File.Name: k824b_k824-038.jpg
Notes: 50264789808 I found this tiny but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. This shrub is summer deciduous, and the foliage of some plants is already starting to turn color. 23oct21 is another shrub like that. The fly is small, less than an 1/4 inch long, and it's interesting to compare it with the large one in this photo from last week - that one is many times larger, but they are recognizably the same sort. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2020)
CommonNames: Robber Fly; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: k824c_k824-022.jpg
Notes: 50265598537 Here are two shots of the same Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) - the same flowers as in my [Previous] photo. I believe it is species Pselliopus spinicollis. It's rather small, 1/2 inch long or less. These aren't the best shots as a wind was starting to pick up just then - see this photo for a better shot of the species. I think of these as winter and spring insects, but this is the second one I've seen this summer, see this photo from a few weeks ago. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis

File.Name: k824d_k824-024.jpg
Notes: 50264725478 This photo shows how the flower buds of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) manage to hold those long stamens and pistil before they open - they wrap them up in a coil and let them unwind when its time. Compare the fully open flowers in this photo. Note that the stamens are not yet ready for business, and the wild bees showed no interest in them. The little Assassin Bug in my [Next] photo appeared on the flower bud in the upper-right while I was shooting the flower. Try as I might, I couldn't get the bug and the flower in focus together - that would have been some shot. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2020)

File.Name: k825a_k825-004.jpg
Notes:
50269566797 Here is another Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey behind a flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. These small predators really can bring down prey much larger than they are, see this photo from a few days ago where one has managed to ambush a skipper butterfly. This one is actually in a perfect position to get another skipper by the proboscis - see this brutal photo from a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2020)

Today's photos all feature flowers of Woolly Blue Curls, and why not? The shrubs won't be flowering much longer, and the summer flowers are remarkable. Today was much like yesterday - in the mid-80s, with 50-60% humidity, and a breeze from the south in the morning. Tomorrow will probably be much like today. So it goes.
CommonNames: Pacific Ambush Bug; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: k825b_k825-013.jpg
Notes: 50269530732 This is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) visiting a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. Hah, compare the tiny bee in my [Previous] photo! This photo shows that even a relatively heavy bee like this one is not able to leverage the stamens onto its back. Compare the different large insect in this photo that maybe can. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2020)
CommonNames: Large Carpenter Bee; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: k825c_k821-016.jpg
Notes: 50269328256 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) getting pollen from one of the very long exserted stamens of a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure this is a Masked Bee aka Yellow-faced Bee in the genus Hylaeus which lack pollen-collecting hairs but instead carry pollen and nectar in their gut and regurgitate it when they get back to their nests. These tiny bees are starting to become common in the woods - at least, I'm noticing them more lately. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Woolly Blue Curls; Masked Bee; Yellow-faced Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Hylaeus
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Hylaeus

File.Name: k825d_k821-010.jpg
Notes: 50269291301 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" on an extravagant flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's robbing the flower by entering from the back rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen, see this photo for more info. I suspect that the bee is family Megachilidae, maybe genus Megachile. These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees - but I also suspect this one is male with no visible pollen-collecting hairs. These bees are pretty common right now on the nearby flowers of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Woolly Blue Curls; Bird's Beak
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Megachilidae; Megachile; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Megachilidae; Megachile; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: k826a_k826-014.jpg
Notes:
50273110566 Here is a new nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) munching a mint flower in the garden this morning - actually it's just munching the violet anther of a stamen of a mint flower, which gives scale. I believe this is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana - but now I'm confused. I was finding lots of these nymphs in June, and I found an adult at the beginning of August, see this photo. I guess this means they are multi-brooded, right? These nymphs are particularly gorgeous, and it's nice to have another crop of them to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. I did make time for a few photos before we left. It was up to about 90∞ when we got back in the afternoon, and humidity remains high for us, 50-60%. A wind has started up from the north, with gusts up to 35 mph so far, so we're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight.

File.Name: k826b_k826-001.jpg
Notes: 50273238872 Here is another Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey on mint flowers in the garden. I find these little predators just about everywhere there are flowers right now. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2020)

File.Name: k826c_k824-002.jpg
Notes: 50272375488 This is looking at the doily part of the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae), with the bowl part up above. See this photo for a whole web. The spider hangs under the bowl and grabs its prey from underneath. Below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey when it's done with them. This spider has had good hunting - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2020)

File.Name: k826d_k823-030.jpg
Notes: 50273194172 These are some of the first fully open flowers of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) that I've seen in the canyon. When I got the photo up on my computer screen, I noticed the tiny orb web of a spider joining the two branches of flowers in the center. Webs are everywhere - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2020)

File.Name: k827a_k827-032.jpg
Notes:
50276087318 Here is another remarkable "looper caterpillar" that decorates itself with flower parts to become virtually invisible. I believe it's the caterpillar of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). This one has been feeding on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) and has decorated itself with bits of the white flowers. It spent some time with its head down in the flowers and came up with a flower petal between its legs - I reckon it's to add to its decorations, but it dropped it and gave up rather than start over. (Hah, its legs are all thumbs!) I only noticed it because it was moving when nothing else was. I've only found a few of these this year, but many more could be hiding right in front of me. What camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2020)

It was up to 90∞ again today but less humid than it was yesterday. The wind wasn't at all bad this morning, but the sundowner winds from the north are picking up again tonight, and we're under another NWS Wind Advisory.
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: k827b_k827-006.jpg
Notes: 50276043508 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) digging into a flower of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) that is mostly hidden by green bracts. I suspect that the bee is family Megachilidae, maybe genus Megachile. These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees. The bee in my photo does seem to have hairs under its abdomen, but it also seems to have hairs on its hind leg - I'm confused. This photo does show the bee's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2020)

File.Name: k827c_k827-021.jpg
Notes: 50276695976 This Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) tried to get a drink from the water tub we keep by the summer-dry creek, but it fell in and drowned. It's now drifting around and around with just its rear end sticking up out of the water - kind of funny, but also kind of sad. Sure, I could add this to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but I would rather post happier photos to that group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2020)

File.Name: k827d_k826-018.jpg
Notes: 50276664161 This is a small Araneus spider on a spiky old flower head of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) - at least it's the rear end of the spider. This is probably the same kind of spider that I showed in this photo a few days ago, maybe a juvenile of one of the large bottlecap-size spiders that are out around Halloween-time. If you look close, you can see that it has humps on its abdomen like that other one, and it's also got one leg on a sensing line to its web nearby. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2020)

File.Name: k828a_k828-002.jpg
Notes:
50280182071 This is looking closer at the adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) in my [Previous] photo that was stretched out across a cactus fruit in the garden this morning. It's a lady cricket with narrow wings and an ovipositor in the rear. It's the males with broad wings (like this) that do the singing later in summer and autumn. The cricket's antennae go on much farther than it is long - see my [Previous] photo for the whole thing. In this photo the antennae are curled up, though they still go out of the frame. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2020)

Something came up today which interfered with my photo time, but I did have time for this cricket in the garden. It was cooler today, only in the low-80s, and there was a pretty steady breeze. It wouldn't have been the best time for photos anyway.

File.Name: k828b_k828-005.jpg
Notes: 50280317217 Here is all of an adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) stretched out across a cactus fruit in the garden this morning. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot. It's a lady cricket with narrow wings and an ovipositor in the rear. It's the males with broad wings (like this) that do the singing later in summer and autumn. I've seen a few of the female crickets so far this year, but no males yet. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2020)

File.Name: k828c_k827-014.jpg
Notes: 50280284927 Here is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) visiting a new flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae). The pistils and stamens of these flowers extend a couple of inches beyond the flower petals, but it occurs to me that the flowers might actually depend on the tiny bees and flies that visit the stamens for their pollination - see this photo of a tiny bee in action. The flowers do produce nectar for larger insects like the butterfly in this photo, but it might be the tiny ones that actually do the work. Just thinking out loud. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2020)

File.Name: k828d_k824-031.jpg
Notes: 50280266007 I found this tiny but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. The fly is small, less than an 1/4 inch long, and it's interesting to compare it with the large one in this photo from last week - that one is many times larger, but they are recognizably the same sort. I have another shot of this one in this photo from the same day that may be the better photo, but I like the way this one shows how the fly's forehead is scooped out between its eyes - that's an important identification trait for the family. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2020)
CommonNames: Robber Fly; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: k829a_k829-018.jpg
Notes:
50283540133 This is another white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly on new late-summer flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Sometimes the white fringe gets tattered like in this photo, and then it looks more like a Propertius Duskywing (E. propertius). The similar Funereal Duskywing (E. funeralis) butterfly also has a fringe, but that butterfly is smaller and darker, more black than brown. They are out in the woods right now, but I'm still waiting to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2020)

Today was even cooler than yesterday, in the mid-70s all day. Very nice, but there was a steady breeze from the south that was quite annoying after a while. There was no morning fog, though it felt like the usual "fog winds" that typically accompany the fog - maybe it was just over the mountains. The cool weather is pleasant, but it's expected to warm up considerably next weekend - which is unfortunately Labor Day Weekend when more people are out and wildfire is more on my mind than usual.
ScientificName: Erynnis tristis; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; E. propertius; E. funeralis
associatedTaxa: Erynnis tristis; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; E. propertius; E. funeralis

File.Name: k829b_k829-032.jpg
Notes: 50284200756 Here are two different crab-like Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey on flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). They are becoming quite common on all the late-summer flowers. Hah - gravity seems to have no effect on these little predators! (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2020)

File.Name: k829c_k829-025.jpg
Notes: 50284306137 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new late-summer flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I never did see the whole spider, but it's probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with those hairy legs and eyebrows. What a perfect pose! (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2020)

File.Name: k829d_k829-004.jpg
Notes: 50283442748 Here is another little Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) in the flower scar on a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden this morning. This is a nymph unlike the adult in my [Previous] two photos - it has what I call little "training wings" and won't get its real wings until it reaches maturity. After posting yesterday's photo, I did hear an adult male making a racket in the bathroom last night. Their time is approaching. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2020)

File.Name: k830a_k830-002.jpg
Notes:
50288385726 Here is a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), probably the species Spilomyia interrupta, though there are other similar species in the same genus. This one is visiting new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These flies have remarkable eyes and a curious habit of flicking their wings while at rest. They have an annoying whine as they fly - they sound furious. I've been trying out my new Nikon D500 camera today - it works. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2020)

-----

Fly Day Sunday? I was trying out my new camera for the first time today, and I just happened to get quite a few shots of interesting flies on the native Coyote Bush, so here they are. I've always had Nikon cameras dating back to my first Nikon FTn in 1969 (as I think it was). Starting in 2004, I've had a D70, D300, D800, D7100, D3500, and now a D500. I've worn out each of my previous cameras except the D800 which was damaged in a fall and replaced on the spot at an Oregon CostCo with the D7100. I was happy with that camera, but it died on May 22, and I've been using a loaner D3500 since then. That's kind of an entry-level camera that wouldn't recognize my old macro lens, so I've been using a 18-55 mm kit lens plus an old Nikon no. 2 closeup lens that I found in a box of old and forgotten photo stuff. I actually like that setup, it's lightweight and easy to use - and I think my recent photos prove that its really the photographer and not the camera that matters, at least with my nature/critter photos.

Anyway, I haven't been in a hurry to replace the D7100 since the D3500 was working OK. I've gotten all my digital cameras from Samy's Camera in Santa Barbara, but that store closed last year. The coronavirus has made a trip to L.A. unreasonable, so I finally made an order to B&H Photo. My 105 mm macro lens is unfortunately backordered, but I can still use my old one - despite problems with focusing. Here's to many more years of photos with my new Nikon D500!
Locality: as I think it was).  Starting in 2004

File.Name: k830b_k830-010.jpg
Notes: 50288334091 This is not a Horse Fly, but it's big enough. It's a large bee-size fly working new late-summer flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's almost the size of a Carpenter Bee! I'm pretty sure it's a Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae. Apparently the larvae feed on rotting cactus. We have cacti in our garden some distance away, but I don't know any other source up here in the mountains. I guess it's kind of a mystery why it's here at all, but I have occasionally seen them before - see this photo and this photo. I've been trying out my new Nikon D500 camera today - it works. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2020)
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae

File.Name: k830c_k830-014.jpg
Notes: 50288453757 This is some kind of Blue Blowfly in the family Calliphoridae working new late-summer flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. There are many different kinds of these flies - including some natives - so I won't try to guess beyond that. These flies are rather attractive as long as they're walking over foliage and not your potato salad. It's interesting that this one is out in the woods away from people - but not too far away. I've been trying out my new Nikon D500 camera today - it works. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2020)

File.Name: k830d_k830-024.jpg
Notes: 50288422347 Here is a little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) visiting new flowers of late-summer flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the fly is the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa) or kin in the Villini tribe. I've been trying out my new Nikon D500 camera today - it works. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2020)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Coyote Bush; Sinuous Bee Fly
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Hemipenthes sinuosa; Villini
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Hemipenthes sinuosa; Villini

File.Name: k831a_k831-022.jpg
Notes:
50291810913 Here are two shots of a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly working new composite flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) taken in quick succession in the woods today. The photo on the right shows how plain the butterfly is below, but the photo on the left shows that it's more interesting on top. These butterflies are common right now, but they are not easy to photograph as they dart between the many small photos on the shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2020)

Today was pleasantly cool in the mid-70s. There was a pretty good breeze from the south again that no doubt had something to keep the temperature down - and made it difficult to get my photos. We're in for a big change for the coming Labor Day Weekend with temps of 100∞ expected. I'm not looking forward to the combination of heat and holiday because of wildfire concerns.

File.Name: k831b_k831-034.jpg
Notes: 50291739463 This is a remarkable wasp (Hymenoptera) visiting new late-summer flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe it's a Carrot Wasp in the family Gasteruptiidae, almost certainly genus Gasteruption. There's no obvious ovipositor, so maybe this one is male? I usually find these wasps on non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the carrot plant family (Apiaceae) - I think that's their connection with carrots, though I've found them on composite Coyote Bush before, see this photo from 2015. It's remarkable how their abdomen arises from the top of the thorax. This one seemed unusually small and dark - hard to focus on and even hard to see! BugGuide says the "Larvae are predators or predators-inquilines (consume larval food, not the larvae) of other Hymenoptera that nest in twigs and in wood . . . Adults feed at flowers, especially on Apiaceae." I don't find them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2020)
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gasteruptiidae; Gasteruption; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gasteruptiidae; Gasteruption; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: k831c_k830-023.jpg
Notes: 50292542442 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on the same shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that I've been showing lately. It's a good place to look for prey, as there is much insect activity in the shrub right now - though I think this one was more interested in getting away from me. These predators are named for the tiny size of their prey, not for any personal resemblance. You can see this one's mighty spike-like proboscis folded under its head - see this photo and this photo for what it does with that proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2020)
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: k831d_k831-008.jpg
Notes: 50291706458 Here are more tricolored berries of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. The berries start green, then turn yellow, then red, and finally black when they are ripe. Birds love the dead ripe ones and usually wait for them. I see Bushtits, Wrentits, and newly arriving Warblers taking the berries at home. I've never actually tried eating the ripe berries, as this plant is related to Cascara Sagrada (Frangula purshiana) which has a strong laxative effect. That's a good way to spread seeds though! I have another recent shot of the berries in this photo taken on a cloudy day, but I think the sun does them some good. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2020)

File.Name: k901a_k901-003.jpg
Notes:
50296340002 This is looking up at a Yellow Jacket wasp (genus Vespula, Hymenoptera) in a small oak tree in the woods today. We were in a cloud all morning, and this wasp was feeling grumpy from the cold - it finally flew away in a huff as I tried to get closer. These are social wasps that make large colonies in the ground, see this photo taken nearby a couple of years ago. They can get nasty as autumn approaches. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2020)

It was cloudy and cool all morning as the marine layer spilled over the mountains driven by the annoying fog winds. The fog started to burn off by noon, but it stayed cool and breezy. The irony is that we're looking at another extreme heat wave for Labor Day Weekend in a few days. There's more info at the California Weather Blog under the title: "Record heat possible once again Labor Day weekend; wildfire risk to worsen following reprieve.

File.Name: k901b_k901-024.jpg
Notes: 50295453528 I found this handsome little fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's small, only about 1060 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.5 mm - close to 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure, though I have to change the numbers for my new camera.) The fly has an oddly spherical head and a bristly abdomen. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is and, any help with ID will be appreciated. It seems like I've been finding lots of different kinds of flies lately. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2020)

File.Name: k901c_k901-010.jpg
Notes: 50295411658 This is the exuviae or shed skin of a Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) loosely attached to an old stem of native native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) between two old flower/seed whorls. It's the perfect shell of the grasshopper nymph after a molt, complete with eye patches. That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. There's some discussion of this at BugGuide here and here. I don't know what kind of grasshopper it is, but I think I see the buds of real wings, so it's not another wingless Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper like this one. It's curious that once I found this one exuviae today, I found another in a different shrub of the same kind. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2020)
ScientificName: exuviae; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus
associatedTaxa: exuviae; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus

File.Name: k901d_k828-021.jpg
Notes: 50296230632 I guess their wedding has a floral theme. Here are mating Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica) among the flowers in the mint patch of the garden. I got this photo a few says ago with my loaner camera before my new D500 arrived, but it's still not a bad photo. It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2020)

File.Name: k902a_k902-012.jpg
Notes:
50299149388 I believe this is an adult Angle-wing Katydid in the genus Microcentrum on an oak leaf - but I'm not sure if it is the California Angle-winged Katydid (M. californicum) or the Greater Angle-wing Katydid (M. rhombifolium), see this photo and Alice Abelia's comment. These katydids have wings that are more leaf-like than the Bush Katydids in the genus Scudderia, compare this photo of a different adult. It's been a good year for katydids, both nymphs and now adults - or is it just that I'm spending more time in the home canyon? (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2020)

Today was a nice day - no morning fog, no particular wind, and a little warmer - in the 80s. Tomorrow is expected to be in the 90s, and from Friday to next Tuesday we're into the 100s during an extended heat event. Sunday sounds like the hottest day. It's unfortunate that this extreme heat is coming on Labor Day Weekend. I'm not looking forward to this combination of heat and holiday because of wildfire concerns. I hope more people than usual will just stay home - that's where I'll be.
CommonNames: Angle-wing Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid; Greater Angle-wing Katydid
ScientificName: Microcentrum; M. californicum; M. rhombifolium; Scudderia
associatedTaxa: Microcentrum; M. californicum; M. rhombifolium; Scudderia

File.Name: k902b_k902-028.jpg
Notes: 50299130643 This is a tiny humpbacked fly on a new composite flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe it's an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it the "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. Like I said yesterday, it seems like I've been finding lots of different kinds of flies lately. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2020)

File.Name: k902c_k902-033.jpg
Notes: 50299065063 Here is the nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) carrying its prey across a flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. The unfortunate prey is a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) - maybe genus Halictus. The predator is probably a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), but it looks odd because it has the blue/green colors that I associate with a recent molt. Well, it hasn't affected its appetite if so. The assassin really nailed the bee with its proboscis - I'm thinking of Trotsky and the ice ax. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2020)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; California Buckwheat; wild bee; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Halictus; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Halictus; Zelus renardii

File.Name: k902d_k902-003.jpg
Notes: 50299039738 Here is a small orb web of a spider between two old flower stalks of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) that have now gone to seed. The spider kept running away whenever I got close, but it always came back - and I got it the photo. The whole web is only about three inches across, and the spider is tiny. I'm sure it's an Araneus spider with humps on its back, but I'm not sure if its one of the tiny ones or a juvenile of one of the big ones. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2020)

File.Name: k903a_k903-012.jpg
Notes:
50303423592 Here is another Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae snuggled among the new flowers of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. It is probably the same Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens) that I recently showed as a mating pair in this photo - note that the colors of all three are different. They are stink bugs, but they don't really stink - they smell like cilantro! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2020)

It was warm today but actually cooler than expected, with highs still in the high 80s. Our Labor Day Weekend heat event starts on Friday morning and extends until Monday evening with triple-digit temperatures that may set all-time records. I'm not looking forward to this combination of heat and holiday.
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Bird's Beak; Western Red-shouldered Stink Bug
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens

File.Name: k903b_k903-019.jpg
Notes: 50303387662 Here is a different Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) about to fly away from me from a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It's rather small, 1/2 inch long or less. I believe it is species Pselliopus spinicollis, see this photo for a shot of the rest of the insect. It's abdomen looks like it's ringed with a picket fence, and I can't tell if there are openings or a clear membrane. (It looks like a membrane from other photos.) I usually find a few of these little predators from February to April, so it's interesting to find them now. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis

File.Name: k903c_k903-001.jpg
Notes: 50302517093 This is the first male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) that I've actually seen this year hiding in the flower scar of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden this morning. They like it here, compare the female cricket on a cactus fruit in this photo. The females have narrow wings, while the males have broad wings that they rub together to make their song - see this photo where I caught one in the act. Only the male sing, and they're just starting their evening chorus this year. This would count as a Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday photo, but I already have my two photos for that group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2020)

File.Name: k903d_k830-031.jpg
Notes: 50303338052 This photo shows the rear end of a cute little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). The markings on the fly's abdomen suggest the species Toxomerus occidentalis, but there are many different kinds of these small syrphids. I've been saving this one for a few days for . . . HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2020)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Toxomerus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Toxomerus occidentalis

File.Name: k904a_k904-020.jpg
Notes:
50306012878 Here is a tiny spider on my finger tip in the woods today. I believe it's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) because of the arrangement of its legs, and it must be species Diaea livens because of its delicate beauty. These spiders are highly variable in color, but the front part is usually a translucent green like no other spider except maybe the species Nigma linsdalei in this photo that's even smaller. I found this one on an old plant of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that's gone to seed. It's about 660 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.8 mm - a bit over 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I was trying to nudge it into position when it crawled onto my hand. I got a few photos and returned it to the Phacelia. These little spiders aren't common. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2020)

It was indeed hot today, but the temperature stopped just shy of the century mark - call it 98.6 degrees. There was some breeze from the south in the morning, becoming dead calm in the afternoon. Now a NWS Fire Weather Watch has been added to our Excessive Heat Warning. It's expected to be hotter tomorrow.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Diaea livens; Nigma linsdalei; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Diaea livens; Nigma linsdalei; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: k904b_k901-023.jpg
Notes: 50306809827 I found this handsome little fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. I posted another shot of this fly in this photo, but I saved this one for Fly Day Friday. It has an oddly spherical head and a bristly abdomen. It's small, only about 1060 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.5 mm - close to 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, and any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2020)

File.Name: k904c_k831-028.jpg
Notes: 50306585591 Here is a small fly (Diptera) on a new flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). It's not unattractive, and it has a faint bluish tint to its abdomen - like a "blue dun" trout fly. The fly is about 850 across pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.6 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but maybe I've seen it before on the same shrub - see this photo. Any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2020)

File.Name: k904d_k831-002.jpg
Notes: 50306711822 This is a Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae on a leaf in the garden, probably tribe Villini. There are lots of different kinds of these flies, so I'll leave it at that. What are those little white tufts where its wings meet its body? I originally saves this photo for the Bug Butt Thursday group, but other photos came up. Now it's time for Fly Day Friday, and other photos came up again - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2020)

File.Name: k905a_k905-007.jpg
Notes:
50309415373 I found this little spider on a sheet of webbing on the edge of our garden this morning. I suspect that it's a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, though I couldn't see a funnel-like opening nearby - like this one. Today's photos are all spiders of one kind or another. Late summer and autumn is the time for these eight-legged predators along with the Ambush Bugs, Assassin Bugs, Robber Flies, and others that I've been showing lately - it's their time to pick off the summer pollinators before they're gone for good. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2020)

I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley this morning, but I did make time for a few photos before we left. It was too hot to continue by the time we got home - I logged 108∞ in the shade on our upstairs deck. Yipes, and it's supposed to be a few degrees hotter tomorrow! Fortunately there's not much traffic and not much wind . . . yet. That might change, I got a report from a friend that there are sundowner-type winds in Mission Canyon behind Santa Barbara. Our NWS Fire Weather Watch has now been upgraded to a Red Flag Warning. Keep watching the skies . . . for smoke!

File.Name: k905b_k904-015.jpg
Notes: 50310217152 Here is another shot of the tiny Diaea livens spider that I showed on my finger tip in this photo yesterday. Before it got to my finger tip, the spider was on this old plant of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) that's gone to seed now. I was trying to nudge it into position when it crawled onto my hand for that other photo. It's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that is particularly gorgeous. These spiders are said to be almost entirely associated with Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but I guess the key word here is "almost." (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2020)
ScientificName: Diaea livens; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Diaea livens; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: k905c_k904-012.jpg
Notes: 50309344843 First I noticed this little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) motionless on a flower stalk of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I got a photo, and then I looked closer and discovered why it's there - the fly has become prey for a tiny spider. I believe the fly is the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa) or kin in the Villini tribe. I believe the spider is a Mesh Web Weaver spider in the family Dictynidae, possibly genus Dictyna - compare this photo and this photo also taken on Mugwort plants nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2020)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Mugwort; Sinuous Bee Fly; Mesh Web Weaver
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Hemipenthes sinuosa; Villini; Dictynidae; Dictyna
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Hemipenthes sinuosa; Villini; Dictynidae; Dictyna

File.Name: k905d_k903-022.jpg
Notes: 50309987856 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new composite flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). It's probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy face and legs. This one isn't as pretty as the gorgeous crab spider in my [Previous] photo, but it has its charms. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2020)

File.Name: k906a_k906-024.jpg
Notes: 50314076336 It was really too hot for insects today - up to 115∞ according to the official NOAA RAWS weather station less than a mile away. Despite the heat, I was surprised to find this Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working the flowers on a ground cover in the garden at home - I believe it's some kind of Creeping Thyme (Thymus, Lamiaceae). These butterflies resemble the California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly in this photo, but they have different markings and appear later in the year. The other difference is hidden on their upper wings - see this photo. This is the first one I've seen this year - despite the heat. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2020) It really did get up to 115∞ according to the RAWS weather station less than a mile away, though I only noticed it up to 112∞ on our upstairs deck at home. Hah, that's not a big difference when it's this hot. The temperatures really have been crazy today - 119∞ at Paradise, 120∞ in San Luis Obispo, 122∞ in Solvang, even 120∞ in Mission Canyon in the Santa Barbara foothills! Aside from the high temperatures, it's the incessant heat that takes it out of you. Think of trying to boil a pot of water with a match - the match is hot enough but lacks enough heat. It was still in the mid-90s when I got up to pee at 4:00 am last night, and already in the 100s by 7:00 am. We're still under a NWS Red Flag Warning. Keep watching the skies . . . for smoke close to home!
ScientificName: Strymon melinus; Thymus; Lamiaceae; Satyrium californica; heat
associatedTaxa: Strymon melinus; Thymus; Lamiaceae; Satyrium californica; heat

File.Name: k906b_k906-013.jpg
Notes: 50314028851 I was waiting for something to appear in this shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) when this tiny spider ballooned in on a line of invisible webbing and landed right in front of me It really is tiny, just 460 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.9 mm - a bit over 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I can actually recognize what kind of spider it is by its eyes and the spots on its abdomen - a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata), see this photo for a larger specimen. And it looks like this tiny one also has eight spots. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2020)

File.Name: k906c_k906-021.jpg
Notes: 50313990566 Sometimes when insects are scarce because it's too hot or too cold, I start looking at weeds in a new way. These are new dandelion-like seeds of non-native Prickly Lettuce (Lactuca serriola, Asteraceae) growing along the road in the woods. The seeds are quite attractive, though every one might grow into a new unwelcome weed. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2020)

File.Name: k906d_k905-016.jpg
Notes: 50313302163 This is looking close at a late-summer flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The large pollen grains seem to explode from the anthers like popcorn, and they have a depressed center - or are they hollow like beads? These fine natives have been flowering for a while, and they are still going strong. They will flower well into autumn, and I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during the hard time of year before the rains come. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2020)

File.Name: k907a_k907-007.jpg
Notes:
50318278607 I found this remarkable Mantidfly (Mantispidae, Neuroptera) hanging under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It's about 3/4 inch long. I'm not sure, but maybe this is species Leptomantispa pulchella - kin at least. The larvae of these insects have an interesting association with spiders, see this photo among others. I've found the larvae on both Crab Spiders and Jumping Spiders, mostly the latter. BugGuide explains it (somewhat cryptically) like this: "First-instar larvae board spiders to gain access to freshly laid eggs; they will not feed on eggs unless they have spent some time on the dorsum of the spider pedicel, where they apparently feed on the host's haemolymph." I don't know what later-stage larvae do. Very interesting! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2020)

Today was 10∞ cooler than yesterday - but that's still 104∞! There has been a cloud cover for much of the day, and it's been quite smoky from wildfires elsewhere in California both north and south. We've been lucky with smoke so far during this heat event, but we got our share of it today. It also feels muggy with 30% humidity. Tomorrow should be another 10∞ or more cooler, and then it gets interesting with Santa Ana winds down south, strong east winds in Portland, and maybe even snow in Colorado - Aspen goes from a Red Flag Warning to a Winter Weather Advisory overnight? Is our weather broken?? Our NWS warnings expire tonight, and it might even be cool enough to sleep.

File.Name: k907b_k907-025.jpg
Notes: 50318055116 It was hot again today, so I mostly stayed in one place looking close at what I could see. I noticed this tiny ornate object on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It really is tiny, about 410 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) An egg or a colony of something? It looks like it belongs in the ocean. It also makes me think about tiling solid shapes with hexagons - a stumper! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2020)

File.Name: k907c_k907-021.jpg
Notes: 50318216617 This is a large Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens, Stratiomyidae) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) near the leaky pipe in my [Previous] photo. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. These flies have antennae like an old-fashioned TV set and wonderful patterned eyes. I think of them as "compost flies" - see this photo of a maggot in our compost bin. They might actually do some good in the compost bin, see the article at Wikipedia, though it's interesting that I actually found this one some distance away from anyone's compost. I've seen them near here before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2020)
ScientificName: Hermetia illucens; Stratiomyidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hermetia illucens; Stratiomyidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: k907d_k907-016.jpg
Notes: 50317997981 Here are a pair of Yellow Jacket wasps (genus Vespula, Hymenoptera) getting water from a leaky pipe down by the creek. (It's not leaking much, but we'll have to fix it one of these days - maÒana.) The wasps seem to be good friends - look how the one on the left has his arm on the back of the other one. There is a subtle color tint in this photo from the smoke in the air this morning from wildfires elsewhere in California, and I like it. I wouldn't want to use any auto white balance setting that would eliminate that color. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2020)

File.Name: k908a_k908-012.jpg
Notes:
50321061643 Here is a little "Parasitic Wasp" (Hymenoptera) snuggled into a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) on a surprisingly chilly day in the woods today. I jostled this wasp while trying to get my photo, and it didn't budge. I don't know what kind of wasp it is, there are many different kinds. I photographed four different predators snuggled into these flowers in a ten foot section along our road today. The flowers are the place to be on this surprising gloomy day. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2020)

The marine-layer fog spilled over the mountains this morning resulting in a surprising chilly day. Hah, it was just 68∞ today after being 104∞ yesterday - and 115∞ the day before! Maybe the combination of fog and smoke from distant wildfires is responsible for the interesting white balance? I don't mind the fog, but it is usually driven by those annoying "fog winds" (my name) which kept everything swaying in the woods today. I took lots of photos to get these keepers. It was interesting to find so many predators in such a small space.

File.Name: k908b_k908-009.jpg
Notes: 50321886792 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. These are our most common assassins, but I'm just starting to see the adults. I photographed four different predators snuggled into these flowers in a ten foot section along our road today. The flowers are the place to be on this surprising gloomy day. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2020)
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: k908c_k908-016.jpg
Notes: 50321021163 Here is a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) looking at me from a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. It's the first one of these gnarly predators that I've seen this season. The focus isn't quite right, but it's a record shot - and I like the pose. I photographed four different predators snuggled into these flowers in a ten foot section along our road today. The flowers are the place to be on this surprising gloomy day. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2020)

File.Name: k908d_k908-014.jpg
Notes: 50321003128 This is a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey in a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. Or maybe it's just snuggling on a surprisingly chilly day? I photographed four different predators snuggled into these flowers in a ten foot section along our road today. The flowers are the place to be on this gloomy day. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2020)

File.Name: k909a_k909-006.jpg
Notes:
50324521393 Here is another California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly puddling - taking moisture - from a garden path this morning. It's somewhat tattered, compare this photo of a fresh specimen. Hah, you can catch more of these butterflies with wet soil than with flowers - I don't think I've ever seen one feeding. On a related topic, I'm pretty sure the mystery object in this photo is the egg of one of these butterflies, compare this photo at BugGuide. I'll have more to say about this as soon as I can get another decent photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2020)

It's interesting that this photo taken this morning has the same interesting white balance as my photos yesterday. (Don't use "auto white balance" to see this!) I'm sure this is from all the smoke in the air, though there seems to be some marine layer stratus up there too. I don't smell smoke, but I can see it. Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. I did make time for a few photos before we left. It was only about 80∞ when we got back in the afternoon. At least the smoke and stratus is cutting the heat some. I'm most worried about the Oregon wildfires.
Locality: Don't use "auto white balance" to see this!)  I'm sure this is from all the smoke in the air

File.Name: k909b_k905-013.jpg
Notes: 50324444358 A spider on LSD? Hah, try a web search! I found this interesting stabilimentum in the center of a spider web on a garden table at home, but I never did see the spider. Many orb-weaver spiders in the family Araneidae add such a decoration of heavy silk to their webs, but I think this might be the work of a Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers. For one thing, the web was nearly horizontal, which these spiders often do. For another thing, that heavy webbing in the center has the frizzy look of cribellate spiders, see this photo for an example and links for more info. I've also found the spiders in the garden before, see this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2020)
CommonNames: Feather-legged Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Hackled Band Orb Weavers

File.Name: k909c_k905-009.jpg
Notes: 50324414673 This is looking into the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in the woods. It didn't want to pose for me, but If you look close, you can make out the spider as a brown blur in the background. I've tried to photograph this spider several times, but whenever I get too close it zips away faster than I can see. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2020)

File.Name: k909d_k905-002.jpg
Notes: 50325234962 Here are a pair of little wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) mating on a leaf of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). These bees are hard to photograph, they are so shiny and black - and this pair never stopped moving around, finally disappearing under the leaf while still coupled together. I believe these are Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina - not very hairy but the female (below) does have pollen-collecting hairs on her hind legs. It's Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2020)
CommonNames: wild bees; California Honeysuckle; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Ceratina

File.Name: k910a_k910-020.jpg
Notes:
50327990683 Here is a tough-looking Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) waiting for dinner by a new flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. Yes, these predators are as effective as they look, see this photo for an example. What else can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2020)

It's eerie and ominous . . . all my photos taken today have the same smoky-orange white balance as my photos from the last two days. (Don't use "auto white balance" if you want to capture this!) This color is from all the smoke in the air from wildfires north and east of us, though maybe there some marine layer stratus up there too - it looks that thick. I don't smell smoke, but I can see it. The smoke was thick enough to cut the heat today into the mid-70s. One good thing is that there was not much wind today.
Locality: Don't use "auto white balance" if you want to capture this!)  This color is from all the smoke in the air from wildfires north and east of us

File.Name: k910b_k910-024.jpg
Notes: 50328628031 Hah, this is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) moving away from me on a dead leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I got other photos of the whole predator today, but this shot of its cute rear end is just right for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2020)

File.Name: k910c_k910-007.jpg
Notes: 50328581376 When I first saw this little spider, I thought it was an ant - especially when it started walking like an ant with a fast jerky movement. It has quite long spinnerets which it is actually using in my photo, though it didn't balloon away. I'm thinking that it might be a Stealthy Ground Spider in the family Gnaphosidae because of those elongate spinnerets, though I found this one in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree rather than on the ground. Maybe I've seen this kind of spider before though a different kind - compare this photo. Of course, this is just my guess. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2020)
CommonNames: Stealthy Ground Spider; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k910d_k907-015.jpg
Notes: 50328732287 This is looking down at the bristly rear end of a small fly (Diptera) getting a drink from the same leaky faucet I showed a few days ago in this photo. The fly a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera - probably genus Cylindromyia, and maybe species Cylindromyia intermedia - though this one's abdomen is darker than they usually are, compare this photo. Sorry, I didn't get a good shot of the whole fly, but this one is just right for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2020)
ScientificName: Diptera; Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Cylindromyia intermedia
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Cylindromyia intermedia

File.Name: k911a_k911-006.jpg
Notes:
50331470113 Here is another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) looking at me from a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. Late summer and autumn is the time for these predators (and parasitiods like in my [Next] photo) to pick off the summer pollinators before they're gone for good. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2020)

The sky looked like marine-layer stratus again today, but it's mostly smoke from the horrible wildfires north and east of us. My photos still have the smoky-orange white-balance they've had for a couple of days - but less so than yesterday. I hope that's a good sign. The smoke was still thick enough today to cut the heat into the low-80s. There was not much wind.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k911b_k911-012.jpg
Notes: 50332275482 I believe this is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea) on an old leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's tiny, about 1200 pixels long in another nearly 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.1 mm, a bit over 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It has interesting antennae and remarkable hind legs - more visible in this photo of a similar (but different) insect. These wasps are parasitoids of other insects - BugGuide says they mostly attack Lepidoptera pupae. I don't find them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2020)

File.Name: k911c_k911-031.jpg
Notes: 50332080176 Here is an attractive little fly (Diptera) getting a drink from the sandstone rock we keep in a water tub by our still-dry creek in the woods today. We keep water in the tub for any critters that might come by, and we keep the rock in the tub so any small critters that fall in can scramble out. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it might be species Minettia flaveola in the family Lauxaniidae - compare this photo at BugGuide, especially the hairs on its head. Another possibility is the genus Suillia in the family Heleomyzidae, though the larvae of those flies are said to develop in fungi, and it's the wrong time of year for that. I find these flies every year, and I still get confused by them every time. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2020)
ScientificName: Diptera; Minettia flaveola; Lauxaniidae; Suillia; Heleomyzidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Minettia flaveola; Lauxaniidae; Suillia; Heleomyzidae

File.Name: k911d_k911-023.jpg
Notes: 50332058976 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) looking at me from a PVC pipe connected to our spring box - the same leaky pipe I showed in this photo a few days ago. I believe it's another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. I deliberately overexposed to get some details of its face. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2020)

File.Name: k912a_k912-014.jpg
Notes:
50335596186 Here is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) hanging off a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It has its front legs up in the air in a defensive pose - like in this photo and this photo. They are becoming common, and for good reason - late summer and autumn is the time for these predators to pick off the summer pollinators before they're gone for good. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2020)

Today was much like yesterday with a sky that looked like marine-layer stratus but was mostly smoke from the horrible wildfires north and east of us. My photos still have the smoky-orange white-balance they've had for a couple of days - but less so than it's been. I hope that's a good sign. The smoke was still thick enough today to cut the heat into the low-80s. There was not much wind.

File.Name: k912b_k912-015.jpg
Notes: 50334890898 Here is another male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding in an old leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The females have narrow wings, while the males have broad wings that they rub together to make their song - see this photo where I caught one in the act. Only the male crickets sing, and they're just starting their evening chorus this year. They work the night shift and try to lie low during the day. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2020)

File.Name: k912c_k911-034.jpg
Notes: 50334853788 I've been trying for some time to get an acceptable photo of one of these skinny black wasps that move so fast on the sandstone rock we keep in the water tub by our summer-dry creek. I believe it is genus Trypoxylon in the family Crabronidae of Square-headed Wasps. Hah, I did a search at BugGuide for "skinny black wasp" and this one came up - sometimes the simplest way is the best. I believe these wasps paralyze small spiders to provision their nests. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2020)

File.Name: k912d_k911-037.jpg
Notes: 50334821678 I found this little Mayfly (order Ephemeroptera) floating with the mosquito wrigglers in the water tub we keep by our still-dry creek. This is not the first one I've found here, but they are not common - compare this photo from last spring of what appears to be the same sort. I know next to nothing about aquatic insects, so it's a treat to find this one. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2020)

File.Name: k913a_k913-044.jpg
Notes:
50339770241 Here is a gorgeous little wasp (Hymenoptera) that appears to be working a new composite flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's so blue it almost looks black. There are a too many blue wasps at BugGuide, so I won't try to guess what kind it is - but any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2020)

Today was much like yesterday with a sky that looked like marine-layer stratus but was mostly smoke from the horrible wildfires north and east of us. My backordered Nikkor 105 mm micro lens finally came from B&H photo, completing my order for camera, lens, and tripod - the whole megillah. I've been trying out the new kit this weekend, and the tripod actually takes the most getting used to. My old Manfrotto 055CF tripod is like a favorite old pair of shoes that are falling apart but fit perfectly. My new tripod is the same sort but like a pair of new shoes - too stiff! Oh well, I'm sure it will loosen up with time - no doubt too much like my old one.

File.Name: k913b_k913-031.jpg
Notes: 50339861887 This is looking face-to-face with an unusual fly (Diptera) on flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. "You will be called Stitchface" - as Homer Simpson says in this clip. I suspect it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because of its general gnarliness, and I'll post another photo later this week (for BBBT or FDF) that shows more of it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2020)

File.Name: k913c_k913-048.jpg
Notes: 50339820822 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) - first the waiting . . . and then with its prey, a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera). Yep, it made the strike while I was looking through my camera, but it was too fast for me to capture. The bee looks like a goner, but it actually got away, again too fast for me to capture. The spider is really tiny, about 390 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.6 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I could barely see it. It's probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with that general hairiness. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2020)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coyote Bush; wild bee
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Mecaphesa

File.Name: k913d_k913-039.jpg
Notes: 50338933433 This is an odd one - the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) with its proboscis in a flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) as though it's after nectar. Well, maybe it is?? Note that it is in the same defensive pose as the adult in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2020)
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: k914a_k914-020.jpg
Notes:
50343751682 Here are two shots of a fresh-looking little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year - in fact it's the first one I've seen this year that I know of. They are usually pretty common in the spring, and I'm not sure why I didn't find them this year. This one wouldn't let me get close, so these are deep crops. They are handsome butterflies, like small checkerspots. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2020)

It was a pleasant day in the mid-80s, but it was pretty breezy in the morning when I was out. There was actually blue sky this morning for the first time in a few days, but a smoke layer that I couldn't smell built up again as the day went on. I had a errand in Santa Barbara this morning, and it was definitely worse. This is a far cry from Oregon where the air is downright hazardous.

File.Name: k914b_k914-012.jpg
Notes: 50343554581 Here is a mating pair of Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) on a new flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I've always assumed that these bugs are color-shifters that can change to match their flowers, but I usually find what I guess are yellowish males riding white females. But see this photo in which the colors are reversed. It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2020)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: k914c_k914-002.jpg
Notes: 50343531436 This is the exuviae or shed skin of a Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on an old seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It's the perfect shell of the grasshopper nymph after a molt. That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. I actually found this one two weeks ago at the same time I found the other exuviae in this photo in a different shrub of the same kind, but I got this photo today. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2020)
ScientificName: exuviae; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: exuviae; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae

File.Name: k914d_k913-022.jpg
Notes: 50342832538 This is looking down my bare leg at another California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly puddling - taking moisture and minerals - from me! Perhaps you can make out the strap of my Birkenstock sandal to the right of the butterfly. Hah, one landed on the back of my camera today, and of course I couldn't get the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2020)

File.Name: k915a_k915-026.jpg
Notes:
50347385332 Here is another Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly working a new almost-autumn flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Its face reminds me of a bulldog! (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2020)

It was another pleasant day in the mid-80s, but it was breezier than yesterday and smokier, with a stratus-like smoke layer that reduced the sunlight. We've had pretty boring weather lately, but that's better than being terrifying!
ScientificName: Ochlodes sylvanoides; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: k915b_k915-020.jpg
Notes: 50347331027 This is a handsome Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I guess it is a male Long-horned Bee in the genus Melissodes or Eucera with those long antennae, probably the former because it is small and active in (almost) autumn.
