The males do have hairy legs, but the females have even hairier legs with proper pollen baskets (scopae) designed to carry pollen - maybe this photo shows them. The males will only be interested in nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2020)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; California Aster; Long-horned Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Melissodes; Eucera; scopae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Melissodes; Eucera; scopae

File.Name: k915c_k915-005.jpg
Notes: 50347152246 This is a dark little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) visiting new visiting a new flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the fly is the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa) or kin in the Villini tribe. It looks quite black in the wild, but it looks quite brown in my photos. If you notice that the flower is more washed out than the flowers in my [Previous] two photos, that's just how they are. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2020)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; California Aster; Sinuous Bee Fly

File.Name: k915d_k914-009.jpg
Notes: 50347133766 Here is a mating pair of "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a flower head of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) that's now gone to seed. They are probably Plant Bugs in the family Miridae or something similar, though I can't really see them here. It's an interesting position, making a tent of their bodies. It's Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Miridae

File.Name: k916a_k916-017.jpg
Notes:
50350103103 Here is another California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) near the creek today. This isn't far from the leaky pipe that I show in this photo, and I've noticed quite a few of these butterflies in the area. They do love to puddle! Aside from the skippers, they are easily the most common butterflies in the woods right now. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2020)

It was another pleasant day but warmer than it's been, up into the 90s. It was breezier than it's been too, but the wind died down instead of getting stronger in the afternoon. There's still a smoke layer, but there was also a hint of blue sky.

File.Name: k916b_k916-020.jpg
Notes: 50350922522 Here is a different Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) about to fly away from me from one of the first open flowers of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). There was a breeze, and the plant was rocking back and forth - I was lucky to get a photo this good. For scale, the round disk of the composite flower is about 5 mm or 3/16 inch across. I believe this little assassin is species Pselliopus spinicollis, no doubt waiting for pollinators to come by. I usually find a few of these little predators from February to April, so it's interesting to find so many of them now. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2020)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Pselliopus spinicollis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Pselliopus spinicollis

File.Name: k916c_k916-023.jpg
Notes: 50350741586 This is webbing between old flower/seed whorls of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Note how the webbing has a thick frizzy look. I believe this is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, and I have a shot of the tiny spider associated with such webbing in this photo from last spring. I've photographed this webbing before on the sage, see this photo from earlier this year. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2020)

File.Name: k916d_k915-012.jpg
Notes: 50350724261 This is the retreat or nest of a spider among the flower bracts of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). If you look close - or maybe lean back from your screen - you can see the spider behind the diaphanous silk webbing. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2020)

File.Name: k917a_k917-026.jpg
Notes:
50353686188 The rest of my photos today show the rear ends of different critters for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, so here's the other end of the beast - the face of an adult Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae). These predators are not tiny, but their narrow heads certainly are. This one is threatening me in the same defensive pose that I show recently in this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2020)

Today started hazy but with mostly blue skies - the smoke situation is definitely improving. Then clouds started moving in from the south, and it is now quite cloudy - with real clouds, not smoke. I believe the clouds are a remnant of former Tropical Storm Karina down in Baja. *Sigh* A bit of summer rain would be wonderful, but it's probably not going to happen this time.

File.Name: k917b_k916-006.jpg
Notes: 50353665598 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) next to what I guess is its exuviae or shed skin after a recent molt. I found the pair of a on a flower head of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) that's now gone to seed. The spider seemed to have a definite relationship with the exuviae and didn't want to leave it. That's interesting, but this is also a good shot the spider's rear end - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2020)

File.Name: k917c_k913-043.jpg
Notes: 50353633138 This is the bristly rear end of a small fly (Diptera) on a flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). I believe the fly is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera - probably genus Cylindromyia, and maybe species Cylindromyia intermedia though its abdomen is darker than they usually are, compare this photo. I just realized that I posted the same kind of fly a week ago in this photo - oh well, it's an interesting fly and just right for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2020)
ScientificName: Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Cylindromyia intermedia
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Cylindromyia intermedia

File.Name: k917d_k911-043.jpg
Notes: 50353596578 This is looking up at the rear end of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) above the retaining wall on the wild edge of our garden. It had wide wings of a male, with cerci instead of an ovipositor at the rear. The green color is interesting, maybe it's just out of a final molt and/or it's a proper Snowy Tree Cricket in the O. Rileyi group. I got this photo on a day when the sky was blocked by a stratus-like layer of smoke that cut the light but also reduced the usual contrast. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2020)
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Wild Blackberry; Snowy Tree Cricket
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; O. Rileyi
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; O. Rileyi

File.Name: k918a_k918-024.jpg
Notes:
50357902002 Here is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly . . . on my finger tip! It's actually "puddling" - taking moisture and minerals from my sweat. It first landed on my right hand, and I transferred it to my left hand so I could work my camera buttons - that's how calm it was. After about ten minutes (and more than a hundred photos!) it had its fill and took off. This was a literal stretch with my 105 mm macro lens - it's much easier to do with a 55 mm lens. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2020)

We were in and out of clouds today, remnants of former Tropical Storm Karina up from Baja. That cut the heat a bit, and it was quite pleasant, though a wind picked up in the afternoon with gusts of 30+ mph so far. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight which makes me nervous.

RIP Notorious RBG
Now what?

File.Name: k918b_k916-014.jpg
Notes: 50357674391 I found this Blue Bottle Fly aka Blue Blowfly in the family Calliphoridae hanging upside down under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. It was totally shaded out, so I tried overexposing by two stops - I really like how it came out. There are many different kinds of these flies, including some natives, so I don't like to guess beyond the family - but maybe this one is the Secondary Screwworm (Cochliomyia macellaria)? I have a key to the genera and species of blowflies, and I may have to actually try to use it. Whatever it is, these flies are rather attractive as long as they're walking over foliage and not your potato salad. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2020)
CommonNames: Blue Bottle Fly; Blue Blowfly; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cochliomyia macellaria
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cochliomyia macellaria

File.Name: k918c_k913-035.jpg
Notes: 50357653836 Here is another shot of the gnarly fly (Diptera) that I showed in this photo a few days ago - the "Stitchface" fly. It's on flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). I suspect it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because of its general gnarliness and the bristles that are barely visible on its rear end. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2020)

File.Name: k918d_k913-030.jpg
Notes: 50357781397 This is a large dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) on my leg below my shorts. This one seemed friendly - I finally had to shoe it away . . . "Shoe fly don't bother me!" I assume it's after minerals in my sweat, though I don't see any sign of a proboscis. It's about the size of a bee, and it flies with a buzzy flight. I believe this is genus Xenox in the Anthracinae group. BugGuide says that they are ectoparasitoids of Carpenter Bees which is appropriate for such large dark flies. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2020)

File.Name: k919a_k919-041.jpg
Notes:
50362015407 Here is a different Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) about to fly away from me from flower bracts of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. Hah, they always seem to be leaving! I believe this little assassin is species Pselliopus spinicollis, no doubt waiting for pollinators to come by. I usually find a few of these little predators from February to April, so it's interesting to find so many of them now. I've posted 18 photos of these assassins at Flickr since 2012, and six of them are taken since August. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2020)

Just two photos today. We had an unscheduled power outage that started at 11:15 pm last night. Dinner time was approaching after a day without power, so we decided to go to town for takeout and ice - and wouldn't you know, the power was back on by the time we got home, around 7:00 pm. I got a fair number of decent photos today which can wait for tomorrow. The hell of it is that we have a maintenance power outage scheduled for all day Monday. I hope enough people complain that they change it - but I doubt it.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis

File.Name: k919b_k916-011.jpg
Notes: 50361991252 This is looking close at a new almost-autumn flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae). The composite flower has opened its first two disk flowers, and more are coming - like eggs starting to hatch. The outer petals are particularly gorgeous at this stage, though they become more washed out with age. See this photo for scale. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2020)

File.Name: k920a_k920-030.jpg
Notes:
50366111292 Here is another tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) walking across leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This is a particularly gnarly genus with all those bumps and prickers on its body - quite a contrast to this one I showed yesterday, though I guess it has its bumps also. You can see a bit of its mighty proboscis folded under its head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are actually shy and retiring in my experience. Like I've said before, late summer and autumn is the time for these predators to pick off the summer pollinators before they're gone for good. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2020)

It was pleasantly cool today, only up to about 80 degrees, but there was the usual annoying breeze from the south all morning. Despite our 20-hour unscheduled power outage yesterday, we have another one of those @#&$ maintenance outages scheduled tomorrow from 8:00 am to 6:00 pm, and I won't be able to post if it runs much later than that. We've had way too many of these outages this year, the result of too many years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Sinea; Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; way too many
associatedTaxa: Sinea; Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; way too many

File.Name: k920b_k920-014.jpg
Notes: 50365911991 I found this handsome fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) near our summer-dry creek today. I believe it is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, Diptera in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree by the creek, maybe the species Thereva fucata, see this BugGuide info page. I've seen these handsome flies before, see this photo from last year and this photo from the year before, both from October. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Mugwort; Stiletto Fly; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thereva fucata

File.Name: k920c_k919-024.jpg
Notes: 50365132238 This tiny micro-fly (Diptera) seems to be collecting blue pollen from a flower of native Sticky False Gilyflower aka Sticky False Gilia (Allophyllum glutinosum, Polemoniaceae). There was a steady breeze as I tried to get these photos, and they are what they are. The fly is only about 380 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.6 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe this tiny humpbacked fly is a Micro Bee Fly in the family Mythicomyiidae, genus Mythicomyia. BugGuide says that they are traditionally treated as Bombyliidae, and one contributor describes them as microbombyliids. I have another shot of a similar fly in this photo. I'll try to get a better shot of the flowers. They are interesting because they go all summer, but they really took a hit during our 115∞ weather event over Labor Day Weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2020)
CommonNames: Sticky False Gilyflower; Sticky False Gilia; Micro Bee Fly; microbombyliids
ScientificName: Diptera; Allophyllum glutinosum; Polemoniaceae; Mythicomyiidae; Mythicomyia
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Allophyllum glutinosum; Polemoniaceae; Mythicomyiidae; Mythicomyia

File.Name: k920d_k919-034.jpg
Notes: 50365783456 I found this little wasp (Hymenoptera) digging around in a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae), when all of a sudden it came up with a grub of some sort, the larva of some other insect! It grabbed its prey, and took wing. I don't know what kind of wasp it is - it looks like a Yellow Jacket (Vespula, Vespidae), but it's only about half the size - I've been seeing quite a few of them getting water from our water tub by the dry creek. I don't know what the unfortunate larva is. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2020)
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Vespula; Vespidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Vespula; Vespidae

File.Name: k921a_k921-007.jpg
Notes:
50370067187 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) looking for a real flower of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). The flowers are mostly hidden by green bracts, but they are in there. I believe this bee is family Megachilidae, maybe genus Megachile. These bees collect pollen under their abdomens rather than on their hind legs like most other bees, and you can see quite a load there. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2020)

We got a phone message from our power company at 8:15 this morning saying that our 8:00 am power outage was being rescheduled. This is no way to run a business, but I am glad the power wasn't out today. The autumnal equinox will happen at 6:30 am tomorrow morning - the official first day of autumn. Happy equinox! It was still pleasantly warm today, up to about 80 degrees. We have a long ways to go before the heat is over.

File.Name: k921b_k921-011.jpg
Notes: 50369168718 Here is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey by a new composite flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods this morning. The spider is probably another genus Mecaphesa with that general hairiness. It was quite windy this morning, and the plant was bobbing in and out of the sun - this photo came out better than I expected. The month of Arachtober is coming soon, so I'm keeping track of all the spiders I find. The group goal is to post spider photos every day in October, but my personal goal is to get new spider photos every day. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2020)

File.Name: k921c_k919-010.jpg
Notes: 50369994277 I found this caterpillar munching foliage of the same vine of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) as the different caterpillar in my [Previous] photo. This one is smaller, a bit over an inch long. I don't know what kind of caterpillar it is. It looks and moves like a "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae), but it has two many prolegs in back. Its front two prolegs look malformed, like stubs of real legs. I don't know what the story is, but it seems to be getting along just fine. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2020)

File.Name: k921d_k917-010.jpg
Notes: 50369805101 I found this caterpillar happily munching old foliage of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) in the woods a few days ago. It's good-sized, maybe two inches long, bigger than the one in my [Next] photo. I don't know what kind of caterpillar it is, but it has a little horn on its tail makes it look like a Sphinx moth (Sphingidae) - do any other caterpillars have that little horn? I found another caterpillar with a horn on the same kind of plant a month ago that looks different - compare this photo. Actually the caterpillars look similar - maybe the color difference has to do with molting? (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2020)

File.Name: k922a_k922-003.jpg
Notes:
50373816547 This is a cute little net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. I have many shots of these cute little insects, though this is the first adult I've manage to photograph since this one on New Year's Day. I've actually seen quite a few of them, often in a place where several leaves come together, but they usually manage to scoot away under a leaf before I can get the shot. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2020)

Today is the autumnal equinox. Six months ago, on the day of the vernal equinox on March 19, the state of California announced a stay-at-home order for the Covid-19 pandemic. Now here's an order I can get behind! I've been staying put in the canyon except for a shopping trip to the Santa Ynez Valley every two weeks, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. That's about six months with photos only from our home canyon. It's a rich place to live.

Equinox to equinox - here's an interesting stumper I wrote about the equinoxes way back on 25 September 1998: "On the solstices, the earth is most tilted toward or away from the sun. On the equinox, it's in the middle, and day and night are (nearly) the same. I counted days on my calendar and found that there are 186 summer days between the spring and fall equinoxes, but only 179 days between fall and spring. What gives?" (Answer.)
Locality: nearly) the same. I counted days on my calendar and found that there are 186 summer days between the spring and fall equinoxes

File.Name: k922b_k922-016.jpg
Notes: 50372929558 Here is another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These grasshoppers looks like nymphs at first with those stubby little wings, but those stubs are all the wings they will ever get. The color of these grasshoppers is confusing. This one is tan, but others are quite dark - compare this photo. They just are variable, but they all have those stubby wings. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2020)

File.Name: k922c_k920-023.jpg
Notes: 50373765902 This looks like a dancing spider! It might be about the size of a bottle cap, though spiders have a way of looking larger than they are. I believe it's an Araneus andrewsi spider with those dark colors and bristly legs, and probably a male, though I can't see its palps in any of my photos. It's the kind of spider you don't want to find crawling on your shoulder, though this one actually seemed shy and retiring. It's on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) near our summer-dry creek. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2020)

File.Name: k922d_k920-008.jpg
Notes: 50372832888 Here is another shot of the tiny ornate "mystery object" that I first showed two weeks ago in this photo on September 7. I'm pretty sure now that it's egg of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, compare this photo at BugGuide. I was put on the right track by recent work by Dom Greves showing White Admiral butterfly eggs in the UK, see this photo - Admirals and Sisters are both in the same Limenitidinae subfamily, and both have eggs like tiny geodesic domes. Across from the egg in my photo is an old pier built around the leaf mid-rib. The once-new caterpillar liked to hang out on this structure, see this photo of what I believe is the same leaf from last June. I think it's so interesting that the new egg is right next to the old caterpillar's work. It's also interesting that Dom Greves shows something similar in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2020)

File.Name: k923a_k923-023.jpg
Notes:
50377285901 Here is another Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly paused for a moment on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in the woods today. It's surrounded by flowering plants of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) that it's really interested in - compare this photo from last week. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2020)

Today was a pleasantly warm autumn day, up into the 80s with the usual annoying breeze from the south that keeps everything in motion. It sounds like it will turn HOT again next week. It may be autumn, but we're at the peak of wildfire season.
ScientificName: Ochlodes sylvanoides; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Ochlodes sylvanoides; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: k923b_k923-019.jpg
Notes: 50377414347 I noticed this small wasp (Hymenoptera) struggling with *something* inside a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. After a few minutes, it came out with a grub of some sort, the larva of some other insect - look to the right of the wasp's head. It grabbed its prey and took wing. I'm not sure what kind of wasp it is, but it has the kind of petiole-like "double abdomen" that I associate with Potter Wasps aka Mason Wasps (Eumeninae, Vespidea) - compare this photo of a different wasp with its mud nest. It's curious that I found a different wasp with similar prey in the same kind of plant, see this photo from a few days ago. The wasps look different, but maybe that's a boy/girl thing? (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2020)
CommonNames: wasp; Bird's Beak; Potter Wasps; Mason Wasps
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Eumeninae; Vespidea

File.Name: k923c_k923-031.jpg
Notes: 50376511508 This is the "cushion of webbing" where the caterpillar of a 01may21 has been resting on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), see this photo for similar caterpillars on their webbing. It's my impression that the caterpillars make this silk cushion to have something to hang onto when they are resting. I believe this caterpillar has left this particular leaf, but I only recently noticed the webbing. Spiders aren't the only ones - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2020)

File.Name: k923d_k922-011.jpg
Notes: 50376488413 Here is a large dandelion-like seed caught up in a spider web in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). Actually it's just the fluff without the seed, probably from California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) which grows nearby. Hah - web and seed fluff are so intertwined that it's hard to tell which is which. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2020)
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: k924a_k924-003.jpg
Notes:
50380129953 Here is an impressive Orb-Weaver Spider with its freshly wrapped prey - probably a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) with that banded abdomen. I believe this is an Araneus andrewsi spider with humps on its back and dark colors and bristly legs, and it's certainly a female with its narrow (but bristly like medieval war clubs!) palps visible here. I found this scene in the cactus patch of our garden today, with its orb web stretched between two cactus pads. See my [Previous] photo for the remarkable pattern on the spider's abdomen. I hope this spider sticks around, as Arachtober starts in a week. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2020)

Today was another pleasantly warm autumn day, up into the 80s. There was hardly any breeze this morning for a welcome change, but there is a breeze now picking up from the north blowing over the east-west mountains - our notorious sundowner winds. It sounds like we're in for another heat wave next week. It may be autumn, but we're still at the peak of our wildfire season.

File.Name: k924b_k924-010.jpg
Notes: 50380800056 Here is another shot of the spider that I show in my [Next] photo, showing the remarkable pattern on its abdomen. This is after I got the other photo. I guess I got too close, and the spider scooted back to its retreat in the cactus patch. I believe this is an Araneus andrewsi spider with those humps on its back and dark colors and bristly legs, and it's certainly a female with its narrow palps visible in my other photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2020)

File.Name: k924c_k923-043.jpg
Notes: 50380934292 This is looking at the rear end of a Yellow Jacket wasp (genus Vespula, Vespidae) getting a drink on the sandstone rock that we keep in the water tub by our summer-dry creek. That pattern on its abdomen looks like frames from an animation. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2020)

File.Name: k924dc_k920-026.jpg
Notes: 50380743316 This is the rear end of the same tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) that I show the rest of in this photo. This is how I first found it hiding behind a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I nudged in out into the open after I got a few photos - including this one for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2020)
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: k925a_k925-015.jpg
Notes:
50384178281 I found this impressive spider today on a dusty shrub of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis, Caprifoliaceae) in the woods today. It might be about the size of a bottle cap, though these spiders have a way of looking larger than they are. I believe it is another Araneus andrewsi spider with those dark colors and bristly legs, and probably a male with that relatively small abdomen, though I can't see its palps in any of my photos. Compare this photo of another male and this photo of another female from yesterday - note that they all have similar patterns on their abdomens. This is the kind of spider you don't want to find crawling on your shoulder, though this one really didn't want to move. I hope this spider sticks around, as Arachtober starts next week, and I like to treat it as a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2020)

We've been doing our twice-a-week shopping run on Wednesdays so we can pick up our farm box at the same time, but something came up this week, so we'll do it next week. But beer was getting low, so it was time for a beer run. I drove up West Camino Cielo road on my way to town to see if the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) was starting to flower with the autumnal equinox. It's happened before, see this photo from 2013 - but not this year, the plants look dead. I really didn't find much until I got home where I found this spider and a few other critters. *Sigh* There's no place like home . . . for spiders and bugs!

File.Name: k925b_k924-038.jpg
Notes: 50384329687 I was trying to get a photo of a tiny wild bee on this flower, when a relatively giant Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) came into my picture and chased the bee away. The flower is native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae), and I believe the fly is the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa) or kin in the Villini tribe. This one looks pretty beat up. I expect that with butterflies that are relatively slow moving, but it seems unusual with a fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2020)

File.Name: k925c_k923-022.jpg
Notes: 50384302067 Here is another small Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on an autumn flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae). The photo is overexposed and cropped too tight, but I still like the fly's desert colors. I guess it's tribe Villini again, maybe genus Paravilla? I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2020)

File.Name: k925d_k919-019.jpg
Notes: 50384088236 This is a small Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) getting ready to land on a flower of native Sticky False Gilyflower aka Sticky False Gilia (Allophyllum glutinosum, Polemoniaceae ). I was trying to get photos of the really tiny fly on the flower stamen at the bottom, when the relatively giant Bee Fly (but still small!) barged into the scene. The tiny one is a Micro Bee Fly (Mythicomyiidae), see these photos that I got at the same time. I don't know what kind of Bee Fly the bigger one is, though I've seen it before on the same flowers, see this photo. They always bob up and down a few times before landing, which helped me get the photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2020)

File.Name: k926a_k926-025.jpg
Notes:
50387929762 Here is a brand-new caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a partially eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. These tiny caterpillars build what UK photographer Dom Greves describes as a "defensive pier constructed with silk and frass along the leaf mid-rib" - see his photo of a different caterpillar in the same Limenitidinae group that builds a similar structure. This one really is tiny, just 950 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.0 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I've been watching this caterpillar's egg for a while, and I have a photo from three days ago on September 23 to prove that it was just an egg with no caterpillar yet. I'll post that photo tomorrow, but see this photo of a similar egg from the same small oak tree a week ago. Looking at the amount it's eaten in three days, it has quite an appetite. I look forward to watching this one grow for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2020)

Today was another pleasantly warm autumn day, up into the 80s but no hotter. There was very little breeze this morning, but there is some wind from the north this evening. The forecast is for more heat next week, but in the 90s not the 100s. It may be autumn, but it's still the peak of our wildfire season.

File.Name: k926b_k926-003.jpg
Notes: 50387711746 This is a small caterpillar-like larva on a flower cluster in the mint patch of the garden. I suspect that it's not a caterpillar of a moth or butterfly at all, but the larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). I never did get a look at its eyes or prolegs to know for sure, so that's just a hunch - see this photo for more info. It does seem the wrong time of year to be finding sawflies. Any advice on ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2020)

File.Name: k926c_k926-018.jpg
Notes: 50386992868 Here is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) stretched out on a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. These predators are common in the woods right now, doing their job of picking off the pollinators before winter comes. They are named for the tiny size of their prey, not for any personal resemblance. You can see this one's mighty spike-like proboscis folded under its head - see this photo and this photo for what it does with that proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2020)
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: k926d_k926-005.jpg
Notes: 50387830922 This is today's shot of the impressive Orb-Weaver Spider that I showed two days ago in this photo. I believe it is an Araneus andrewsi spider. This photo shows what happened to its prey in the other photo - it's a fly, though it's unrecognizable now as a glob of black goo. I've seen spiders turn their prey into balls of goo before, and I believe it's a kind of external digestion to liquify their prey before sucking it up. It's interesting that the spider left its prey hanging by a thread all day yesterday before eating it. I guess it likes to hang its meat for a while first. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2020)

File.Name: k927a_k927-035.jpg
Notes:
50392240096 Here is another small wasp (Hymenoptera) that was digging into an old flower of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. I believe this is a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea) - compare this photo of a different wasp with its neat mud nest. I've found two of these wasps digging into the flowers and coming out with a grub to provision their nests - see this photo and this photo. The one in my photo today never did come up with a grub, at least not yet, but it kept trying . (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2020)

Today was another pleasant autumn day, up to 80∞ but no hotter. It was quite breezy this morning from the south, but the wind eased up in the afternoon. Our forecast is for more heat next week, but in the 90s not the 100s like further south. It may be autumn, but it's still the peak of our wildfire season.

File.Name: k927b_k927-039.jpg
Notes: 50391497703 I found this impressive Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure the spider is the White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), though the band under its eyes is yellow rather than white. This spider is good sized and almost hairless. I don't know why its tending a flower that is nearly spent, as there are fresh flowers nearby. Consider these recent spider photos as a warm-up for Arachtober which starts next week. The group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it as a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day. It helps to know where to look. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2020)

File.Name: k927c_k927-017.jpg
Notes: 50392315972 Here's another shot of a brand-new caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a partially eaten leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. If you compare this photo from yesterday, you can see that the caterpillar has extended its "defensive pier constructed with silk and frass along the leaf mid-rib" quite a bit - from 7.4 mm to 12.2 mm as I figure by counting pixels in these 1:1 macro shots. (See here for how I figure.) Looking at the amount it's eaten in one additional day, it has quite an appetite. I look forward to watching this one grow for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2020)

File.Name: k927d_k923-002.jpg
Notes: 50392095956 These are three different eggs of California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterflies, all on its Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) host plant. Hah, when I first found one of these on September 7, I had to consider it a "tiny ornate mystery object" - see this photo. I was put on the right track by recent work by Dom Greves showing White Admiral butterfly eggs in the UK, see his photo - Admirals and Sisters are both in the same Limenitidinae subfamily, and both have distinctive eggs like tiny geodesic domes. They really are tiny, just 480 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.0 mm - a bit more than 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The egg on the right is the one that produced the caterpillar in my next and previous photos which I first found three days after I photographed the egg. I got the photo on the left today, but I'll leave the September 23 date to mark the occasion. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2020)

File.Name: k928a_k928-008.jpg
Notes:
50395295813 This is another small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a flower cluster of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. I believe it's another genus Mecaphesa spider with that general hairiness, compare my [Previous] photo of a Crab Spider without hairs. I believe this one is male with those enlarged palps. It's tiny, less than 1/8 inch long, though the spread of its arms is greater. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2020)

There was a wind from the south again this morning, and I had better luck looking for spiders - that don't move - rather than insects - which do. Just as well, as the month of Arachtober starts in a few days. The group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it as a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day. It helps to know where to look ahead of time. It was hot today as well as windy, up to about 90 degrees. It is expected to be hotter tomorrow and the next day.

File.Name: k928b_k928-024.jpg
Notes: 50395962241 Here is another shot taken today of the same impressive Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I showed yesterday in this photo. It's moved to a new flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae), and It's got a Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) for prey. I believe the unfortunate fly is genus Villa in the tribe Villini - maybe species Villa lateralis which is said to have clear wings unlike most members of the group. I'm pretty sure the spider is the White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), though the band under its eyes is yellow rather than white. This spider is good sized and almost hairless. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2020)

File.Name: k928c_k927-037.jpg
Notes: 50396100882 Here is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a new composite flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. It's probably another genus Mecaphesa spider with that general hairiness, compare my [Next] photo of a Crab Spider without hairs. It's tiny, less than 1/8 inch long. It seems to be building a little structure by tying flower petals together with webbing - like a little wedding bower. It was windy when I got this photo, and it's a lucky shot as the plant was bobbing back and forth. I went back today to see if I could get a better shot of its bower, and it was even windier. I'll try again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2020)

File.Name: k928d_k925-020.jpg
Notes: 50396084332 I believe this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on its web in the woods a couple of days ago. It's on the sheet-like part of its web, but it had a funnel-like opening nearby - where it would quickly retreat when I got too close. The web is covered with dust and other end-of-summer debris - including ashes from wildfires far away that settled down when the smoke was bad a couple of weeks ago. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2020)

File.Name: k929a_k929-020.jpg
Notes:
50399783007 Here is another shot taken today of the same impressive Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I've been showing in this photo and this photo over the last two days. It's waiting for prey on a flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae), and it's been going from flower to flower on the same plant for all the time I've been following it. I'm pretty sure the spider is the White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), though the band under its eyes is yellow rather than white. This spider is good sized and almost hairless. Hah, I'll probably have one more photo of it when the Arachtober group starts in two days. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2020)

It was quite hot today in the 90s, but I'm happy there was not much wind - even in the evening when there was talk of a sundowner wind event. We're under a NWS Heat Advisory for the rest of the week. The Santa Ynez Valley will be in the 100s each day.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Telegraph Weed; White-banded Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Misumenoides formosipes
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Misumenoides formosipes

File.Name: k929b_k929-002.jpg
Notes: 50399745967 This is another small caterpillar-like larva on new flower buds of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. Maybe it's not a caterpillar of a moth or butterfly at all, but the larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera), see this photo for another one I'm not sure of and more info on telling them apart. It does seem to have some webbing, so does that suggest caterpillar? Any advice on ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2020)

File.Name: k929c_k928-029.jpg
Notes:
50399701637 Can you see the Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding among dead leaves and old flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae)? Sometimes I stare right at them without seeing - and they suddenly appear. This one is female with narrow wings and an ovipositor in the rear. It's the males with broad wings (like this) that do the singing in late summer and autumn. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2020)

The old flowers of this shrub are persistent, which distinguishes the genus Diplacus from Mimulus. I first learned this shrub as Diplacus using the old Munz A California Flora manual from 1970. Then Jepson came along and renamed it as Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, and it took me many years to remember that new name. It's been easier to go back to its real name.
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Diplacus; Mimulus; Diplacus; A California Flora; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; real
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Diplacus; Mimulus; Diplacus; A California Flora; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; real

File.Name: k929d_k927-028.jpg
Notes: 50399674382 Here are a mating pair of "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). They might be the same Western Red-shouldered Stink Bugs (Thyanta pallidovirens) that I recently showed in this photo, and these bugs do seem to be highly variable in color. I reckon that's the smaller male on the left. It's almost Wednesday, so happy Insect Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2020)
CommonNames: Bird's Beak; Western Red-shouldered Stink Bugs
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens

File.Name: k930a_k930-031.jpg
Notes:
50403159992 This is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers of non-native Lantana (Verbenaceae) in the garden this morning. It's a female, more plain than the males with no "dogface" pattern, simpler spots on its wings, and a paler yellow color that has a hint of green. Those pointy forewings are distinctive for both male and female, and distinguish them from other Sulphur butterflies. It's an attractive butterfly, but you have to see a male with its wings open to appreciate why it is the official California State Butterfly - like this one. I was seeing these butterflies in July, and now I'm seeing them again, so I believe this is their second brood. There's a mystery here, as their host plant is said to be False Indigo (Amorpha californica, Fabaceae) - which I've never found locally on San Marcos Pass, see this photo from another pass over our mountains a few miles away. I suspect they utilize another host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week - and we missed last week so the need was great. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. I did make time for a few photos before we left. It was HOT today, up to 101∞ at home and up to 108∞ in the Santa Ynez Valley where we were. Now we're under a NWS Red Flag Warning as well as a Heat Advisory. Yipes!
ScientificName: Colias eurydice; Verbenaceae; Amorpha californica; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Colias eurydice; Verbenaceae; Amorpha californica; Fabaceae

File.Name: k930b_k930-013.jpg
Notes: 50402278458 Here is a new nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a narrow leaf of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I believe this is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. I was finding lots of these nymphs in June, and I found an adult at the beginning of August, see this photo - and now more nymphs. I guess this means they are multi-brooded, right? These nymphs are particularly gorgeous, and it's nice to have another crop of them to photograph as we move into autumn. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2020)

File.Name: k930c_k930-005.jpg
Notes: 50402955866 Why is it that sometimes spider webs are invisible in my photos and other times they jump out like this one? I'm sure part of the answer is backlight - shooting into the sun at something with dark shadow behind. I wish that always worked! This is the perfect web of an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider, shot with shadow and backlight in the garden today. It's a good Halloween web, about the size of a volleyball, but no spider was to be found. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2020)

File.Name: k930d_k929-008.jpg
Notes: 50402940186 Here are signs of a struggle on the web of an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the woods yesterday morning. Maybe the victim was a moth with wing scales? The web is about the size of a volleyball, but there was no sign of the spider. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2020)

File.Name: ka01a_ka01-010.jpg
Notes:
50406258646 Here is one more shot taken today of the same impressive Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I've been showing in this photo and this photo and this photo over the last few days. It's been a good model for me. Here it is waiting for prey on a flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae), and it's been going from flower to flower on the same plant for all the time I've been following it. I'm pretty sure the spider is the White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), though the band under its eyes is yellow rather than white. This spider is good sized and almost hairless. I'm glad it stuck around for October 1 aka Arachtober 1. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2020)

The month of Arachtober has finally arrived! The group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day. It's autumn, and this is the time for spiders to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good. It's their time. Despite being autumn, it's still fiendishly hot, up to 102∞ on the upstairs deck today. That's too hot for spiders or insects or nature photographers, but I did my best and found a few (like myself) who lack the good sense to stay in the shade. I'm hoping it will cool down this weekend.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Telegraph Weed; White-banded Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Misumenoides formosipes
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Misumenoides formosipes

File.Name: ka01b_ka01-013.jpg
Notes: 50405515028 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. These caterpillars usually move by forming loops with their bodies, but this one is standing up pretending to be a stick. The fading flowers are brown and white, a good match for the red and white colors of the caterpillar - when it was laying flat on the flowers, I could barely see it. See this photo for a kindred caterpillar that is also a master of disguise. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2020)

File.Name: ka01c_k929-006.jpg
Notes: 50406347667 Here another nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae) on a narrow leaf of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe it is the nymph of a Bush Katydid in the genus Scudderia, likely Scudderia mexicana. This might be the same nymph I showed yesterday in this photo - at least it's on the same plant, taken the day before. It's a nice shot of the nymph's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2020)

File.Name: ka01d_k927-032.jpg
Notes: 50406331097 This is another shot of the small wasp (Hymenoptera) digging into an old flower of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) - see this photo for more of the same wasp. I believe this is a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea) - compare this photo of a different wasp with its neat mud nest. I've found two of these wasps digging into the flowers and coming out with a grub to provision their nests - see this photo and this photo. The one in my photo today didn't find a grub, but it's a good shot of its abdomen which comes is in several distinct pieces. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2020)
CommonNames: wasp; Bird's Beak; Potter Wasp; Mason Wasp
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Eumeninae; Vespidea
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Eumeninae; Vespidea

File.Name: ka02a_ka02-011.jpg
Notes:
50410011716 Here is a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in its shelter under old leaves of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The spider's web is nearby, off to the right, and it has a sensing line to pick up vibrations visible by its feet. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) The spider was nearly invisible, hiding in the shadows under the leaves - I had to overexpose this shot to shed some light on it. I've seen its web before and even have a photo of part of it here, but today was the first time I've actually found the spider. Arachtober 2. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2020)

Today was another hot one, just under 100∞ on our upstairs porch, but fortunately there was little wind. The air is pretty smoky again mostly from wildfires far away - I can see it, but I can't smell it. It should be a few degrees cooler tonight and tomorrow - it didn't get out of the 90s last night.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ka02b_ka02-024.jpg
Notes: 50409986621 If you look close under its head, you can see that this large spider has captured a small prey - like a tiny corncob. This is the same impressive Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I've been showing in this photo and several more lately, taken in the woods today. It's prey seems to be a small grub of some kind. It's interesting that this particular plant stem is running out of flowers, so is the spider now digging for grubs within the old flower heads instead of waiting for prey on fresh flowers? It will also be interesting to see what it does next. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2020)

File.Name: ka02c_ka02-032.jpg
Notes: 50409266073 Here is another dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) dropping in on a new flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the fly is the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa) or kin in the Villini tribe. They often bob up and down over the flower before landing. It was hot in triple digits again today, and these flies and the Flesh Flies in my [Previous] photo were about the only flies I saw - but that's good enough for . . . HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2020)

File.Name: ka02d_ka02-018.jpg
Notes: 50409252818 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, probably a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. They come in all different sizes from big to small, but they all have reddish eyes and red-tipped butt like the glowing ember of a cigar. Despite their unpleasant common and scientific names, I like these flies - they are large and husky, like they could take on anything, and they are satisfying to photograph. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2020)

File.Name: ka03a_ka03-009.jpg
Notes:
50413695693 Here is a Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) on a slightly soiled leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods this morning. They have amazing eyes, but this one got away from me before I could move in closer - see this photo for another one's eyes. I've been seeing these interesting insects on the window screens at night lately - are they mostly nocturnal? (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2020)

Today was a few degrees cooler than yesterday, but still up to 95∞ on our upstairs porch, and it felt muggy with 30-40% humidity. There hasn't been much wind, but it's been pretty smoky from wildfires far away - Santa Ynez actually came in as "Unhealthy" today with 158 PM2.5, but it's not that bad up here in the mountains. It should very slowly cool down over the coming week. Hah, there's even a rumor of rain next weekend.

File.Name: ka03b_ka03-005.jpg
Notes: 50414285641 This is a medium-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in its retreat in a shrub of Lavender (Lavendula, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I like the way it has one leg extended in a come-hither attitude, like spider striptease. That leg is actually holding a sensing line that goes to its web off-screen on the left, like an angler waiting for a strike. I posted a shot of the web to the Web Wednesdays group last week, see this photo. The spider is along the main line on top, off to the right, but unfortunately not in the frame. Arachtober 3. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2020)

File.Name: ka03c_ka03-021.jpg
Notes: 50414401332 Here is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a new autumn flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods this morning. I suspect the bee is a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina - not very hairy but this female does have pollen-collecting hairs on her hind legs. We have a number of different autumn flowers that are mostly composites, and I believe they are important for keeping the pollinators alive during this hard time of the year before the winter rains come. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Ceratina

File.Name: ka03d_ka03-002.jpg
Notes: 50413521798 These small caterpillars are doing some real damage to the new flower buds of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I found this one on a narrow leaf near the flowers. It might be the same kind as I showed in this photo a few days ago, but it is larger and brown rather than green. I believe from the number of prolegs that it really is a caterpillar (Lepidoptera) and not a sawfly larva. It also has some webbing - I'll try to get a better photo for the Web Wednesday group. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2020)

File.Name: ka04a_ka04-021.jpg
Notes:
50420659562 Here is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This is the butterfly's host plant, see this photo of a days-old caterpillar on a similar leaf. I reckon the butterfly in my photo today is just enjoying the view. This one looks in good shape, though it's missing a chunk of its hidwings. I'm still seeing quite a few of these lately, especially around the leaky pipe that lets them get a drink. They might even be more common now than the little skippers in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2020)

Today was a few degrees cooler than yesterday, but still into the 90s on our upstairs porch. There was that annoying breeze from the south, not a big wind, but it keeps everything moving and makes getting my kind of photos difficult. It should continue to very slowly cool down over the coming week. Hah, there's even a rumor of rain next weekend.

File.Name: ka04b_ka04-005.jpg
Notes: 50420570277 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a shrub of non-native Lavender (Lavendula, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. The spider's orb web is on the right and its cobweb-like retreat on the left, and there is a "sensing line" which connects the two and allows the spider to sense the vibrations of its prey. What I find most interesting is that the sensing line is under tension, actually pulling the center of the web towards the spider. I guess it carries vibrations better that way, like an old-fashioned cup and string telephone which only works if the string is tight. Yeah, this would be a good one for Web Wednesday, but I've shown these webs before, and I need a spider photo for today. It's been so hot lately that I'm afraid the spiders and insects are dropping like flies. Seriously. I hope their populations rebound as it starts to cool down now. Arachtober 4. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2020)

File.Name: ka04c_ka04-014.jpg
Notes: 50419668993 Here is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) walking along a stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) that's recently finished flowering. I've been seeing a fair number of these on all different plants. It might even be that I'm seeing more of these predators than their possible prey. It's been so hot lately that I'm afraid the insects are dropping like flies. Seriously. I hope the spider and insect populations rebound as it starts to cool down now. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2020)

File.Name: ka04d_ka04-043.jpg
Notes: 50420312456 This is another Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect that I found dead on a spider web today not too far from the live one I showed yesterday in my [Previous] photo (here). I'm not sure if the are the same species or not, but the difference that strikes me is their eyes - this dead one has lost the star in its eyes. I don't understand, but it's a potent reminder of death. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2020)

File.Name: ka05a_ka05-038.jpg
Notes:
50425404936 Here is a remarkable Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have eyes on each end! It's working a new flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe this is Geminaria canalis - there's nothing else is like it. You can see its long proboscis and true eyes on the left, but what's remarkable are the those dark "faux eyes" on its back end that reflect light just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. I have another nice shot of the whole fly in this photo, and see this photo to appreciate its value as mimicry. I'll have another photo (at least) for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday and/or the Fly Day Friday groups. I don't believe I've ever seen one of these remarkable flies before in our home canyon. That's a good thing about being homebound - finding new things by looking closer. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2020)

It still got up over 90∞ today, but it was cooler for much of the day as clouds moved in from the south, a remnant of Tropical Storm Marie up from Baja. There was the usual breeze from the south also. It was a few degrees cooler today, and I did find several spiders for the Arachtober group after almost being skunked yesterday. I guess it really can be too hot for spiders and insects.
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Geminaria canalis; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Geminaria canalis; Salticidae

File.Name: ka05b_ka05-041.jpg
Notes:
50425502187 This is the patterned rear end of a fairly large Orb-Weaver Spider in the cactus patch of our garden this morning. I believe it is an Araneus andrewsi spider with humps on its back and dark colors and bristly legs. I got this photo of the same spider wrapping a fly for its prey, and this photo of the same spider digesting that unfortunate fly. But then the spider retreated to a dark place among the cactus spines and really didn't want to move. After a week of this, I finally nudged it out with a stick today and got my photo. Arachtober 5. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2020)

(And if you're here from the Arachtober group, you might also enjoy this photo from today showing a tiny Jumping Spider grappling with its prey - a tiny caterpillar! I believe that's something unusual.)
Locality: And if you're here from the Arachtober group

File.Name: ka05c_ka05-005.jpg
Notes: 50425301286 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) grappling with its prey - a tiny caterpillar. I had to check my records, and I believe I've only found one other example of any kind of spider going after a caterpillar, see this photo involving a Crab Spider. I'm not sure what kind of Jumping Spider this is or what the caterpillar is - but I've seen the caterpillar before, compare this photo on the same flower buds of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2020)
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; caterpillar; Mexican Bush Sage

File.Name: ka05d_ka04-024.jpg
Notes: 50425274586 This is looking close at a single flower of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae plant family, still flowering though its been going for a while. The flower petals are usually hidden in the bracts, but sometimes they spill out like here on the left. I stripped away the bracts of this one to expose the hidden flower on the right. It's got a deeper throat than you imagine just looking at the top. See this photo for how a wild bee gets into he flower. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2020)

File.Name: ka06a_ka06-021.jpg
Notes:
50429362976 I found this large black and yellow wasp (Hymenoptera) hiding under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It was overcast and on the cool side for a change, so I reckon the wasp was waiting for it to warm up. At first the wasp looks like other social Vespid Wasps (family Vespidae), but I'm not so sure - for one thing, it doesn't have enough black on its abdomen, which is not petiolate like the one in this photo. I'm not sure, and I won't try to guess what it is, but any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2020)

It was cloudy and cooler all morning, a remnant of Tropical Storm Marie up from Baja. It mostly cleared up in the afternoon, and it warmed up into the mid-80s. There was the usual annoying breeze all morning. A few days ago, we were near 100∞. It was cooler today and yesterday, and I have been finding spiders for the Arachtober group after almost being skunked over the weekend. I guess it really can be too hot for spiders and insects.
CommonNames: wasp; Coast Live Oak; Vespid Wasps

File.Name: ka06b_ka06-007.jpg
Notes: 50429484337 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey among flowers of a non-native Mint plant in the garden this morning. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa again with that general hairiness, compare this photo of a different Crab Spider without hairs. This one really is tiny, just 450 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.8 mm - a bit more than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Of course the span of its reach is four or five times that! Ha, the individual mint flowers are about the same size as the buckwheat flowers in my [Previous] photo (here), so compare the spiders in each photo! Arachtober 6. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2020)

File.Name: ka06c_ka06-012.jpg
Notes: 50429289981 Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for something to grab among flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with that general hairiness, compare this photo of a Crab Spider without hairs. The buckwheat flowers are winding down and slowly turning to a rusty red/brown color, but there are still viable flowers in the clusters - look for the pink anther tips of the stamens on the left of my photo. I wonder if the spiders deliberately wait for their prey by these flowers? (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2020)

File.Name: ka06d_ka06-017.jpg
Notes: 50429437737 This is another cute Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) visiting flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is genus Villa in the tribe Villini. The buckwheat flowers are winding down and slowly turning to a rusty red/brown color, but there are still viable flowers in the clusters - look for the pink anther tips of the stamens in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Villa; Villini
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Villa; Villini

File.Name: ka07a_ka07-015.jpg
Notes:
50434143401 This tiny fly (Diptera) has been ovipositing - laying eggs - on unopened buds of this autumn-flowering Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) shrub in the woods this morning. It's about 920 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot from its nose to the tip of its ovipositor, which comes to 3.8 mm - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe from its tube-like ovipositor and iridescent eyes and patterned wings that it's a Tephritid Fruit Fly in the family Tephritidae - probably species Goedenia formosa which I found at BugGuide. It was wagging its wings constantly while moving around on the plant. I'll have another photo (at least) for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday and/or the Fly Day Friday groups. As always, any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2020)

It was clear but cooler than it's been today, barely up into the 80s. Unfortunately, it was also quite breezy from the south, at least up and down our east-west canyon. Hah, I'm trying to listen to the VP debate while writing these comments - it's hard to concentrate on flies and spiders ;=)
CommonNames: fly; Sawtooth Goldenbush; Tephritid Fruit Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Tephritidae; Goedenia formosa
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Tephritidae; Goedenia formosa

File.Name: ka07b_ka07-027.jpg
Notes:
50434239302 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey among new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa again with that general hairiness. These flowers look different from the ones I've been showing lately, e.g. in this photo, because the shrubs are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants. The flowers in my photo today are the female seed-bearing pistillate flowers, while the ones in the other photo are the male pollen-bearing staminate flowers. The male shrubs usually appear first, but for some reason the female shrubs are later than usual this year. This spider is ready for any pollinator that comes by. Arachtober 7. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2020)

(And if you're viewing this from the Arachtober group, you might also enjoy this photo from today showing a tiny grub in a spider web - something unusual.)
Locality: And if you're viewing this from the Arachtober group
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; dioecious

File.Name: ka07c_ka04-036.jpg
Notes: 50433321383 For some reason, I'm thinking of a child in pajamas snuggled in bed looking up at a mobile hanging from the ceiling . . . but no, it's stranger than that. This is a small grub on the dusty web of (I believe) a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider, and it seems to be gazing up at an old exuviae or shed skin of the spider. The grub doesn't seem to be under duress, so maybe it just fell out of the oak tree overhead? I went back the next day, and the grub was gone. I don't know if a spider would attack a grub, but see this photo of a spider with a caterpillar for prey. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2020)

File.Name: ka07d_ka02-026.jpg
Notes: 50434142277 I found this new orb web of a spider in an old flower cluster of last year's native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods a few days ago. The web is small, less than two inches across, and quite invisible unless the light is just so. It might be the work of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), though it's smaller and not so well-defined as the one in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2020)

File.Name: ka08a_ka08-007.jpg
Notes:
50439670972 This is a spectacular wasp (Hymenoptera) on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today - at least it's the rear end of the wasp, it was moving around so much that that's all I got. What a color - so blue it's almost black, eyes and wings too! I suspect this is a Spider Wasp in the family Pompilidae, probably genus Aporus. It's interesting that I also have this photo of a similar wasp taken on the same shrub back on 19 October 2015. I regularly see the giant Tarantula Hawks like in this photo, but my wasp today is much smaller, only a half-inch long or a bit more. Was it looking for the Crab Spider in my [Next] photo?? This is a good shot of its very blue rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2020)

We were in a cloud early this morning, and under clouds for much of the rest of the day after the fog lifted. It barely got up to 70∞ today. Our chance of rain tomorrow has evaporated, but at least we might get some drizzle. The seasons are working again!
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Pompilidae; Aporus
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Pompilidae; Aporus

File.Name: ka08b_ka08-019.jpg
Notes: 50439426686 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey among new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's similar to this photo from yesterday, but these are the male staminate flowers of the shrub which look quite different from the female pistillate flowers in the other photo. Different as they are, these are flowers of the same dioecious shrub that has male and female flowers on different plants. I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with that general hairiness. They are our most common spider than hangs out on flowers. Arachtober 8. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2020)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious; Mecaphesa

File.Name: ka08c_ka08-001.jpg
Notes: 50438674218 I found this little "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the side of a ripening cactus fruit in the garden this morning. I don't know what sort of bug it is, but it has a texture like Harris Tweed! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2020)

File.Name: ka08d_ka07-010.jpg
Notes: 50439336366 Here are two more shots of the tiny fly (Diptera) that I showed in my [Previous] photo (here). I'm pretty sure it's a Tephritid Fruit Fly in the family Tephritidae - probably species Goedenia formosa which I found at BugGuide. In the photo on the left, you can see it ovipositing - laying eggs - under the scales of an unopened flower bud of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Both photos feature the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2020)
ScientificName: Diptera; Tephritidae; Goedenia formosa; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Tephritidae; Goedenia formosa; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: ka09a_ka09-012.jpg
Notes:
50442958117 This is a very large female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on an old flower cluster of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). I figure it's not just large but large with eggs! I'm still hearing crickets calling at night, but less so now, and their trill is slower with the cooler temperatures. I suspect mating is coming to an end and egg-laying is about to get under way. Now that's something I would like to see. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2020)

It was cool again today, only up to about 70∞ and overcast all morning. Rain was in the forecast a few days ago but no longer - we might get some drizzle tonight. Then it will start warming up again, into the 90s in a few days. That's how our autumn weather is - it rocks . . . back and forth.

File.Name: ka09b_ka09-002.jpg
Notes: 50442050543 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) with prey in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's a bit overexposed, but I can still see that its prey is another spider that's really tiny! Seems odd, but I guess it's no different that one of us eating a hamburger. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is - there are many different kinds, and I never did get a look at it except through my camera lens. Arachtober 9. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2020)

File.Name: ka09c_ka05-035.jpg
Notes: 50442024328 Here are front and back views of a remarkable Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have a face on each end! It's working a new flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods a few days ago. I believe this fly is Geminaria canalis - there's nothing else is like it. You can see its long proboscis and true eyes on the left, but what's really remarkable are the those dark "faux eyes" on its back end that reflect light just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. I have another nice shot of the whole fly in this photo, and see this photo to appreciate its value as mimicry. These unusual flies are never common, and this the first one I've ever seen in our home canyon. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Geminaria canalis; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae; Geminaria canalis; Salticidae

File.Name: ka09d_ka04-002.jpg
Notes: 50442688041 This is a tiny humpbacked fly flying into a flower of non-native Lavender (Lavendula, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe it's an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it the "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2020)

File.Name: ka10a_ka10-012.jpg
Notes:
50447580368 Here is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on an old stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I've been seeing a fair number of these on all different plants, but this one seems especially colorful. They are named for the tiny size of their prey, not for any personal resemblance. You can see this one's mighty spike-like proboscis folded under its head - see this photo and this photo for what it does with that proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2020)

I was expecting drizzle this morning and warming during the day, but it didn't happen - it was mostly cloudy but dry, and only in the 60s all day. I reckon it will start to warm up tomorrow, and it sounds like we're heading into another hot week.

File.Name: ka10b_ka10-003.jpg
Notes: 50448368987 This isn't the best photo, but it really shows the swollen palps of this male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on old flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. Hah, if you didn't know what male spider palps look like before, you do now! I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa with that general hairiness. It was a very low-contrast cloudy morning with just the hint of a breeze that kept everything in constant motion - I did my best, and I like how it came out. Arachtober 10. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2020)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: ka10c_ka10-026.jpg
Notes: 50447450348 Here is an odd caterpillar of a Lycaenid butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - the group that includes Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks - on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is the caterpillar of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly with all those spiky hairs, compare this photo and Alice Abelia's comment. It seems late to be finding this, but I've found them in October before, see this photo from October 22 last year. I guess they are just late, and I hope they won't miss all the fresh flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2020)
