CommonNames: Lycaenid butterfly; California Buckwheat; Gray Hairstreak
ScientificName: Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Strymon melinus
associatedTaxa: Lycaenidae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Strymon melinus

File.Name: ka10d_ka09-020.jpg
Notes: 50448245312 I found this empty shell of a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods yesterday. I believe it's a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae, see this photo for a live one. The curious thing is that I found it now at the end of our usual summer drought rather than during our wet winter season, when I usually see them. We haven't had rain for months, but this snail's shell looks perfectly fresh. Are they out now at night? Is it an omen? Our prospects for a wet winter aren't looking too good right now with a developing La NiÒa, so any help will be appreciated - even a snail's. I found this just in time for snail Saturday, so HSS! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2020)

File.Name: ka11a_ka11-028.jpg
Notes:
50455822371 Here is another Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly perched on a dead stem in the middle of a patch of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) that you can't see here. It might be another Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly like the one in this photo, but maybe it's an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) with those dark antennae knobs like in this photo. I didn't really notice at the time. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2020)

Today was a lovely autumn day, warm enough for shorts in the 70s, but bring along a long-sleeved shirt just in case. It's curious how it can feel hot in the sun and cool in the shade just a few feet away. Our NWS Heat Advisory runs from Tuesday until Friday with temps in the high 90s. *Sigh* I'm not looking forward to more heat.
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; California Aster; Woodland Skipper; Umber Skipper
ScientificName: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides; Poanes melane

File.Name: ka11b_ka11-013.jpg
Notes:
50455735312 This is an impressive Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in a still-flowering shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I'm sure it is some kind of genus Phidippus with those reddish colors on its abdomen, but the grizzled black and white on its face and legs is just as impressive. It might be species Phidippus adumbratus which is part of the Phidippus insignarius group. I've seen three of these so far this month, but this is the first one that didn't vanish as soon as we noticed each other. It's a grand spider, larger than most jumpers. Arachtober 11. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2020)

Oops, I missed International Jumping Spider Day on October 10 yesterday, but HIJSD anyway!
ScientificName: Salticidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Phidippus; Phidippus adumbratus; Phidippus insignarius group
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Phidippus; Phidippus adumbratus; Phidippus insignarius group

File.Name: ka11c_ka11-019.jpg
Notes: 50455610557 Here is a handsome nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. This might be the same kind of nymph that I was finding inside of seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae) last winter, compare the nymphs in this photo. I've also found a very similar nymph on Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae), see this photo from last August. It's confusing because the nymphs change so much as they mature, and the adults are quite variable too. I reckon "nymph of a true bug" is good enough for ID. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2020)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: ka11d_ka11-031.jpg
Notes: 50455546197 This is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I don't know why these predators favor this plant, but they do - look at my [Previous] photo for one example, and there's more if you search. This one looks like it's ready to pounce, but I don't think that's their style. They move very slow and deliberately - I'm not really sure how they catch their prey, but they do. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2020)

File.Name: ka12a_ka12-015.jpg
Notes:
50465184051 I found this handsome grasshopper in flowering shrub of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe it's a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes, Melanoplinae). They seem to favor autumn-flowering composites like this shrub and Coyote Bush. What brilliant patriotic colors it has on its hind legs! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2020)

It did get up into the 90s today at the start of a new heat episode. The wind was mild and from the south. We're under a NWS Heat Advisory for the rest of the week, and triple-digit temperatures are expected. *Sigh* I'm not looking forward to more heat.
ScientificName: Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae
associatedTaxa: Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae

File.Name: ka12b_ka12-012.jpg
Notes: 50465030941 Tiny spiders catch tiny prey, and I believe this one has a really tiny Thrips (Thysanoptera) insect. This is another shot of the tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the right in my [Previous] photo (here). I got this photo a few minutes after the other one, and the spider's prey seems to have changed color from orange to black - maybe the spider caught two of them?? For scale, that yellow group of disk flowers at the bottom of my photo is about 3 mm or 1/8 inch, and the spider is noticeably smaller. I can also measure it directly by counting pixels - the spider is about 480 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) By the way, this one is female with narrow palps, compare the male in this photo that has much larger palps. Arachtober 12. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2020)

File.Name: ka12c_ka12-008.jpg
Notes: 50465070957 Here are two shots of tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with prey on autumn flowers of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae). I believe both spiders are genus Mecaphesa with their general hairiness. For scale, that yellow pedestal of disk flowers is about 3 mm or 1/8 inch, and the spiders are noticeably smaller. Tiny spiders catch tiny prey! The one on the left seems to have a little caterpillar or grub. The one on the right has captured a tiny Thrips (Thysanoptera) insect - or maybe two, see my [Next] photo. In both cases, the spider walked up and grabbed its prey instead of spinning a web or waiting for the critter to bump into it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2020)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Thysanoptera

File.Name: ka12d_ka11-020.jpg
Notes: 50464176243 This is a another handsome nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on stem of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods yesterday. It looks different from the similar nymph I showed yesterday in this photo, but who knows? These nymphs change so much as they mature, and the adults are quite variable too. I reckon "nymph of a true bug" is good enough for ID. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2020)

File.Name: ka13a_ka13-027.jpg
Notes:
50474729257 Here is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture and minerals - from damp sand near our well today. It's a female, more plain than the males with no "dogface" pattern, and a paler yellow color that has a hint of green. Those pointy forewings are distinctive for both male and female, and distinguish them from other Sulphur butterflies. It's an attractive butterfly, but you have to see a male with its wings open to appreciate why it is the official California State Butterfly - like this one. I was seeing these butterflies in July, and now I'm seeing them again, so I believe this is their second brood of the year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2020)

It got up into the 90s again today but no hotter. We're under a NWS Heat Advisory for the rest of the week, and triple-digit temperatures are in the forecast - so I hope it stays in the 90s. *Sigh* I'm not looking forward to more heat.

File.Name: ka13b_ka13-011.jpg
Notes:
50474440166 Here is a fairly large Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) subduing its prey while hanging from its web in the woods today. The victim appears to be some sort of Social Wasp (family Vespidae), though its eyes look different from the wasp in my [Previous] photo which has also become spider prey. I never did get a look at the spider's face, but I believe its some kind of Araneus spider. It's abdomen is much smaller than the spider in this photo, but maybe it's a male of the same kind? I hope I can find it again for a better view. Arachtober 13. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2020)

(And if you're viewing this from the Arachtober group, you might also enjoy this photo also from today showing another wasp in a similar situation - but be warned, it's a disturbing photo.)
Locality: And if you're viewing this from the Arachtober group

File.Name: ka13c_ka13-033.jpg
Notes: 50474341171 This is a pretty gruesome photo, so you might want to skip ahead - but I find it fascinating. It's a Social Wasp (family Vespidae) that's been caught in a spider web and trussed up into a small bundle. It's head is on the left. It reminds me of a car that's been through a "car crusher" - not just flattened but compacted into a small cube like a hay bale. My [Next] photo show another wasp in a similar situation. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2020)

File.Name: ka13d_ka13-022.jpg
Notes: 50474433612 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture and minerals - from damp sand near our well by the summer-dry creek today. Hah, you can catch more of these fine butterflies with wet soil than with flowers - I don't think I've ever seen one feeding. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2020)

File.Name: ka14a_ka14-006.jpg
Notes:
50484826582 First I noticed the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on an old flower stalk of non-native Lavender (Lavendula, Lamiaceae) in the garden this afternoon. Then I realized that it's crawling away from its exuviae - the shed skin after molting. In the close shot of the exuviae on the right , you can see the shell of the predator's head folded under its body, with antennae and proboscis visible. That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our biweekly shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley - that's once every two weeks, not twice a week. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. There was no sun in the garden yet before we left, but I did make time for a few photos when we got home - no spiders though. It was HOT today, up to 96∞ at home and up to 100∞ in the Santa Ynez Valley where we were. Our NWS Heat Advisory is good for two more days.
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Lavendula; Lamiaceae; exuviae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Lavendula; Lamiaceae; exuviae

File.Name: ka14b_ka13-005.jpg
Notes: 50483852833 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey among flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods yesterday. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with that general hairiness. The buckwheat flowers are winding down and slowly turning to a rusty red/brown color, and this spider seems to have exactly the right colors to match. This spider is male with those large palps, compare the narrow palps of the female spider in this photo. It's been my ambition to get a new spider photo each day during Arachtober, but I missed today, and this shot from yesterday will have to do. It was our shopping day that we do every other week. I did get a nice shot of an Assassin Bug crawling away from its exuviae when we got home in the afternoon, see my [Next] photo (here). Arachtober 14. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2020)

File.Name: ka14c_ka13-012.jpg
Notes: 50484484526 It's remarkable how these large Orb-Weaver Spider webs seem to disappear when the light isn't just so - I guess that's the point! This is the web of the large spider I showed in this photo a couple of weeks ago. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) Like its web, the spider was nearly invisible, hiding in the shadows in its shelter under old leaves of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) - also visible in the background of this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2020)
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: ka14d_ka11-036.jpg
Notes: 50484407251 The belly of the beast! This is a fairly large Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) on its web in the cactus patch of the garden, That's a pretty safe place, I couldn't get closer than this without sustaining some injury from cactus spines. I'm pretty sure this is the same spider I showed in this photo a week ago which I believe is Araneus andrewsi with humps on its back and dark colors and bristly legs. It's curious that after I got that other photo, the spider disappeared until I got this one a foot or two away. Then it disappeared again. I figure it must be somewhere in that cactus patch, but there are lots of places for it to hide. I'll be looking for it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2020)

File.Name: ka15a_ka15-009.jpg
Notes:
50492085038 I found this handsome grasshopper in flowering shrub of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe it's another Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes, Melanoplinae). It might even be the same individual as the one in this photo from a few days ago, it is actually in the same shrub. Maybe these grasshoppers don't get around much! They have brilliant red, white, and blue patriotic colors on their hind legs, mostly hidden here. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2020)

It seemed cooler this morning, but it still got up to the mid-90s in the afternoon. Our NWS Heat Advisory lasts until tomorrow evening, then we may get some slight cooling - into the 80s at least. We have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow from 8:00 am to 6:00 pm, and I should be able to post if it doesn't run much later than that. It seems a bad idea with the heat and wildfire danger right now. We've had way too many of these outages this year, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
ScientificName: Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae; way too many
associatedTaxa: Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae; way too many

File.Name: ka15b_ka15-017.jpg
Notes: 50492899742 Here is a mama spider with two egg cases and a whole bunch of tiny spiderlings in the woods today. I believe she is species Tidarren sisyphoides in the group of Combfooted Spiders aka Cobweb Weavers in the family Theridiidae. Her abdomen is about the size and shape of a small grape. These spiders have comb-like hairs on their legs that are visible in this photo - and see this photo of a different species. This particular spider is known for making a retreat in a fallen leaf that hangs in the middle of its web - the leaf looks accidental, but I'll wager the spider places it there. One thing I miss on my new Nikon D500 camera is an on-camera flash, it would have been handy for this photo. But just as well, I used a foil reflector to get some light into the spider's cave, and it worked just fine. I'll try to get back to this spider for a better shot of her brood. Arachtober 15. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2020)

File.Name: ka15c_ka11-008.jpg
Notes: 50492723161 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a spent autumn flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae - at least my photo shows the spider's cute rear end. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with that general hairiness and preference for flowers. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2020)

File.Name: ka15d_ka12-019.jpg
Notes: 50492002488 This is another furry Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) visiting an autumn flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae - at least my photo shows the rear end of the fly. I believe it is genus Villa in the tribe Villini. I was photographing a crab spider on a flower to the left, when I noticed this fly. I swung my camera around and got this photo - too large and scrunched up against the side of my frame, but still a nice shot for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2020)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Villa; Villini
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Villa; Villini

File.Name: ka16a_ka16-002.jpg
Notes:
50496523111 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on an autumn flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) - at least my photo shows the spider's rear end. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with that general hairiness and preference for flowers. For scale, that yellow group of disk flowers is about 3 mm or 1/8 inch, and the spider is noticeably smaller. Arachtober 16a. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2020)

Just two photos today, as we had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages that was scheduled from 8:00 am to 6:00 pm and ran to 7:30 instead. It's already late, and I hope my comments make sense. I had some good fly photos today for the FDF group, but they can wait. It was hot again today, into the 90s. It might be a few degrees cooler tomorrow.

File.Name: ka16b_ka15-025.jpg
Notes: 50496631757 Here is a handsome fly (Diptera) tangled up in a spider web. The fly is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, Diptera - an uncommon fly in our mountains, and I wonder what twist of fate brought it to this end. The web belongs to a large Araneus spider that I've been watching, see this photo and this photo. Arachtober 16b and HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2020)

File.Name: ka17a_ka17-005.jpg
Notes:
50499944252 And now for something completely different . . . This is a fledgling Band-Tailed Pigeon (Patagioenas fasciata aka Columba fasciata) waiting for mama's attention in a thicket of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) and Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. We first found this bird along our driveway last Tuesday (Oct. 13), and I reckon it fell out or flew out of its nest that we could see in an oak tree overhead. We knew better than to intervene, as parent birds will often tend to their lost birds on the ground, so we left the bird on its own. Then I lost sight of it and was thinking of foxes - until I found it again today! It had moved about ten feet up an embankment, hard to see but looking calm and well-fed, so I guess mama is caring for it. I hope this will be a continuing story. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2020)

It was still warm today, up to about 90∞. Our NWS Heat Advisories are no more, and it sounds like it will slowly cool down a few degrees each day coming up until we finally have highs in the 60s by the end if the week.
CommonNames: Band-Tailed Pigeon; Wild Blackberry; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Patagioenas fasciata; Columba fasciata; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Patagioenas fasciata; Columba fasciata; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: ka17b_ka17-014.jpg
Notes: 50499030948 Here is a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) next to its shelter under old leaves of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The spider's web is nearby, off to the right, and it has a couple of sensing lines to pick up vibrations visible by its feet. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) The spider has been hiding in its retreat, and this is the first time I've seen it outside. This one is probably the size of a walnut. It's interesting to compare the spider in my [Previous] photo - also genus Araneus and recognizably similar to this one - but tiny. This is the only one of these large spiders that I've found this year, though Halloween is still a couple of weeks away. Arachtober 17a. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2020)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; Araneus
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides; Araneus

File.Name: ka17c_ka17-026.jpg
Notes: 50499701811 A spider with a mask! This is a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a dead leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It's only about 930 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.9 mm, a bit more than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders, see my [Next] photo, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former because there is no dip between the humps on its abdomen. See Alice Abelia's comment on this photo - which probably shows that latter of the two species. Arachtober 17b. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2020)

File.Name: ka17d_ka16-004.jpg
Notes: 50498945388 I'm pretty sure this is a Redcoat Seed Bug (Melanopleurus belfragei, Lygaeidae) rearing up on a dead stem of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) in the woods yesterday. You can't see the distinctive red and black crisscross pattern of its folded wings, but it is a nice shot of its proboscis folded under its head. I usually find these bugs on composite shrubs like this one in autumn. We have a number of these red and black true bugs, see my list in my description for this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2020)
ScientificName: Melanopleurus belfragei; Lygaeidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Melanopleurus belfragei; Lygaeidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: ka18a_ka18-020.jpg
Notes:
50504000347 Here is another female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Its abdomen looks lumpy, and I reckon it really is full of eggs ready to be laid. I'm still hearing crickets calling at night, but less so now. I suspect mating is coming to an end and egg-laying is getting under way - in plant stems according to Google. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2020)

Today was pleasant with high temps in the mid-80s, but there was a pretty strong wind from the north with 20-30 mph gusts. I did what I always do on windy days - take many photos and keep few.

File.Name: ka18b_ka18-002.jpg
Notes: 50503769191 This isn't the best photo, but it shows a remarkable spider. It's a tiny Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) hanging off a dead branch of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. I can't see where the actual front and back of the spider are, but I guess its size is only about 850 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) If you look close, you can see a strand of webbing and the spider's odd way of holding on to each end of the line separately - it must move by playing out one end of the line as it reels in the other. You can find more shots of these remarkable spiders and their interesting webs if you search my photos. It was my good luck to find this one during Aractober, and I hope it sticks around. Arachtober 18a. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2020)
CommonNames: Triangle Web Spider; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: ka18c_ka17-022.jpg
Notes: 50503886892 This is another shot of the spider I showed a few days ago in this photo. I believe the spider is species Tidarren sisyphoides in the group of Combfooted Spiders aka Cobweb Weavers in the family Theridiidae. This is the group of spiders that includes common House Spiders and Black Widows, though this one is more of a wild spider out in the woods. My photo today shows the spider in its retreat with egg sacs and newly-hatched spiderlings. The spider has made its retreat from fallen leaves that hang in the middle of its disorganized web - the leaves look accidental, but I'll wager the spider places them there. As in my other photo, I used a foil reflector to get some light into the spider's dark retreat. Arachtober 18b. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2020)

File.Name: ka18d_ka13-003.jpg
Notes: 50502959828 Here is one more shot of the fledgling Band-Tailed Pigeon (Patagioenas fasciata aka Columba fasciata) that I found along our driveway a few days ago, see my [Previous] photo for the whole story. Today's photo is back when I first found it last Tuesday morning (Oct.13), but then it disappeared. I never posted this photo because I was hoping for a happy ending . . . while thinking of foxes. I did find the little bird yesterday alive and well, and mama is no doubt taking care of it. I reckon the white fuzz in this photo is baby down getting crowded out by true feathers - including the primary flight feathers which probably got the bird out of the nest and into this predicament. It's interesting that I found another one of these fledgling birds out of the nest way back in 2012, see this photo which looks much the same as my photo today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2020)

File.Name: ka19a_ka19-027.jpg
Notes:
50507176188 I was sitting on the porch with a cold one after my walk today when I noticed this tattered California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly sitting on the metal arm of the deck chair right across from me. I reckon we were both worn out and ready for a break! This butterfly was staggering, and it is getting close to the end of their time. I got this photo hand-held. I'm not as steady as I used to be, so I almost always use a tripod, and this might be the first time I've deliberately used the VR feature of my new lens. It seems to work. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2020)

Today was pleasantly warm with high temps in the low-80s. There was the usual morning breeze from the south but no winds from the north like yesterday. A nice day, though it still doesn't feel much like autumn.

File.Name: ka19b_ka19-024.jpg
Notes: 50507813801 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. The buckwheat flowers are winding down and slowly turning to a rusty red/brown color, and this spider seems to have exactly the right colors to match. This spider seems to be male with those enlarged palps, compare the narrow palps of the female spider in this photo. Arachtober 19a. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2020)

File.Name: ka19c_ka18-024.jpg
Notes: 50507936617 Who is that masked spider?? This is another shot of the small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) that I found in its retreat under a dead leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae), see this photo from two days ago for more info. My photo today shows some of the spider's real face along with the faux eyes of its mask - including slender lady palps . I believe this spider is Araneus bispinosus without the dip between the points of its abdomen. It's interesting that I've seen these spiders with a mask before, see this photo and this photo - though I've seen many more of them without the mask. They just are variable. Arachtober 19b. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2020)
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Araneus bispinosus
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Araneus bispinosus

File.Name: ka19d_ka19-010.jpg
Notes: 50507871332 Ants as pollinators? Here are a pair of ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) visiting new female pistillate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure, but the ants might be species Formica moki, compare this photo. I don't usually think of ants visiting flowers, but they certainly can and do. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants. The male shrubs usually appear first, but for some reason the female shrubs are later than usual this year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2020)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Formica moki; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Formica moki; dioecious

File.Name: ka20a_ka20-011.jpg
Notes:
50511765487 I was looking for crab spiders on the autumn flowers of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today, when I noticed this tiny caterpillar on one of the flowers. It was actually burrowing into the column of orange disk flowers, but I managed to nudge it out for a photo. Maybe you can also see a couple of tiny Thrips (Thysanoptera) insects. For scale, the column of disk flowers is about 3 mm or 1/8 inch across. I have no idea what kind of caterpillar it is. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2020)

Today was just right with high temps about 80∞. There was the usual morning breeze from the south but it was quite calm later in the day. A nice day, though it still doesn't feel much like autumn.
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Thysanoptera

File.Name: ka20b_ka20-035.jpg
Notes: 50510802593 I noticed this shroud of webbing on the branches of a non-native weed along our road today which I believe is American Black Nightshade (Solanum americum aka Solanum nodiflorum, Solanaceae). At first I thought it might be the work of caterpillars, though it seems the wrong time of year for that, but then I realized that it's the work of Spider Mites (family Tetranychidae, Acari, Arachnida). I have a shot of a single mite in the insert, and maybe you can see more tiny orange specks on the web itself. The biggest mystery to me is why I haven't noticed them before, but maybe the webbing only catches the light when the sun is just so - and in fact the light only lasted for a few minutes while the sun was between two trees to the south. I'm glad they are only on this one weed and not on natives or our garden plants. The mites are clearly arachnids with eight legs, and not spiders because they only have one body part, therefore they fair game for Arachtober! Arachtober 20a. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2020)
ScientificName: Solanum americum; Solanum nodiflorum; Solanaceae; Tetranychidae; Acari; Arachnida
associatedTaxa: Solanum americum; Solanum nodiflorum; Solanaceae; Tetranychidae; Acari; Arachnida

File.Name: ka20c_ka20-022.jpg
Notes: 50511659822 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. There are many different kinds of these spiders, and I don't know which one this is. It really is tiny - only about 840 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's tiny but cute! Arachtober 20b. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2020)

File.Name: ka20d_ka20-029.jpg
Notes: 50511649842 This is a Yellow Jacket wasp (genus Vespula, Hymenoptera) in a small oak tree in the woods today. These are social wasps that make large colonies in the ground, see this photo taken nearby a couple of years ago. They can get aggressive as autumn approaches. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2020)

File.Name: ka21a_ka21-038.jpg
Notes:
50514273428 Here is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly paused for a moment on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) by our summer-dry creek today. I'm mostly looking for spiders this month, but I'm always ready for anything else that comes along - especially a nice late-season butterfly like this. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2020)

It was a few degrees cooler again today, not quite up to 80∞. Cooler weather is approaching and probably fog and drizzle. There's even a chance of light rain this weekend. This will be our first precipitation since last spring.

File.Name: ka21b_ka21-022.jpg
Notes: 50514958721 This is a little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) dragging its prey along a stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that still has a few flowers. Its prey is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), and it's really just trying to get away from the photographer - me! One it noticed me, it never stopped moving, dragging its meal along the whole time. I was lucky to get this photo - definitely an action shot. Arachtober 21a. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2020)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coyote Bush; Syrphid Fly
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: ka21c_ka21-002.jpg
Notes: 50515080637 Here are two shots of a really tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on it's web in he woods today. The spider is only about 640 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The whole web is a few inches across. I've only seen a couple of these lately, all tiny - maybe they are juveniles? In time they will decorate their web with the bodies of their prey, but so far this one only has a white stabilimentum to mark where its future trash line will be. Arachtober 21b and Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2020)

File.Name: ka21d_ka21-034.jpg
Notes: 50514886951 This is another shot of a shroud of webbing covering leaves of a non-native weed along our road today. I believe the plant is American Black Nightshade (Solanum americum aka Solanum nodiflorum, Solanaceae) and the webbing is the work of Spider Mites (family Tetranychidae, Acari, Arachnida). I have a shot of a single mite with this photo from yesterday, and you can see many more of them as the tiny orange specks on the web in this photo. Not all the specks are mites, I guess the rest are frass or mite poop. In time they will strip the plant, and I'm glad I've only seen them on this one weed and not on natives or our garden plants. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2020)
ScientificName: Solanum americum; Solanum nodiflorum; Solanaceae; Tetranychidae; Acari; Arachnida
associatedTaxa: Solanum americum; Solanum nodiflorum; Solanaceae; Tetranychidae; Acari; Arachnida

File.Name: ka22a_ka22-014.jpg
Notes:
50518579852 I found this adult Katydid (Tettigoniidae) in a still-flowering plant of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's a female with a visible ovipositor that you can see at the end of her abdomen about midway along her wings. I believe this is one of the Bush Katydids in the genus Scudderia, probably the Mexican Bush Katydid (Scudderia mexicana). I've been following their development starting from the gorgeous nymphs in this photo. I was also finding nymphs last spring and an adult in early August (here), so I'm thinking this is a second brood? We also have Angle-winged Katydids in the genus Microcentrum with plain green nymphs and more leaf-like wings, see this photo. The adults of both kinds seem to be much less common than the nymphs. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2020)

We were in a cloud today, and it was even a bit chilly with a high temp of about 60∞. I like the soft low-contrast light that the fog brings, but I don't like the "fog winds" that usually come with it. There's nothing to do except bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to get a keeper.
CommonNames: Katydid; Mugwort; Bush Katydids; Mexican Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana; Microcentrum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana; Microcentrum

File.Name: ka22b_ka22-002.jpg
Notes: 50517661853 Here is another small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It looks like it's waiting for prey, but it was chilly today and potential prey on the wing was scarce. On the other hand, the spider in my [Previous] photo did find a meal today, albeit a tiny one. Arachtober 22a. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2020)

File.Name: ka22c_ka22-010.jpg
Notes: 50517632418 This tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) has caught a really tiny critter for lunch. I'm not sure what its prey is - it looks like a little shrimp, maybe a Thrips (Thysanoptera) insect? The spider is only about 550 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.3 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I found this spider on an old calyx of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. Arachtober 22. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2020)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Thrips; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Thysanoptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Thysanoptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: ka22d_ka22-005.jpg
Notes: 50518325696 Here is another Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This is a broad-winged male that also lacks the long ovipositor of the narrow-winged female, compare this photo. These crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but they hide "Ostrich-style" figuring that if they can't see you then you can't see them. It's not the best strategy for hiding, but it makes for great butt shots - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2020)

File.Name: ka29a_ka29-014.jpg
Notes:
50546097157 I believe this is a small Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka Frontinella pyramitela, Linyphiidae) hanging under the bowl part of its web in the woods today. It's tiny, less than 3 mm or 1/8 inch long. See this photo of a complete web. I was surprised to find his one today, as most of their webs seem to be abandoned at this late date. It's hard to get a clean shot of these spiders since they hang under their web, which means you have to shoot through the web to get a photo from above. Arachtober 29a. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2020)

I apologize for being absent for the last week. Our DSL modem suddenly stopped working, and we had to wait for a new one to arrive by mail - and then had to wait for tech help to get it working. Bad timing, since I've been trying to post a new spider photo every day for the Arachtober group, but so it goes. I have been getting new spider photos every day even though the internet is down - my own private Arachtober. I'll post (at least) one from each of the missing days just to prove I've been doing my duty.

File.Name: ka29b_ka27-019.jpg
Notes: 50546067382 Here is a small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) ready to balloon away from a spent flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Actually it didn't balloon, but it let out a line of webbing which tangled in the same shrub and then ran along the line. It certainly has its butt up in the air, so HBBBT as well as Arachtober 29b(27). (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2020)

File.Name: ka29c_ka25-007.jpg
Notes: 50545899151 This is the rear end of a small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) hiding among leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. You can see the spider's spinnerets sticking out at the tip of its tail. The yellowish tubes along the edge of the leaf are some kind of gall that I don't understand, see this photo on the left. I have other spiders for Arachtober today, but this will do for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2020)

File.Name: ka29d_ka23-023.jpg
Notes: 50545124153 Here are two shots of a different Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with cryptic colors that I found on a staminate shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's mostly done flowering for the year. The spider appears to be ballooning in the shot on the right with its butt up in the air. This is not one of the flower-loving Mecaphesa spiders like the ones I've been showing. It resembles a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but I think it's more likely genus Ozyptila with stubby front legs with just two spines on the first tibia, visible on the right. The spider is really tiny, just 470 pixels in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.0 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Arachtober 29c(23). (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2020)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coyote Bush; Ground Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Xysticus; Ozyptila
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Xysticus; Ozyptila

File.Name: ka30a_ka30-005.jpg
Notes:
50549278531 This is one of the smallest spiders I've ever tried to photograph in the woods today. It's abdomen is only about 350 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.5 mm - less than 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) First I noticed a small orb web on this branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), then I followed the web lines back to the spider. I believe from the color pattern on its back that it is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), probably a juvenile. These spiders make a proper orb web with a more haphazard retreat off to the side, see this photo. Arachtober 30a. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2020)

It was warm today up into the 80s - after drizzle and light rain last weekend. That's how it is with our autumn weather, it swings . . . back and forth. I'm still playing catch up with my photos from last week when our internet was down, trying to post at least one spider photo from each of the missing days just to prove I was doing my duty. I should be able to finish tomorrow.

File.Name: ka30b_ka29-025.jpg
Notes: 50549258586 I don't know what kind of fly (Diptera) this is, but it's a handsome one. I found it taking moisture from damp soil near our well head, and here it is on a small rock nearby. I assumed at the time that it was some kind of atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because of its bristly abdomen, but I couldn't find it at BugGuide. Alice Abelia identified it for me as Vanderwulpia atrophopodoides, and it is indeed a Tachinid - thanks Alice. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2020)

File.Name: ka30c_ka23-008.jpg
Notes: 50548484033 I found this odd fly (Diptera) on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks like it's made of leather! I believe it is a Heleomyzid Fly in the family Heleomyzidae, probably tribe Heleomyzini. The clue for finding this at BugGuide was that the margins of its wings are minutely bristly - look close along the right side. I don't know anything else about it. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Heleomyzid Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heleomyzidae; Heleomyzini
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Heleomyzidae; Heleomyzini

File.Name: ka30d_ka26-006.jpg
Notes: 50549181471 I believe this is a male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the genus Mecaphesa on an old flower/seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). This spider has enlarged knob-like palps like all male spiders, compare this photo of a female with slender palps. Even though the palps are different, male and female look about the same. But compare the male Crab Spider in my [Previous] photo with much longer front legs. There's something going on here that I don't understand. Arachtober 30b(26). (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2020)

File.Name: ka30e_ka25-012.jpg
Notes: 50549259637 Here are two shots of a male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a fading leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It's tiny, just 800 pixels in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 3.4 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I can tell it's a male because of its long legs and those enlarged palps which have a sexual function. But maybe this one isn't the usual genus Mecaphesa like in my [Next] photo? I believe that one is a male of the real genus Mecaphesa, with shorter arms and different palps. Maybe the one in my photo today is the same as shown in this photo and this photo with a very different mate that shows extreme sexual dimorphism? Yep, I'm confused - any help will be appreciated. Arachtober 30c(25). (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2020)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Mecaphesa; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Mecaphesa; Mecaphesa

File.Name: ka31a_ka31-003.jpg
Notes:
50553064372 This is of of those large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders in its retreat under a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods this morning. Its orb web is off to the left, the size of a basketball hoop. The spider is genus Araneus in the family Araneidae, probably Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) This one has a good hiding place that never gets any direct sun at this time of year except for a few minutes around 10:00 am. Hah, I waited for it today! These large spiders have been scarce this year, and I've been looking for them. They make those large Halloween webs that you don't want to walk into at night - where's the spider?? Arachtober 31a. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2020)

The Arachtober group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it as a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. I've added the condition to find all my daily spiders in the canyon at home. We had an internet outage all last week, but even so I've managed to find (and belatedly post) my new spiders for every day. I've also learned a lot from other group photos, mainly that there are more spiders in the world than I find at home in October. Now to get back to photographing all the other critters in the woods.
Locality: and belatedly post) my new spiders for every day.  I've also learned a lot from other group photos

File.Name: ka31b_ka30-012.jpg
Notes: 50552149928 Here is another hairy Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) ready to attack in a shrub of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) that still has a few open flowers on it. The flowers of this shrub are just about finished, and I wonder what the spiders do when they lose the flowers that attract their prey? Arachtober 31b. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2020)

File.Name: ka31c_ka28-011.jpg
Notes: 50552850501 This is a odd nest of webbing in a small shrub of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) that is starting to green up for a new growing season. The web is a small tube of webbing, open on this end at least, about the size of a small cigar - or a silk condom? I'm sure this is the work of a spider, but I'm not sure what kind - maybe family Clubionidae? Arachtober 31c(28). (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2020)

File.Name: ka31d_ka26-026.jpg
Notes: 50552964762 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) about to balloon away from a leaf of non-native American Black Nightshade (Solanum americum aka Solanum nodiflorum, Solanaceae) in the woods. The spider resembles a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but I think it's more likely genus Ozyptila - see this photo for more info. The leaf is remarkable, an organic soup of mites and (I guess) their nymphs. This is the work of Spider Mites (family Tetranychidae, Acari, Arachnida), see this photo for more info. Arachtober 31d(26). (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2020)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; American Black Nightshade; Ground Crab Spider; Spider Mites
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Solanum americum; Solanum nodiflorum; Solanaceae; Xysticus; Ozyptila; Tetranychidae; Acari; Arachnida

File.Name: ka31e_ka24-015.jpg
Notes: 50552928122 Here are two shots of a tiny orb-weaving spider in the woods, but it's a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family instead of Araneidae. Judging by the shape of its abdomen, I believe it is species Metellina mimetoides. It's tiny, just 410 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This is the first one I've seen since last spring, so I guess this is a juvenile just getting started. Arachtober 31e(24). (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2020)

File.Name: kb01a_kb01-015.jpg
Notes:
50557141421 This handsome fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf today sure looks like a Syrphid Fly, but I believe it is an atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because of its obvious calypters, well-developed subscutellum, and lack of spurious wing veins. I believe it is genus Xanthomelanodes in the subfamily Phasiinae that includes a number of species that lack the strong bristles that Tachinid Flies often have. Yeah, this is a good "butt shot" that would be perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but it's also a good shot for a Sunday. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2020)

I got this photo today, but the rest of today's photos come from last week when our internet was out. I kept taking photos every day, though I couldn't post them - mostly spiders for Arachtober but a few other critters as well. I do like to start my day's photos with a shot taken today. It was more or less cloudy and breezy all morning, not the best day for new photos, but I like this one of the fly's rear end - even if it isn't Thursday.

File.Name: kb01b_ka29-021.jpg
Notes: 50557089276 Here is a tattered Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly taking moisture from wet sand and gravel near the water tub in our canyon. It's the first one I've seen since February. We have a trio of large butterflies that overwinter here as adults, including the Red Admiral (Nymphalis californica) and California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica), and this Mourning Cloak is the first of them that I've managed to photograph this season. Maybe it's an omen that weather is coming? Probably not, but it's still a welcome guest. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2020)
ScientificName: Nymphalis antiopa; Nymphalis californica; Nymphalis californica
associatedTaxa: Nymphalis antiopa; Nymphalis californica; Nymphalis californica

File.Name: kb01c_ka27-011.jpg
Notes: 50557058721 This is another Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding in a tangle of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) and Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It has pretty good camouflage - I was staring right at this critter without seeing until it clicked, and there it was! This is another female cricket with narrow wings and an ovipositor. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2020)
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: kb01d_ka25-016.jpg
Notes: 50557027696 Here is a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (genus Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) - a female pistillate plant that is starting to develop seeds. You can see its mighty spike-like proboscis folded under its head. It was a gloomy day with some drizzle last weekend, and this predator didn't want to move. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2020)

File.Name: kb01e_ka23-016.jpg
Notes: 50557005161 This large black Bumble Bee (Bombus) is hanging onto a branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) after being caught outside by cold and wet weather last weekend. It was moving around but not much. There was a steady breeze, and it took many shots to get this keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2020)

File.Name: kb02a_kb02-019.jpg
Notes:
50561665162 Here are two shots of an impressive Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in an shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. The shrub still has a few flowers among the faded ones, and I first found the spider on a cluster of them. I'm sure it is some kind of genus Phidippus spider with those orange colors on its abdomen, but the grizzled black and white on its face and legs is just as impressive. If you look close on the left, you can see spiky hair and just a hint of its blue/green chelicerae. It looks something like the spider I found in this photo last month, except that one has a red abdomen. Both might be species Phidippus adumbratus which is part of the Phidippus insignarius group. It's a grand spider, larger than most jumpers. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2020)

There was a steady breeze while I was trying to photograph this spider, and I did what I always do in the wind - take lots of photos and go through them for the few keepers. It was warm today also, almost up to 80∞. I'm still posting photos from our "missing week" with no internet, and I probably will be for some time.
ScientificName: Salticidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Phidippus; Phidippus adumbratus; Phidippus insignarius group
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Phidippus; Phidippus adumbratus; Phidippus insignarius group

File.Name: kb02b_kb02-003.jpg
Notes: 50561626407 This is a tiny humpbacked fly on a new composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe it is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it the "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. The column of yellow disk flowers is only about 1/8 inch across, which gives scale. I like the lighting in this photo - it only lasted a few moments as the low almost-winter sun moved by a tree. That's why I don't like to use flash. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2020)
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Geron
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Geron

File.Name: kb02c_ka31-008.jpg
Notes: 50561463711 This is another tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) waiting for prey on an autumn flower California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. You can get a sense of scale if you realize that the central column of yellow disk flowers is only about 1/8 inch across. See this photo for an example of the tiny prey these spiders hope to catch - while avoiding being caught! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2020)
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: kb02d_ka27-016.jpg
Notes: 50561438386 Heads or tails? This is a handsome nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on an old flower/seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods last week while our internet connection was down. Compare this photo from a couple of weeks ago of another nymph in the same shrub that looks similar but different. Adults and nymphs are both quite variable, so I reckon "nymph of a true bug" is good enough for ID. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2020)

File.Name: kb02e_ka26-012.jpg
Notes: 50560663848 Here are two shots of a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods last week while our internet connection was down. I found it on its small orb web, but it didn't stay there. I'm guessing that it's genus Eustala, and maybe species Eustala rosae. One clue is that it has a row of small bumps along it's back towards its rear end, see my notes. This one seems less decorated than the images in my books, but it really has a remarkable pattern of furry hairs - almost like feathers. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2020)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Eustala; Eustala rosae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Eustala; Eustala rosae

File.Name: kb03a_kb03-082.jpg
Notes:
50564906946 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I find quite a few of these predators in these plants that don't seem to attract many insects, and I don't know why. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2020)

There were enough high clouds today to cut the heat a bit, we were only in the 70s. There was the usual morning breeze from the south but not too bad. I've been distracted today. I'll be glad when this election is over.

File.Name: kb03b_kb03-015.jpg
Notes: 50564871006 Here are two shots of a debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's tiny, the ball of debris is only about 500 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.1 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) You can see some of the actual larva under its mantle of debris, impressive jaws on the left and hairy tail on the right. Apparently these debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter other predators - though I don't know what they find in woods that is so white - maybe Cudweed (Gnaphalium)? It's great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look for legs! (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2020)
CommonNames: debris-carrying larva; Green Lacewing; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Gnaphalium
associatedTaxa: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Gnaphalium

File.Name: kb03c_kb03-002.jpg
Notes: 50564833221 Here is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on an old flower/seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure these are the eggs of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera), but I won't try to guess what kind. The eggs are about 200 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.8 mm. (See here for how I figure.) The eggs appear to be fresh and whole - there's new life in there! I don't know what's up with that orange bump on the egg on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2020)

File.Name: kb03d_kb03-012.jpg
Notes: 50564057498 I suppose I could say "See the camouflaged caterpillar?" - but there is no caterpillar here that I know of. This is just a stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) that I found in the woods today with bicolored foliage that looks like it's braided along the stem. Yes, I rotated my camera 90∞ counterclockwise to make better use of my screen space. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2020)

File.Name: kb04a_kb04-029.jpg
Notes:
50568174236 I found this tiny but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on an old faded flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. Maybe it's genus Holopogon, see this photo and Dougee's comment. These flies are predators, and they catch their prey like little dragonflies. This one is small, less than an 1/4 inch long, and it's interesting to compare it with the large one in this photo from last year which is many times larger but recognizably the same sort. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. I'll save another photo for Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2020)

It was another warm day in the 80s with the usual morning breeze. I'm still distracted by the election. I'll be glad when it's over.
CommonNames: Robber Fly; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Holopogon
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Holopogon

File.Name: kb04b_kb04-047.jpg
Notes: 50567383853 Aractober is over, but I can look for spiders in the woods! Most of the flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in our mountains have faded to rusty brown by now, but there are still a few white flowers with pink anther tips to attract the pollinators - and the spiders which prey on them. This tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) has found a place guarding a little island of the small flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2020)

File.Name: kb04c_kb04-006.jpg
Notes: 50567338433 This is the web of an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's like the web in my [Previous] photo but much smaller, only a little more than a CD. I couldn't find the spider - those old flower/seed whorls are little spider hotels with lots of hiding places for something tiny! It might be he web of the small gray spider I show in this photo - at least it's in the same place. It's interesting that this web looks just like the web in my [Previous] photo except for its size. These orb webs are a good design that is scalable. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2020)

File.Name: kb04d_ka29-033.jpg
Notes: 50567308103 This is the web of one of those large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders (Araneus, Araneidae) in our canyon last week that I haven't posted a photo of yet. It's one of those webs that you don't want to walk into at night - where's the spider?? It's interesting that this web looks just like the web in my [Next] photo, but that one would probably fit into the the center space of this one. These orb webs are a good design that is scalable. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2020)

File.Name: kb05a_kb05-015.jpg
Notes:
50570739248 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) in the throat of a new autumn flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The wonderful large pollen grains seem to explode from the anthers like popcorn, but the bee doesn't seem interested. The bee has a red abdomen that's hard to see against the red background of the flower, maybe it is genus Lasioglossum in the family Halictidae? I like the combination of bee and flower in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2020)

Interesting day - mostly cloudy but still warm and mostly dead calm. I believe this is the work of Tropical Storm Odayls down south, but this will change dramatically tomorrow as a low pressure system approaches from the north bringing high temps in the 40s, wind, and a good chance of rain beginning over Friday night. This might be out first proper winter storm of the year. I'm still distracted with the election returns. I'll be glad when it's finally over.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Lasioglossum; Halictidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Lasioglossum; Halictidae

File.Name: kb05b_kb05-010.jpg
Notes: 50570687818 Here is a spider with its prey on a plant of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) - a female pistillate plant that is starting to develop seeds. I'm guessing that the spider is a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae because of its eye arrangement and the tuning-fork shaped mark on its carapace - maybe an immature Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes). I'm pretty sure the fly is a Tephritid Fruit Fly in the family Tephritidae with a pattern on its wings that reminds me of a Japanese Rising Sun flag. The fly looks pretty gruesome, but that's life and death in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2020)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Sheet Web Weaver; Hammock Spider; Tephritid Fruit Fly
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Linyphiidae; Pityohyphantes; Tephritidae
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Linyphiidae; Pityohyphantes; Tephritidae

File.Name: kb05c_kb03-008.jpg
Notes: 50571553657 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) trying to get away from me on a stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) - and exposing its rear end in the process. See this photo from a couple of days ago for another shot of the same predator. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2020)

File.Name: kb05d_kb02-024.jpg
Notes: 50571396856 This is just the rear end of the impressive Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in an shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that I showed in this photo a few days ago. It's genus Phidippus for sure. I went back to the same buckwheat shrub today, and the spider was still there. It's a grand spider, larger than most jumpers. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2020)

File.Name: kb06a_kb04-035.jpg
Notes:
50574767757 Here are two more shots of the tiny but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) that I found on an old faded flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods a couple days ago. Maybe it's genus Holopogon, see this photo and Dougee's comment. On the other hand, BugGuide says that species has "Posterior tibiae strongly swollen, about twice as large as the anterior", and I don't see that here. These flies are predators, and they catch their prey like little dragonflies. The head is scooped out between the large eyes like a damselfly - to help with binocular vision? This one is small, less than an 1/4 inch long, and it's interesting to compare it with the large one in this photo from last year which is many times larger but recognizably the same sort. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2020)

It's the darnedest thing that I found this fly in the same place again today on Friday, two days later, but I like these earlier photos better. It was windy today, and I didn't much care for any of my photos. It started fairly warm in the 70s, but it cooled down to the 50s as the wind picked up speed - with 45 mph gusts so far. This has to do with our first real storm of the season bringing showers later tonight and tomorrow - our first rain in six months! We're actually under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory for possible snow Saturday and Sunday nights! I don't expect snow, but it will turn cold - in the 30s. I'm still distracted with the election returns. I'll be glad when it's finally over.
CommonNames: Robber Fly; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Holopogon
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Holopogon

File.Name: kb06b_ka29-009.jpg
Notes: 50574702677 This is a striking little fly (Diptera) on a new flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods a week ago. It has a hairless body, clear wings, and a remarkably long proboscis. I believe it is a atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, maybe genus Neacreotrichus in the subfamily Phthiriinae. It's an unusual fly, but maybe I've seen it before - compare this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2020)
ScientificName: Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Neacreotrichus; Phthiriinae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Neacreotrichus; Phthiriinae

File.Name: kb06c_ka22-021.jpg
Notes: 50574666667 I did a double take when I noticed this wasp-like fly (Diptera) peering at me from an oak leaf. It really looks like a wasp, but it's just a fly. I got a couple of quick shots, and it got away from me before I saw the rest of it. I guess it's a Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae). It doesn't have the patterned eyes of the species Spilomyia interrupta in this photo, but maybe it's something similar. This is one of the photos I couldn't post while our internet was down a week ago. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2020)

File.Name: kb06d_ka21-042.jpg
Notes: 50574634192 I was thinking when I got this photo that it's just another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae, and it probably is. But it looks so clean and fresh, and it has a fringe around its head like an Elizabethan lace ruff - and like Ruth Bader Ginsburg! This is one of the photos I couldn't post while our internet was down a week ago. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2020)

File.Name: kb07a_kb07-017.jpg
Notes:
50577959177 We had about 2/10 inch of rain this morning - our first rain of the new water-year. I wasn't expecting to find anything new, but I checked the leaf mulch above our retaining wall . . . and found these tiny Disk Fungi or Cup Fungi on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale. I can also measure them directly by counting pixels - the bigger ones are about 200 pixels across which comes to 0.85 mm. (See here for how I figured.) I'm sure these are ascomycetes rather than basidiomycetes, but that's all I know about them. Hah, I copied this comment from my [Previous] photo, but they are different fungi! I don't know whether these fungi magically appeared today or have been here for a while, possibly in a dormant state. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2020)

First rain and Joe Biden will be President . . . satisfactory. (This was my favorite fictional detective Nero Wolfe's highest praise.) We made a quick shopping trip to town this afternoon, and random people were celebrating and honking their horns with infectious good vibes. We did have some real rain today, and even some rain that bounces - hail or graupel. It was cold today, in the 40s and heading for the 30s tonight. It feels like winter is coming - and springtime too!
Locality: This was my favorite fictional detective Nero Wolfe's highest praise.)  We made a quick shopping trip to town this afternoon
CommonNames: Disk Fungi; Cup Fungi; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; ascomycetes; basidiomycetes; satisfactory
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; ascomycetes; basidiomycetes; satisfactory

File.Name: kb07b_kb07-010.jpg
Notes: 50577921167 We had about 2/10 inch of rain this morning - our first rain of the new water-year. I wasn't expecting to find anything new, but I checked the leaf mulch above our retaining wall . . . and found these tiny Disk Fungi or Cup Fungi on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale. I can also measure them directly by counting pixels - 200 pixels across comes to 0.85 mm, so it checks out. (See here for how I figured.) I'm sure these are ascomycetes rather than basidiomycetes, but that's all I know about them. These are different fungi from those in my [Next] photo. I don't know whether these fungi magically appeared today or have been here for a while, possibly in a dormant state. Hah, some of these tiny cups hold rain drops - bath tubs for springtails! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2020)
CommonNames: Disk Fungi; Cup Fungi; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kb07c_kb04-021.jpg
Notes: 50577012343 Here are two shots of a tiny ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) munching a new female pistillate flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods a few days ago. I've seen this before, see this photo of probably the same kind of ant on the same plant taken a few weeks ago. I'm not sure, but both ants might be species Formica moki, compare this photo. I don't usually think of ants visiting flowers, but they certainly can and do. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2020)
