File.Name: kb08a_kb08-005.jpg
Notes:
50581808661 This is a Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) growing with other lichens on a dead stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I used to think it was the only specimen in our canyon, but I've found a few more on dead branches in an oak tree some distance away. I get a notion to photograph this one again every once in a while because it is so striking. If you compare this photo from a couple of years ago, you can see that it has a few more apothecia now - those small orange disks which are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. This lichen resembles the more common Golden-Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) in this photo, but it lacks the ciliate apothecia. It really looks like something that belongs in the ocean! I've had a hankering to photograph mushrooms and lichens and slime molds and snails and slugs and moss and liverworts lately - all the things that the rain brings. We had out first winter rain yesterday, but it was a small storm, and all these good things take time. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2020)

The chilly winds were blowing today - up to 50 mph according to a neighbor's weather station! It's cold - temps in the 40s all day and already in the 30s tonight. There are frost warnings in the Santa Ynez Valley. We did have a few showers today, but we still have less than 1/4 inch of rain from our first winter storm. It was really too windy for photos today, but I got lucky with this lichen shot.
CommonNames: Powdered Orange Bush Lichen; Chamise; Golden-Eye Lichen
ScientificName: Teloschistes flavicans; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus
associatedTaxa: Teloschistes flavicans; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus

File.Name: kb08b_kb07-001.jpg
Notes: 50581000308 This is somebody's nest that I found in a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the mulch above our retaining wall yesterday - complete with raindrops. The nest looks like it's made of excelsior, those curly wood shavings used as a packing material. I believe this is the pupal cocoon of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta), and the material is actually made up of hairs that the caterpillar plucks from its own body. Usually I find these with the white-haired remains of a flightless female, like in this photo which has the same excelsior-like material at the bottom. Maybe this one is a male that flew away? Or maybe it's been parasitized? (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Western Tussock Moth

File.Name: kb08c_kb05-028.jpg
Notes: 50581808012 Here are ripe nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They are proper tree nuts with a husk on the outside, and then a hard shell which you can crack to expose the nut meat inside. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. (I've only nibbled, so caveat emptor if you want to eat a lot of them!) What a remarkable shrub - it grows in both woodland and chaparral, it has gorgeous white flowers (see this photo), deciduous leaves that turn golden in the fall (see my [Previous] photo), and it has these interesting edible nuts. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2020)

File.Name: kb08d_kb05-030.jpg
Notes: 50581766242 Our woodland is mostly evergreen, but we have a few trees and shrubs that drop their leaves and develop fall color, including some well-known trees like Sycamore and Maple. My photo today is a shrub that's not so well known - native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers (see this photo), edible nuts (my [Next] photo), and pretty autumn leaves like in this shot. This photo is from a few days ago. It was very windy today after a bit of rain yesterday, and I think half the yellow leaves have now fallen. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2020)

File.Name: kb09a_kb09-007.jpg
Notes:
50585441061 I was surprised to find this Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly taking moisture from oak mulch in the woods this morning. It was chilly, and I wasn't finding many insects of any kind. I was seeing quite a few of these butterflies in the woods last spring as they were migrating north from Mexico, and I wonder if this one is trying to migrate back home again? On the other hand, I sometimes find these butterflies on new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) higher in the mountains, see this photo from early in November 2016, so maybe a few just overwinter here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2020)

It was sunny but chilly today, with temps in the 40s and low-50s. There were no more big winds from the north like we had yesterday, just the normal breeze from the south. It was in the mid-30s last night, but I don't think we had frost. There is a chance of a bit more rain on Thursday.

File.Name: kb09b_kb09-015.jpg
Notes: 50585397306 Here is a winged (alate) Termite (order Isoptera) on a seed head of non-native English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. A small swarm of termites was emerging from somewhere under a nearby Buckwheat shrub, but I was lucky to get a shot of this one by itself. This wasn't a particularly large swarm, compare this photo and this photo. Rain yesterday, termites today - I guess that's how they synchronize their swarms with other termite colonies so that they'll all emerge at the same time, thereby increasing the chance of mating across swarms to form new colonies. Maybe they are Subterranean Termites (family Rhinotermitidae)? I don't believe they damage houses, at least these are far away from any house. I seem to find them in both spring and autumn. I went through my notes, and I have about equal records from October-January and March-May. They seem to have these two distinct mating flights. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2020)

File.Name: kb09c_kb09-019.jpg
Notes: 50585358711 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) looking at me from under a flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that's gone to seed in the woods today. The flowers are turning brown at the end of their time, but as the local Santa Barbara author Stewart Edward White once wrote, "The brown of California is the brown of ripeness; not of decay." I only saw a few insects today, and I guess this is what they were doing - sitting out a chilly day. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2020)

File.Name: kb09d_kb09-010.jpg
Notes: 50584562593 This is why we need more rain than the paltry 2/10 inch we got over the weekend. It's a plant of native Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon, barely hanging on. We had about 80% of normal rainfall last winter, but the last three months been god-awful hot, and our woodland plants are starting to suffer. We need more rain soon, though the prospects are not so good in this La NiÒa year. One good thing about being a naturalist photographer is that whatever happens will be interesting - even if it is disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2020)

File.Name: kb10a_kb10-002.jpg
Notes:
50588858347 This swelling is a galled stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this is the work of a Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera. I know nothing else about it, but it's interesting that moths can cause plant galls as well as wasps and flies. I assume that's an exit hole where the moth got out - as opposed to some parasitoid getting in. It's said that the accumulated frass in the empty galls serves as a culture medium for fungi and a large number of other insects that take up residence after the moth is gone. Ron Russo in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) says "The intricacies of these complex relationships stagger the mind for such a common, yet so disregarded, shrub." (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2020)

It was chilly again today with high temps in the 50s and some breeze from the south. I didn't find any interesting insects or spiders, but I did find some interesting galls - so that's my theme for today. I find that galls nicely fill the gap between October spiders and winter mushrooms, that time when it's too cold for most insects and still too dry for mushrooms, and it's convenient that this is when so many galls become conspicuous. There's always something to photograph in our woods.
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: kb10b_kb10-004.jpg
Notes: 50587959023 I found this gall on a small leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. Ron Russo in his book Field Guide to Plant Galls (UC Press, 2006) describes two similar galls on different Ceanothus species caused by Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) flies - the Vein Gall Midge (genus Asphondylia) and the Midrib Fold Midge (genus Contarinia). The latter sounds more like mine, though the author describes it as densely pubescent and mine is only slightly hairy. I reckon this is something different though related. I don't find a lot of these galls, they are something you have to look close for. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2020)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Vein Gall Midge; Midrib Fold Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia; Contarinia
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia; Contarinia

File.Name: kb10c_kb05-034.jpg
Notes: 50588676316 Here is a tiny gall of a Pumpkin Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus minusculus) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. This one is about 520 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Small as it is, this wasp-induced gall is home to the tiny wasp larva that lives inside. Note that this gall causes a color change in the leaf surface. I guess it counts as a "nutrient sink" diverting some nutrients from the leaf - but not much. I went back to reshoot this one today, and it was gone - only the color remained. This is another gall like the one in my [Previous] photo that develops on the tree and then falls away into the leaf litter below. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2020)
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kb10d_kb05-024.jpg
Notes: 50587899108 This is the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) growing on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's actually growing from a leaf bud at the base of the visible leaf. It's small, just 1640 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 6.9 mm - about 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The galls form on the leaf buds of the tree and grow to the size of a blueberry - and then they fall away to the ground, see this photo. I'm confused by the timing of these galls, since I also find them in summer, see this photo from July. They are a wonderfully pure geometric shape. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kb11a_kb11-022.jpg
Notes:
50591523013 I found this small parasitic wasp on an oak leaf in the woods today. I reckon it's either a Braconid or Ichneumon wasp which are both in the same superfamily Ichneumonoidea, and I figure that's good enough. These are the wasps that lay their eggs on the larvae of other insects, see this photo for an example. Insects are still scarce with the chilly weather we've been having, so it was nice to find this wasp today - even if I know nothing else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. I did have time for a walk before we left. It still feels chilly in the morning, but it did get up into the 60s in the afternoon.

File.Name: kb11b_kb11-024.jpg
Notes: 50592351827 I think every spider web in our canyon disappeared during the small rain and big winds that we had last weekend. Here is the first new web that I've seen since then, stretched between dead stems in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's the web of an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae), and it must be a tiny one as the web is only a couple of inches across. I can't see the spider, and I'll bet it's hiding in the clump of vegetation on the right. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2020)

File.Name: kb11c_kb01-002.jpg
Notes: 50591451138 Here is a tiny black ant crawling on an odd nest of webbing in a small shrub of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) that is starting to green up for a new growing season. This is the same webbing I showed in this photo for Arachtober, but this is a different shot for Web Wednesday. I'm thinking this web might be the work of a Leafcurling Sac Spider in the genus Clubiona of the family Clubionidae, see my [Previous] photo for what may be the spider responsible. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2020)

File.Name: kb11d_k929-015.jpg
Notes: 50592244182 This is a photo from late in the month of September that I was saving to post during Arachtober, and then I missed my chance when we lost our internet for a week. Better late than never in the case of this photo. It shows a spider in a small tube of webbing that is maybe like the webbing in this photo and my [Next] photo. I'm thinking the spider is a Leafcurling Sac Spider in the genus Clubiona of the family Clubionidae. It's interesting that this one seems to have an orange belt around the narrow part of its abdomen - the spider is carrying a passenger that I'm sure is the larva of a Mantidfly insect in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera. I've seen these passengers on spiders before, see this photo and this photo. There are large mantidflies like the Wasp Mantidfly in this photo, but I assume this one will become something smaller like the one in this photo. I really only know what I've seen for myself about these interesting insects. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2020)

File.Name: kb12a_kb12-025.jpg
Notes:
50595702862 This is one of the last open flowers on our shrubs of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today - recognizable by the pink anther tips that look like they are dusted with sugar crystals. The individual flowers are about 1/8 inch across, but you usually don't notice them in the large clusters of hundreds of flowers like in this photo. The flowers are now turning brown as they go to seed, but as the local Santa Barbara author Stewart Edward White once wrote, "The brown of California is the brown of ripeness; not of decay." These shrubs have been flowering since early June at least, see this photo. It's been a good run. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2020)

It was warmer today, in the mid-60s, though nights are still in the 40s. There was the usual morning breeze from the south, and now the wind is picking up this evening. Some insects are starting to appear after our colder weather a few days ago knocked them down, but they are mostly the usual flies - and they're for Fly Day Friday rather than Thursday.

File.Name: kb12b_kb12-004.jpg
Notes: 50594793808 Here is an chance encounter between a spider and an insect on an old grass stem in the woods today. I never would have noticed the spider on top of the grass stem if I hadn't spotted the insect first - it disappears by lying so flat. That spider is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver spider in the Tetragnathidae family, probably the species Metellina mimetoides with that angular-shaped abdomen. The insect is a little net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea), probably genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. I found this encounter in a shady place in the woods where the sun never shines at this time of year, and I did my best to get the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2020)
ScientificName: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Fulgoroidea; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae
associatedTaxa: Tetragnathidae; Metellina mimetoides; Fulgoroidea; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: kb12c_kb11-006.jpg
Notes: 50595636117 I believe this is just another Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae) in the woods yesterday. They seem to come in all different sizes, from big to small, but they all have reddish eyes and a red-tipped butt like the glowing ember of a cigar. Despite their unpleasant common and scientific names, I like these flies - they are large and husky, like they could take on anything. My photo shows the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2020)

File.Name: kb12d_kb11-018.jpg
Notes: 50595496731 This is a little blue-tail fly showing its backside on an oak leaf in the woods. I won't try to guess what kind of fly it is - there seem to be unlimited different kinds and after a while they all start looking the same. This certainly shows the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2020)

File.Name: kb13a_kb13-036.jpg
Notes:
50599271752 Here is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. This fly might be the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California during winter months. These flies usually perch just out of reach up high and take off when I get close, but maybe this one was still feeling the morning chill. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2020)

I was looking for flies today for the Fly Day Friday group, and I found them in a small oak tree in a sunny place by our summer-dry creek - lots of flies, mostly dark ones like this fly from yesterday but some interesting ones too. Today was much like yesterday, chilly in the morning and warming up into the 60s in the afternoon. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight with winds from the north, and there is some wind picking up as I write this.

File.Name: kb13b_kb13-028.jpg
Notes: 50599087711 This is an interesting fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It has a striking color combination of bars and bands. It also has serious bristles on its abdomen, so I'm thinking it might be another Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae - in particular, it might be in the same Vanderwulpia genus as the more colorful fly I show in this photo. The only other choice I see at BugGuide is Vanderwulpia sequens, see the species page. The only problem with this ID is that BugGuide has no records from California, though it does have records for Arizona which is our neighbor. Maybe this is a record shot? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2020)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; Vanderwulpia; Vanderwulpia sequens
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; Vanderwulpia; Vanderwulpia sequens

File.Name: kb13c_kb13-001.jpg
Notes: 50598326768 This is another small fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it has attractive colors that distinguish it from most other common flies. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2020)

File.Name: kb13d_kb13-025.jpg
Notes: 50599154977 Here is a small fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It has small spines along the margins of its wings, so one possibility for ID is family Heleomyzidae. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2020)

File.Name: kb14a_kb14-018.jpg
Notes:
50603200887 Here is another Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on seed capsules of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. This is a broad-winged male cricket that lacks the long ovipositor of the narrow-winged female, compare this photo. These crickets work the night shift and usually try to hide during the day - but this one isn't even trying to hide. I reckon the crickets are just about done for the year, and I was lucky to find this one today. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2020)

Today was much like yesterday, chilly in the morning and warming up into the 60s in the afternoon. It was windy last night, though it's not at all bad today. There is a 30% chance of rain on Tuesday night - but it can't be much rain. We have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow from 8:00 am to 4:00 pm, but I should be able to post if it doesn't run much later than that. This is a real inconvenience at this time of year with the cold and short days - and more outages are coming next week. We've had way too many of these outages this year, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; way too many
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; way too many

File.Name: kb14b_kb14-003.jpg
Notes: 50603103762 Here are two shots of a tiny Orb-Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's on its web in the shot on the left, though the web is quite invisible - in fact, this is the same web that I showed in this photo last week. The spider didn't like my attentions, and it scooted off to its retreat in an old seed head of the shrub. I think of these seed heads as little "spider hotels" for tiny creatures - see this photo for a fresher example and disregard the cricket. This spider really is tiny, just 400 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) At first I was thinking maybe genus Hypsosinga for its size as much as anything else, see this photo - but looking at my photos today, I can see that my spider is hairier than those spiders. I'm thinking now that it might be a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) - hardly more than a spiderling. Compare the spider in this photo from last year which is about twice the size. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2020)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Hypsosinga; Metepeira
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Hypsosinga; Metepeira

File.Name: kb14c_kb14-013.jpg
Notes: 50603081432 This is the shadow of a fly (Diptera) on the other side of an oak leaf in the woods this morning. Flies are mostly what I'm finding in the woods on these chilly mornings. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2020)

File.Name: kb14d_kb14-022.jpg
Notes: 50602917041 This is another shot of "just a fly" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It's sticking its tongue out! It does appear to have minutely bristly leading edges on its wings, so one possibility for ID is family Heleomyzidae, compare this photo from yesterday taken in the same small tree. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2020)

File.Name: kb15a_kb15-014.jpg
Notes:
50607960367 Here is a Yellow Jacket wasp (genus Vespula, Hymenoptera) in a small oak tree in the woods today. It was acting kind of groggy, and maybe it just woke up on a morning that was warmer than it's been but still kind of chilly for a wasp. Judging by its eyes, this wasp has great peripheral vision but not much depth perception. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2020)

Just two photos today, as we had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages that was scheduled from 8:00 am to 4:00 pm and ran to 5:30 instead. It already feels late. It was fortunately pretty warm today, well into the 70s with a calm breeze. Tomorrow is expected to be even warmer - and windier.

File.Name: kb15b_kb15-009.jpg
Notes: 50607896862 This is another tiny Orb-Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). If you know the shrub, you can appreciate the size of this spider! It's got a neat but small orb web off to the right, and it's tending a sensing line to the web with its screen-right legs. I'm pretty sure this is another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira), and it's tiny - though not as tiny as the one I showed yesterday in this photo in a different shrub. This one is about 500 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.1 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I could hardly see it in this shady place, and I used a reflector to direct some light. That's a handy tool if you prefer natural light. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2020)

File.Name: kb16a_kb16-001.jpg
Notes:
50612068752 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. For scale, that column of yellow disk flowers is only about 1/8 inch across, and the spider would just about fit on top of it. Arachtober is over, but I can still go looking for these tiny crab spiders - but they are actually getting scarce as the weather turns chilly. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2020)

Hah, it wasn't chilly today - we actually got up to 80∞ with a warm dry wind from the east as well, maybe spillover from the Santa Ana winds down south. Tomorrow is expected to be 15∞ cooler with rain likely on Tuesday night and Wednesday. That's how our autumn weather is - it rocks . . . back and forth.
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: kb16b_kb16-022.jpg
Notes: 50611865801 This little fly (Diptera) is not unattractive with its large round head and iridescent green-blue colors, but of course it's a nasty Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae. More specifically, I believe it is a Hairy Maggot Blow Fly in the genus Chrysomya, and possibly the Oriental Latrine Fly (Chrysomya megacephala) - which sounds yucky, but compare the images on this page at the Natural History of Orange County, California web site. But what's it doing here in the woods?? (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Blow Fly; Hairy Maggot Blow Fly; Oriental Latrine Fly

File.Name: kb16c_kb16-026.jpg
Notes: 50611817151 Here is a fierce-looking wasp (Hymenoptera) hanging under a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) with a distinctly petiolate (stalked) abdomen. I found this one near our summer-dry creek, and I suspect it's nesting under small bridge nearby. We used to have lots of these under the eaves of our front porch, but a Canyon Wren moved in and pretty much wiped them out by eating their larvae. What a surprise. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2020)
CommonNames: wasp; Coast Live Oak; Western Paper Wasp
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mischocyttarus flavitarsis
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mischocyttarus flavitarsis

File.Name: kb16d_kb16-030.jpg
Notes: 50611910727 This is a tiny nymph on one of the last open flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today - but I sure don't know what it's a nymph of. It's tiny, about 460 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Any help will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2020)

File.Name: kb17a_kb17-023.jpg
Notes:
50615776962 Here is another tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on faded flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. This one is really tiny, just 388 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.6 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I'm not sure why I've been finding so many tiny crab spiders lately - see my photos here and here for starters. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2020)

It was cooler and windy today, a sign of a small storm coming in tonight and tomorrow. We actually have an even chance of light rain tomorrow. Despite the rain, we have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow from 8:00 am to 6:00 pm - our second outage this week, with a third one coming on Thursday. This is a real inconvenience at this time of year with cold and short days. We've had way too many of these outages this year, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; way too many

File.Name: kb17b_kb17-008.jpg
Notes: 50614885478 This looks like a small gall, but it's actually an odd insect - a small Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. It feels hard like it has a shell. I found this one on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It seems to match the species Allokermes essigii in these photos that I found on the web. Unlike some other scale insects, I usually find these alone or in small groups. They don't appear to do any damage to the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2020)
CommonNames: Gall-like Scale Insect; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Allokermes essigii
associatedTaxa: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Allokermes essigii

File.Name: kb17c_kb17-017.jpg
Notes: 50615653482 These are deformed flowers on a shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today, and I'm not sure if the deformity is a gall or a disease. It looks kind of gooey, but it feels dry. If it is a gall, I would guess it's the work of Gall Midges (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), but I have my doubts. Nothing like this is mentioned in my book on Plant Galls of California, and I think I first noticed it two years ago and not before, see this photo. I did find this article on "First report of Neofusicoccum australe causing canker disease and branch dieback on Arctostaphylos glauca in California" that reports on materials collected in the Santa Ynez Mountains in 2014. I'll try to contact the author to see if it's the same thing. It's disturbing if this is indeed a new plant disease that's spreading in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2020)

File.Name: kb17d_kb16-010.jpg
Notes: 50615501066 Here is a really tiny spider on the inner circle of its orb web. It's probably another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), but it's even tinier than the one in this photo several days ago which is in the same shrub a couple of feet away. I can't remember if I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, but if I did, the spider is only 245 pixels across, which comes to 1.0 mm. (See here for how I figure.) If you look close, you can see something remarkable about its web - its inner web makes a clockwise spiral, while its outer web makes a (rough) counter-clockwise spiral. You can see this better in this photo. Are all orb webs like this?? I'll have another photo of this spider's whole web tomorrow for the Web Wednesday group. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2020)

File.Name: kb18a_kb18-008.jpg
Notes:
50619381982 Here is a small Beetle (Coleoptera) on a well-chewed leaf of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. I've seen these iridescent beetles on this plant before and nowhere else. It resembles the Blue Milkweed Beetle (Chrysochus cobaltinus) in this photo at iNaturalist - but it's not on milkweed. At least it might be something else in the same Leaf Beetle(Chrysomelidae) family. It's a handsome beetle, so green/blue that it looks black. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2020)

It was overcast all morning when I was out getting my photos today, but it was dry - our storm washed out before it got here. We did have another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages that was scheduled from 8:00 am to 6:00 pm - and this one was actually done by 4:30. Now we have another outage tomorrow scheduled from 8:00 am to 4:15 pm. Will that one run late??
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Chrysochus cobaltinus; Chrysomelidae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Chrysochus cobaltinus; Chrysomelidae

File.Name: kb18b_kb18-002.jpg
Notes: 50619344797 Somehow I spotted this tiny spider on a cactus spine in the garden this morning. The spider really is tiny, just 500 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.1 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The spider seemed to be stringing webbing between the different spines of this one cactus pad. I have no idea what kind of spider it is, though it appears to have ladylike palps. I've been finding lots of tiny spiders lately. Hah, and look close at the tip of the cactus spine - it's serrated, hard to get out! (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2020)

File.Name: kb18c_kb16-009.jpg
Notes: 50619305417 This is the whole web of the really tiny spider I showed yesterday in this photo that I suspect is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). You can see the spider in the center of the web. The whole web is just a couple of inches across. I found this web just a foot away from the fragment in my [Previous] photo in the same shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), and I'm sure it's been rebuilt since the wind that destroyed the other one. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2020)

File.Name: kb18d_kb16-005.jpg
Notes: 50619178831 It was pretty windy last weekend, and this is all that's left of this spider web in a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It's an interesting fragment, like a classical statue without arms. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2020)

File.Name: kb19a_kb19-003.jpg
Notes:
50622931706 Here is another photo for my series of tiny spiders. It's probably the same tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago, at least it's on the same composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. For scale, the yellow center of the flower is about 1/8 inch across. The spider was waiting for prey when I disturbed it while getting a photo - it threw its front legs up in the air and showed me its backside! It stayed like this for a full minute before ducking down out of sight under the petals. I was thinking of the crane kick in the Karate Kid movie. Is this some kind of defensive pose, maybe trying to look larger? If you look close, you can see that it also has a safety line. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2020)

We had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages today that was scheduled from 8:00 am to 4:15 pm - and this one was actually done by 4:00. Thanks. This was our third power outage in five days, enough is enough. It was chilly and dark at home today without power, but sunny and bright in the canyon with the usual morning breeze.

File.Name: kb19b_kb19-021.jpg
Notes: 50622899711 Here is a bristly little fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf in the woods today. Its abdomen looks bristly enough for it to be a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae, but I'm not sure. These flies are quite small, they move constantly, and they don't like to be photographed - I'll keep trying to get better shot. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2020)

File.Name: kb19c_kb16-023.jpg
Notes: 50622867951 This is another Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a few days ago. These wasps have a distinctly petiolate (stalked) abdomen with distinguishes them from paper wasps in the genus Polistes. These wasps look ferocious but I've never been bothered by them - even though they nest under the eaves of our front porch. At least they used to nest here before a Canyon Wren moved in this summer and pretty much wiped them out by eating their larvae. I hope they come back. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2020)
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Polistes
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Polistes

File.Name: kb19d_kb13-041.jpg
Notes: 50622077388 I believe this is another Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae) on an oak leaf in the woods, but it's different from the ones I usually find like this one. They both have reddish eyes and a red-tipped butt like the glowing ember of a cigar, but this one also has bright orange antennae, and the ember of its butt is bigger with a different shape. I'm not sure if this is a matter of age, gender, species, or a even a parasite - but it works fine for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2020)

File.Name: kb20a_kb20-011.jpg
Notes:
50626187768 Is this tough-looking wasp chewing on a toothpick?? It's another Yellow Jacket wasp (genus Vespula, Hymenoptera) in a small oak tree in the woods today. Hah, I was trying to get a photo of something else about head-high in the tree, when I turned and found myself eye to compound eyes with the wasp. I think we were both surprised, but I managed to get the shot before it flew off. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2020)

It was mostly overcast today as a weak low pressure trough moved through. No rain, but the soft light was a treat - I managed to get photos in a few shady places that I can't usually reach in contrasty bright sunlight. It was also warmer, up to 70∞ - the clouds were like a big soft blanket pulled across the sky.

File.Name: kb20b_kb20-015.jpg
Notes: 50626975717 Here is another photo for my series of tiny spiders. I believe it's a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, possibly an immature Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes) - compare this photo from February last year. These spiders get fairly large as they mature, but this one is small, just 700 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm - less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This one is female - you can see her slender ladylike palps. It's hanging on its web in an old vine of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae), but the web is hard to see in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2020)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Hammock Spider; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Pityohyphantes; palps; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; Pityohyphantes; palps; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: kb20c_kb20-003.jpg
Notes: 50626949007 I found this interesting fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is a Heleomyzid Fly in the family Heleomyzidae, maybe tribe Heleomyzini. The clue for finding this at BugGuide was that the margins of its wings are minutely bristly - look close along the left side. This feature is easier to see in this photo of what seems to be the same kind of fly from a month ago. I don't know anything else about it. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Heleomyzid Fly

File.Name: kb20d_kb19-014.jpg
Notes: 50626914962 This is "just a fly" on an oak leaf in the woods, probably a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Polleniidae. Those golden hairs on its thorax are distinctive - sometimes they make a real fur coat like in this photo from a couple of years ago, but they shed away as they age. This is one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2020)

File.Name: kb21a_kb21-003.jpg
Notes:
50631123036 This is another shot taken today of the tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) on the inner circle of its web that I showed in this photo and this photo last week. I suspect it's an immature Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). I made a point of getting this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can measure the spider by counting pixels - it's 300 pixels long, which comes to 1.3 mm - about 1/2 of 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) For something remarkable about the inner spiral of its web, see my comments for this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2020)

It was mostly sunny in the morning while I was out getting my photos today, though some high clouds showed up in the afternoon. It was fairly warm again, up into the 70s, with the usual morning breeze from the south. It's becoming a challenge to find new things to photograph each day - some real rain would help!

File.Name: kb21b_kb21-016.jpg
Notes: 50631169342 Here are two shots of spring galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I found them both in the same small oak tree today. They are about 1/4 inch high and broad, shaped like little mushrooms or golf tees. The one on the left seems to have a round exit hole on top where the tiny wasp got out - or did a tiny predator get in?? These are bisexual galls because they will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual oak apple galls in this photo which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle of alternating generations repeats. Galls are complicated like this, it's part of what makes them so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; bisexual galls; unisexual

File.Name: kb21c_kb20-027.jpg
Notes: 50631022711 This is a Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae among the dry seed heads of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods yesterday. The family name is a reference to the five-segmented antennae. These insects comprise one of the largest families of true bugs with hundreds of different species in North America alone, so I won't try to guess which one it is. I also saw a late-stage nymph nearby in the same plant. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2020)

File.Name: kb21d_kb20-019.jpg
Notes: 50630994641 Here are new dandelion-like seeds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) making their escape into the wide world. In time the little flying seeds end up everywhere, though I believe their numbers are down this year. For some reason the seed-bearing pistillate plants of this dioecious shrub didn't do as well as the pollen-bearing staminate plants, so there aren't as many seeds as usual. I don't know why. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2020)

File.Name: kb22a_kb22-053.jpg
Notes:
50635901061 Here is a tiny spider sitting in the center of its web stretched over a couple of leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The whole web is just a couple of inches across, and this is just the center of it. I have no doubt the spider is a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata), though its spots are on its other side - see this photo. It's tiny, only 560 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) This is another another photo for my ongoing series of tiny spiders, as is my [Next] shot. I'll try to get a better photo of its web for this week's Web Wednesdays group. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2020)

It was fairly warm again today, up into the 70s. There wasn't much wind this morning, though it's picking up as I write at night with 20 mph gusts so far. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory through Monday night, so tomorrow might be a windy day.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Six-spotted Orb Weaver

File.Name: kb22b_kb22-006.jpg
Notes: 50635924642 This is another shot of a tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on a composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's probably the same tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) that I showed recently in this photo and this photo, at least it's on the same flower which I don't think it has left in a week. Hah, I shouldn't be surprised - the Funnel Web Weaver spider on our bathroom window sill hasn't moved in months! For scale, the yellow center of the flower is about 1/8 inch across. I like this pose, encompassing all of a very small world. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2020)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; California Aster; Crab Spider
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Thomisidae

File.Name: kb22c_kb22-047.jpg
Notes: 50635877552 This fly (Diptera) looks like any other bristly fly, but you have to appreciate how small it - just 1150 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.9 mm - about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) That's smaller than most flies, e.g. house flies run 6-7 mm or 1/4 inch according to Google. I don't know what kind of fly this is, but I like photographing flies - they have so much structural detail. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2020)

File.Name: kb22d_kb22-022.jpg
Notes: 50635859187 Here is another tiny gall of a Pumpkin Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus minusculus) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. This one is about 420 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.8 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Small as it is, this wasp-induced gall is home to the tiny wasp larva that lives inside. Note how this gall causes a color change in the leaf surface. I guess it counts as a "nutrient sink" diverting some nutrients from the leaf - but not much. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2020)
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kb23a_kb23-002.jpg
Notes:
50639972062 I found this dragonfly hanging from a tangle of old stems of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in a small oak tree in the woods this morning. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata, and this is the first one I've seen since last spring. It was chilly this morning when I found this one, probably still in the 40s or low 50s. These dragonflies will stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and/or stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. It's a real challenge to try to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2020)

It was chilly today but not really cold. It was also breezy, and the combination of chilly winds made finding subjects for new photos difficult. It's a tough time in the woods right now, we haven't had a quarter inch of rain in six months, and we're coming after the warmest August-October period on record in California. There's more info at the California Weather Blog under the title: "Very dry weather across California through mid-December.
ScientificName: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata
associatedTaxa: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata

File.Name: kb23b_kb23-006.jpg
Notes: 50639892047 Here is another tiny spider on what appears to be old webbing under the curve of an oak leaf in the woods today. I noticed it up over my head in the tree and got the photo standing on my tiptoes with my tripod fully extended. The spider is another Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) like the one I showed yesterday in this photo, and this time you can see its spots. Note that the spider has eight spots, four on each side. Despite its common name, the spiders in our canyon (at least) all seem to have eight spots rather than six. The spider is tiny, just 640 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) My series of photos of tiny spiders continues! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2020)

File.Name: kb23c_kb22-028.jpg
Notes: 50639764916 This is a new composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia, Asteraceae) that's just starting to open in the woods yesterday. It's small, the whole collection of yellow disk flowers is only about 1/8 inch across. These flowers tend to lose their brilliant colors as the mature, but the fresh ones are worth getting close to - and you might find tiny crab spiders like this one. What's most remarkable about these flowers is that they are still out as we approach winter, at a time when we haven't had a quarter inch of rain in six months and following the warmest August-October period on record in California. In some years they keep flowering into the new year, but I don't know about this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2020)

File.Name: kb23d_kb22-036.jpg
Notes: 50638991358 This is just another fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf that I can't identify. This is my favorite small oak tree because it gets winter sun for most of the morning, and it attracts a variety of insects - mostly flies these days. What's remarkable is how many different kinds of flies there are. Sure, some have colors like bees, and some are tiny and delicate, but mostly they are just dark and hairy - and despite this, there are so many different kinds if you look close. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2020)

File.Name: kb24a_kb24-007.jpg
Notes:
50643426566 Here is another tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) on its web stretched across a couple of leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This is the third of these spiders I've found in three days, see this photo and this photo, I guess this is their time. This one seems unusually colorful with some red on its abdomen. It really is tiny, just 600 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.5 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) My series of photos of tiny spiders continues again! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks - this time on Tuesday rather than Wednesday to avoid any crowds. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be. We got back after noon, and I got out for a short walk before we lost the sunlight completely. I'm glad I did - this spider was a nice find.
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kb24b_kb24-026.jpg
Notes: 50643454887 I found this pupal cocoon of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta) on a dead branch in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods today. The cocoon looks like it's made of excelsior, those curly wood shavings used as a packing material, but it's actually made up of hairs that the caterpillar plucks from its own body, see the caterpillar in this photo from last May. Usually I find these cocoons with the white-haired remains of a flightless female moth, like in this photo which has the same excelsior-like material at the bottom. The cocoon in my photo today lacks the white hairs, but maybe it's a male that flew away after emerging? I recently found another one like this, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2020)
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kb24c_kb24-011.jpg
Notes: 50643382627 Here are three different galls on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that all merit the name "rolled leaf galls". I believe these are all proper galls and not the work of leaf-roller insects because the cellular structure of the leaf has actually been changed and not just mechanically folded and tied down. I believe the gall on the right is the work of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla, see this photo and this photo. The leaf is rolled over the top, which is different from the leaf in the center which is rolled under the leaf. The oddest one is on the left, which is a real "leaf roll" that leaves some of the edge of the leaf free and seems to be closed on each end, like a suchi roll. I don't know what critter is responsible for either of these other galls. I suppose I should open a few up, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: kb24d_kb20-005.jpg
Notes: 50643358362 This is another gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) growing from a leaf bud at a juncture of leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's small, just 1540 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 6.5 mm - about 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The galls form on the leaf buds of the tree and grow to the size of a pea - and then they change color and fall away to the ground, see this photo. I'm confused by the timing of these galls, since I also find them in summer, see this photo from last July. They have a wonderfully pure geometric shape. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kb25a_kb25-023.jpg
Notes:
50646767126 Here are two shots of a Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly on fallen oak leaves this morning showing how different they look above and below. In the shot on the left, the butterfly has its proboscis out like its trying to get moisture from the leaves . . . but they are dry. This is the second one of these I've seen this year, after this one from a few weeks ago. That one was tattered, but this one looks fresh. We have a trio of large butterflies that overwinter here as adults, including the Red Admiral (Nymphalis californica) and California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica), and this Mourning Cloak is the still the only one of them that I've managed to photograph this season. I rarely see any of these butterflies during summer. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2020)

It was another sunny but chilly autumn day. I don't think it ever got up to 60∞ here on the shady side of our canyon, though it was probably warmer in the sun. The wind seemed to come up abruptly around 1:00 pm, which put an end to my photo time.
ScientificName: Nymphalis antiopa; Nymphalis californica; Nymphalis californica
associatedTaxa: Nymphalis antiopa; Nymphalis californica; Nymphalis californica

File.Name: kb25b_kb25-011.jpg
Notes: 50645989658 This is the whole web of the really tiny spider I showed a few days ago in this photo that I suspect is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). For scale, the plant stems are old seed-stalks of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) that are only about one inch apart. The spider itself is probably tucked into those old leaves on the lower stem. This web is just a foot away from the much larger web in my [Previous] photo (here). They are both orb webs in the same shrub, but they are quite different. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2020)

File.Name: kb25c_kb25-007.jpg
Notes: 50646788232 Here is the web of an Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) strung between two old stems of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The web is about the size of a salad plate - much larger than the web in my [Next] photo. This web is in the same place as the web in this photo that I showed last week that was mostly destroyed by a wind event. I don't know whose web it is, though the spider is probably hiding in one of those old seed heads that are real spider hotels for the tiny critters. I've already got my two photos for the group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2020)

File.Name: kb25d_kb24-022.jpg
Notes: 50646764647 This is the same tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) spider that I show in my [Previous] photo (here) on its web stretched across a couple of leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday. The spider in tiny, only about 1/10 inch across, and the whole web is only a couple of inches across. The thin web is almost invisible in sunlight, and completely invisible in the shadow. I tried different combinations of exposure settings and this is acceptable though not dramatic - it's really having the web in sunlight with the dark background in shadow that makes it work. See my [Previous] photo for more info about the spider. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2020)
CommonNames: Six-spotted Orb Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kb26a_kb26-002.jpg
Notes:
50650963342 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) trying to get away from me in a shrub of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. It almost got away into the shadows, but I used a reflector to direct some light onto its rear end and got the photo. It's the first one of these I've seen all month. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2020)

Last year we actually had a bit of snow on Thanksgiving Day, see my photo. No snow and no rain this year, it's chilly but sunny and dry. We'll connect with our kids and grandkids by Zoom later tonight. Happy Thanksgiving!

File.Name: kb26b_kb26-020.jpg
Notes: 50650936752 Here are two more shots of the same tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) spider that I show in this photo and this photo yesterday and the day before. I got these photos in the same place today. The spider is only about 1/10 inch long, and those tiny black eyes are so cute! See my other photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2020)

File.Name: kb26c_kb25-040.jpg
Notes: 50650785096 This photo shows the rear end of an insect larvae which I can't identify. It's entering a "rolled leaf gall" that I believe is the work of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla - it's the gall on the right in this photo I posted a couple of days ago. The larva appears to be consuming the aphids inside and scattering their eggs. Maybe it's the larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) insect with those bundles of hairs on its rear, but I wish I could see the rest of it. This isn't the best photo as the wind picked up and the sun was moving into shadow at the same time, and of course the larva was gone when I went back today - but its a remarkable image anyway. I'm not sure what the larva is, though I have a vague recollection of seeing one like it before. Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2020)

File.Name: kb26d_kb21-021.jpg
Notes: 50650853072 Here is another shot of what appears to be a different Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae) on an oak leaf in the woods. It's different from the ones I usually find like this one. They both have reddish eyes and a red-tipped butt like the glowing ember of a cigar, but this one also has bright orange antennae, and the end of its butt is bigger with a different shape. I have another shot of this kind of fly in this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2020)

File.Name: kb27a_kb27-011.jpg
Notes:
50655067016 This is a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly on a sandstone rock surrounded by fallen oak leaves in the woods today. It's surprisingly fresh looking, like it's just emerged. This is the first one I've seen in the canyon since February, see this photo of that tattered specimen. It's another one of a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days. I photographed a Mourning Cloak just a few days ago, which leaves the California Tortoiseshell as the one I need to find this season. I rarely see any of these butterflies during summer. I saw this butterfly and waited some time for it to return. I couldn't get close, so this is a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2020)

The weather continues chilly but sunny and dry, with a breeze from the south. They are having gusty Santa Ana Winds from Ventura on southward, but we miss those winds because we have no direct path to the desert here in Santa Barbara. We do have our own Sundowner Winds to devil us, but that's another story.

(San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2020)

Notes:
50658865356 Here is another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) trying to hide under a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) in the woods in the woods yesterday. I used a foil reflector to shine some light on it. These predators have been scarce all month, though I found a different one just a couple of days ago in a different shrub, see this photo, and it's still in the same place today. I suspect they're just hanging out in safe places and waiting for warmer days. (San Marcos Pass, 27 November 2020)

The weather continues chilly but sunny and dry, with the usual morning breeze from the south. Santa Barbara rainfall history is available as a PDF here, though the records for San Marcos Pass only go back to 1965. For the period of September through November, we've only had 0.22 inches of rain this year which ties us for the fourth driest year in the record so far. It's interesting that two of those dry years ended up with less than normal rainfall, but two of them (1991 and 1977) also ended up with greater than average rainfall. There's still hope for a real winter!

File.Name: kb28b_kb28-009.jpg
Notes: 50658083858 Here are two shots of a small fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf that I can't identify. I don't think it's "just a fly" as so many of them are. These are not very common, they are rather slender with a pointed abdomen, they are quite bristly on their rear ends, and they really don't like being photographed - they often escape just as I'm about to get the photo. I guess it is a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae, but I'm not sure. Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2020)

File.Name: kb28d_kb28-012.jpg
Notes: 50658751886 This is a really tiny gall on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm pretty sure it's the gall of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. These galls hold the unisexual generation of wasps that are all female. Adults will emerge in the spring and lay eggs to make the bisexual generation of wasps that have their own galls. They will emerge next summer to mate and create a new generation of unisexual galls. Sorry, but galls are complicated like this. This gall is really tiny, only about 400 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Note how the gall causes a little dark scar of the leaf vein, which I sometimes notice after the galls have fallen away. They have little Micky Mouse ears! (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp

File.Name: kb29a_kb29-017.jpg
Notes:
50662377133 Here is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) digging into a new composite flower of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia, Asteraceae) in the woods this morning. This fly might be the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be common in central California during winter months. Flowers of any kind are pretty scarce in this dry autumn as we approach winter, and they are important for giving the little bees and flies and spiders something to do during a tough time of year. Fly Day Sunday? I have been seeing a lot of flies lately, and today's photos are just some of the different kinds of flies that I happened to find today. It was warmer, up into the 60s at home on the shady side of the canyon and no doubt warmer in the sun. Wind was not an issue. We've had unusually little wind this fall, probably because we've had few winter storms and almost no rain.

File.Name: kb30b_kb30-036.jpg
Notes: 50666240663 I happened to notice this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). The sun was on the other side of the leaf, so I bumped up the exposure by a full stop which makes for some interesting lighting. The spider is about 770 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.2 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I assume this spider is another in the genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen. It seems odd to find it in on a plant that is not flowering - but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2020)

File.Name: kb30c_kb30-008.jpg
Notes: 50666960726 Here are two shots of a spring gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They are about 1/4 inch high and broad, shaped like little mushrooms or golf tees. These are bisexual galls because they will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual oak apple galls in this photo which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle of alternating generations repeats. At least that's the usual story, but summer has already passed, and the gall doesn't have an exit hole. So is the wasp still in there? Or did something go wrong? Or can they delay their time of emerging? I'm also confused by that little light-colored "tooth" below the gall. I can see leaf spines on either side of the gall, so this must be something else. Lots of questions, but this that's part of what makes galls so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; bisexual galls; unisexual

It's interesting that there are also a pair of "rolled leaf galls" on either end of the rolled oak leaf, I'll add a note. I don't know who makes these galls either, but I believe they are the ones on the left in this photo of different kinds. I've been looking close at these galls lately, and I'm getting confused. File.Name: kc01a_kc01-015.jpg
Notes:
50670573197 Here is a tiny humpbacked fly on a new composite flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe it is an atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae, probably in the genus Geron. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane instead of a proboscis. Julie calls it the "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. The fly was in partial shade with a bright background in full sun, so I bumped up the exposure and let the background go. I like the high-key effect. I have another nice photo that shows what it does with that long proboscis, but I'll save that one for Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2020)

It was warmer today - up to 70∞ - but there was an annoying breeze this morning that kept everything moving. At least the big winds in the forecast for last night never happened. I like the diversity of my photos today. I did my usual walk and found some surprising new things along the same trail I've been walking nearly every day since mid-March as we mostly isolate in the canyon. Every naturalist-photographer should have such a place.

File.Name: kc01b_kc01-004.jpg
Notes: 50669719298 This isn't the best photo, but it shows a remarkable spider. It's a tiny Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) hanging off a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I can't see where the actual front of the spider is, but what you see is only about 770 pixels in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.2 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) If you look close, you can see a strand of webbing and the spider's odd way of holding on to each end of the line separately - it must move by playing out one end of the line as it reels in the other. You can find more shots of these remarkable spiders and their interesting webs if you search my photos. I got today's photo is deep shade with a breeze this morning. I had to bump up the ISO higher than I like, but I'm pleased with how it came out. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2020)
CommonNames: Triangle Web Spider; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloboridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloboridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: kc01c_kc01-019.jpg
Notes: 50670429921 I found this mysterious orb on an old flower Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. It's small, about 670 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.8 mm, less than 1/8 inch (See here for how I figured.) Maybe it's an egg or a pupal case from which something has escaped? It looks like it's wrapped in silk, but it seems too large to be the work of a spider. It reminds me of craft projects I've seen with string or paper mache wrapped around a balloon with glue, then the balloon is popped after the glue dries - like this. I wonder how it was made without a balloon? I actually found another one just like it a few years ago, see this photo. I love these little mysteries. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2020)

File.Name: kc01d_kc01-021.jpg
Notes: 50670360861 Here are two different galls in the same shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Both galls have exit holes, but are they galls of the same or different critters? The gall on the left is the work of a Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera. The gall on the right might be a deflated version of the same thing, but it might also be the gall of a Coyote Brush Twisted Stem Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia baccharis) in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. The moth galls are said to be monothalamous because they only contain one larva, whereas the midge galls are said to be polythalamous and contain several larvae - like the gall on the right. But the midge galls are said to have elliptical exit holes while these are round, which suggests the moth gall. I don't know if they are the same or different. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2020)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth; Coyote Brush Twisted Stem Gall Midge
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Rhopalomyia baccharis; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; monothalamous; polythalamous
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Rhopalomyia baccharis; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; monothalamous; polythalamous

It was my good fortune to actually find two tiny spiders on (or at least by) their webs today, but there was a breeze from the southeast that made getting my photos difficult. Fortunately the wind was gusty, so there were moment of relative calm to get photos. They are having Red Flag Warnings and Wind Advisories down south due to expected Santa Ana winds, but fortunately we don't get those winds here, with no direct path to the desert. We do have Sundowner Winds to devil us, but that's another story.

We have another one of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow from 8:00 am to 6:00 pm. I should be able to post if it doesn't run much later than that - I hope so, I have some dynamite "butt shots" to share for BBBT! This is a real inconvenience at this time of year with cold nights and dark days. We've had way too many of these outages this year, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
Locality: or at least by) their webs today

File.Name: kc03a_kb30-031.jpg
Notes:
50677434908 This is the impressive rear end of a large bristly Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. It's quite large for a fly, the size of a small bumblebee, maybe 3/4 inch long. A moment later it seemed to realize I was sitting there, and it took off - but not before I got my one and only photo. It was curious that nearly the the same thing happened today, and I got a nice photo of the fly's other end - but I'll save that one for Fly Day Friday tomorrow. This shot certainly counts for HBBBT! Just two photos today, as we had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages that was scheduled from 8:00 am to 6:00 pm - and ran until 9:15. It was chilly and dark at home without power, but sunny and bright in the canyon with some morning breeze. It's late, I still haven't looked at my new photos from today, though I know I got a nice one of this fly for tomorrow.

File.Name: kc04a_kc04-022.jpg
Notes:
50681100207 I found this handsome little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today, but I'm not sure what kind of bee it is. I'm thinking that it might be a Blood Bee - a cleptoparasite in the genus Sphecodes, Halictidae. There are also Andrena and Lasioglossum bees with these colors, so any help with ID will be appreciated. I'm not surprised to find flies in the woods right now like in the rest of my photos today, but this bee is a puzzle as there are few flowers available now - especially since we're still waiting for our first real rain of the season. Today was chilly on the shady north-facing side of our easy-west canyon but warmer on the sunny side across the way. Yep, we live on the chilly side, and I usually go to the other side for my photos - at least until rain and mushrooms appear. Last year we'd had three inches of rain by now with more coming - I found my first Banana Slug on December 1, see this photo. This year we haven't had 1/4 inch of rain yet. Every year is different, but there's always something to photograph - but maybe not what we want.

File.Name: kc04c_kc04-024.jpg
Notes:
50681005347 Here is another shot of the large bristly Tachinid Fly that I showed yesterday in this photo - at least it's the same kind of fly. It's a Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. It's quite large for a fly, the size of a small bumblebee, maybe 3/4 inch long. This one landed on an oak leaf right in front of me. A moment later it seemed to realize I was standing there, and it took off - but not before I got a few photos. It's curious that nearly the same thing happened a few days ago when I got a nice photo of the fly's other end for Bug Butt Thursday group. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2020)

Usually I find these flies working new flowers of pistillate (female) Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family, but those shrubs finished flowering a month ago. I guess this one is out looking for a host for its young. It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" but these bristly flies are apparently predators of the equally bristly "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae. They are specifically said to prey on the Edwards' Glassy-wing (Pseudohemihyalea edwardsii) moth, see this photo and this photo. I haven't seen any of the caterpillars yet this year, and I wonder if our drought conditions are creating some timing issues?
