File.Name: kc05a_kc05-011.jpg
Notes:
50684930727 Do you see the cricket? You can look right at them and not see, and suddenly there they are! This one is a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) hiding among seed capsules of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's a female with a long ovipositor which is hardened or sclerotized for egg-laying - in plant stems according to Google. I reckon the crickets are just about done for the year, and I was lucky to find this one today. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2020)

It was sunny again today, but there was a steady breeze from the southeast this morning to make getting my photos difficult. It was cooler too, barely into the 60s. We'll be under an official NWS Fire Weather Watch on Monday because of wind and low humidity, but fire weather seems to be our constant state of affairs these days.
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; sclerotized
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; sclerotized

File.Name: kc05b_kc05-025.jpg
Notes: 50684033368 Here is another shot of the remarkable Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) in my [Previous] photo. It's finished wrapping its prey now and is hanging off a dead leaf. It is still holding onto its line with its bottommost leg, but you can barely see it. These are cribellate spiders, and you can see the spider's cribellum near its rear end, see this photo for some discussion. I did have a beam on sunlight filtered through the oak trees for this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2020)

File.Name: kc05c_kc05-017.jpg
Notes: 50683989888 This isn't the best photo, but it shows a remarkable spider doing something remarkable. It's a tiny Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) wrapping its prey with webbing. It was turning its prey around and around while pumping its abdomen rhythmically. What's most remarkable is how its web is broken and it's holding the different ends in different hands/legs. They always do this, and they must move by playing out one end of the line as they reel in the other, see this photo. I got this photo in failing light today, and I like how it came out - despite the blur. See my [Next] photo for another more static shot of the same spider in better light. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2020)

File.Name: kc05d_kc04-020.jpg
Notes: 50684685961 Here is another shot of the handsome wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that I found on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday, see my [Previous] photo. I'm pretty sure now that this is a Blood Bee in the genus Sphecodes, Halictidae. I figure it gets the common name from the color of its abdomen, but it does have pretty brutal habits. These bees are cleptoparasites which means that they enter the nest of another bee, kill most of the eggs, and replace them with their own eggs before making an exit. Other cleptoparasitic bees let their larvae kill the host's offspring, but these bees do it themselves. Sometimes they are called Cuckoo Bees because they are similar to cuckoo birds which also lay their eggs in another bird's nest. Interesting stuff. I'm still puzzled by what this bee is doing out in the woods right now when there are few flowers and even fewer wild bees available. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Coast Live Oak; Blood Bee; Cuckoo Bees
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Sphecodes; Halictidae; cleptoparasites
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Sphecodes; Halictidae; cleptoparasites

File.Name: kc06a_kc06-013.jpg
Notes:
50688419858 Here is a small but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on an old plant stem in the woods today. The fly let me get close, and I actually got this photo at 1:1 magnification so I can use my camera as an expensive remote micrometer. The fly is about about 1970 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 8.3 mm, a bit over 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I actually found two of these predator flies today, see my [Previous] photo. That photo has backlight, while this photo has direct lighting with the sun behind me, and I really can't tell if the flies are the same or different - so much for "photographic evidence"! I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. Today I was lucky and found two. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2020)

It was mostly dead calm this morning, with breezy moments - just the opposite of yesterday when it was breezy with calm moments. Sunny and dry weather continues, but it might change for the worse tomorrow with real winds - we're under both a NWS Wind Advisory and a Red Flag Warning. We're also under a three week stay-at-home order starting at midnight tonight - but I figure I've been doing that since March!

File.Name: kc06b_kc06-010.jpg
Notes: 50689206377 I found this small but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on an old faded flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. The sun is behind the fly in this photo, and I really can't tell if it's the same or different from the fly in my [Next] photo found nearby without the backlight. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. Today I was lucky and found two. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2020)

File.Name: kc06c_kc06-021.jpg
Notes: 50689068891 This is another Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) hanging off a dead plant stem and holding the end of its web, see this photo and this photo. This one looks furrier than most, I guess that's one way they are variable. I like that beam of "God light" in the background - aka "crepuscular rays" - though it's on a very small scale. It's had to say just where the spider starts, but I estimate it's about 850 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.6 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I also got a photo of this one's triangular web, but I'll save it for Web Wednesday. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2020)

File.Name: kc06d_kc04-006.jpg
Notes: 50689034151 Here is another photo of a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) similar to the one I showed yesterday. It's anchored by one end of its line and holding the other end, see this photo and this photo. They remind me of tiny octopuses or cuttlefish! I found this one a couple of days ago. This must be their time. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2020)

File.Name: kc07a_kc07-025.jpg
Notes:
50692635968 Here is a little Stilt Bug (Berytidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. You can see just a hint of its long proboscis folded under its head. These bugs look like skinny Assassin Bugs, but I believe they only suck plants and not other insects. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2020)

It was overcast and windy with 30+ mph gusts today, and none of my photos is quite sharp. It might get worse tonight before it gets better, we're under a NWS Red Flag Warning until noon tomorrow. We got a call about a possible Public Safety Power Shutoff, but we're out of the warning area so far. This wind makes me uneasy.

File.Name: kc07b_kc07-013.jpg
Notes: 50693327576 I went back to the same place I was yesterday and found that this small but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) was sitting on the same faded flower where I left it - compare this photo from yesterday. The little fly was awake, and it moved around while I was watching it, I guess it just didn't feel like getting up on such a cloudy and windy day. When I looked for it, I found the other one of the two Robber Flies from yesterday just where I left it too. I'll look for them both again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2020)

File.Name: kc07c_kc07-022.jpg
Notes: 50692560303 Here are three different galls of Live Oak Bud Gall Wasps (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) growing on different small trees of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I got the center photo today, and the others over the last week. The galls are small, about 1/4 inch in diameter. They form on the leaf buds of the tree and grow to the size of a pea - and then they change color and fall away to the ground. The gall on the left is fresh, the one on the right was gone the next day, and I don't know what the story is with the one in the center that has an interesting mottled texture. They have a wonderfully pure geometric shape. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasps; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kc07d_kc06-006.jpg
Notes: 50693260076 These are dandelion-like seeds of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) growing along the road in the canyon yesterday. If you look, tou can see that they have two different kinds of seeds, some with hairs and some without. The hairless seeds are all along the outside of the receptacle disk, and I reckon they come from the individual ray flowers of the composite flower. They also seem to lack the pappus hairs that form the "wings" of the seed. The seeds with wings are the first to leave. I looked at the same plant today, and they were all gone - blowing in the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2020)

File.Name: kc08a_kc08-027.jpg
Notes:
50697175587 I found another dragonfly hanging from a tangle of old stems of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in a small oak tree in the woods this morning. Compare this photo of another one that I found in the same place a couple of weeks ago. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. It wasn't particularly chilly this morning, but it was cloudy and windy. These dragonflies will stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and/or stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch like this. It's a real challenge to try to find them - and a surprise when you suddenly notice one! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2020)

We've had interesting weather lately with cloudy skies and winds to 45 mph last night. It's odd that the wind came from the east or northeast. As I understand it, there was high pressure over the desert resulting in Santa Ana-like east winds that got entrained by a low pressure system over the ocean and pulled in a counter-clockwise rotation to the northwest and over us. The Santa Ana winds don't usually bother us, but this time they came right over the mountains. Anyway, the winds finally died down before noon today, and it's been surprisingly warm, in the 70s at home and 80∞ at the Santa Barbara airport. Can't complain!
ScientificName: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata
associatedTaxa: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata

File.Name: kc08b_kc08-014.jpg
Notes: 50697139027 This is a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a dead grass stem in the woods today. It's only about 850 pixels across long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.6 mm, a bit more than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders with humps on its abdomen like in this photo, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former because there is no particular dip between the humps on its abdomen. See Alice Abelia's comment on this photo - which probably shows that latter of the two species. Despite the wind this morning, it was a good day for tiny spiders! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2020)
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneidae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis

File.Name: kc08c_kc08-017.jpg
Notes: 50697011761 Here is a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) in the woods today. These tiny spiders are only about 1/8 inch long or less. Note that this one has eight spots, four on each side of its abdomen. Despite its common name, the spiders in our canyon (at least) all seem to have eight spots rather than six. I believe this one had a web across the leaf, but it was knocked down by the strong winds last night. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2020)

File.Name: kc08d_kc08-008.jpg
Notes: 50696249563 This is a small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This spider was hiding under a leaf, but I guess I bothered it enough trying to get a photo that it came out in the open for a quick shot before going back into hiding after I was done. These are the spiders I usually find on flowers, but it'll be a long wait until we have flowers again. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2020)

File.Name: kc09a_kc09-007.jpg
Notes:
50700115323 I found another tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) in the woods today that looks very much like the one I found yesterday, compare this photo. One difference is that my spider today has a secret face with red eyes and a happy smile - look close at its abdomen! Mark Egger pointed out in my other photo that there is a Hawaiian happy-face spider in the genus Theridion (like this), but the spiders I find with faces on their rear ends usually look more demonic! This spider is probably less than 1/8 inch long. I believe it is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, probably the former because there is no particular dip between the humps on its abdomen. See Alice Abelia's comment on this photo - which probably shows the latter of the two species. I found it in a grassy place, but that seems wrong. I wonder if it was blown here from somewhere else by the recent winds? (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks. The nearly statewide stay-at-home order now in effect didn't change anything for us, though I noticed that the Chumash Casino was open, and the restaurants in Solvang are now takeout only. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be - though I do wish we'd finally get some rain! I did have time for a short walk in the woods alsog our driveway before we left today, and I was happy to find this tiny spider in a beam of sunlight.
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Theridion; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneidae; Theridion; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis

File.Name: kc09b_kc09-004.jpg
Notes: 50700826541 This is somebody's nest under a fallen leaf of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica,Lauraceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure who's nest it is, but I guess some kind of spider. It seems to be made of webbing that's been woven into a sheet - like tissue paper. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2020)

File.Name: kc09c_kc08-036.jpg
Notes: 50700027983 This is webbing inside a galled leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is one of several different kinds of "rolled leaf gall" that we seem to have on these trees, it's the one on the right in this photo. I believe the gall is the work of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla that do make webs, but I suspect that the webbing in my photo today is the work of some spider that has just moved into the space. Other insects do move into these galls, see this photo of a predator invading one. I'm not sure who's responsible, but it is real webbing - so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: kc09d_kc06-024.jpg
Notes: 50700813792 You have to look close at my photo to see this unusual web of a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) - the real in-person web is just as hard to see, the webbing is so fine that it disappears in the sunlight. The spiders make these odd webs shaped like a pie-slice of a complete web and hang upside-down on the thread that goes off to the left in this photo. I've been finding quite a few of these spiders lately, see and this photo. I went back to this web on the next day to see if I could get a better shot, but the wind destroyed all signs of the web. Maybe tomorrow. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2020)

File.Name: kc10a_kc10-016.jpg
Notes:
50704527711 Here is a tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) tending its web from its shelter on a nearby seed capsule of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) in the woods today. These spiders spin a proper orb web that is connected to a separate shelter by a special sensing line that the spider uses to sense a strike, see this photo for another example. I have a close shot of this spider in this photo. It's tiny but attractive, less than 1/10 inch across, and its orb web is only a couple of inches across. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2020)

It was a pretty crummy day for new photos as clouds started spilling over the mountains driven by a wind from the south. I never left the canyon to see if this was a real marine layer condition, but it felt just like the "fog winds" we usually get with our June gloom weather. It was chilly today, only in the 50s after 70s a couple of days ago. There's no rain in the extended forecast.

File.Name: kc10b_kc10-004.jpg
Notes: 50703733678 Here is another photo of a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) holding its line with its front feet and anchored to an old flower/seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. It's probably the same furry individual I show in this photo that's associated with the web in this photo. This isn't the best photo today - it was windy and cloudy this morning - but look at those white egg cases right above the spider. Spider eggs?? Nah, I don't think any spider has eggs that large, and I'm pretty sure from their shape that these are the eggs of some "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera), but it's interesting that the spider picked this place for a perch. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2020)
ScientificName: Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloboridae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloboridae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: kc10c_kc08-005.jpg
Notes: 50703693468 This tiny spider didn't want to pose for me on this oak leaf, but at least I got this photo of its rear end. I'm pretty sure it's a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, probably an immature Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes) - compare this photo from February last year. That serrated stripe down its back is a distinctive marking. These spiders get fairly large as they mature, but this one is small, only about 1/8 inch long. See this photo for a similar small spider taken last month. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2020)

File.Name: kc10d_kc10-032.jpg
Notes: 50703669203 This photo shows the ovipositor of the same little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) that I showed in this photo last week. Its ovipositor is apparently hardened or sclerotized for egg-laying, but I don't know where she lays her eggs that requires such a serious structure. Hah, it resembles a pipe stem! HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2020)

File.Name: kc11a_kc11-009.jpg
Notes:
50708168846 Here is a small but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on an old faded flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I found what might be the same fly in the same place last weekend, see this photo - and I found it again in the same place a day later, see this photo - but I looked for it again yesterday and didn't see it. There was a chill today, and I guess that's why it was parked here. This fly is small, a bit over 1/4 inch long. I don't have many photos of these predators because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2020)

It was chilly today with a high of about 50∞ on our shady side of the canyon. It was breezy in the morning becoming downright windy in the afternoon. Dry weather will probably continue until the new year at least, making this the driest start of the rain year in the records for San Marcos Pass. There's more info at the California Weather Blog with the subtitle: "Record-dry start to 'wet season' in some places.

File.Name: kc11b_kc11-012.jpg
Notes: 50708201107 I found this striking "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) among seed capsules on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. This bug is bug-eyed! I'm not sure what kind it is, but its protuberant eyes and antennae with a hairy base and color like a cast-iron pan seem distinctive. Any help with ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2020)

File.Name: kc11c_kc11-005.jpg
Notes: 50708150722 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), see this photo for another shot of (probably) the same spider nearly a month ago. I'm pretty sure it's another Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira), and it's tiny - smaller than the one I showed yesterday in this photo. This one is only about 525 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The "folium pattern" on its abdomen is quite distinctive, compare this photo and this photo of similar tiny spiders. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2020)

File.Name: kc11d_kc06-031.jpg
Notes: 50707308798 This is looking face to face with a small fly (Diptera) on leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I don't know what kind of fly it is, but I don't think it's "just a fly" as so many of them are - at least those gray and orange colors are more interesting than the usual black. I usually have trouble getting the focus right on face shots, but I'm happy with this one. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2020)

File.Name: kc12a_kc12-015.jpg
Notes:
50712392621 I found this handsome grasshopper munching seeds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Look by its front foot, I'm sure it's eating the seeds and not the dandelion-like fluff that's attached to them. I believe this is another Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes, Melanoplinae). (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2020)

The chilly winds were blowing today with gusts of 30+ mph making it hard to get new photos. This grasshopper was nearly the only insect I saw today, though I did notice that the little Robber Fly I showed yesterday in this photo was still on the same perch. The wind makes me all jittery, I hope this is the end of it.

We have another one of those @#&$ maintenance power outages tomorrow from 9:00 am to 5:00 pm. I should be able to post if it doesn't run much later than that - like our last outage that ran until 9:30 instead of 6:00. This is a real inconvenience at this time of year with cold nights and dark days. We've had way too many of these outages this year, the result of years of deferred maintenance coupled with a new sensitivity to liabilities.
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae; way too many
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae; way too many

File.Name: kc12b_kc12-002.jpg
Notes: 50712335436 Here are two shots of one of the "rolled leaf galls" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I show in my [Previous] photo. It's the one on the left in this photo which shows several different kinds of these rolled leaf galls. These particular galls seem to be real "leaf rolls" that are closed on the ends, like a sushi roll. They seem to be developing on the leaves right now. I suppose I should open a few up, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2020)

File.Name: kc12c_kc12-006.jpg
Notes: 50712298261 This is an oak leaf with galls along the edges. They are one of several different kinds of "rolled leaf gall" that we seem to have on our trees, the one on the left in this photo. I'm sure they are proper galls and not the work of leaf-roller insects because the cellular structure of the leaf has been changed and not just mechanically folded and tied down. I know nothing else about these galls as they are not listed in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I suppose I should open a few up, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. See my [Next] photo for a closer view of the top and bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2020)

File.Name: kc12d_kc08-021.jpg
Notes: 50712233556 Here are new seeds of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family escaping from their seedpods. I have photos of the large showy flowers here and here. I still think of these plants by their old name Zauschneria californica, but even I have to admit that they sure look like genus Epilobium when they are in seed like this - see this photo for another species of that genus. They look like dandelion seeds with wings to fly - but I reckon the actual seeds are too large to go very far. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2020)
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Zauschneria californica; Epilobium
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Zauschneria californica; Epilobium

File.Name: kc13a_kc13-031.jpg
Notes:
50716549353 Hah, I found this very well-endowed male spider on an oak leaf in the woods today. Those are its enlarged male palps in front, and they look like just what they are! I believe this is a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, probably a Hammock Spider (Pityohyphantes) - compare this photo from February last year, though this one is smaller with different palps. I didn't notice at the time, but it seems to have an orange belt around the narrow part of its abdomen - the spider is carrying a passenger that I'm sure is the larva of a Mantidfly insect in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera. I've seen these passengers on spiders before, see this photo and this photo. I believe this is the first one I've seen on this kind of spider. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2020)

We had another of those @#&$ maintenance power outages today that was scheduled from 9:00 am to 5:00 pm - and this one was actually done by 3:15. Thanks. There was a breeze today that's likely to become a wind tomorrow as a weak storm front moves through. I'm expecting more wind than rain.
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Hammock Spider; Mantidfly

File.Name: kc13b_kc13-010.jpg
Notes: 50716496188 Here is a tiny spider about to balloon away from a seedhead of native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). If you look close, you can see a line coming from its elevated rear end which is enough to carry it away. It was gone a moment later, like it was snatched away. I didn't notice what kind of spider it is, but I suspect it's a Crab Spider in the genus Ozyptila - compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2020)

File.Name: kc13c_kc13-021.jpg
Notes: 50716412403 Here is a tiny blue (or "blue dun") fly (Diptera) on a "rolled leaf" of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The fly is tiny, only about 630 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm - about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I believe this is a Silver Fly aka Aphid Fly in the genus Leucopis of the family Chamaemyiidae, compare this photo at BugGuide and this photo of mine. Apparently their larvae are predators of aphids, see this photo from a few years ago that shows a connection. It's interesting that the fly in my photo is on a "rolled leaf gall" which is caused by Woolly Oak Aphids, it's the gall on the right in this photo which shows several different kinds of these galls. I like how everything is connected! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Silver Fly; Aphid Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Leucopis; Chamaemyiidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Leucopis; Chamaemyiidae

File.Name: kc13d_kc13-001.jpg
Notes: 50717133562 This will be the driest start of the rain year in the records for San Marcos Pass if our dry weather continues until the end of the year, and the drought is taking a toll on our woodland. It hasn't just been dry, but August, September, and October were each the hottest months on record in California. My photo today shows part of a colony of perennial Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) in the woods this morning which is looking particularly sad, compare this photo from a few springs ago. I'm also worried about native Prickly Phlox and Bush Monkey Flower shrubs which are looking terrible. Hopefully these plants will recover after a good rain, but maybe not. That said, we do have a chance for light rain tonight, but I'm not expecting more than a trace - not enough. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2020)

File.Name: kc14a_kc14-003.jpg
Notes:
50720710493 Here is a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly among fallen oak leaves in the woods today. If you look close, you can see that it has its proboscis out like it's trying to find moisture under the leaves - something that normally works at this time of year but not with this very dry start to the rain year. This is one of a trio of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days. I photographed a Mourning Cloak a couple of weeks ago, which was also trying (and failing) to find moisture in the leaves. I'm still waiting to catch a California Tortoiseshell, though I think I've seen a couple fly by. I rarely see any of these butterflies during summer. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2020)

The chilly winds were blowing again today with 20-30 mph gusts all day. I did notice some water pooled on a tarp this morning, so I reckon we did get a trace of rain last night - but not enough to measure or make a difference. So maybe this Red Admiral butterfly really did find some moisture hidden under the leaves?

File.Name: kc14b_kc14-010.jpg
Notes: 50721383386 This is a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly looking at me from from its perch on a sandstone rock in the canyon today. I like its shadow. I also like how you can see two little dark lines below its eyes - these are actually its front legs. All brush-footed butterflies in the family Nymphalidae stand on just four legs and have their front legs reduced like this, but it's something we rarely notice. This is one of a group of large butterflies that overwinter as adults and come out on warm days - including the Mourning Cloak and California Tortoiseshell which are both in the same family with reduced front legs. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2020)

File.Name: kc14c_kc14-020.jpg
Notes: 50720592713 Here is a gall on a branch of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today that shows a clean exit hole. I believe it's the gall of a Dried Peach Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis simulata) in the family Cynipidae that dates back to last year, see this photo of a different gall taken in the same shrub. The gall has lost its peach fuzz, and you can see how woody it is underneath. The whole gall is about 1/2 inch across, and the exit hole is about 1/10 inch across. It's interesting that there appears to be webbing inside the gall, so maybe some other critter has moved in? (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2020)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Dried Peach Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae

File.Name: kc14d_kc14-023.jpg
Notes: 50721391937 This is a galled stem of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) that's swollen because it is holding the caterpillar of a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella or kin) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the order Lepidoptera. Yep, moths can induce galls as well as wasps and flies, and Ceanothus shrubs can host them as well as oaks. See this photo on the left for another moth gall on a different shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2020)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: kc15a_kc15-024.jpg
Notes:
50725042627 Here is another dragonfly (Odonata) on a winter-bare twig in the woods today. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, like the one in this photo taken last week. The difference is that this one was actually out hunting and not just sitting out a chilly morning in a sheltered place. These dragonflies will stay active in the canyon all winter long, though they sit out cold and/or stormy days by finding an out-of-the-way perch to wait it out. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2020)

It was chilly again his morning, but I got this photo is a sunny place, and that makes all the difference - for dragonflies and their photographers! There was the usual morning breeze from the south. There is another weak storm coming on Thursday that will mostly miss us - all but the wind.

File.Name: kc15b_kc15-025.jpg
Notes: 50724182088 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the woods today. Hah, I'm pretty sure this is the same little predator I showed in this photo back on Thanksgiving Day. I've been seeing it on the same leaf most every day but always in deep shade. It's been stationary for weeks now, but it moves whenever I get close. Today there was a beam of light, and I got the shot. It's in the same defensive pose that I've show before e.g. in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2020)
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: kc15c_kc15-020.jpg
Notes: 50724960742 This is a spiky little spider snuggled into a dead leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The spider and leaf are so dark and contrasty that they were hard to see even right in front of me - and hard to photograph too. It's definitely an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) with large humps - almost horns - on its abdomen. It's quite small, maybe 1/4 inch long. I'm thinking it might be a juvenile Araneus andrewsi with those humps and dark colors and bristly legs - but who knows. I believe that this is the spider that goes with the web in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2020)
ScientificName: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Araneus; Araneidae; Araneus andrewsi
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Araneus; Araneidae; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: kc15d_kc13-028.jpg
Notes: 50724115158 Here is another shot of dandelion-like seeds of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) growing along the road in the canyon. I like these shots, compare this photo taken a week ago. If you look close, you can see that they have two different kinds of seeds, some with hairs and some without. The hairless seeds are all along the outside of the receptacle disk, and I reckon they come from the individual ray flowers of the composite flower. They also seem to lack the pappus hairs that form the "wings" of the seed. The seeds with wings are the first to leave. This photo would have worked for the Macro Mondays challenge this week to shoot something high key, but I forgot all about it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2020)

File.Name: kc16a_kc16-039.jpg
Notes:
50728566162 Here is an odd colony of wool-producing critters on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. The photo on the right is a closer shot of the top of the colony on the left. Maybe they are Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae, but they also look like Mealybugs in the family Pseudococcidae. I've been confused by these before, see this photo. If you do a search for "Stegophylla" at BugGuide, you'll find a nice photo by Alice Abelia (among others) but no info. I'm not at all sure about this, and any guidance will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2020)

I got this photo in a place where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice, and I never could have got it if it wasn't dead calm this afternoon. It was a bit warmer today also, all in all a nice almost-winter day. There is an outside chance of a trace of rain tomorrow, but all I'm expecting is more wind.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids; Mealybugs
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Stegophylla; Aphididae; Pseudococcidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Stegophylla; Aphididae; Pseudococcidae

File.Name: kc16b_kc16-013.jpg
Notes: 50728505372 Here is a tiny spider tucked into an old seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider is only about 450 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.9 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I've photographed this spider before, see this photo, but in my photo today I can see that it has the markings of a tiny Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira) like in this photo. It's responsible for the excellent web in this photo. I think of these old Black Sage seed heads as little "spider hotels" for tiny creatures. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2020)

File.Name: kc16c_kc14-015.jpg
Notes: 50727653933 This is somebody's nest under a fallen leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods a couple of days ago. I'm not sure who's nest it is, but I would guess it's the work of a caterpillar rather than a spider. The nest is actually hollow with an opening on the left where something presumably got out rather than in. I believe the material is real webbing and not some kind of cotton wool, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2020)

File.Name: kc16d_kc10-009.jpg
Notes: 50728358331 This is webbing between old flower/seed whorls of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Hah, it looks like rigging on a dusty model ship! I believe this is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, and I have a shot of the tiny spider associated with such webbing in this photo from last spring. I've photographed this webbing before on the sage, most recently in this photo from September. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2020)

File.Name: kc17a_kc17-004.jpg
Notes:
50731775652 Here is another shot of a small but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on an old faded flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I found what is probably the same fly in a in the same place week ago, see this photo - and I found it again nearly two weeks ago in the same place, see this photo. This fly is hunkered down, but it's still responsive and moves around. I've also found an Assassin Bug that's been mostly stationary for weeks now, see this photo. I wonder if they will stay put all winter long?? This fly is small, a bit over 1/4 inch long. I don't have many photos of these predators because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one perched in the woods like this - but it helps if you already know where it is ahead of time! This photo does put the focus of the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2020)

As expected, we didn't get even a trace of rain last night, but we did get serious north winds with 30-40 mph gusts all day. It's been chilly too, heading for the 30s tonight. Our NWS Wind Advisory expires late tonight, and I hope that's the end of it.

File.Name: kc17b_kc17-016.jpg
Notes: 50730918243 This is another gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) growing on a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This one stands out for its beautiful colors and its size - it's tiny, just 510 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm, less than 1/10 inch and smaller than a BB. (See here for how I figured.) I rarely find them this young, and these galls just are variable - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2020)
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: kc17c_kc17-020.jpg
Notes: 50731715352 This swelling is a galled stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this is the work of a Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera. I know nothing else about it, but it's interesting that moths can cause plant galls as well as wasps and flies. This gall is considered an integral stem gall as opposed to detachable galls like the one in my [Next] photo that can fall away. See this photo for another integral moth gall on a different shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2020)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; integral stem gall; detachable galls
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; integral stem gall; detachable galls

File.Name: kc17d_kc04-024.jpg
Notes: 50730862268 Here is a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) trying to get away from me on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods last week. At least my photo shows the bee's rear end, see this photo and this photo for the rest of the bee. I'm pretty sure that this is a Blood Bee in the genus Sphecodes, Halictidae. I figure it gets its common name from the color of its abdomen, but it does have some pretty brutal habits. These bees are cleptoparasites that enter the nest of another bee, kill most of the eggs, and replace them with their own eggs before making an exit. Sometimes they are called Cuckoo Bees because they are similar to cuckoo birds which also lay their eggs in another bird's nest. Interesting stuff. This is also a pretty good bug butt shot that I never used last week, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2020)
CommonNames: wild bee; Coast Live Oak; Blood Bee; Cuckoo Bees
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Sphecodes; Halictidae; cleptoparasites
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Sphecodes; Halictidae; cleptoparasites

File.Name: kc18a_kc18-011.jpg
Notes:
50734929672 This is "just a fly" on an oak leaf in the woods today that is smaller than the flies in my [Previous] photo. I guess it's a female with those widely spaced eyes. I like photographing flies - they have so much structural detail. I don't know what kind of fly this is or anything about it, and any help with ID will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2020)

Even flies have been getting scarce as we approach the new year with hardly any rain during what should be our wet season. It's still very dry, but at least the big winds we had yesterday have gone away. The next two months are all important, as they should be the rainiest of the year.

File.Name: kc18b_kc18-009.jpg
Notes: 50734069813 Here are two different flies on oak leaves in the woods, the one on the left from today and the one on the right from two days ago. I'm thinking they might be the same kind of fly, maybe male (left) and female (right) judging by the spacing of their eyes - but I really don't know. I like these photos because I usually have trouble getting the focus right with head-on shots, and both of these are not bad. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2020)

File.Name: kc18c_kc16-033z.jpg
Notes: 50734053363 This is "just a fly" on an oak leaf in the woods. This is also the fly on the right in my [Next ] photo. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but I like all the bristles. As always, any help with ID will be appreciated. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2020)

File.Name: kc18d_kc03-007.jpg
Notes: 50734025413 Here are two shots of a tiny blue (or "blue dun") fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This fly really is tiny, only about 1/10 inch long. I believe it is a Silver Fly aka Aphid Fly in the genus Leucopis of the family Chamaemyiidae. Apparently their larvae are predators of aphids, see this photo from a few years ago that shows a connection with aphids, and this photo shows another of these flies on a "rolled leaf gall" caused by Woolly Oak Aphids on another oak leaf. These photos are two weeks old but still worth posting. I already have my two photos for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Silver Fly; Aphid Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Leucopis; Chamaemyiidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Leucopis; Chamaemyiidae

File.Name: kc19a_kc19-004.jpg
Notes:
50737675713 Here are mating Weevils (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. They are fairly large for weevils, probably more than 1/2 inch long - especially the larger one on the bottom that I reckon is the female. I believe they are Broad-nosed Weevils (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae), but they are larger than most of those I find like this one. One possibility for an ID is the genus Adaleres - compare this photo at BugGuide of a weevil on a different Manzanita found by Alice Abelia. I have a close shot of the smaller male in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2020)

Just when I think I've seen everything in our still-too-dry almost-winter woods, I find something new! Today was a nice day, a notch warmer without much wind. Winter begins on the solstice after one more day. It feels chilly enough, but we're missing all the banana slugs and mushrooms that winter weather should bring.
ScientificName: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Entiminae; Curculionidae; Adaleres
associatedTaxa: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Entiminae; Curculionidae; Adaleres

File.Name: kc19b_kc19-022.jpg
Notes: 50738344681 This is looking close at the top weevil in my [next] photo of a mating pair on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods today. Hah, it looks like a top dog with a long snout! I believe this is a Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae), but at maybe 1/2 inch long, it's larger than most of those that I find like this one. One possibility for an ID is the genus Adaleres - compare this photo at BugGuide of a weevil on a different Manzanita found by Alice Abelia. This weevil was surprisingly hard to photograph up close. It looks plain brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting - many color swatches combine to make an eye-catching brown. Brown Elfin butterflies are also like this, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2020)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Entiminae; Curculionidae; Adaleres; pointillist
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Entiminae; Curculionidae; Adaleres; pointillist

File.Name: kc19c_kc18-006.jpg
Notes: 50737552288 Here are two shots of "rolled leaf galls" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) both above and below the leaf. I believe they are proper galls and not the work of leaf-roller insects because of the way the cellular structure of the leaf has been changed and not just mechanically folded over and tied down. It's interesting to compare the galls in my [Previous] photo which have the lower edges free, but in this photo they look more attached to the bottom of the leaf with just one spine of the upper gall free. (I'll make a note.) I'm not sure if they are the same or different. I find these galls interesting and mysterious. They seem to be developing on the leaves right now. I suppose I should open a few up, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2020)

File.Name: kc19d_kc19-001.jpg
Notes: 50737523923 This is another oak leaf with galls along the edges. They are one of several different kinds of "rolled leaf gall" that we seem to have on our trees, maybe the one in this photo and in my [Next] photo. But I'm not sure. These galls all look similar on top of the leaf, but the folded edge of the leaf is free in this one but not in the others. These galls seem to be developing on the leaves right now. I suppose I should open a few up, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2020)

File.Name: kc20a_kc20-022.jpg
Notes:
50742307096 Here is a dandelion-like seed of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) balanced for a moment between two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It was moving like a dancer, turning around and around, gone a moment later. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2020)

The winter solstice happens tonight at 2:02 am PST. It's the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer after tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky. It's a time of new hope for the future - and we really need it this year. Happy solstice everyone!
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Coast Live Oak; winter solstice
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: kc20b_kc20-015.jpg
Notes: 50742374312 I found this handsome Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the woods today. I don't know what kind it is. There are lots of different kinds of these green bugs - including some that change color from brown to green like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2020)

File.Name: kc20c_kc20-007.jpg
Notes: 50742326117 I believe these are tiny scale-like pupae of a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Or are they larvae? They are tiny, about 260 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to a bit more than 1.0 mm long. (See here for how I figured.) The one on the bottom with three "arms" seems to be floating in a puddle of goo. One possibility for ID is Stanfordís Whitefly (Tetraleurodes stanfordi), see p.44 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading for California natural history. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2020)
CommonNames: Whitefly; Coast Live Oak; Stanfordís Whitefly
ScientificName: Aleyrodidae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tetraleurodes stanfordi
associatedTaxa: Aleyrodidae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tetraleurodes stanfordi

File.Name: kc20d_kc20-005.jpg
Notes: 50742284567 Here is one more shot of once-mating Weevils (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) like in my [Previous] two photos - except this one was taken today. I guess this photo shows cuddling aprËs-sex, a dogpile of weevils. I believe these are Broad-nosed Weevils (subfamily Entiminae), maybe the genus Adaleres. They are surprisingly difficult to photograph up close. They look plain brown from a distance, but up close they look more like a pointillist painting - many color swatches combine to make an eye-catching brown. Brown Elfin butterflies are also like this, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2020)
ScientificName: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Entiminae; Adaleres; pointillist
