File.Name: kc21a_kc21-046.jpg
Notes:
50746034157 Here are two shots of a tiny fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It has very long antennae with whorls of hairs on each segment like a bottle brush. I suspect it's a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae. Similar flies cause quite a few galls on our native plants, try a search of my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2020)

Maybe it's an omen that I found this fly today on the first day of winter. It's in the "Nematocera" group, a group that includes Crane Flies that I associate with winter and rainy weather. Today is the winter solstice, but it sure didn't feel like winter - it was warm and dry, nearly 70∞ on our shady deck and up to 80∞ in sunnier places with weather stations on the web. It will cool down tomorrow, and there's a rumor of a real Christmas gift - widespread rain and mountain snow later Sunday evening into Monday. Maybe.

(We did go out tonight to see the great conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn. Nothing ominous, not really exciting, but it was pretty cool to see the "horns of Saturn" with nothing but binoculars.)
Locality: We did go out tonight to see the great conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn.  Nothing ominous
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Gall Midge
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Nematocera
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Nematocera

File.Name: kc21b_kc21-017.jpg
Notes: 50745981072 Here is a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) with a wrapped prey on an old flower/seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure what its prey is, but I can make out hairs and maybe a red eye on top. It's really well-wrapped, almost like shrink-wrap. I actually found another one of these spiders with prey today that seemed to release a fluid from its mouth to smooth out its own wrapped prey. I got photos, but they are not the best as the light was fading - I'll see if I can clean one up for tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2020)

File.Name: kc21c_kc21-005.jpg
Notes: 50745825801 This leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) contains different "rolled leaf galls" than the leaf in my [Previous] photo. The big difference is that the leaf is folded under the bottom rather than over the top. I've been looking close at these galls that fold under, and I've noticed that some have a free edge like in today's photo and this photo, while others have an edge that is more sealed like the galls in this photo. I'm not sure if they are the same or different. I find these galls interesting and mysterious. They seem to be developing on the leaves right now. I suppose I should open a few up, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2020)

File.Name: kc21d_kc20-024.jpg
Notes: 50745789381 This is a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I believe contains a colony of Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae. It's a "rolled leaf gall" that always folds up onto the top of the leaf which distinguishes it from the galled leaf in my [Next] photo which fold down over the underside of the leaf. I have opened one of these galls and found aphids, see this photo and this photo. Similar aphids also produce masses of wool on the underside of oak leaves without folding them, see this photo. I don't understand the connection between the two. There's some info on p.46 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF) that suggests they are different stages of the same aphids. This is a document worth downloading. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: kc22a_kc22-016.jpg
Notes:
50749638632 This is the same spider I show in my [Previous] photo taken yesterday holding its wrapped prey in the woods today. If you compare yesterday's photo, you can see that the wrapping has gone from white to black - except for that red "eye". The different spider that I show two photos back has prey that has also turned black. I'm sure that this has something to do with that mysterious liquid that the spider is applying in my [Previous] photo. This is strange and mysterious, and any help understanding it will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2020)

It was a pleasant morning in the 60s, but it started to change after about 1:00 pm as the temperature started to drop into the 50s. It still sounds good for rain next Monday - but that's still days away.

File.Name: kc22b_kc21-037.jpg
Notes: 50748735363 Here is a sequence of three shots taken within a minute showing a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) doing *something* with its wrapped prey. This is a different spider in a different place than the one in my [Previous] photo. The spider seems to be releasing a fluid from its mouth which is spreading over the wrapped prey like a liquid plastic or varnish, and the prey looks quite different a day later - see my [Next] photo. This is strange and mysterious, and any help understanding it will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2020)

File.Name: kc22c_kc22-002.jpg
Notes: 50749526562 Here is another shot of the tiny Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) with a wrapped prey that I showed yesterday in this photo. The spider is hanging off an old flower/seed whorl of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Its prey is unrecognizable. If you compare yesterday's photo, you can see that the wrapping has gone from white to black - except for those red "lips" that are probably eyes. See my [Next] two photos for another part of this story. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2020)

File.Name: kc22d_kc21-041.jpg
Notes: 50748620078 This is another tiny fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods yesterday, compare my [Previous] photo of a quite different fly. I believe this one is a Hybotid Dance Fly in the family Hybotidae - see Thijs de Graaf's comment to this photo. It's got a round head like a Dance Fly (Empididae) but a shorter proboscis which points somewhat forward rather than straight down. These flies are predators, and this one might be genus Platypalpus which are said to have a swollen middle femur to hold their prey while feeding. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2020)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Hybotid Dance Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Hybotidae; Empididae; Platypalpus
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Hybotidae; Empididae; Platypalpus

File.Name: kc23a_kc23-002.jpg
Notes:
50753302247 This is the pupal cocoon of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. The material of the cocoon looks like webbing which is appropriate for Web Wednesday - but it's something else. It seems to have two distinct parts - a bristly brown section joined with a soft white section. I believe the brown part is the actual cocoon, now empty. It looks like it's made of excelsior, those curly wood shavings used as a packing material, but it's actually made up of hairs that the caterpillar plucks from its own body. And the soft-looking white part is actually all that's left of the female moth that emerged from the cocoon. The female moths are born without wings and are really just living sacks of eggs that mate with a winged male, lay their eggs directly on their old cocoon, and die - check out this remarkable photo at BugGuide. There's an excellent photo essay on "Life History of Western Tussock Moths, Orgyia vetusta" by Joyce Gross here. See this photo for a cocoon without the white part, maybe a male, and see this photo for the remarkable caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2020)

Beer and other necessities were getting low again, so it was time to make our usual shopping run to the Santa Ynez Valley that we do every two weeks. I've been staying put in the canyon for the rest of the time, and I can't think of any place I'd rather be - though I do hope we finally get some rain next week. I did have time for a short walk in the woods before we left today, and I was happy to find this cocoon.
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae; Lepidoptera; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: kc23b_kc22-008.jpg
Notes: 50752417178 Here is a small spider orb web strung between stems of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). The tiny spider is actually sitting on the plant stem, I'll make a note. It's barely visible, but I have another shot of it in the same place, see this photo from ten days ago - it's another tiny Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira, Araneidae). This one is sitting at the end of a radius strand that is acting as its "sensing line" to transmit vibrations from its web to its retreat. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2020)

File.Name: kc23c_kc21-004.jpg
Notes: 50753102636 I can tell by the hump on its abdomen that this is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web in the woods a few days ago. The whole web is about the size of a CD disk, but this spider is on the "inner circle" of its web which is maybe an inch across - see this photo of a whole web and note the relative sizes of the inner and outer circles. (And see this photo for something remarkable about these webs!) In time it will decorate its web with the bodies of its prey, but so far this one only has a clean white stabilimentum to mark where its future trash line will be. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2020)

File.Name: kc23d_kc21-029.jpg
Notes: 50753168392 If you look close on the left, you can see a tiny spider sitting on its orb web between dead branches of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I show the spider up close on the right, and I can see from its markings that it's a Labyrinth Spider (Metepeira) like in this photo and this photo. This one really is tiny, just 300 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.3 mm. (See here for how I figured.) I reckon its a juvenile. The spider is sitting on its inner web, which has a different character than the outer web, see my [Next] photo. I already have my two photos for the Web Wednesday group, but HWW anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2020)

File.Name: kc24a_kc24-015.jpg
Notes:
50756804896 Here is a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata) spider on an umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) in the woods today. These tiny spiders are only about 1/8 inch long or less. This one is hanging from its web, which is barely visible across the top of the leaf. I have another shot of the same spider in this photo. These delicate creatures are probably my favorite spiders to find in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2020)

There was a cloud cover this morning, and even a few tiny drops of rain - like virga that was barely making it to the ground. It sounds like Christmas Day will be pretty nice, followed by a 70% chance of rain late Sunday into Monday. The NWS seems to be backing off their forecast a bit, not a good sign for a storm that is still three days away. I hope the rain happens, we need it.

File.Name: kc24b_kc24-007.jpg
Notes: 50756839532 This is looking up at a little spider hanging under its web about as high up as I can reach in small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's a Sheet Web Weaver in the family Linyphiidae, probably another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. That tuning fork pattern on its carapace and serrated stripe down its back are distinctive markings. This is a good shot of the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2020)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Sheet Web Weaver; Hammock Spider

File.Name: kc24c_kc24-025.jpg
Notes: 50756704041 Here is another little fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf that has a banded abdomen like the fly in my [Previous] photo but is also subtly different - e.g. it has a gray thorax instead of black. I suspect this one is a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae - it's bristly enough, and I can see from another photo that it has a subscutellum, see this page at BugGuide. That certainly is a bristly butt, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2020)

File.Name: kc24d_kc24-034.jpg
Notes: 50756782122 This is another shot of a small fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf that I still can't identify, compare this photo from a month ago. These flies are not very common, they are rather slender with a pointed abdomen, they are quite bristly on their rear ends, and they really don't like being photographed - they often escape just as I'm about to get the photo. I would guess it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae, but I'm not sure. Any help with ID will be appreciated. I already have my two photos for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2020)

File.Name: kc25a_kc25-003.jpg
Notes:
50760030533 Here is new growth on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. These summer-deciduous plants lose their leaves over the summer and get by with their photosynthetic green stems until winter rains arrive. This photo shows the first new leaves that I've seen this year, and they are remarkable because we've had very little rain in this record-dry start to our rainy season. The woods look stressed. These new leaves represent hope for the new growing season - and in fact we have our first real rain in the forecast for Sunday night and Monday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2020)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
ScientificName: Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all
associatedTaxa: Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all

File.Name: kc25b_kc25-036.jpg
Notes: 50760718306 This is looking up at what I believe is Redcoat Seed Bug (Melanopleurus belfragei, Lygaeidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - at least it's something in that genus. It has Santa colors for Christmas! You can't see the distinctive red and black crisscross pattern of its folded wings in this photo, but it's a nice shot of its long black proboscis folded under its head. We have a number of similar red and black true bugs, see my list in my description for this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2020)
CommonNames: Redcoat Seed Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Melanopleurus belfragei; Lygaeidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Melanopleurus belfragei; Lygaeidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: kc25c_kc25-011.jpg
Notes: 50760802422 Here is another small but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on an old faded flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I've been finding these predators for most of the month, compare this photo from December 6 in the same place. They are active - they move around between the flowers and sometimes fly away like this one did today when I got too close. I don't have many photos of these flies because they don't visit flowers to feed. It takes sheer luck (and good eyes) to find one in the woods like this - but it helps to know where they perch. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2020)

File.Name: kc25d_kc24-029.jpg
Notes: 50760646361 This is another shot of the small fly (Diptera) on an oak leaf that I showed yesterday in this photo. I suspect that it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae - it's bristly enough, and I can see that it has a subscutellum, see this page at BugGuide. It's an attractive little fly with bands on its abdomen and stripes on its thorax - kind of like the fabulous fly in this photo that might be the species Vanderwulpia sequens. Could this fly be the same kind? Whatever it is, HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2020)

File.Name: kc26a_kc26-014.jpg
Notes:
50764148528 This is the tiny nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) inside a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in the woods today. It's hard to see, but the nymph seems to have its proboscis inside one of the tiny seeds. The nymph is really tiny, just 425 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.8 mm - about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I've found quite a few critters inside these seed capsules over the years, try a search of my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2020)

This was the only new insect I found in the woods today, but it really wasn't a very good day for looking - chilly, windy, with some clouds starting to move in. I hope these are all signs of our coming storm that now has a 90% chance of bringing real rain starting Sunday night. We're actually under a NWS Winter Storm Watch as temps will drop into the 30s at night. We might get an inch or two of rain from this storm which is our first significant rain since last April. The woods have been distressed with our extended dry conditions this year, and this storm will make a difference - and maybe bring a few more photo-ops for me!

File.Name: kc26b_kc25-020.jpg
Notes: 50764781146 These aren't great photos individually, but they show a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi, Uloboridae) doing something remarkable - turning around on its web. That's had for these spiders because they actually have a break in the line of webbing and hold each end separately, see this photo for a close shot. They must move by playing out one end of the line as they reel in the other - but how do they turn around? The spider in my photos today came in headfirst from the right with a small wrapped prey. In my first shot it's hanging like a cross with the broken web in each "arm"; in the second shot it's doing some fancy footwork to change direction; and in the third shot it has completed its turn and is reeling itself backwards into its nest in an old seed whorl of Black Sage like this. It's a tricky maneuver, and the spider never dropped its package! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2020)

File.Name: kc26c_kc25-029.jpg
Notes: 50764850547 Here are the same pair of galls growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) that I show in this photo back in November of last year. The galls change a lot in a year, though you can still see a hint of their pretty spots under the brown color if you look on the right. These are galls of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera. If you look close, you can see a hole on the bottom of the gall on the right that might be an exit hole where a mature wasp got out - or did some predator get in?? Compare the exit hole on the gall in this photo that is smaller and much neater. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2020)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: kc26d_kc24-003.jpg
Notes: 50764684371 Here are galled flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I say they are galled flowers and not fruit, but they must be the galled ovaries of flowers - see this photo. That odd beaked shape is typical. I'm pretty sure these are the work of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera), see this photo of an emerging adult - at least the husk of one. I probably have a shot of the actual adult fly in this photo. These shrubs flower in June, so why do I find the galls in mid-winter? I guess they stay on the shrub until the next flowering time so that the cycle can repeat, and they're only now starting to develop. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2020)

File.Name: kc27a_kc27-035.jpg
Notes:
50769244637 Here is a colony of dried-out liverworts (Marchantiophyta) on a piece of sandstone that has broken away from the larger outcrop just behind it. They look like alien hieroglyphics. I believe these are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla), and they have a remarkable ability to revive in a matter of hours after being dried out for months. They are real "resurrection plants" - see this photo and this photo for before and after shots. I'm hoping to get another "after shot" tomorrow with our storm that's coming tonight - the first significant rain of the season. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2020)

We did get a few sprinkles this morning, but then it cleared up and a wind picked up from the south. Our storms come from the north, but they pull in clouds from the south in a big counter-clockwise vortex. The clouds hit our east-west mountain wall and rain happens - with orographic enhancement as moist air climbs over the mountains and drops its load. Our biggest storms happen when a storm from the north connects with a "pineapple express" of moist tropical air and just sits over us. These are the storms that give us 80+ inch rain years like 1968-69 and 1982-83. This storm isn't one of those, but I am hoping for an honest inch or two.

File.Name: kc27b_kc27-048.jpg
Notes: 50768334373 This would be the driest start of the rain year in the records for San Marcos Pass if our dry weather would continue until the end of the year, and the drought is taking a toll on our woodland. It hasn't just been dry, but August, September, and October were each the hottest months on record in California. This photo shows a plant of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) in the woods this morning which is looking particularly sad, compare this photo from a few springs ago. Still it seems to be hanging on and will recover with some water. Fortunately rain is coming tonight, and our record will fall before the new year. I thought to document some of the signs of our distressed flora before they are gone. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2020)

File.Name: kc27c_kc27-009.jpg
Notes: 50768309623 This would be the driest start of the rain year in the records for San Marcos Pass if our dry weather would continue until the end of the year, and the drought is taking a toll on our woodland. It hasn't just been dry, but August, September, and October were each the hottest months on record in California. This photo shows a branch of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) that is not looking no good but seems to be hanging on. Fortunately rain is coming tonight, and our record will fall before the new year. I thought to document some of the signs of our distressed flora before they are gone. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2020)

File.Name: kc27d_kc27-005.jpg
Notes: 50769013106 This would be the driest start of the rain year in the records for San Marcos Pass if our dry weather would continue until the end of the year, and the drought is taking a toll on our woodland. It hasn't just been dry, but August, September, and October were each the hottest months on record in California. This photo shows a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) where the dead growth outnumbers the living, but it seems to be hanging on. Fortunately rain is coming tonight, and our record will fall before the new year. I thought to document some of the signs of our distressed flora before they are gone. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2020)

File.Name: kc28a_kc28-004.jpg
Notes:
50773281466 Here are raindrops on a small bunch of cactus spines in the garden today. Most of the rainfall was too heavy to get caught like this, but it finally slowed down to a drizzle in the afternoon and I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2020)

We had rain last night and today that was even better than expected - I was hoping for an inch or two, and we've had 3-1/2 inches so far! We've been on track to have the driest September-December period in the records for San Marcos Pass, but this good rain today has pushed us into eleventh place - a record I'm happy to lose. I started to take a walk today, but the continuing drizzle turned me around. I'll try again tomorrow to see how our liverworts are doing, see this photo from yesterday.

File.Name: kc28b_kc28-001.jpg
Notes: 50773356192 Here is another photo taken today of the same distressed plant of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) that I showed in this photo yesterday. We've had good rain since then, 3-1/2 inches so far last night and today, and it's still coming down! Is it my imagination or are the plants already looking a bit happier? (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2020)

File.Name: kc28c_kc27-007.jpg
Notes: 50773207841 This would be the driest start of the rain year in the records for San Marcos Pass if our dry weather were to continue until the end of the year, and the drought has been taking a toll on our woodland. It hasn't just been dry, but August, September, and October were each the hottest months on record in California. This photo shows a small shrub of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) in the woods which is looking particularly sad, and I'm not sure if it will recover. Normally I find green buds at the base of the old leaves like in this photo, but this plant has none. Fortunately we finally had good rain last night and today, 3-1/2 inches so far, and it's still coming down! It will be interesting to see if this shrub revives after the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2020)

File.Name: kc28d_kc27-003.jpg
Notes: 50773296747 This would be the driest start of the rain year in the records for San Marcos Pass if our dry weather were to continue until the end of the year, and the drought has been taking a toll on our woodland. It hasn't just been dry, but August, September, and October were each the hottest months on record in California. This photo shows a shrub of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) in the woods this morning which is looking particularly sad, and I'm not sure if it will recover. Fortunately we finally had good rain last night and today, 3-1/2 inches so far, and it's still coming down! It will be interesting to see if this shrub revives after the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2020)

File.Name: kc29a_kc29-011.jpg
Notes:
50777669327 Here is another small but perfectly formed Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) in its usual place on an old faded flower of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) in the woods today - with a few raindrops. I've been finding these predators in the same place for most of the month, compare this photo from December. They are active - they move around between the flowers and sometimes fly off and then return. I wonder if this one stayed out in our good rain yesterday?? (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2020)

Today was chilly but sunny and dry after our rainy day yesterday. I was looking for mushrooms and banana slugs today, but I guess it's still too soon. This Robber Fly was the only insect I saw today, and I already knew where it was. We ended up with 3-1/2 inches of rain from our storm which brings our total up to 3-3/4 inches, still only about 40% of normal. It was a good rain, but that's how bad our drought has been since September. There's a rumor of more rain next week.

File.Name: kc29b_kc29-002.jpg
Notes: 50777499851 I found this odd scene on a soil embankment in the woods today - a pupal cocoon of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae, Lepidoptera) that seems to be incorporated into some webbing of (probably) a spider - or is it caterpillar webbing?? See this photo from a week ago for more info on the interesting cocoon and what it's made from. I didn't appreciate how odd this was when I got the photo, and I'll try to get back tomorrow for a closer look. Note the green moss on the soil that revives as quickly as the liverworts in my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2020)

File.Name: kc29c_kc29-023.jpg
Notes: 50777457706 A couple of days ago I posted this photo of dried-out liverworts (Marchantiophyta) on a piece of sandstone that has broken away from the larger outcrop just behind it. Here is the same rock again with green liverworts just two days later. I believe these are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla), and they have a remarkable ability to revive in a matter of hours after being dried out for months. These are smaller and (sometimes) more ribbon-like than the different liverworts in my [Previous] photo. They will stay green as long as the porous sandstone rock holds moisture, then they will dry out and wait for rain to do it all over again. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2020)

File.Name: kc29d_kc29-001.jpg
Notes: 50777400736 This is a bank of newly revived liverworts (Marchantiophyta) growing on sandstone in the woods today. I believe these are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica) - larger with a more definite shape than the different liverwort in my [Next] photo. These liverworts fade to black as they dry out and recover in a day with some rain, compare this photo which may actually be the same colony. In time they will develop little sexual sporophytes, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2020)

File.Name: kc30a_kc30-023.jpg
Notes:
50780773443 Here are winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) on the pea gravel near our mailbox today. A small swarm of termites was emerging from somewhere under a nearby Buckwheat shrub that I never could find. This wasn't a particularly large swarm, compare this photo and this photo from years past. Rain yesterday, termites today - I guess that's how they synchronize their swarms with other termite colonies so that they'll all emerge at the same time, thereby increasing the chance of mating across swarms to form new colonies. Maybe these are Subterranean Termites in the family Rhinotermitidae? I don't believe they damage houses, at least these are far away from any house. I seem to find them in both spring and autumn. Birds love the swarms - little flying hors d'oeuvres. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2020)

Today was sunny but chilly again. There are still no signs of mushrooms or banana slugs, and insects are still scarce - except for the termites. The wind started to pick up this afternoon, and we're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight and tomorrow. There's still a rumor of more rain next week.

File.Name: kc30b_kc30-012.jpg
Notes: 50781453181 Here are three different shots of the same tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) that I've shown before in this photo and this photo that I got in the woods today. This spider is tiny, just 740 pixels across in these macro shots, which comes to 3.1 mm - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Its web was knocked down by our recent rain, so the spider is just hanging out by its old nest in this plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) until it can rebuild. Many people who lost homes in the wildfires this summer are in the same situation. I only noticed this tiny spider in this place today because I've found it here before. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2020)

File.Name: kc30c_kc30-007.jpg
Notes: 50780657343 This is somebody's nest under a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure who's nest it is, but I would guess it's the work of a spider by the way strands of webbing are pinned down to the leaf with a little gob of goo - something I've seen with other spider nests. Spider nest or not, I believe this does count as webbing, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2020)

File.Name: kc30d_kc25-004.jpg
Notes: 50781496397 This is webbing between dead-looking leaves of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). This is more webbing that looks like rigging on a dusty model ship, compare this photo of similar webbing on a different shrub. I believe this is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae, and I have a shot of the tiny spider associated with such webbing in this photo from last spring. I'm still hoping these dead-looking shrubs will revive after our recent rain, and I can see a green leaf bud on this stem even before the rain. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2020)

File.Name: kc31a_kc31-001.jpg
Notes:
50784758238 This half-eaten two-tone oak leaf caught my eye in the woods today. The way the leaf is eaten away from the main veins reminds me of the work of a California Sister caterpillar like the one in this photo, but it's subtly different in that the veins are not reinforced with silk and frass - it must be the work of something else. I can't explain why the leaf is half dead, but I like the effect. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2020)

It was cold and windy today with 20-30 mph gusts for most of the day and wind chill to match. I had an errand in the morning, and it was still blowing when I got home. I did my duty went out for a few photos. Happy New Year and good riddance to the last year.

File.Name: kc31b_kc31-004.jpg
Notes: 50784683413 I found this odd "folded leaf" on a small oak in the woods today. It looks like the "rolled leaf galls" that I find on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I believe are caused by aphids, see this photo, but this one is odd because it's growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) that is quite different. Maybe the aphids got confused, or maybe the folds hold something altogether different like caterpillars? I found several of these today on this one particular shrub. I really should open up more of these galls, though I hate to disturb whatever is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2020)

File.Name: kc31c_kc29-019.jpg
Notes: 50784624363 Here is a stem gall on a branch of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by our still-dry creek. I'm not sure whose gall it is, or even whether it's a sawfly or a gall midge - oak galls get all the attention. Hah, Ron Russo who wrote the book on western galls quotes a colleague as saying: "We would need two more lifetimes to sort out all the gall insects on willows." This small tree still has most of its leaves, but they've turned yellow and are falling as I write this - and new buds on the stem are all ready to replace them. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2020)

File.Name: kc31d_kc27-018.jpg
Notes: 50785360521 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) was hiding in a leaf cluster of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in the woods a few days ago. I reckon its another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy abdomen. It really didn't want to be disturbed and quickly exited - showing me its rear end on the way. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2020)

File.Name: j101a_j101-004.jpg
Notes:
32690123668 Hah - here is a two-headed mushroom in the woods! This is another Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus mushroom, see this photo from the same place. It even has a tell-tale spot of white mold on the left side, a sign of the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus which seems to prefer this mushroom above all others, see this photo for more info. These mushrooms start with minutely velvety brown caps, like baking potatoes. The brown fuzz seems to wear off, revealing reddish caps underneath. They have yellow stems which become red below, with a "pinched" base - even this odd one! They have large irregular yellow pores which stain blue when bruised, you can see some bluing on the right. They are said to be edible but not choice. I haven't tried them, mostly because they are nearly always parasitized by the Bolete Eater which is not very appetizing. Even a slight infection like this is like finding a spot of mold on your bread. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2018)

Today was sunny and cold, and thankfully not windy. There's a NWS Freeze Warning for the Santa Ynez Valley tonight, and even a Frost Advisory for Santa Barbara on the coast! There seems to be growing confidence in rain this weekend. I'm finding a few mushrooms now, and rain will bring more. I'll keep photographing them as long as I find them!
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; Hypomyces chrysospermus
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; Hypomyces chrysospermus

File.Name: j101b_j101-013.jpg
Notes: 45839612824 This is a cluster of Western Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. (*Sigh* The tree is probably drought-dead.) There's more of them in the shadows. These mushrooms are quite poisonous, so don't eat them. I can't imagine why anyone would confuse them with edible Chanterelle mushrooms when they are massed like this, but I've been fooled for a moment by solitary specimens growing from buried wood. The possible greenish tint, rubbery texture, and sharp gill edges should settle it. These mushrooms are slightly bioluminescence - see this interesting photo by Michelle C. Torres-Grant. I still want to try that! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2019)

File.Name: j101c_ic31-022.jpg
Notes: 46511430242 I believe these are Vulgar Milk Cap (Lactarius argillaceifolius var. megacarpus) mushrooms growing under (mostly) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. They should have white latex when you scratch the gills, which gives it away as a Lactarius, but these specimens are old and dry with no milk. (You can see where I scratched the gills on the right.) The mushrooms are nearly the size of dinner plates - I put my car keys in the photo for scale. My book says they are possibly poisonous, but they don't really look very appetizing anyway. I have another photo of fresher specimens from a few years ago here. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2018)
CommonNames: Vulgar Milk Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j101d_ic31-043.jpg
Notes: 31622342647 Here is a different colony of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Hypholoma fasciculare aka Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from buried wood in a thicket of honeysuckle and blackberry, compare my [Previous] photo. You can see that the mushrooms in the back have turned dark from the falling spores in a natural spore print. These purple-gray spores are a mark of the fungus family Strophariaceae. The mushrooms are said to be poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I wish all poisonous mushrooms were so obliging! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2018)

File.Name: j102a_j102-001.jpg
Notes: 46526651412 Here is another remarkably pallid Cortinarius mushroom growing under a large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree close to home. This one might be Cortinarius regalis, see this photo of a similar mushroom. You can clearly see the mushroom's "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills when young. They tear away from the cap as the mushroom matures, and hang from the stem - usually decorated with the rusty-red spores that fall from the gills. I have more photos of this structure here and here. These are actually one of my favorite mushrooms when they are young and fresh like this, less so when they get old and dingy and all look the same. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2019)

File.Name: j102b_j101-017.jpg
Notes: 45854555674 Today was sunny and cold again, and thankfully not very windy. It does feel a few degrees warmer tonight, and the NWS Freeze Warning for the Santa Ynez Valley seems to have been lifted. Our chance of rain this weekend is now "likely" for Saturday night, and another Pacific storm system could move into the area next week! This is a plain white Russula mushroom growing under (mostly) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. Maybe it is the Boring White Russula (Russula albidula), as author David Arora calls it - but there are other white Russulas like Russula cremoricolor just to confuse us. This one is creamy white, with a viscid cap after a rain, and a peppery radish-like taste that's not unpleasant - at least in a small nibble. (That might count against both of my proposed species which are both said to have an acrid taste.) One test for Russulas as a group is to try breaking the stem - if it snaps like chalk, it's a Russula. Really! (Looking at my photo, I might have broken the stem digging it out - that's bad form.) This one is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and is the first and only one I've found so far this year. There are a couple of blades of dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) framing the mushroom, as well as some stems of California Bedstraw (Galium californicum, Rubiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Boring White Russula; Coast Live Oak; Roundfruit Sedge; California Bedstraw
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula albidula; Russula cremoricolor; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula albidula; Russula cremoricolor; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae

File.Name: j102c_ic31-029.jpg
Notes: 31637581007 Here is a bank of new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing with moss on a sandstone outcrop. My mom used to call these Resurrection Ferns because they seem to appear from nowhere when the rains arrive. They actually grow from a thick mass of rhizomes at the base of the fronds, but that gets obscured by leaves and debris that collect during our dry summer when they go dormant. These ferns are creatures of our most shady woods. I found these ferns (and some of the mushrooms I've been showing) in shady Kinevan Canyon, a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2018)

File.Name: j102d_ic27-027.jpg
Notes: 46578464251 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) in a flowering shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. These orb-weaving spiders make a proper orb web (left) and then build a little retreat off to the side held up by cobweb-type webbing (right). In my photo, the spider's retreat is that yellow leaf suspended to the upper-right from the orb web. There is always an all-important strand of webbing stretched tight between the retreat and the orb web that the spider uses to sense activity which you can see clearly in my photo, see this photo for a closer look. The spider seems to be having luck, as I can see several small flies caught in the orb web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2018)

File.Name: j103a_j103-011.jpg
Notes:
32719032198 I found this little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) buzzing around the vertical face of a sandstone outcrop down by the creek today. I first thought that it was a Bee Fly like this one. Both of these critters like to hover in front of the rock, but this bee also landed repeatedly and seemed to be taking moisture or minerals from the stone - it was "puddling" like a butterfly. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, but I guess it is in the family Apidae. I'm also not sure what it's doing out on a cold winter morning like this, but here it is. I figure the photo on the right is a pretty good shot of the bee's behind - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2019)

I was a few degrees warmer today - warm enough that our cold-grounded dragonfly finally left its perch after six days, see this photo from December 29. We're still on track for rain this weekend and maybe a stronger storm next week.

File.Name: j103b_j103-020.jpg
Notes: 32718862698 Here are strange flowers and new berries of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. tomentosum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The berries have a glow about them. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, and I made a stop along the way to get this photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 3 January 2019)

File.Name: j103c_j102-022.jpg
Notes: 46593070891 Some critter has rolled this oak leaf up like a cigar! The tree is Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and I guess that this is the work of a caterpillar of some sort. I found another one just like it a few feet away on a different branch. I wounder how any critter could do this? (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2019)

File.Name: j103d_ic21-013.jpg
Notes: 46592962111 Here are two shots of a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the middle of its web. I believe this one is a male because of its enlarged palps, visible on the right. I got these photos more than a week ago, but I never got around to posting them. The spider seems proud of its rear end, sticking it up in the air like that - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2018)

File.Name: j104a_j104-004.jpg
Notes:
31666456307 Here is "just a fly" on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm pretty sure this one is another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. I always have trouble focusing on the front of something that is looking at me, but this shot looks pretty good. I should add this to the Fly Day Friday group, but I already have my two photos for the week - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2019)

There was a steady breeze this morning which made getting my kind of photos difficult, but this one will do for a real "Fly Day Friday". We're still on track for rain tomorrow - then another chance of rain for Sunday - and then another chance of rain for next Wednesday! The NWS Forecast Discussion has it in their usual understated way that "January 2019 has the makings for an above average precipitation month in southwest California." Very good!

File.Name: j104b_j102-020.jpg
Notes: 39642371313 Here is a tiny mosquito-sized fly hanging dead from the style of a manzanita flower, with another flower behind. The flowers belong to native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure that the fly is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae - the round head and flattened proboscis (in another photo) are my field marks. I find these flies on willow flowers later in the spring, but they are also predators of other insects - see this photo for both strategies. I'm not sure if the one in my new photo is after nectar or prey. I'm also not sure what led to its demise. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2019)

File.Name: j104c_j101-020.jpg
Notes: 45883122254 This is "just a fly" perched for a moment on a narrow leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's an attractive fly with a hint of blue (or blue dun) colors. I have no idea what kind it is, though I probably have another shot of the same kind of fly in this photo from last week. I should add this to the Fly Day Friday group, but I already have my two photos for the week - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2019)

File.Name: j104d_ic29-028.jpg
Notes: 39641940393 I found this very strange fly on a plant of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It was a cold day with contrasty light so I had to use a bit of "slow flash" to get this shot. I wasn't even sure if the fly was alive when I found it, but it did finally turn to look at me. I suspected that it had a fungus infection (like this) because its face looks so strange. The antennae look like they belong to a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) which sent me to BugGuide. And sure enough, I think I found it - genus Myopa of that group, see the images at BugGuide here. One thing that had me confused is that my fly seems to have no proboscis whereas Conopidae flies usually have one that is prominent, like this. BugGuide has several images of these flies with no visible proboscis, so I figure they can keep it folded under their large heads. What a great fly - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2018)

File.Name: j105a_j105-035.jpg
Notes:
46570925502 Here is another California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) making its way through the woods, leaving a slime trail behind as it goes. This one is smaller than some, only 4-5 inches long. They are something that the rain brings, though I found this one on my morning walk today before the rain started. It's curious that I also found mushrooms today - before the rain. Do they all know it's coming?? More likely I was just looking for them with rain on my mind. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. I want to photograph every one I find. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2019)

Our rain today held off until the late afternoon, but then it came down pretty good for a while. We've had about 8/10 inch so far, and I'm hoping there's still more coming. We're under a NWS flood advisory right now, and that's worth paying attention to because this is almost the anniversary of the Montecito debris flow that took lives last year on January 9. We seem to be in a wet pattern right now because we also have a chance of more rain tomorrow, Wednesday, and next weekend - bring it on!

File.Name: j105b_j105-017.jpg
Notes: 39658201813 Well I'll be darned - I found a Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushroom in the woods today during my walk this morning! Its small, only about two inches tall, but its my first for the year. I found it in the same place I found this one last year and this one the year before. (Always remember where you find them - they will be back!) They should be large mushrooms, the size of your open hand, with blunt ridges instead of proper gills. This one is a baby, but I'm still happy to find it - for the table of course, but also because it's a sign that the fungus mycelium has survived seven years of drought in the ground as mycorrhiza on the roots of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These mushrooms are edible and delicious, a local favorite. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2019)
ScientificName: Cantharellus californicus; mycelium; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j105c_j105-011.jpg
Notes: 39658005033 I guess these are the Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) mushrooms in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They look beautiful and edible, but they taste terrible - with a strong phenolic odor and taste, like an old fashioned doctor's office. Don't eat them! All parts of the mushroom stain bright yellow as you handle it, including the very base of the stem. They also have a thick skirt-like ring on the stalk, with patches of tissue on the underside. The gills appear white at first, but they will turn chocolate-brown as the spores mature. This mushroom and the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis, photo here) are the reason I'm wary of all our woodland Agaricus mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2019)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Agaricus; Coast Live Oak; Felt-Ringed Agaricus
ScientificName: Agaricus xanthodermus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus hondensis
associatedTaxa: Agaricus xanthodermus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus hondensis

File.Name: j105d_j105-005.jpg
Notes: 46622760291 Here are two shots of a pink-spored Entoloma mushroom in the woods this morning, growing under (mostly) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They have a chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool, so I guess they are what David Arora calls the "Nitrous Entoloma" (Entoloma nidorosum) or kin - at least that's a convenient tag for them. Other species in our mountains have dark caps and/or lack the chlorine smell. Not edible, and you wouldn't want to try them because of the bleach-like smell. They are handsome though! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2019)
ScientificName: Entoloma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Entoloma nidorosum
associatedTaxa: Entoloma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Entoloma nidorosum

File.Name: j106a_j106-008.jpg
Notes:
45725623115 I found this dragonfly today clinging to a stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's been grounded by inclement weather - our good storm last night. It might well be the same dragonfly I showed in this photo a week ago, though it's a different shrub maybe 30 feet away from the previous one. I believe this is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. I usually find these dragonflies hanging onto plants on cold or wet days in winter like today. Except for the days when they are grounded, these predators seem to be active all winter long looking for prey in sunny places. Despite its bright colors, they are well camouflaged - I'll bet I've walked right past them without noticing on many cold winter days. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2019)

Rain, rain, rain - the storm door is open! We ended up with an inch of rain last night, and now it's raining again with a new storm that might deliver another inch or two overnight. Our mid-week storm is now being deprecated and may not deliver, but our storm next weekend is sounding stronger. I try not to get too excited about storm forecasts a week out - but our rainfall total has dropped down to 80% of normal, and all this activity is encouraging after too many drought years.
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Variegated Meadowhawk

File.Name: j106b_j106-003.jpg
Notes: 46587805782 This is a bolete mushroom made unrecognizable by the parasitic "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that favors these mushrooms. The mushroom was probably the Oak-loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus), but who can tell? The first stage of an infection is a white moldy-looking growth, visible in my [Previous] photo. This is followed by a yellow powdery stage. Then there is a final reddish-orange terminal stage which is visible in my photo, by which time the mushroom's flesh softens and becomes putrescent. Apparently, the white and yellow stages produce asexual spores, and only in the final reddish stage are spores sexually produced (as ascospores). A different Hypomyces fungus is responsible for the edible "Lobster Mushrooms" that we sometimes find in Oregon, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2019)
CommonNames: bolete; Bolete Eater; Oak-loving Bolete; Lobster Mushrooms
ScientificName: Hypomyces chrysospermus; Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; ascospores; Hypomyces
associatedTaxa: Hypomyces chrysospermus; Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; ascospores; Hypomyces

File.Name: j106c_j106-001.jpg
Notes: 39674809933 Here are more reddish-capped bolete mushrooms with pores instead of gills. They might be Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus mushrooms like the one in this photo or this photo, but to my mind they look subtly different - no brown on the cap, the stem is not yellow on top and red below, and the stem is not pinched on the base. They might be something else like the Cracked-cap Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron aka Xerocomellus chrysenteron). Whatever they are, you can see a tell-tale spot of white mold on the exposed pores, a sign of the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus in my [Next] photo. I found these growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2019)
CommonNames: bolete; Cracked-cap Bolete; Bolete Eater; Coast Live Oak; California Bay

File.Name: j106d_j105-037.jpg
Notes: 45724956075 Here are a pair of Slippery Jack (Suillus) mushrooms growing under native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family. The mushrooms have pores rather than gills, caps that would be slimy after a rain but are now dry, gland-dotted stems, and no sign of a veil. It's the gland-dotted stems that distinguish them from other boletes. There are many different kinds - none are poisonous as far as I know, but they are soft and slimy which does not sound very appetizing. I got this photo yesterday before the rain came. Some critter has been nibbling on the specimen to the left. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2019)

File.Name: j107a_j107-030.jpg
Notes:
45931629274 Here is today's shot of the dragonfly I found yesterday clinging to a stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's been grounded by inclement weather - another good storm last night. I showed it in this photo, and it might well be the same dragonfly I showed in this photo a week ago in a different shrub maybe 30 feet away. I believe this is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. In this photo, I'm looking almost straight down at the dragonfly which is parked with wings pointing up and down. It's a good view of its distinctive white and yellow racing stripes on the side. I don't know how long he'll be here, but I'll get another shot if it's still there tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2019)

Rain, rain, rain - the storm door is indeed open! We ended up with 2.87 inches of rain last night, bringing our total for the weekend to almost four inches. We're back to (slightly) above normal rainfall for the year, and there's more rain coming. Our mid-week storm is now considered likely, and our storm next weekend is sounding like another good rain producer. There's even a rumor about more rain the week after. All this rain activity is encouraging after too many drought years.
Locality: slightly) above normal rainfall for the year

File.Name: j107b_j107-036.jpg
Notes: 32781812008 Here are brand-new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family - with raindrops from our good storm last night. This particular shrub is growing near our mailbox, and I just happened to be there when the sun poked through the clouds for a few minutes this afternoon - the only direct sunlight we had all day. The new flowers aren't always pink like this, but it's a plus when they are. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2019)

File.Name: j107c_j107-022.jpg
Notes: 46656345891 I found these yellow Cortinarius mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees after our good rain last night. I don't believe they grew overnight, but the rain brought them up out of the mulch. Maybe this one is Cortinarius xanthodryophilus - see the species page and photos at Mykoweb. This one is considered to be subgenus Phlegmacium with a swollen base and not subgenus Bulbopodium with a "rimmed base" - a distinction that (maybe) makes sense if you compare this photo. The caps are viscid when wet like this - they are hard to pick up! I've called this one Cortinarius fulmineus in the past, but I reckon it's the same thing. Edibility is unknown. The problem is that all these Cortinarius mushrooms end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2019)

File.Name: j107d_j107-003.jpg
Notes:
45931048534 Here are three different kinds of Jelly Fungus (Tremelliales) growing on dead oak wood. The one on the left is a "gelatinous blob" of Witch's Butter. The traditional name for this is Tremella mesenterica, but it sounds like Tremella aurantia is a better name. The two differ microscopically and in host preference. The second one is a brown version of the first one, maybe Brown Witch's Butter (Tremella foliacea) - at least it's brown and looks like Witch's Butter, though I'm not sure of the name. The final one on the right is Black Witch's Butter (Exidia glandulosa). All of these dry up into almost nothing between the rains, but they're looking good now after our storm last night. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2019)

(Update - that Brown Witch's Butter is certainly not Tremella foliacea - compare this photo of he real thing. It may be that the one in this photo is Exidia thuretiana which is related to the jelly fungus in this photo but without the mineral inclusions. There's more info at Wikipedia.)
CommonNames: Jelly Fungus; Witch's Butter; Brown Witch's Butter; Black Witch's Butter; Brown Witch's Butter
ScientificName: Tremelliales; Tremella mesenterica; Tremella  aurantia; Tremella foliacea; Exidia glandulosa; Tremella foliacea; Exidia thuretiana
associatedTaxa: Tremelliales; Tremella mesenterica; Tremella  aurantia; Tremella foliacea; Exidia glandulosa; Tremella foliacea; Exidia thuretiana

File.Name: j108a_j108-014.jpg
Notes:
39706046083 Here are the first new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family that I've seen this year - with a tiny wasp! The tree is related to sassafras and avocado and has similar flowers. They are surprisingly intricate, though the flowers in my photo aren't quite fully open yet - compare this photo and notice the stamens. I don't know what to say about the wasp except that it's really tiny - about 300 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.2 mm, less than 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I'll get another photo of the flowers when they are fully open and in the sun, but I love that tiny wasp! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2019)

We had a break from the rain today, but there was a breeze mostly from the south - our usual "rain wind". We have even odds of a small rain tomorrow, and then "rain likely" on Friday and Sunday - and even more rain next week? All this rain activity is encouraging after too many drought years.

File.Name: j108b_j108-008.jpg
Notes: 46618281822 This is the cortina of cobwebby hairs that initially covers and protects the gills of the slimy Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroom in my [Previous] photo, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. These slimy mushrooms also have a "slime veil" that covers and protects the gills that's visible on the right. In time the cortina fibers will tear free and hang from the stem as a ring of sorts, but in the meantime they get stained rusty-brown by the falling spores of the mushroom. See my [Previous] photo for more info about this mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2019)
CommonNames: cortina; Banded Cortinarius; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j108c_j108-007.jpg
Notes: 46670624691 Here is a Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. More properly, it is part of the Cortinarius collinitus group which has been split into several variants. Maybe it is really Cortinarius trivialis, but the old name is familiar and works for me. They are very slimy mushrooms with the remains of a universal slime veil on the cap and stem. Both cap and stem are slimy, which places them in the subgenus Myxacium. You can see remnants of the "cortina" hairs on the stem below the cap, stained rusty-brown from falling spores - see my [Next] photo for a closer look. It's strange and beautiful, one of my favorites. The green grass-like leaves in my photo are the dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) - also one of my favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2019)
CommonNames: Banded Cortinarius; Coast Live Oak; Roundfruit Sedge
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius collinitus; Cortinarius trivialis; Myxacium; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius collinitus; Cortinarius trivialis; Myxacium; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae

File.Name: j108d_j108-010.jpg
Notes: 45755387535 This is a brand new earthstar "mushroom" just coming up in the woods along our road. I added scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy. I guess this is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus), though it seems larger and more patterned that that species usually is. Earthstars from this particular place just are more ornate - I've shown them before e.g. here and here and here. These fungi are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make the star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity, which effectively lifts the spore sack off the ground. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. They look like something that crawled from the sea and stopped here! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2019)

File.Name: j109a_j109-012.jpg
Notes:
45768757525 I'm smiling because there's water flowing in San Jose Creek as it wends through Kinevan Canyon up here in the mountains! There's not a lot of water yet, but it's a start - and there are signs that the water was higher during the good rain last weekend. It's remarkable how quickly the water in the creeks drops when the heavy rain stops, and watch out for the floods when the rain starts! The next good rain might do it right, see this photo of the same creek after our good rains last March. We even had a trickle in our creek at home after the weekend. This isn't the day to be talking about floods though, as today is the anniversary of the Montecito debris flow that took 20+ lives last year on January 9. We actually had nearly as much rain this weekend as we had during the mud flows, but this time it all soaked in instead of running off. What a difference. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2019)

We did get a bit of rain today, just 0.14 inches of slow soaking rain, the kind that's good for mushrooms and other creatures the rain brings. Our next storm comes on Friday night with a 100% probability right now, and this might be the strongest storm yet. There's still talk of more rain next week. All this rain activity is encouraging after too many drought years . . . but watch out for floods!

File.Name: j109b_j109-036.jpg
Notes: 39718799783 Here are tiny sporangia (or plasmodiocarps?) on a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I also found something similar on fallen leaves on the ground today - see the note on my [Previous] photo and see this photo from a few years ago. In the past I've called these Diderma effusum, but I'm really not sure. They have a texture like eggshell if you look close, which suggests that they have a calcareous outer layer - which matches this species. Hah, it almost looks like they're trying to organize into script - now that would be a good sci-fi story! (Sounds crazy? See this photo/video on Flickr of a slime mold running a maze!) I'll have another photo of the whole branch tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2019)

File.Name: j109c_j109-005.jpg
Notes: 32808333048 This is a group of fairly large Tricholoma mushrooms with "notched gills" and a "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. Another photo shows that they develop extensive reddish-brown stains on the gills. I believe they are Tricholoma dryophilum, though I used to regard them as part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group. These are large hefty mushrooms, with a deep stalk, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. Edibility is unknown, not recommended for the table. I wouldn't be interested anyway with that farinaceous smell and taste. See this photo of probably the same mushrooms. I didn't notice at the time, but I think you can see the same sort of Slime Mold I show in my [Next] photo - look on the leaves below the mushroom on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2019)
ScientificName: Tricholoma; Tricholoma dryophilum; Tricholoma pessundatum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Tricholoma; Tricholoma dryophilum; Tricholoma pessundatum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j109d_j106-005.jpg
Notes: 46683389051 There aren't many spider webs in the woods with all the rain we've been having lately, but I did find this one across a little depression in a sandstone outcrop by the creek. The strands are decorated with tiny drops - too small to be raindrops. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2019)

File.Name: j110a_j110-022.jpg
Notes:
46643161672 Here are new leaves of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - with raindrops. We had light rain yesterday and no wind since, which is just right for the palmate leaves to hold drops. If it rains any harder, all the drops get knocked off. This lupine is a perennial that is just starting to come up for the new year. Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know. Why do some plants hold rain drops so well, while others shed them so fast? The other green grass-like leaves on the left are the dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) that is common in our woodland. I know that lupines with raindrops is almost a photo clichÈ, and I'm a sucker every time! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2019)

Today was sunny and calm with some clouds from the south. It's curious that our storms are usually followed by wind - but sometimes not. Our next storm is expected on Friday night, and it might be a good one with 2-3 inches expected. Our extended forecast is starting to look like Oregon with a good chance of rain every day at least until next Thursday! All this rain activity is encouraging after too many drought years.
ScientificName: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae

File.Name: j110b_j110-002.jpg
Notes: 45780564925 This is a cute little Cup Fungus (Pezizaceae or kin) growing on old wood chips that are all that's left of a large oak tree that came down across a neighbor's road a few years ago, see this photo. It looks like a proper fairy tub! Maybe this odd mushroom is the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda or kin) which is said to grow on wood and wood chips. It's an inch or two across. The cups expand and become wavy as they age. (Don't we all.) There is no stalk and no ribs at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2019)
CommonNames: Cup Fungus; Spreading Brown Cup Fungus

File.Name: j110c_j109-020.jpg
Notes: 45970976374 Here are a pair of odd black fungi on the dark bark of a fallen oak limb. That gooey mass on the left is Black Witch's Butter (Exidia glandulosa), a Jelly Fungus in the family Tremelliales. It's right next to an odd Crampball (Daldinia grandis aka Annulohypoxylon thouarsianum) fungus that looks and feels like a lump of charcoal. I can't explain the name, but it's also known as King Alfred's Cakes. It takes all kinds! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2019)
CommonNames: Black Witch's Butter; Jelly Fungus; Crampball
ScientificName: Exidia glandulosa; Tremelliales; Daldinia grandis; Annulohypoxylon thouarsianum

File.Name: j110d_j104-007.jpg
Notes: 46642689442 This is looking at the rear end of a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. If you look close in the shadows, you can maybe see more of its private parts than you want to! Insect photos have been scarce in this wet week, but I at least got this one for . . . HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2019)

File.Name: j111a_j111-002.jpg
Notes:
45792948675 Here are tiny sporangia of slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) emerging from their plasmodial slime on the stem of a fallen oak leaf. Hah - they remind me of the Hubble Space Telescope image of The Pillars of Creation. I guess both photos are showing the creation of something - stars or spores, different scale but creation nonetheless! Maybe these are the same slime molds I show here and here, but I reckon it's too soon to tell. They are tiny, only about 330 pixels tall in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.3 mm, less than 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I got this photo in the oak mulch above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography, lucky me and lucky for my back and knees! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2019)

We've had no rain yet, but there's a 100% chance of heavy rain starting later tonight. On the other hand, there was a 100% chance of rain this afternoon, and it didn't happen! We've still had all the early signs of a good storm today - wind from the south and a cloud cap forming over the mountains, signs of convection. Storms come from the north but pull clouds from the south. A good soak is expected tonight, maybe two or three inches. All this rain activity is encouraging after too many drought years.

File.Name: j111b_j111-008.jpg
Notes: 46708118291 This is a mass of fungus "hairs" on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm sure they count as rhizomorphs rather than mycelium which is much finer. These hairs might actually be the future stems of Horsehair Fungus (Marasmius androsaceus aka Gymnopus androsaceus aka Setulipes androsaceus), see this photo. Time will tell. I got this photo in the oak mulch above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography, lucky me and lucky for my back and knees! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2019)

File.Name: j111c_j111-037.jpg
Notes: 46655255162 I found this odd "cloverleaf" of mushrooms on a sandstone rock outcrop by a half-buried root. Sorry, I didn't notice what kind of root it is - probably oak or manzanita. I didn't want to break up the pattern, but I found another mushroom further along the root and moved it into the photo. I believe the mushrooms are Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Hypholoma fasciculare aka Naematoloma fasciculare), see this photo. I usually find these mushrooms on dead wood, but maybe the roots are dead - they look pretty ragged. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2019)

File.Name: j111d_j111-032.jpg
Notes: 46654941812 I like mushrooms, but even I have to admit that this one is downright ugly! It's a large bolete-type mushroom with pores rather than gills that resembles "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas aka Rubroboletus eastwoodiae) but lacks a bulbous stalk. It might be Boletus amygdalinus aka Suillellus amygdalinus. The test for this species is to slice the mushroom and see if it has golden flesh that turns blue almost instantly. I didn't remember that test today, but see this photo of an old specimen a few years ago that shows the color reaction. (There's also a nice photo at Mykoweb.) I'll have to get back to this one. I've always thought to avoid eating red-pored boletes, but there are reports that this one may be edible - well, do you feel lucky? And it doesn't look very appetizing. I found this one in a weedy place along West Camino Cielo road, but a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree was nearby. There were quite a few of these mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2019)
ScientificName: Boletus satanas; Rubroboletus eastwoodiae; Boletus amygdalinus; Suillellus amygdalinus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j112a_j112-026.jpg
Notes:
32845614428 Here is another shot of the same cluster of Western Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms that I show in this photo. I think this is a much better photo, taken on a cloudy day rather than sunny, to avoid that contrasty light. I prefer sun for insects and flowers, but I much prefer cloudy skies for fungi. These mushrooms are growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. They are quite poisonous, so don't eat them. See my previous photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2019)

We ended up with 1.6 inches of rain from our storm last night - on the low end of what's expected, but I'm not complaining. We got another 0.15 inch from showers today. The creek is running - just a trickle, but it's flowing. Most of the rain soaked into the ground where it should be. Our next storm is due on Monday. It sounds much like this one, bringing another inch or two. We're actually under a NWS Winter Storm Watch for that storm, but I'm sure the snow will be higher. Our extended forecast is starting to look like Oregon weather with a good chance of rain every day and night until next Friday! All this rain activity is encouraging after too many drought years.

File.Name: j112b_j112-028.jpg
Notes: 39755438593 This is another fairly large Tricholoma mushroom with "notched gills" and a "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. They develop extensive reddish-brown stains on the gills as they age. I believe they are Tricholoma dryophilum, though I used to regard them as part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group. These are large hefty mushrooms, growing in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. Edibility is unknown, not recommended for the table. I wouldn't be interested anyway with that farinaceous smell and taste. See this photo of probably the same mushrooms, though I got that shot in Kinevan Canyon and this one at home. The mushroom in my prestent photo is pushing up a fairly large "mushrump" in the oak mulch. I believe that's David Arora's felicitous word for the mound of leaves that forms when mushrooms push up through the leaves from underneath. That's often all you see of the mushrooms that are developing! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2019)

File.Name: j112c_j112-015.jpg
Notes: 45804911925 Here are a pair of Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. More properly, they are part of the Cortinarius collinitus group which has been split into several variants. Maybe they are really Cortinarius trivialis, but the old name is familiar and works for me. They are very slimy mushrooms. If you look close at the edge of the cap on the mushroom to the right, you can appreciate how thick that slime layer is! You can also see remnants of the "cortina" hairs on the stem below the cap, stained rusty-brown from falling spores - see this photo for a closer look. That is typical for this genus. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2019)

File.Name: j112d_j112-024.jpg
Notes: 45804621915 This is looking under the cap of an Agaricus mushroom showing the partial veil tearing free from the margin of the cap and exposing the gills within. When the veil comes completely free, it hangs from the stalk as a skirt-like ring. The veil membrane of this mushroom is rather thick and felty with brown cottony patches. This mushroom has a sweet almond smell and tastes good in a nibble. I guess it is the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens aka A. moronii), though it only developed weak stains along the margin when I rubbed it. An older specimen did have brownish stains on the cap and no yellow at all. Another possibility is Agaricus silvicola, though that one stains yellow. I suspect they are edible and delicious, but I'm wary of all our woodland species of Agaricus. "Once bitten, twice warned!" (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2019)
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus albolutescens; A. moronii; Agaricus silvicola; Agaricus
