File.Name: j113a_j113-008.jpg
Notes:
46734886011 Here are more tiny slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) emerging from their plasmodial slime on a fallen oak leaf. I reckon it's too soon to tell if the new sporangia will develop stalks or remain sessile, but I like the way they line up along the edges of the leaf. They are tiny, only about 180 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 0.7 mm, less than 1/32 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This is another photo taken in the oak mulch above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography, lucky me and lucky for my back and knees! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2019)

It's looked and felt like rain all day, but we're still waiting for our next storm to arrive. The chance of rain is 100% tomorrow, and rain is likely every day this week until Friday! All this rain has been wonderful, but there are rumors that this will be the end of it for several dry weeks. There's more info in the new post at the California Weather Blog. I hope it rains buckets if we're heading for a dry spell!

File.Name: j113b_j113-020.jpg
Notes: 46734596091 I found yellow, blue, and (almost) white Cortinarius mushrooms in the woods today! All are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees and are about the same size. Maybe the one on the left is Cortinarius xanthodryophilus, and maybe the pallid one with violet gills on the right is Cortinarius regalis - but maybe not. I have separate photos of (probably) these two here and here. I don't know what the blue mushroom in the center is, though I've called it Cortinarius glaucopus in the past - see my [Previous] photo. These mushrooms are all quite striking when young and colorful like this, but it's the fate of all Cortinarius mushrooms to end up quite brown and dull. QuÈ l·stima. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2019)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius xanthodryophilus; Cortinarius regalis; Cortinarius glaucopus
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius xanthodryophilus; Cortinarius regalis; Cortinarius glaucopus

File.Name: j113c_j113-012.jpg
Notes: 45818699505 I'm not sure what Cortinarius species this mushroom is, but it's quite striking when young like this. I found it under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It has a viscid cap and orange cortina hairs on the stem that turn that color by catching the falling spores. Hah, without those hairs I might confuse it with a Blewitt! It has a bulbous base, though I'm not sure if it's bulbous enough to place it in the subgenus Bulbopodium, and this one might be subgenus Phlegmacium. I've put this one in the Cortinarius glaucopus group in the past, but that's just the generic name I give all these blue-colored mushrooms. They start quite blue and pretty but end up quite brown and dull. That's the fate of all Cortinarius mushrooms, and the reason why edibility is unknown for many species including this one. The problem is that all these Cortinarius mushrooms end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart. I would appreciate any information about this mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2019)

File.Name: j113d_j113-025.jpg
Notes: 32858735588 Here is yellow Witch's Butter (Tremella aurantia) growing with some kind of Crust Fungus or Parchment Fungus in the family Stereaceae. The other fungus doesn't look like Stereum hirsutum, but maybe it's Stereum complicatum? The Witch's Butter apparently grows as a parasite on the Stereum, and I almost always find them together. These are growing on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These fungi are a handsome combination. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2019)

File.Name: j114a_j114-002.jpg
Notes:
31805448317 I was taking a follow-up photo of the tiny slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) that I showed yesterday in this photo when a visitor showed up - look in the lower left of my photo. It's a Globular Springtail (Symphypleona, Collembola) - maybe family Dicyrtomidae and genus Ptenothrix? It moved quickly across the dead leaf and disappeared over the edge. The slime molds are less than a millimeter across which gives scale. They look quite different after just one day, solidifying and going from white to gray. They are still developing, but maybe they are Diderma effusum? I'll keep an eye on them for as long as I can find them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2019)

Our storm today pretty much crapped out with just 1/10 inch, though it was blustery all day. Now the forecast is for heavy rain on Tuesday and again on Thursday. There's a NWS Flash Flood Watch in effect Tuesday afternoon, and I'm sure people below last year's Thomas Fire are paying attention. I hope the new storm delivers more rain than today's storm - but doesn't cause flooding!

File.Name: j114b_j114-016.jpg
Notes: 46746894451 I believe these are tiny Mycena acicula (or kin) mushrooms growing from oak bark by an old woodpile. I included my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo, so the caps are only a few millimeters across. This is the smallest beautiful mushroom that I actually have a name for - the red caps and yellow stems are quite striking. I have more photos here, here, and here. Note that these new ones look a little different with deeper colors and shorter stems - are there different kinds? You have to get down on your hands and knees to see these tiny mushrooms, though I moved these, bark and all, to a place above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2019)

File.Name: j114c_j114-018.jpg
Notes:
46746577921 I found this attractive mushroom growing by itself from an old stump of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I assumed without thinking too much that it was a single Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Hypholoma fasciculare aka Naematoloma fasciculare) mushroom, see this photo, partly because there was another cluster of them nearby. After I picked it, I started thinking it might be some sort of Pluteus mushroom - like the Deer Mushroom (Pluteus cervinus) but yellow instead of brown. Now I'm pretty sure it is indeed a solitary Sulfur Tuft. A spore print will settle the matter, but the mushroom is pretty soggy and might not deliver spores. I'll report tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2019)

I did get a spore print overnight - smoky-gray rather than pink or flesh-color, so it's definitely Sulfur Tuft. It was so strange to find it growing solitary, that I didn't recognize it.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Deer Mushroom; Sulfur Tuft

File.Name: j114d_j114-010.jpg
Notes: 31804831647 These are the same two bolete mushrooms that I showed in this photo from a week ago, now totally enveloped by the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus. The mushrooms are probably Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus, though there's no way of knowing that now! The invading fungus goes through a number of different stages, starting with this white moldy-looking growth. This is followed by a yellow powdery stage, and finally by a reddish-orange final stage which is visible in this photo. It happens fast from the first sign of an infection in my first photo a week ago. I found these growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2019)
CommonNames: bolete; Bolete Eater; Coast Live Oak; California Bay

File.Name: j115a_j112-006.jpg
Notes:
31817142787 Here are two shots of what I believe is a Oak-loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus dryophilus) mushroom growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. This species stains blue when bruised, so you can write messages on the pores for someone else to find after you replant the mushroom - though I believe the color fades after a while. But the reason I'm showing this photo is something else that I didn't notice until I got the photo up on my computer screen - there's a tiny snail at the base of the mushroom on the right! I added a closer view, and I'll make a note. It looks like a tiny version of our "ordinary" land snails, compare this photo. Using the spines of the fallen oak leaves for my ruler, it's only a millimeter or two across. I've found tiny snails in the woods before, including this one riding on a Banana Slug! I couldn't get outside today because of the rain, so I found some recent photos that all show something besides mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2019)

We did get good rain today, over 1.6 inches so far, though it seems to be winding down now. A good rain but not a great rain. That's less than the 2-4 inches that was forecast, but maybe that's just as well coming so near the anniversary of the Montecito debris flow that took 20+ lives last year on January 9. All evacuation orders have now been lifted. Our next storm comes on Wednesday night and Thursday. I hope this one will be more of a great rain.
CommonNames: Oak-loving Bolete; Coast Live Oak; snail

associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j115b_j112-009.jpg
Notes: 46033687594 I was startled to see this earthworm moving through the leaf litter a couple of days ago while I was photographing nearby mushrooms. it's four or five inches long and moved rapidly - much faster than a comparable Banana Slug. I assume that's its head on the left with a segmented structure - at least that's the way it was heading. I was in the woods, so I assume this is a native creature that belongs here. I admit, I rarely see worms and don't know anything about them. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2019)

File.Name: j115c_j109-024.jpg
Notes: 46033494544 Here are tiny slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) on a fallen leaf of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae). I like the way they mostly - but not always - follow the veins and stem of the leaf. I didn't notice until I got this photo up on my computer screen, but there's what looks like a little beetle on the leaf stem in the top-center. I added a closer view, and I'll make a note. I first thought it was be poking its proboscis into a slime mold - but beetles have chewing mouthparts. I think Boris got it right in his comment below, that it's an antenna I see in my photo, and the beetle is likely species Xerasia grisescens which is associated with oaks and favors fungi. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2019)
ScientificName: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Xerasia grisescens
associatedTaxa: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Xerasia grisescens

File.Name: j115d_j107-007.jpg
Notes: 32882802108 Here are odd fungi growing under the bark of dead oak wood. They are small - that "cup fungus" in the top-center is about 1/4 inch across. I'm sure it's some kind of Ascomycete. That white fungus that looks like a maze might be genus Poria aka Perenniporia. There's a tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insect below the cup fungus just to the left of center. There are whole worlds under the bark of fallen tree limbs that we seldom see and don't understand. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2019)

File.Name: j116a_j116-044.jpg
Notes:
31829945917 I've had my eye on this Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushroom for a few days and figured that now is the time to pick it before our possibly heavy rains come tonight. Actually, these mushrooms aren't much affected by the rain, but it was time. It's a large mushroom, the size of your open hand, with blunt ridges instead of proper gills. We sautÈed it with garlic and added it to our pasta tonight, I have a recipe with this photo. Yum! This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2019)

Our new storm started a couple of hours ago, and we already have about 3/4 inch in our rain gauge - but now it's slowed down. It sounds like this will be our last storm for a while, so I hope pours tonight - though not quite enough to cause flooding of course! The NWS is still talking about 2-5 inches of rain in our mountains - IF it happens.

File.Name: j116b_j116-030.jpg
Notes: 31829772487 Here is a little a forest of toothpick-thin Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) fungi growing from the woodland mulch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves. They are pretty common in the dark parts of the woods where the sun never shines, but you have to get down on your hands and knees to find them. A troop like this is not so common. The green plants are California Bedstraw (Galium californicum, Rubiaceae) on the left and California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) on the right. What a wonderful myco-world this is! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2019)
CommonNames: Fairy Hair; Coast Live Oak; California Bedstraw; California Honeysuckle
ScientificName: Macrotyphula juncea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Macrotyphula juncea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: j116c_j116-012.jpg
Notes: 46771228701 I'm pretty sure this is a brilliant Boletus flaviporus aka Aureoboletus flaviporus mushroom that my son found today in deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They have viscid reddish-brown caps and a viscid stem in wet weather at least, but their standout feature is surely their intensely yellow pores that do not stain blue. They are said to be edible, but mediocre. Another specimen that I found was infected by the "Bolete Eater" fungus, see this photo. They are striking mushrooms that are known only from California. I haven't seen one for a few years. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2019)
ScientificName: Boletus flaviporus; Aureoboletus flaviporus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Boletus flaviporus; Aureoboletus flaviporus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j116d_j113-001.jpg
Notes: 39805761373 This is webbing across the opening of a small acorn cup of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - with raindrops. I assume it's the webbing of a spider. I didn't measure the acorn, but it can't be much more than 1/2 inch across. This is another photo taken in the oak mulch above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography, lucky me and lucky for my back and knees - and happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2019)

File.Name: j117a_j117-014.jpg
Notes:
45868250935 I left my macro lens at home today and went for a drive to see what I could find after our good rains last night. I mostly found streams and waterfalls, but this scene also caught my eye - lichens, mosses, ferns, new wildflowers, and even a couple of mushrooms if you look real close, oh my! The ferns are California Polypody (Polypodium californicum, Polypodiaceae). The flowers are Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family, though I don't believe they actually have flowers yet - these are just red leafy bracts, but the flowers will come soon. I'll have to get back to this place with my real macro lens for a closer shot, but I like the way this photo shows the whole scene. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2019)

We did get our good rain last night - buckets of it as I was hoping for! We even had some thunder and lightning. We ended up with 5.87 inches of rain, bringing our total for the water year up to 21.51 inches and 152% of normal! Note that that's still only 64% of our normal total rain for the year, and there's some question about what will happen next. There was a NWS Flash Flood Watch declared late last night and a Flash Flood Warning early this morning. That means that flooding is imminent! The warning was cancelled at 7:15 this morning. I'll bet that certain county officials are breathing easier now that major floods didn't happen - without evacuations.
ScientificName: Polypodium californicum; Polypodiaceae; Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae
associatedTaxa: Polypodium californicum; Polypodiaceae; Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae

File.Name: j117b_j117-001.jpg
Notes: 31841311697 This little waterfall along our driveway rarely flows, but here it is! It falls about 20 feet total, more than this photo shows. It's a small drainage and it takes some heavy rain to get it working. We had almost six inches of rain last night - enough to get our waterfall going. The woodland around the waterfall shows Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees, with an understory of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) and Wild Blackberry and still-dormant Poison Oak and Nuttall's Snowberry. I seem to get this shot whenever the waterfall is going, see this photo from last year, this photo from the year before, and this old photo from 2011 at Fotolog. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; California Bay; Coastal Wood Fern; Wild Blackberry; Poison Oak; Nuttall's Snowberry

File.Name: j117c_j117-043.jpg
Notes:
39817463883 Here are two photos of an impressive waterfall as viewed from along San Marcos Road (the "old pass road") this afternoon. The photo on the left shows the top two sections of the waterfall on the right. If you look close, maybe you can spot the Cottonweed tree between the two falls in both photos. I've sat in that little notch at the top when the falls are dry, but there's no way to get there when they are cranking like this. I don't know a name for these falls, but Jack Eliott calls them Hankins Falls . Darn, I don't even have a name for this creek which I've driven past hundreds of times. I guess it's the West Fork of Maria Ygnacio Creek? There are several of these large waterfalls visible from town after a good rain, but they usually stop flowing soon after the rain stops. This one must have been much larger early this morning. (San Marcos Road, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 January 2019)

If you look close, maybe you can also see that the shrubs on that steep hillside are turning white with new flowers. This is Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that's slowly making it's way up the mountains as it flowers. I haven't seen it on top of the mountains yet, but I haven't got out much in this rainy week, and it might be here now. I have a nice photo of the flowers from last year here.
Locality: Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that's slowly making it's way up the mountains as it flowers. I haven't seen it on top of the mountains yet

File.Name: j117d_j117-008.jpg
Notes: 32907014278 This is the muddy Santa Ynez River flowing around a bend near Manzanita Lane today. It's was no doubt higher in the heavy rain early this morning, but it's still respectable at about 4,000 cfs going into Lake Cachuma further downstream. It's remarkable how the water in all the creeks drops almost immediately when the heavy rain stops. That's still a lot of water going down to Lake Cachuma. The reservoir was about 32.3% full this morning, so it's got a long ways to go. If you look close along the right bank of the river in my photo, you can see winter-bare willow and cottonwood trees. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 January 2019)

File.Name: j118a_j118-040.jpg
Notes:
32919565968 This is a tiny Jumping Spider watching me from a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. These spiders prove that "cute spider" is not an oxymoron! There are many different kinds of these little jumpers that are hard to tell apart, but maybe this one is a female in the genus Pelegrina? I've found them before early in the year, e.g. this photo from last year in February. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2019)

I only found soggy mushrooms today after our good rains yesterday, though I did find more slime molds. They can wait. The sun was out, the breeze was mild, and I actually found some tiny critters to photograph today! I look forward to many more mushrooms in a few days.

File.Name: j118b_j118-017.jpg
Notes: 31853440117 Here are two shots of a gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a new stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I believe it's a Spiny Assassin Bug in the genus Sinea, though maybe not Sinea spinipes as I've called it before. It does have spiny front legs. These are tough-looking predators, though they are shy and retiring in my experience. These photos give a good view of its remarkable proboscis, neatly folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2019)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Sweetpea; Spiny Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Sinea; Sinea spinipes
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Sinea; Sinea spinipes

File.Name: j118c_j118-028.jpg
Notes: 46070066894 This is another goofy-looking net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that I found today on a bristly leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it is genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. This one has clear wings, but I've found another one in the same genus with patterned wings -see this photo. I'm not sure what species this is as it lacks an easy ID feature like spotted wings, but it's still a handsome 'hopper. Hah, it has a face like the grille of a big truck! I'm not sure what this one is doing out in January as I usually find them in late summer and autumn. Even BugGuide has no records from December to May. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2019)

File.Name: j118d_j118-051.jpg
Notes: 46069976004 Here is another early caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on new leaves of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). It's hard to know how to focus of these spiky caterpillars - even using manual focus, which is all I ever use. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example in this photo from last August. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following winter or spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. I found the first one a month ago, see this photo. These seem early, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2019)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: j119a_j119-048.jpg
Notes:
39842522553 This is a somewhat tattered California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family today. These butterflies overwinter as adults and are active on sunny winter days like today. Their cryptic underwings are remarkable. These three shots may look like a nice sequence, but they weren't taken that way. If they look a little fuzzy, it's because they are nearly 1:1 crops from my original photos, I couldn't get closer without disturbing the butterfly. Hah - it's lucky I reduce all my photos to 800 pixels high! I wanted to show a butterfly because I saw my first Blue (Polyommatinae) butterfly today, probably a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo). No photo yet, but I'll be looking for them now that I know they're out there. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2019)

It was sunny and warmer today, this butterfly is proof. It sounds like we're in for a couple of windy days coming up as a little mostly-dry storm trough moves through, though it was quite calm today. It's curious of some storms come through with no wind at all, while others blow like hell!
CommonNames: California Tortoiseshell; Bigberry Manzanita; Blue; Pacific Azure; Echo Azure
ScientificName: Nymphalis californica; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Polyommatinae; Celastrina echo
associatedTaxa: Nymphalis californica; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Polyommatinae; Celastrina echo

File.Name: j119b_j119-027.jpg
Notes: 32931911608 I believe this is a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on a branch of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. Apparently this beetle was introduced from Europe to control aphids and only started to spread in 1973 (
CommonNames: Seven-spotted Lady Beetle; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Coccinella septempunctata; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Coccinella septempunctata; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j119c_j119-007.jpg
Notes: 46807624471 Here are two shots of the same debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera). I got this photo above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography, but it was quite dark in the morning north-slope shade, and I had to follow it around for a while until it decided to pause for a moment so I could get my photos. You can see the critter inside in both photos on the right. They certainly look like predators with those impressive jaws! Apparently these debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter other predators - though I don't know what they find in woods that is so white. It's great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look closer! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2019)

File.Name: j119d_j119-043.jpg
Notes: 32931581758 Here are tiny slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) all in a row of on a dead stick in a pile of (mostly) dead oak leaves. I like their shadows! These slime molds have well developed stalks, unlike some others I've found that seem to be sessile, compare this photo. They also appear to have an outer layer of lime (calcium carbonate) which indicates the order Physarales. Maybe they are the same genus Didymium that I've shown before - see this photo and the comment by Paco Moreno G·mez. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2019)

File.Name: j120a_j120-019.jpg
Notes:
46096143434 This is a hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) munching a seedling of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family - or is it non-native Smooth Vetch (Vicia villosa ssp. varia)? Slugs have teeth?? Well, sort of. They do have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch the leaves and mushrooms that it consumes. They remind me of Dr. Zoidberg from Futurama! I found this one along the road in our canyon today after a week of "banana slug weather". They are something that the rain brings, and it's still a mystery to me how they survive our hot dry summers. I regard them as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2019)

The wind picked up all at once today - it sounded like a truck coming through the canyon! The winds backed off after that initial gust, but clouds moved in for the rest of the day. We have a slight chance of a shower tonight and no chance at all of seeing the eclipse. Funny thing, coming back from my walk today my macro lens suddenly stopped focusing - I mean I can't turn the focus ring. I brought it home and tried exercising it for a while, and then it did work again - and then it didn't. I've had that lens since 2004, and used it almost every day for quite a few years now. It may be time to retire it.
CommonNames: California Banana Slug; Wild Sweetpea; Smooth Vetch
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Vicia villosa; varia
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Vicia villosa; varia

File.Name: j120b_j120-027.jpg
Notes: 45906194835 These are strange antler-like fruiting bodies of the Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin). I don't think the count as "mushrooms" as they are not at all fleshy, but they are fungi. These are growing on old wood chips that are all that's left of a large oak tree that came down across a neighbor's road a few years ago, see this photo. There are many other species that you need a microscope to distinguish, so this ID is really just a placeholder. The white dusting on the black stems is from asexual spores (conidia). Later on they form sexual spores from "perithecia" at the top of the fruiting body. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2019)

File.Name: j120c_j120-062.jpg
Notes: 46768418842 This is a group of beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms poking up through the fallen oak leaves in a place that gets a little soggy and supports Juncus rushes. This may actually be a related species of the mushroom because the cap isn't especially velvety or frosted as they sometimes are? Then again, these specimens may just be old, before the rains last week. The widely-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but brittle - like thin glass tubing. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! If you follow the long stem at the bottom, you can see a copious tuft of leaf mulch held together by white mycelium at the base which is distinctive of these mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2019)

File.Name: j120d_j120-070.jpg
Notes: 46095679404 Here are old and new brown-spored Agaricus mushrooms in deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. This mushroom has a sweet almond smell and tastes good in a nibble. I've been calling it the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens aka A. moronii), and the older specimen does have brownish stains on the cap and no yellow at all. Another possibility is Agaricus silvicola, though that one is said to stain yellow. I suspect they are edible and delicious, but I'm wary of all our woodland species of Agaricus. "Once bitten, twice warned!" The green grass-like leaves in my photo are the dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) that is common in our woodland. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Amber-Staining Agaricus; Roundfruit Sedge
ScientificName: Agaricus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus albolutescens; A. moronii; Agaricus silvicola; Agaricus; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Agaricus albolutescens; A. moronii; Agaricus silvicola; Agaricus; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae

File.Name: j121a_j121-002.jpg
Notes:
46781023702 The last time I was at this place near Lake Cachuma about three weeks ago, there was no sign of these perennial California Peony (Paeonia californica, Paeoniaceae) plants. Now there is already a flower bud! When they finally open, the flowers are quite striking, see this photo from last year. This plant is growing in a place that burned a decade or more ago as a "fire follower" plant that lingers. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The red-tinged foliage is as handsome as the flowers. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, and I made a stop along the way to get a few photos. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2019)

A little storm front moved through last night. We got less than half of 1/10 inch or rain, but the storm packed big winds today. It was really too windy for photos today, but I got a few, mostly close to the ground. For some reason, my macro lens was working fine today. I couldn't turn the focus ring yesterday, and today I can. I wonder what it will be tomorrow? I reckon I'm due for a new lens, but I'd just as soon wait a while before I spring for one.

File.Name: j121b_j121-010.jpg
Notes: 31891691997 Here are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing with mosses on bare ground in once-burned chaparral near Lake Cachuma. I believe there are three different kinds of liverworts in this photo. The large colony in the center is the probably Hairy Crystalwort (Riccia trichocarpa). It's interesting how the "leaves" all seem to be growing from one central place - where the colony started? If you look close, you can also see a different liverwort with flatter darker-green leaves without hairs e.g. in the upper-left of my photo. I'm not sure, but I believe this is a different Riccia species, maybe the Black Crystalwort (Riccia nigrella). Finally, if you look in the lower-left, you can see something that looks like moss - but isn't. I believe that is a genus Fossombronia liverwort. They have a ruffled thallus that looks like lettuce, and sometimes produce tiny lollypop-like sporophytes, see this photo. I like these secretive little plants that hide in plain sight! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2019)
ScientificName: Marchantiophyta; Riccia trichocarpa; Riccia; Riccia nigrella; Fossombronia; thallus
associatedTaxa: Marchantiophyta; Riccia trichocarpa; Riccia; Riccia nigrella; Fossombronia; thallus

File.Name: j121c_j121-005.jpg
Notes: 31891557327 I found quite a few of these small "Little Brown Mushrooms" with depressed caps and decurrent gills that go down the stalk coming up near Lake Cachuma today. I believe they are Omphalina pyxidata or kin. They were growing with the mosses and liverworts and grasses on otherwise bare ground, see my [Next] photo. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, and I made a stop along the way to get this photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2019)

File.Name: j121d_j120-018.jpg
Notes: 46832920791 This is another shot of the hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) munching a legume seedling in the woods. See my [Previous] photo. Slugs don't have teeth, but they have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch the leaves and mushrooms that it consumes. I'm actually most impressed that it stopped eating this plant - which means it somehow pinched off the stem. They remind me of Dr. Zoidberg from Futurama! I regard them as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2019)

File.Name: j122a_j122-041.jpg
Notes:
39880408503 I found this dragonfly today on the bark of a conifer log by our mailbox. I believe it's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. I figure it's a different one than I've been showing, e.g. in this photo, at least it's in a different place. This dragonfly was not grounded by inclement weather - it was flying around, but it kept landing in one place or another. These predators seem to be active all winter long looking for prey in sunny places. They do have some bright color bands, but they are remarkably well camouflaged when at rest. I only noticed this one because I saw it land. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2019)

It was sunny and warm enough today for this dragonfly to be active! There was some residual breeze, but it was not a problem. After all our wonderful rain last week, we might be heading into an extended dry spell. This has something to do with the "Polar Vortex" and the ìNorth American Temperature Dipoleî which I don't claim to understand. I do understand that the NWS missed the boat on our first storm last week that failed to appear despite the forecast. If they can get a forecast wrong for overnight rain, then maybe they're also wrong about the month-long forecast? I admit, there is a difference between overnight weather and long-term trends.

File.Name: j122b_j122-035.jpg
Notes: 46793146902 This is another tiny Jumping Spider watching me from a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. There are many different kinds of these little jumpers that are hard to tell apart, but maybe this one is a female in the genus Pelegrina - in particular, species Pelegrina aeneola. I've found them before early in the year, e.g. this one from a few days ago and this one from last year in February. I think I also photographed a male of the same species, but I'll save that one for Bug Butt Thursday. Sure, this spider is looking right at me. There's something unnerving about making eye contact with such a tiny critter, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2019)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pelegrina; Pelegrina aeneola
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pelegrina; Pelegrina aeneola

File.Name: j122c_j122-011.jpg
Notes: 46792982712 I'm pretty sure these are Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushrooms that I found growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. I sliced the gills to show the white latex "milk" they contain which doesn't much change to yellow. The caps are viscid and slightly zoned with a concentric pattern, visible (in part) on the second mushroom behind the main one on the left. The stems are also slightly "scrobiculate" with darker and lighter spots, which is somewhat visible in my photo. I don't think those drops are raindrops, but rather they are some sort of metabolic discharge. I didn't nibble this one, but I believe it has a spicy "acrid" taste that is not quite pleasant. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2019)
CommonNames: Golden Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j122d_j122-009.jpg
Notes: 31903560577 I found these pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms in the woods today. The gills start pallid, but in time they pick up the spore color like the upturned one. These don't have that chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool that some of these mushrooms have, like this one, so I guess they fall into the "Entoloma rhodopolium" group - possibly Entoloma lividoalbum, but who knows. They are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2019)
ScientificName: Entoloma; Entoloma rhodopolium; Entoloma lividoalbum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Entoloma; Entoloma rhodopolium; Entoloma lividoalbum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j123a_j123-024.jpg
Notes:
45941805665 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - but you have to look close to see it! Most of the red at the top of the plant is just leafy bracts, but there is one real flower poking out of the left side of the stem - I'll make a note. The whole plant looks reddish, and that may be because they are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place, though I also see plants under Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to the roots of their host species. They come up mushroom-like, all ready to flower, see this photo from earlier this year. This is the first flower of these plants that I've found this year, and also the first wildflower of any plant that isn't a tree or shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2019)

I was hoping to find new mushrooms in the place I went today, but I only found the one Russula in my [Previous] photo. It was down in the oak mulch with no visible sign except a slight bump in the fallen leaves. I reckon the ground is plenty wet, but the air already feels dry - especially after all the wind we had over the weekend. The mushrooms are hiding, but it was nice to find this wildflower.
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; haustoria

File.Name: j123b_j123-014.jpg
Notes: 46131102124 I found these pretty Russula mushrooms pushing up a mound of leaves (a "mushrump") in the deep mulch under oak and manzanita today. I'm not sure what they are. We have white Russulas like this and deep red-capped Russulas like this - but the one in this photo is right in between. I didn't try nibbling it, but I should have. I won't even try to give them a name. The big mushroom has some fuzz on the stem which I reckon is another fungus/mold like the "Bolete Eater" fungus in this photo - but maybe different. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2019)

File.Name: j123c_j123-019.jpg
Notes: 31914723787 Here are a pair of odd "pupal husks" attached to leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they belong to one of the Lady Beetles (Coccinellidae), possibly the Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle (genus Chilocorus) which I've found on this shrub before, see this photo. I'm not sure how old these husks are - they could date from last week or last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2019)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Lady Beetles; Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle

File.Name: j123d_j119-022.jpg
Notes: 39890926353 This is another spider orb web on an old seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The whole web is only two or three inches across, but it looks just like the large webs I find in autumn. Spider webs all got washed away by our good rains last week, so this one was made since then. And since then, this web was blown away by the big winds over the weekend. Between the winds and the rain, these spiders have their work cut out for them. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2019)

File.Name: j124a_j124-050.jpg
Notes:
32991425688 I found this striking Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. At least I believe it's an Ichneumon, but I don't have a clue what kind it is beyond that - it's a large group. The colors are remarkable, especially that turquoise band on the wasp's rear end. I found it perched on this oak leaf, and it was gone a moment later. Maybe I've found the same wasp before, see this photo - though that one doesn't have that pretty turquoise band. If you know about this wasp, I would appreciate a comment. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2019)

It was sunny and warmer today - up to the low-60s even on our shady side of the canyon. I'm having better luck finding insects than mushrooms right now - and it was raining cats and dogs just a week ago!

File.Name: j124b_j124-030.jpg
Notes: 46141664424 This is a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - almost certainly Largus californicus - on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I had to chase it for awhile before I got this acceptable photo, but I also got shots of it's rear end as it ran away from me, see my [Previous] photo. This one has a damaged wing. I seemed odd to find this large bug in January, so I went through my notes. It turns out that I have photos of them in every month of the year except from February to April. I'm not sure what that shows, except that I have found them before in January. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2019)

File.Name: j124c_j124-031.jpg
Notes: 46814466122 Here is a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - almost certainly Largus californicus - showing its rear end. I found this one in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), and I had to chase it for awhile, only getting photos of it's rear end as it ran away from me. I finally got a decent photo the whole insect on this same leaf, see my [Next] photo. I reckon all my photos today count as "butt shots", but this one counts extra because it's so vivid - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2019)

File.Name: j124d_j124-001.jpg
Notes: 31925138117 I found this small cricket "butts up" in the flower scar at the top of a cactus fruit in the garden. I believe it is a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae). I guess it's a nymph because it's so small, but the adults lack wings, so I can't be sure if this is a nymph or adult. What's odd is that I've found them here before, see my photos here, here, here, and here. I don't know what the attraction is, but other crickets like it here as well - see this photo of a different Tree Cricket on the fruit. I have another photo of a cricket out in the open here. If you look close, this this also a pretty good "butt shot" - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2019)

File.Name: j125a_j125-002.jpg
Notes:
45963186625 This is a tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This critter is tiny, about 1040 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.1 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This might be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring, but don't they usually have jet-like hairs coming out of their rear ends like this? I did get another photo of this critters rear that shows white hairs just starting to emerge - but I'm going to save that photo for next week's BBBT. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2019)

It was sunny and warmer again today - up to the mid-60s even on our shady side of the canyon. I'm still having better luck finding insects than mushrooms right now, though I suspect there are mushrooms hiding down in the oak mulch like this one. I'll look for them tomorrow.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j125b_j125-006.jpg
Notes: 39912720313 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I don't know what kind of prey it expects to find in this place, but here it is. I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy abdomen, though it seems odd to find it away from flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2019)

File.Name: j125c_j125-030.jpg
Notes:
46825263162 Here are pretty new flowers of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. These shrubs have been flowering for a couple of weeks lower in the mountains, but these are the first flowers I've seen up here on San Marcos Pass. In a good year, the shrubs cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains with flowers, like snow - see this photo from a few years ago, gazillions of individual flowers. (You can see the beginnings of a bloom in this recent photo of a waterfall.)

I was actually looking for tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera ) insects on the flowers like I found last year, see this photo. I didn't find any . . . until I got home, where I spotted what I believe is a psyllid nymph on the lowest flower in this photo. I'll make a note, so mouse around if you don't see it. I believe it is a genus Ceanothia nymph, maybe Ceanothia ceanothi - see Chris Mallory's comment on my earlier photo. If you look under the wings of the psyllid in that photo, it has similar markings to this one. Sure, I'll go back to this place - and I'll have to be more observant! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2019)

File.Name: j125d_j119-005.jpg
Notes: 46825065582 I found this little fly (Diptera) on a fallen oak leaf in the mulch above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography. I got this photo in deep shade, and white balance is what it is. The fly is small but not really tiny, about 1230 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.8 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I can see that it's a fly in the "Nematocera" group with many-segmented antennae. If I had to guess, I would suggest a Winter Crane Fly in the family Trichoceridae, maybe genus Trichocera. It does look something like a very small crane fly, and note that it does have well-defined ocelli - a defining trait according to BugGuide. Whatever it is, it's my only fly photo for the week - so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2019)

File.Name: j126a_j126-021.jpg
Notes:
31947061937 I found this somewhat tattered Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly getting water from the rock today - a sandstone outcrop in this case. It's a kind of vertical puddling, as the rock holds a lot of rain water for a surprisingly long time. The California Tortoiseshell butterfly in my [Previous] photo was taking its moisture from the fallen oak leaves at the base of this outcrop. Both of these butterflies overwinter as adults and are active on warm winter days like we've been having lately. This specimen looks like its been around. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2019)

My usual rule with regards to posting photos is "butterflies first" - but I had to think about it today, as I got many good photos of things that that were not butterflies! It was sunny and warmer again today - up to the upper-60s even on our shady side of the canyon and no doubt warmer in the sun. I even found a Banana Slug and some new mushrooms today! The good news is that there's a chance of rain next weekend, just what we need to keep the mushrooms coming.

File.Name: j126b_j126-018.jpg
Notes: 39923544073 This is another California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly on fallen oak leaves in the canyon today. The leaves look dry, but they trap a surprising amount of water under the surface, and this butterfly is actually puddling - taking moisture from under the leaves. These butterflies overwinter as adults and are active on warm winter days like we've been having lately. This specimen looks pretty sharp! I also photographed a Red Admiral butterfly today, see my [Next] photo. The warm weather brings them out. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2019)

File.Name: j126c_j126-033.jpg
Notes: 33012940948 I found this little Stink Bug (Pentatomidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) inside a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family today. What's remarkable is that I found what is maybe the same bug inside the same seed capsule in December, see this photo - and again November, see this photo! Now it does look quite different now, green instead of tan. My first thought was that it is teneral - just done with a molt - but I don't believe adult insects ever molt, so I can't explain the color change. I also can't guarantee that it's the same insect in all three photos, but it does look like a secure place to snuggle in for the long haul, with a food source right below in the form of seeds. Hah - like a dragon sitting on its hoard! One possibility for its identity is the Consperse Stink Bug (Euschistus conspersus). I'll check back on it again as winter continues. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2019)
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Consperse Stink Bug
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; teneral; Euschistus conspersus
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; teneral; Euschistus conspersus

File.Name: j126d_j126-061.jpg
Notes: 39923208183 This strange-looking creature is a spider - but it looks more like an upside-down furry squid! I believe it's a Triangle Web Spider (genus Hyptiotes) in the family Uloboridae of the Cribellate Orb Weavers which make the odd triangular webs that I show in this photo. The spider is probably species Hyptiotes gertschi. Here it is holding one end of its web close to a dead twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) with lichens today. If you look close, you can see that it's holding on to each end of its web separately - it must move by playing out one end of the line as it reels in the other. One additional neat trick they have is to bounce up and down with their web, no doubt by adjusting the tension on the ends - a way to ensnarl prey as well as to shock intruders like me. I've been looking for these odd spiders since I first noticed them last winter, see this photo from January 1. I also found them in the Oregon woods last summer, see this photo, and one specimen last October at home just in time for Aractober, see this photo . We also have a close relative, the Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in this photo. These spiders are tiny - this one is only 800 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) These tiny spiders have become one of my favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2019)
CommonNames: Triangle Web Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Coast Live Oak; Feather-legged Spider
ScientificName: Hyptiotes; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes gertschi; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Uloborus diversus
associatedTaxa: Hyptiotes; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes gertschi; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Uloborus diversus

File.Name: j127a_j127-037.jpg
Notes:
33025378998 Here are the first flowers of three different Gooseberry (Ribes) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae plant family that grow in our mountains. All these shrubs have spines which distinguish them from the spineless Currants in the same genus. On the left is Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) which is distinguishable by its glandular petiole and ovary - and leaves too. I got this photo near home. On the right is the Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) which is distinguished by its mostly glabrous petiole and ovary - and foliage too. There are other differences between these two, like the color of the anthers, but I'm not sure that's consistent. I got this photo near Arroyo Burro Saddle further along the mountains. The photo in the center shows the Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) with spines but very different flowers that are better in groups - see this photo. I got this photo not too far from home, on the way to Painted Cave. All of these spiny gooseberry shrubs flower about the same time at the end of January, see this photo of all three species from February 1 two years ago. All three shrubs seem to be right on schedule this year. (San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 January 2019)

It got up to 70∞ today, making this the warmest day of the year so far! I'm not complaining, but I'm glad there's rain in the forecast for next weekend.
Locality: San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro Saddle
CommonNames: Gooseberry; Currants; Bitter Gooseberry; Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry
ScientificName: Ribes; Grossulariaceae; Ribes amarum; Ribes californicum; Ribes speciosum
associatedTaxa: Ribes; Grossulariaceae; Ribes amarum; Ribes californicum; Ribes speciosum

File.Name: j127b_j127-020.jpg
Notes: 31959174367 Here is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) munching a new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). The stripes do not wrap around like a candy cane, but they are handsome non-the-less. The caterpillar actually has its head in the flower through an opening probably made by a bee, but I couldn't capture that any better in a photo. I have no idea what moth it will become. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 January 2019)

File.Name: j127c_j126-047.jpg
Notes: 39935421433 I found this fine Amanita in the woods yesterday growing in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It has the classic signs of its genus, the volval cup at the base, and the warts on the cap which are the remains of a universal veil, but not so much of a partial veil ring on the stalk- it seems to fall apart. My first thought is that it's the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata), possibly with some influence from the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina). The two mushrooms are said to form a confusing series of intermediates. This may even be the Panther Amanita - compare this photo and this photo. Now compare the yellow-capped Amanita I usually find under Madrone trees in this photo that I've called A. gemmata before but might be something else. Yep. I'm confused. This is an attractive mushroom, even though it's probably quite poisonous - don't eat it. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Jonquil Amanita; Panther Amanita
ScientificName: Amanita; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita gemmata; Amanita pantherina
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita gemmata; Amanita pantherina

File.Name: j127d_j126-035.jpg
Notes: 33024725568 This is "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom in the leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. You can see a bit of the blushing color on the stalk and cap. This mushroom has a ring, but it doesn't have any kind of volval cup on the base. (I might have broken the base off this one when I picked it, but see this photo from two years ago.) I often find these mushrooms both in the fall and again in March or April. This year I'm finding them in (what I hope is) the middle of the season - if the rains keep up. They just are highly variable, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2019)
ScientificName: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: j128a_j128-011.jpg
Notes:
39947579323 Just count the legs and divide by eight! I believe these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) caught in the act of love, so many legs entwined, transferring genetic material from the male's palps to the female's epigynum. I found them today in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe that's the male on the left and the female on the right, and at this moment they've made contact - compare my [Previous] photo. It's a tender moment . . . and complicated! Mating is difficult for these predators who are ready to attack anything that moves. I guess it's always difficult. See my comments on this photo for some of the anatomical details - but that leaves out all the romance and the finesse of courtship. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2019)

It was a degree or two cooler today, but just as pleasant as it's been. As I recall, we often get a week or two of spring weather in the middle of January, but it sounds like winter is about to return. We now have two chances of rain coming up - a small storm on Wednesday night and Thursday and a larger storm over the weekend. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; palps; epigynum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; palps; epigynum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j128c_j128-039.jpg
Notes: 46911946091 I believe this is the tiny spider that makes the small orb webs I've been finding on old stems of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae), see this photo for an example. I found this spider just to the side of a similar web. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) with that "folium pattern" on its abdomen, compare this photo and this photo. It doesn't seem to have a typical retreat like the spider in this photo, but it's using the seed head of the sage instead. I show a similar spider in this photo. The spider is tiny, just 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Maybe it's just a kid? It's got a hint of a cat face on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2019)

File.Name: j128d_j128-046.jpg
Notes: 31970677027 I found this Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) spider today in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) near one corner of its web. Neither spider nor web is especially large or small. The spider is about 1/2 inch long, bigger than the tiny orb weavers like Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis that I also find in the oaks. I suspect that it's a juvenile Araneus andrewsi because of its dark colors and bristly legs, but who knows? (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2019)

File.Name: j129a_j129-015.jpg
Notes:
46923198441 This is the first open flower I've found this year of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's growing in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year - and maybe never shines at any time of year. White balance is what it is. They are beautiful flowers, more elegant than our usual yellow "wild mustards" that are mostly non-native. They were flowering at the end of December two years ago, but I didn't find them until March last year when the rains were unusually late. It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but please don't dig it up. It tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. The flowers are also tasty, and I reckon it's OK to sample one or two and leave the rest. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2019)

It was windier than expected this morning, and then clouds moved in. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. The frogs are especially noisy right now. I hope all this is a sign of our next storm coming in. We might be in a rainy period until next Tuesday at least! This rain activity is encouraging after too many drought years - especially after warnings of an extended dry period.

File.Name: j129b_j129-006.jpg
Notes: 46870880562 Here are flower clusters on a shrub of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing in the mountains. Hah, there are hundreds or thousands of clusters on a single shrub, and enough shrubs to cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains like snow - see this photo from a few years ago, gazillions of individual flowers! We have several different white-flowered Ceanothus species that have similar white flowers, but the foliage is different. This one is unique in having (mostly) alternate leaves and large seed capsules - but see this photo. There's a tiny insect near the center of my photo today - can you find it? I really meant to stay by this shrub waiting for insects today, but the wind kicked up and frustrated my plans. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2019)

File.Name: j129c_j129-004.jpg
Notes: 46198060064 Here is native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family growing over another shrub in the woods. The vines have exuberant new growth, coming from the enormous "man-root" of the plant, see Gerry Queener's photo of a related species here. We also have this plant's cousin Marah fabaceus which grows more in the backcountry and has smaller flowers that are more yellowish than white. It's confusing where they overlap, but M. macrocarpus has 8-12 smaller seeds in its spiky fruit, and M. fabaceus has just four larger ones - see this photo for the difference. Both of these plants are monoecious with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. My photo mostly shows the plentiful male staminate flowers. The female pistillate flowers are more solitary and hard to find before they turn into fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2019)
ScientificName: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; monoecious
associatedTaxa: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; monoecious

File.Name: j129d_j127-027.jpg
Notes: 33047347898 These are pretty new flowers of Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It's the one on the right in my triptych a few days ago, see this photo. This one is distinguished by its mostly glabrous petiole and foliage. Obviously this isn't really the "first flower" - but they are the first flowers I've seen this year. I like that background, but it's really a sign of "bad bokeh" rather than good. All those 7-sided shapes are reflections of the 7-sided diaphragm in my favorite old Nikkor 105mm macro lens. Nikon's newer macro lens has a 9-sided diaphragm which is said to be less intrusive - but I do like the old effect! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 January 2019)

File.Name: j130a_j130-021.jpg
Notes:
46881453382 I found this tiny Cricket today on an old seed head of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) where it seemed to be snuffling around the retreat of a tiny spider - like the one in this photo. It didn't like me trying to get so close, so it scooted up a dead stem and got cornered at the top. I rotated my camera to make better use of my screen pixels and got the photo. It sure looks like a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) - which I last saw on a cactus fruit in the garden, see this photo! This cricket really is tiny - just 1500 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.9 mm, less than 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's not much larger than the leafhopper in my [Previous] photo. I guess it's a nymph because of its size, but the adults lack wings, so I can't be sure if this is a nymph or small adult. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2019)

Today started sunny and calm, but a wind soon kicked up - from the south rather than the north like yesterday. I believe this is our usual "rain wind" - the storms come from the north but pull clouds from the south into their counter-clockwise vortex. Our first storm is expected late tonight and tomorrow. A much bigger storm is expected on Saturday with some potential for mudslides - maybe five inches of rain?! The odds for each storm sit at 100%. There's more info in the new post at the California Weather Blog - where meteorologist Daniel Swain takes back his recent claim that we're heading into an extended California dry spell. I was getting worried so I hope it rains buckets!
CommonNames: Cricket; Black Sage; Chaparral Camel Cricket
ScientificName: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae

File.Name: j130b_j130-008.jpg
Notes: 46208884694 Here is a little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the tip of a narrow leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's tiny, about 1250 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.7 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) There seem to be endless kinds of green leafhoppers, so I'll leave it at that! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2019)

File.Name: j130c_j126-010.jpg
Notes: 31992307587 The webbing on this plant is the work of web-spinning caterpillars instead of spiders. There are several of the early caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in this photo. It's curious that two of their preferred host plants are actually growing together here - Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) and Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). The caterpillars seem to be mostly on the latter plant, but their webbing is on both. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example in this photo from last August. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following winter or spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. The summer caterpillars are completely social, but the winter/spring caterpillars are less so. I'm not sure what these are doing together. I like this webbing made by critters other than spiders - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2019)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j130d_j124-042.jpg
Notes: 31992223917 This is webbing between old flower/seed whorls of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). This might be "cribellate" webbing which is fuzzy and lacks any kind of sticky stuff, see this photo and this photo for more examples. Maybe this is the work of a cribellate species of Mesh Web Weaver in the family Dictynidae. I've photographed this webbing before on the sage, see this photo. Spider webs all got washed away by our good rains two weeks ago, so this webbing was made since then. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2019)

File.Name: j131a_j131-008.jpg
Notes:
39979539763 I was looking under a piece of loose bark on a log in our woodpile today - looking for slime molds or snails or whatever, see my [Previous] photo and this one. I found this pair of small crickets! I believe they are the same Chaparral Camel Crickets (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) that I showed yesterday in this photo. These two are small, only about 1/4 inch long - not counting their antennae of course. They look different in color, and maybe the top one is a bit larger - maybe male and female, though they both seem to have the same two little horns on the tip of their abdomens. This seems to be their time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2019)

Thunder, lightning, wind, and rain - oh my! Our storm last night was stronger than expected, and we ended up with 2-1/4 inches in our rain gauge. This was supposed to be our small storm with the a larger one coming tomorrow night and Saturday. The NWS says in a Hydrologic Outlook report that there will be "between 4.00 and 7.00 inches across south facing foothills and mountains" - that's us. Yeah, mudslides below the burn areas are an issue. Watch out!

File.Name: j131b_j131-003.jpg
Notes: 39979365533 I found these tiny slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) on the inside of a piece of bark that I removed from a log in our woodpile today. They're small, mostly less than a millimeter across. They are also sessile, without an obvious stalk. They look similar to the white ones I've been finding, e.g. in this photo, though they don't have an obvious calcareous outer layer. I've seen these before, compare this photo from a couple of years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2019)

File.Name: j131c_j127-015.jpg
Notes: 32002785747 Here is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). This is a good shot of the bee's rear end, so HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 January 2019)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: j131d_j125-003.jpg
Notes: 39979229763 This is the rear end of the same tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that I showed in this photo a few days ago. It's sitting on a leaf of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This critter is only a bit over 1/8 inch long. I think it might be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring. They usually have long jet-like hairs coming out of their rear ends like this, but I guess this one is just starting to get them - kind of like getting your first whiskers. This certainly shows the critter's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2019)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j201a_j201-028.jpg
Notes:
46040326955 The male spider on the left is making a move on the female spider on the right! If you look close, you can see the enlarged palps of the male and the dingus-like epigynum of the female - I'll add notes. (The lady also has large chelicerae, but those are different from palps.) I'm pretty sure these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) like the other ones I show "doing it" in this photo a few days ago. They are in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree - a different one from my other photo. It was a dark and blustery day to get this photo, but there they were, and I had to try. I guess this is their time, and I hope they finish before the major storm comes in later tonight! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2019)

It was dark and blustery today as our newest storm moves in, a better day for photographing mushrooms on the ground than amorous spiders in a tree. Our last storm brought our total rainfall up to 23.77 inches, 134% of normal. Our next storm is just starting to drizzle as I write this, with a promise of significant rain. There are evacuation orders for areas below last year's wildfires. We're under a NWS High Wind Warning and a Flash Flood Watch. It will be an interesting night!
ScientificName: palps; epigynum; Linyphiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
