File.Name: j201b_j201-002.jpg
Notes: 33078654338 I found this little fly (Diptera) on a green leaf of a ground-hugging plant of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) above our retaining wall at a comfortable height for photography. The fly is tiny, only about 500 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I can see that it's a fly in the "Nematocera" group with many-segmented antennae, and it's much smaller that the fly in this recent photo. It has long antennae, so maybe it's a Fungus Gnat or Gall Midge in the superfamily Sciaroidea. Whatever it is, it's my only fly photo for the week - so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2019)
CommonNames: fly; California Honeysuckle; Fungus Gnat; Gall Midge

File.Name: j201c_j201-014.jpg
Notes: 33078573768 Here is an impressive cluster of Western Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. We seem to have quite a few of these right now - they are large because we've been having good rain, but they are here at all because we have a number of drought-dead trees in the woods and that is their habitat. They are quite poisonous, so don't eat them. I can't imagine why anyone would confuse them with edible Chanterelle mushrooms when they are massed like this, but I've been fooled for a moment by solitary specimens growing from buried wood. The possible greenish tint, rubbery texture, and sharp gill edges should settle it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2019)

File.Name: j201d_j201-007.jpg
Notes: 32012872737 I found these yellow Cortinarius mushrooms pushing up through deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees after our good rain yesterday. I don't believe they grew overnight, but the rain brought them up out of the leaves. Maybe this one is Cortinarius xanthodryophilus - see the species page and photos at Mykoweb. This one is considered to be subgenus Phlegmacium with a swollen base and not subgenus Bulbopodium with a "rimmed base" - a distinction that I really don't understand. The caps are viscid when wet like this - they are hard to pick up! I've called this one Cortinarius fulmineus in the past, but I reckon it's the same thing. Edibility is unknown. The problem is that all Cortinarius mushrooms end up looking alike, so there's no good way to tell them apart - but they are handsome mushrooms when they are fresh like this. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2019)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius xanthodryophilus; Phlegmacium; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius fulmineus
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius xanthodryophilus; Phlegmacium; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius fulmineus

File.Name: j202a_j202-015.jpg
Notes:
33089029378 This is looking up San Jose Creek from the bridge on West Camino Cielo road - in the rain today. The creek looks respectable after our four inches of rain this morning, but I bet it was higher at the height of the rain. It's remarkable how quickly the water in the creeks drops when the heavy rain stops, but watch out for the floods! That's a winter-bare native White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia, Betulaceae) tree with cones and catkins in the foreground, a gnarled truck of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) on the left, California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) behind it, and an unusual Incense Cedar (Calocedrus decurrens, Cupressaceae) tree across the creek on the right. I believe the cedar was introduced long ago, though they've been seeding and proliferating in Kinevan Canyon, and they are native in the backcountry. (Or is it native here??) I think that leaning Alder tree in the background just fell last year - compare this photo from 2017. I'm sure there are new mushrooms in those woods just waiting to be found! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2019)

Today is Groundhog Day. Needless to say, any groundhog or gopher that stuck its head out today just got wet and did not see its shadow - portending early spring? We'll see. We did get substantial rain last night and this morning, a bit over four inches of the wet stuff - but still on the low end of the 4-7 inches that was forecast. It was an exciting morning with some flooding in town and highway closures. I heard a rumor that we got 3/4 inch of rain in 15 minutes up here on San Marcos Pass! All this rain brings our total for the water-year up to 27.83 inches which is 83% of our average total for the year. That's encouraging after too many drought years in a row!
CommonNames: White Alder; Western Sycamore; California Bay; Incense Cedar
ScientificName: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae
associatedTaxa: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae

File.Name: j202b_j202-020.jpg
Notes: 39999846903 This is an unnamed side creek cascading into San Jose Creek at the lower end of Kinevan Canyon, not far from the bridge in my [Previous] photo. I got this photo in light rain today. Even the little creeks are rushing after our four inches of rain early this morning, though they come down quickly after their peak during the heaviest rain. The green foliage in the foreground is mostly California Bay. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2019)

File.Name: j202c_j202-022.jpg
Notes: 46964165971 There's been a lot of water under the bridge since I've seen this much water under the bridge where West Camino Cielo Road crosses San Jose Creek at the lower end of Kinevan Canyon! The creek is cranking after our four inches of rain this morning. I have a very similar shot in this photo from January 31, 2016. It was still raining when I got this photo, but the heaviest rain was done. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2019)

File.Name: j202d_j202-008.jpg
Notes: 46050233975 Here is another photo of the little waterfall along our driveway - it rarely flows, but here it is! It falls about 20 feet total. It's a small drainage and it takes some heavy rain to get it working. We had four inches of rain last night and this morning - enough to get the waterfall going. It was cranking in the heavy rain this morning, but it's remarkable how quick it subsides when the rain slows down enough to get out with my camera. The woodland around the waterfall shows Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees, with an understory of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) and Wild Blackberry and still-dormant Poison Oak and Nuttall's Snowberry. I seem to get this shot whenever the waterfall is going, the last time was just a couple of weeks ago, see this photo. Also compare this photo from last year and this photo from the year before. I'll probably show it again the next time it rains this hard! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; California Bay; Coastal Wood Fern; Wild Blackberry; Poison Oak; Nuttall's Snowberry

File.Name: j203a_j203-018.jpg
Notes:
46063258995 Here is a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) in the rain today. The flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats and skirts in the air. I took many shots to get this keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2019)

We had our big storm yesterday, but the rain kept coming today - light but steady. I got tired of being inside, so I took a rain walk with an umbrella for my camera. We've ended up with another 1.7 inches in the last 24 hours and almost 8 inches since the rains started early Thursday morning. Highway 154 is apparently closed ëIndefinitelyí due to a clogged culvert near Lake Cachuma. Ah, that's why it's been so quiet - and we locals can still get through. They can take their time fixing the problem!

All this rain brings our total for the water-year up to 29.54 inches which is more that 150% of normal to date and 88% of our average total for the year. Rain is likely for the next two days - 100% chance of more rain on Monday and a chance of snow on Tuesday night. This is actually the third storm in the series that could bring another 1-3 inches. This rain is a delight after too many drought years in a row.

File.Name: j203b_j203-021.jpg
Notes: 40011961643 This is a small Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) getting out of the rain today by hanging under one of last year's ripe cactus fruit. I don't believe it's a proper Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family because it doesn't have the long palps that these flies have - see this photo. Maybe it's a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae? It has pretty patterned wings like some of those flies have, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2019)
CommonNames: Crane Fly; Large Crane Fly; Limoniid Crane Fly

File.Name: j203c_j203-017.jpg
Notes: 32035262187 Here is a group of almost-white Cortinarius mushrooms growing in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the rain today. I think they might be Cortinarius regalis, see this photo of a similar mushroom, though that one has a more bulbous stem than the one in my present photo. You can't see many rusty-colored cortina hairs on the stalk, though the gills and caps are colored by the developing spores. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2019)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius regalis
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius regalis

File.Name: j203d_j203-007.jpg
Notes: 46976355101 This is a lovely native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree in the rain this morning. This is a tree I can photograph from the shelter of our front porch when it's raining too hard to go out any further with my camera. It's a young tree, maybe 20 feet tall. They have beautiful foliage and smooth bark like skin. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2019)

File.Name: j204a_j204-037.jpg
Notes:
46075420985 Here is another photo of the beautiful "stink bug" that I show in my [Previous] photo - without the raindrops. I believe it is species Banasa dimidiata in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I got this photo in the afternoon when I got back from my walk today. It's on the same new leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) where it was in the morning, though it has come out from under it. These bugs look almost too pretty to be native, but apparently they are. I've seen one before, see this photo from December 2015. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2019)

Rain, rain, rain! We've had another two inches of rain since yesterday at this time, bringing our total for the water-year up to 31.55 inches - getting close to our 33.85 inch average total for the year. Now it's turning cold, and we had some hail a short time ago. Showers are expected to continue through Wednesday morning, and now we're under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory with a chance of snow tomorrow evening. Hah, I'll believe it when I see it.
ScientificName: Banasa dimidiata; Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Banasa dimidiata; Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: j204b_j204-001.jpg
Notes: 46075197085 I found this beautiful "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera huddled under a new leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) during a shower this morning - with raindrops. I believe it is species Banasa dimidiata, and it has gorgeous colors - here magnified in places by the rain drops. These bugs look almost too pretty to be native, but apparently they are. I've seen one before, see this photo from December 2015. I got this photo in the morning, then I got another photo in the afternoon when I got back from my walk - see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2019)

File.Name: j204c_j204-024.jpg
Notes: 46937045092 I feel like this dragonfly is becoming an old friend! This is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata, clinging to a stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's been grounded by inclement weather - our good rains for the last several days including this morning. It might well be the same individual that I've been showing e.g. in this photo - and follow the links from there. I usually find these dragonflies hanging onto plants on cold or wet days in winter like today. Except for the days when they are grounded, these predators seem to be active all winter long looking for prey in sunny places. Despite its bright colors, they are well camouflaged - I'll bet I've walked right past them without noticing on many cold winter days. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2019)
CommonNames: Variegated Meadowhawk; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: j204d_j204-007.jpg
Notes: 46936817962 I believe this is another Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) tucked in between leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trying to stay dry during the showers today. I got this shot during a moment of sunshine on a mostly overcast day with some rain. Now look closer - the cricket has apparently just finished molting and is standing next to his old skin! I've been finding a number of these small crickets lately, see this photo, this photo, and this photo. I must explain that the cricket was originally vertical, and I rotated this shot 90∞ counter-clockwise to make better use of my screen space. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2019)
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j205a_j205-004.jpg
Notes:
46087353975 This little Bumble Bee (Bombus) was caught by a sudden change in the weather today when it went from sunny and calm to windy and rainy in just a few minutes! Here it is hanging on to a branch of non-native hybrid Waverly Sage (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I suppose it's most remarkable that this bee was out working the flowers just a few minutes before I got this photo! I've also seen maybe the same kind of bees on new flowers of native Bitter Gooseberry that I show in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2019)

Today was a pretty wild day to try to get new photos - there was bright sun and dark clouds, wind and rain, even a bit of slushy snow! We ended up with another 4/10 inch of rain since last night, and we're getting close to the end of this round of storms. It's cold tonight, and I won't rule out more weather. Then we'll have a few days to dry out, though wind might be a problem. Hah - there's another chance of rain on Saturday!

File.Name: j205b_j205-007.jpg
Notes: 32059452757 This is another liverwort (Marchantiophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon, but I believe it is different from the usual California Liverwort (Asterella californica). For one thing, it's growing in a sunnier place. The sporophytes are taller and smoother and less divided, and the creeping "leaves" ("thallus") are smaller, compare this photo. This one might be Palmer's Asterella (Asterella palmeri). I had the same liverwort misidentified in this photo, but I've changed it. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2019)
associatedTaxa: Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; sporophytes; Asterella palmeri

File.Name: j205c_j204-019.jpg
Notes: 46948977482 Here is one more shot of the dragonfly I showed yesterday in this photo. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata, clinging to a dead stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's been grounded by the rainy weather we've been having, but it was gone today which was colder but a little less rainy. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2019)
CommonNames: Variegated Meadowhawk; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: j205d_j204-039.jpg
Notes: 46086915255 I can't resist one more photo of the true bug that I show in my [Previous] two photos - here it is looking right at me. I believe it is species Banasa dimidiata in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera. These bugs look almost too pretty to be native, but apparently they are. What colors! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2019)

File.Name: j206a_j206-009.jpg
Notes:
47012012261 Here is one more shot of the dragonfly that I've been showing lately, e.g.this photo yesterday. It's our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata, clinging to a dead stem of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's been grounded by the rainy weather we've been having. I thought it was gone with the rain yesterday, but maybe I just didn't notice it - or it came back. Our rain is past now, but it was icy cold this morning. I've been finding few insects in the woods, and I reckon they're all "sheltering in place" like this one. It's nice to have a captive model like this - I moved in close and got my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2019)

We didn't get any more rain today, but it was icy cold this morning - the first actual ice I've seen all winter. It was quite breezy as well, and I got most of my photos close to the ground. Our power will be out all day tomorrow for "scheduled maintenance" - I'll post if I can.
CommonNames: Variegated Meadowhawk; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: j206b_j206-030.jpg
Notes: 33136575418 I found this gorgeous bolete mushroom in the woods today in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I believe this fine mushroom is Zeller's Bolete (Xerocomellus zelleri aka Boletus zelleri). It has a brilliant color combo - dark "fuscous" cap with a frosted look and not at all cracked, yellow pores that do not stain blue, and a red stem with only hints of yellow. They are quite beautiful - though this specimen is a little the worse for being out in the rain last week, which seems to bring out more red in its cap. Note that Mykoweb says flatly that this species only grows under conifers, but author David Arora says the are abundant under oak. I've found this one before, e.g. this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2019)
CommonNames: bolete; Coast Live Oak; Zeller's Bolete
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Xerocomellus zelleri; Boletus zelleri
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Xerocomellus zelleri; Boletus zelleri

File.Name: j206c_j206-018.jpg
Notes: 32070542417 Here is one more photo of the Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) growing with other lichens on a dead stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I just get a notion to photograph this one again every once in a while because it is so striking! It resembles the more common Golden-Eye Lichens (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) in this photo, but it lacks the ciliate apothecia - those small orange disks which are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. It really looks like something that belongs in the ocean! To be honest, I could have gotten this photo at pretty much any time in the past year, these lichens don't change much even when they dry out - compare this photo from a year ago that is similar though the lichen is a bit desiccated. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2019)
CommonNames: Powdered Orange Bush Lichen; Chamise; Golden-Eye Lichens
ScientificName: Teloschistes flavicans; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus

File.Name: j206d_j205-020.jpg
Notes: 40047133363 This scene includes liverworts, mosses, and a spider web. I believe the liverworts are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla), but I don't know about the rest. This is the only spider web I've found that survived our week of rains. All the other spiders will have to rebuild, but this one web was down in a hollow where it survived. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2019)

File.Name: j207a_j207-016.jpg
Notes:
32081875277 This is the rear end of a tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) tucked into a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I reckon it's trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it. (Of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers!) I've been seeing quite a few of these crickets lately, see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2019)

Our power was out all day for "scheduled maintenance" to replace a couple of utility poles. Power was supposed to be back on at 5:00 pm, but it didn't happen. We finally got "brown out" power around 6:30 and real power sometime after 7:00. Now why does the power company schedule this maintenance on a cold and dark winter day? Anyway, I got a late start on my photos tonight, so two is enough here. I had more nice ones too.
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j207b_j207-034.jpg
Notes: 46971315612 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is sticking its butt up in the air and letting out a line of web from a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). The spider is small, but it still seems a little large to balloon away, and in any event it didn't go anywhere, so maybe it was just casting a line so it could get away from my prying camera eye. I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with that hairy abdomen, though it seems odd to find it away from flowers. I think I found the same kind of spider a couple of weeks ago, see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2019)

File.Name: j208a_j208-010.jpg
Notes:
47032335761 I found this bristly fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I suspect it's another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera), and it does have a subscutellum (more visible in another photo) which BugGuide suggests is a useful character for identifying these flies. The larvae of these flies parasitize other insects like caterpillars, so it makes sense they would be out early in the season when many insects are just getting started. That said, all insects including flies have been scarce since our big rains last week and cold days since then. I was fortunate to get this photo today for Fly Day Friday, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2019)

We have a 100% chance of rain tonight - and again on Sunday and maybe also next Wednesday and Thursday! These aren't big storms, but they are wet. We're also under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory tonight, and it's definitely cold enough for some snow.
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera; subscutellum
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera; subscutellum

File.Name: j208b_j208-018.jpg
Notes: 46980125252 Here are two shots of different nymphs of Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insects, both taken today. The nymphs are each about 1000 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 3.9 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I found the one on the left on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) and the one on the right on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). They might both be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring, but there are obvious differences too. Whatever they are, they are cute - the one on the left has a face like the grille of a big truck, and the other one looks jet-propelled with those white bristles coming out of its rear end! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2019)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j208c_j207-027.jpg
Notes: 46979997892 At first, I only saw the orange-colored spider in the center of my photo - then I noticed the other one in the shadows behind it! These are Sheetweb Spiders in the family Linyphiidae, and I believe they're taking a break from mating activity. That's the male with swollen palps in the sun and the lady with thin palps in the shadows. It may be that the male is recharging his special "sperm web" - see this photo for more info. Whatever he's doing, his lady looks impatient! These might be the same spiders I showed in courtship a week ago, see this photo. They are on the same web in the same small oak tree. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2019)

File.Name: j208d_j208-005.jpg
Notes: 46979865792 Here are more almost-white Cortinarius mushrooms growing in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I think they might be Cortinarius regalis, though compare this photo of a similarly colored mushroom with a less bulbous stem - they sure look different. You can't see many rusty-colored cortina hairs on the stalk of this one, though you can see a bit of the cobwebby cortina hairs on the left edge of the mushroom on the left and some hairs on the edge of the cap on the right. These mushrooms have noticeable remnants of a universal veil on the cap. Hah, the shape reminds me of a paleolithic Venus figurine. Happy Fungi Friday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2019)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius regalis
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cortinarius regalis

File.Name: j209a_j209-016.jpg
Notes:
46129303565 This Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) has found shelter under a dead leaf in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. The leaf is actually about head-high in the tree. Hah, it looks like other spiders have used the same leaf for shelter - look at that webbing in the cavity above the spider on the left, though I also see old insect frass in there. This spider is a lady with those slender palps. It might be a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, see this photo. Three of my photos today show critters that have found shelter from the rain last night. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2019)

Rain, rain, rain! We did have about 6/10 inch of rain last night, and there's a 100% chance of more rain tomorrow. We're still under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory through Sunday night because of this new cold winter storm - which I assume is the same storm that is bringing snow to Seattle and Portland right now. We also have chances of more rain later in the week. All this weather is slowing down the progression of the year - which is fine by me.
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spider; Coast Live Oak; Hammock Spider

File.Name: j209b_j209-009.jpg
Notes: 33167642578 I found this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) "sheltering in place" from the rain last night under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe this spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen. I've found a few more of these light-colored crab spiders lately, see my photos here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2019)

File.Name: j209c_j208-017.jpg
Notes: 47042682481 Here's another photo of the little Stink Bug (Pentatomidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that's spending the winter inside a seed capsule of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. What's remarkable is that I found what is maybe the same bug inside the same seed capsule in January, see this photo - again in December, see this photo - and again November, see this photo! Now it does look quite different now, green instead of tan. Did it change color or did a different bug take its place? I can't guarantee that it's the same insect in all four photos, but it does look like a secure place to snuggle in for the long haul, with a food source still right below in the form of seeds. It's also remarkable that I found what I think is the same bug out of its nest on a nearby plant stem a couple of weeks ago. I'm confused! I'll keep checking back as winter continues. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2019)
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae

File.Name: j209d_j207-032.jpg
Notes: 32100841967 Here are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These are the male pollen-bearing staminate catkins. The female pistillate flowers look like tiny acorns and appear later in the spring. In years past, I've found the first flowers as early as mid-January and as late as mid-March. (That's not counting the occasional oddball catkin in November, see this photo!) I don't expect the main flowering until March or April. These trees support much life in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2019)

File.Name: j210a_j210-013.jpg
Notes:
46331529124 Silly spider, get out of the rain! Compare this photo of the same spider on a sunny day a couple of weeks ago - it looks about the same except for the raindrops. It's an Orb Web Weaver (Araneus, Araneidae) in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree, though its web has been washed away by the recent rains. The spider is about 1/2 inch long, bigger than the tiny orb weavers like Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis that I also find in the oaks. I suspect that it's a juvenile Araneus andrewsi because of its dark colors and bristly legs, but who knows? (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2019)

Another day, another 4/10 inch of rain! There were strong wind gusts today, the NOAA weather station on San Marcos Pass recorded gusts of 30+ mph all afternoon with a peak of 46 mph. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, though the worst of it seems to have passed. Now we have a couple of days to dry out - until our next 90% chance of rain on Wednesday! All this rain activity is wonderful after so many recent drought years.
CommonNames: Orb Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus andrewsi
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: j210b_j210-006.jpg
Notes: 46141592665 This is another lovely native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree in the rain this morning. This tree happens to be growing almost sideways reaching for the sun on our north-facing mountains. It's a young tree, but bigger than the one I show in this photo. They have beautiful foliage and smooth bark like skin. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2019)

File.Name: j210c_j210-011.jpg
Notes: 33179941388 Here is a little forest of sexual sporophytes of (I believe) the California Liverwort (Asterella californica, Marchantiophyta) growing on the face of a sandstone outcrop. These look different from the liverworts in this photo which might be Palmer's Asterella (Asterella palmeri) - less like a helmet and more like little surfing palm trees! We have more different kinds of liverworts in our woods than I previously thought. There's a checklist of different California species at iNaturalist, but I haven't yet found a proper key. They seem to be having a good year with all the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2019)
ScientificName: sporophytes; Asterella californica; Marchantiophyta; Asterella palmeri
associatedTaxa: sporophytes; Asterella californica; Marchantiophyta; Asterella palmeri

File.Name: j210d_j209-005.jpg
Notes: 47054984111 This is new growth of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Phrymaceae plant family. Look how the stem on the left is swollen compared to the normal stems on the right. I believe it's been galled by an insect, possibly the Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaci) in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. It's interesting how the leaves look different on the galled and ungalled stems. I can tell at a glance which stems are galled. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2019)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge

File.Name: j211a_j211-021.jpg
Notes:
32125619867 This is a small Coral Fungus growing from a woody root-burl of (I believe) California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). The root is alive but not thriving. This fungus is small, the whole cluster is maybe an inch across. I'm not sure what it is, and it seems unusual to find it on wood . One possibility is Clavicorona pyxidata aka Artomyces pyxidatus which is said to grow on wood but is said to be rare on the West Coast. Another possibility is the Crested White Coral (Clavulina cristata), though I think of that as growing on the ground. These coral-like mushrooms were originally all placed in the Clavariaceae family, but it turns out that clavarioid species are not all closely related. Whatever this mushroom is, it looks fantastic - like it belongs in the ocean! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2019)

It was sunny and cold today. There's an NWS Frost Advisory in effect for the city of Santa Barbara on the coast tonight, and it's much colder up here in the mountains. It sounds like we're in for another good rain starting late Tuesday night, maybe another 2-4 inches up here in the mountains. Yipes, there's snow on Maui??
CommonNames: Coral Fungus; California Honeysuckle; Crested White Coral

File.Name: j211b_j211-013.jpg
Notes: 40102273753 Last month, I showed three different "favors" of Witch's Butter fungi in this photo - colored yellow, brown, and black. I don't know what the brown one is in that photo, but this photo shows the real Brown Witch's Butter (Tremella foliacea) - which looks quite different. It's growing on a fallen tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Oak bark is dark, and it's interesting that the almost-white bark of this fallen tree is colored entirely by crustose lichens - look close and you can see apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the fungus and algae. The fungus is said to grow as a parasite of Stereum fungi, see this photo of a different Tremella, but I don't see it here. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2019)
CommonNames: Brown Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Tremella foliacea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Tremella foliacea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; apothecia

File.Name: j211c_j211-016.jpg
Notes: 47014756912 I found these striking red-capped Russula mushrooms in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They are surely in the "Russula emetica" group, probably Russula silvicola with that white stem. I nibbled the small one, and it tastes "acrid" - hot and spicy, like slightly "off" wasabi, not quite pleasant. It's probably poisonous, but it sure is beautiful. We also have other Russula's in the woods with caps that aren't so red, see this photo. These are one of our prettiest mushrooms. This is when I usually find them, towards the end of our mushroom season. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2019)
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula silvicola
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula silvicola

File.Name: j211d_j211-007.jpg
Notes: 32125139007 I found this nice colony of pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms in the woods today. The gills start pallid, but in time they pick up the spore color like the upturned one. These don't have that chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool that some of these mushrooms have, like this one, so I guess they fall into the "Entoloma rhodopolium" group - maybe Entoloma lividoalbum, but who knows. I have another photo of similar mushrooms in here. These are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. Note that distinctive palmate leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2019)
ScientificName: Entoloma; Entoloma rhodopolium; Entoloma lividoalbum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Entoloma; Entoloma rhodopolium; Entoloma lividoalbum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: j212a_j212-009.jpg
Notes:
33203055748 I walked right past this scene today - and then stopped and looked back! It's a caterpillar that looks like a stick - at least it would if it wasn't on such a green plant. This is a well-camouflaged looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I've seen them on this plant before, compare this photo from last spring. Geometrid caterpillars are distinctive in having a base of just two pairs of prolegs and three pairs of true legs at the other end. I've found them before on quite a few different plants, try this search of my photos for "camouflage caterpillar Geometridae". They have great camouflage - they stand out straight like little twigs. I'll have a closer photo of its head for my [Next] post tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)

It was quite windy all morning - with winds from the south. That's our usual rain wind - the storms come from the north but pull clouds from the south into their counter-clockwise vortex. Our next storm is expected late tonight and tomorrow, and I'm thinking it might be a good one. There's some uncertainty to the forecast, but the expectation is 2-4(5) inches in the mountains. The winds from the south could provide significant orographic enhancement as the storm meets our east-west mountains.
CommonNames: looper; inchworm; Geometrid Moth; Wild Sweetpea

File.Name: j212b_j212-042.jpg
Notes: 46354416924 I found these new Blewit (Clitocybe nuda aka Lepista nuda) mushrooms in the woods today growing under a mix of oaks (Quercus, Fagaceae). They are edible and popular, but I have two caveats. They start blue like their name, but they soon fade to tan or brown like the ones on the right are just starting to turn, see this photo and this photo. And we also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar, see this photo for a comparison shot. Despite the possibility of confusion, I always feel comfortable identifying these. You just have to see a few with someone who knows them to get the knack. That's good advice with all mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)
ScientificName: Clitocybe nuda; Lepista nuda; Quercus; Fagaceae; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe nuda; Lepista nuda; Quercus; Fagaceae; Cortinarius

File.Name: j212c_j212-019.jpg
Notes: 46354274004 Here are a couple of Fluted Black Elfin Saddle mushrooms, distinctive with a fluted stem and dark cap. I've always called this mushroom Helvella lacunosa, but that species has now been split up. This one might be Helvella dryophila which is associated with hardwoods like the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees I found them under today. I find something very similar in Oregon under conifers, see this photo on the left. I call these "brain mushrooms" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. These are the first ones I've found this year, but their time is just beginning, towards the end of the mushroom season. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)
CommonNames: Fluted Black Elfin Saddle; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Helvella lacunosa; Helvella dryophila; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Helvella lacunosa; Helvella dryophila; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j212d_j212-033.jpg
Notes: 47077542661 This mushroom was hidden behind a wall of dead brush that I couldn't get through, though I managed stick my camera in a for a photo. I'm pretty sure it is one of the Amanita section Vaginatae mushrooms that used to be known as genus Amanitopsis. They have a membranous "universal veil" at the base and no ring or "partial veil" at all. This may be the Grisette (Amanita vaginata) with a grayish cap that is deeply striate - at least it's in that group of related species. I found this one in the woods today under a mix of oaks (Quercus, Fagaceae) and Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I don't find these very often. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)
ScientificName: Amanita; Vaginatae; Amanitopsis; Amanita vaginata; Quercus; Fagaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Vaginatae; Amanitopsis; Amanita vaginata; Quercus; Fagaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: j213a_j212-012.jpg
Notes:
46173996455 This is looking close at the head of the caterpillar I showed yesterday in this photo - a close shot, not just a crop. It's a looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. In this close photo you can see the caterpillar's true legs and antennae and odd eyes - a semicircle of simple eyes or ocelli instead of the compound eye of the adult moth. (I only see five ocelli, not the six that should be there?) You can also see a couple of the spiracles along its side which are breathing pores. Note that it has a functional spiracle on the first body segment with legs, but then it skips the next two segments. That's just how caterpillars are. There's more info on "external morphology of Lepidoptera" at Wikipedia. Live and learn! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)

We've had about 7/10 inch of rain so far today. That's less than expected, but the rain was light and steady - it never really let up today, and I stayed indoors. Heavier rains are expected tomorrow, though the mountains southeast of us may get the best of it.
CommonNames: looper; inchworm; Geometrid Moth; Wild Sweetpea

File.Name: j213b_j212-002.jpg
Notes: 46173886995 I believe these are Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushrooms growing in sandy soil on the edge of our garden, not far from Coast Live Oak. I sliced the gills with my knife, and they don't seem to have any latex even though they are fresh. (My book says the latex is "white or watery white, unchanging, often scanty or even absent.") They also don't have the maple-syrup smell of the real Candy Cap (Lactarius fragilis), though they are still said to be good to eat. I haven't tried them. They resemble the Yellow-Staining Milkcap (Lactarius xanthogalactus) mushrooms in my [Previous] photo, though the caps are more reddish and not at all zoned. It's curious that I haven't found them anywhere else in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)

File.Name: j213c_j212-035.jpg
Notes: 46363962734 I believe this is the Yellow-Staining Milk Cap (Lactarius xanthogalactus) mushroom, formerly known as Lactarius vinaceorufescens or Lactarius chrysorheus. (Those darn mycologists!) I sliced the gills with my knife, and they produce a white "milk" that turns yellow almost immediately. They have an obscurely zoned cap, an acrid taste, and are said to be poisonous. These were growing under Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae), though I also find them under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Milk Cap; Bigberry Manzanita; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Lactarius xanthogalactus; Lactarius vinaceorufescens; Lactarius chrysorheus; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Lactarius xanthogalactus; Lactarius vinaceorufescens; Lactarius chrysorheus; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j213d_j130-006.jpg
Notes: 46173760195 This Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) is hiding behind a veil of webbing under a leaf of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. This might be the same spider I've been showing lately, e.g. in this photo - at least it's the same tree. Spider webs have been scarce after the recent rains, and I had to go back more than a week for this photo, though I haven't used it before. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2019)

File.Name: j214a_j214-009.jpg
Notes:
32156303297 I found this lobster-like wingless Bristletail insect in the order Microcoryphia aka Archaeognatha aka Thysanura (in part). I believe it is a Jumping Bristletail in the family Machilidae of that order. I found this one under a piece of oak bark in our woodpile, and what an odd beast it is! Like the Chimera of Greek mythology, it seems to be an amalgam of different parts - scales like a moth, antennae like a katydid, and mouthparts like a crane fly. That's not to mention a few odd appendages I can't identify! The compound eyes are especially interesting, because they meet in the center of its face. By the way, I don't like to tear rotting logs apart. It's a way to find uncommon salamanders, but it's too destructive. I figure it's important that we don't destroy the habitats we're trying to photograph! That said, I figure it's OK in a woodpile - there's not much future to that habitat anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2019)

A hard rain did fall this morning! Sometime after 6:00 am it actually reached 1/4 inch in 5 minutes which comes to three inches per hour - but that heavy rain only lasted a few minutes not a full hour. It woke me up. We actually ended up with 3.0 inches over the last two days. Not bad, but most of the "atmospheric river" rain went further south. I'm not complaining. We're now up to 34.94 inches for the water year, well over 100% of normal - almost three feet of rain! All this rain activity is wonderful after so many recent drought years.

File.Name: j214b_j214-008.jpg
Notes: 33222804328 This is another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) showing its rear end under a piece of oak bark in a woodpile today - with raindrops. It's most of the cricket anyway - it's antennae go on and on. I've found these crickets here before, see this photo from the same woodpile. There was a break in the rain today, so I went out with the hope of finding something for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group. I actually got two photos before the rain drove me back inside again, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2019)

File.Name: j214c_j214-001.jpg
Notes: 47097852901 This is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider finding shelter from the rain this morning under a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's a male spider with those swollen palps. These male spiders do make webs, though they've all been washed the heavy rain this week. These spiders have remarkable rear ends that stick out like a handle, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2019)

File.Name: j214d_j212-016.jpg
Notes: 40133071063 I found this odd spider clutching a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) before the latest batch of rains started. It let me get close with my camera without moving. I didn't realize how odd it is until I got the image up on my computer screen - look how its front legs are shriveled and undeveloped. Maybe it has some horrible spider wasting disease, or more likely it's just come out of a molt and its legs aren't pumped up yet? I'm not sure what kind of spider it. My first thought is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), but it may actually be a Running Crab Spider (Philodromidae) - it's colors match the species Philodromus rufus. I would need to see the front legs to be sure. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)
CommonNames: Eastwood Manzanita; Crab Spider; Running Crab Spider
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Thomisidae; Philodromidae; Philodromus rufus
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Thomisidae; Philodromidae; Philodromus rufus

File.Name: j215a_j214-020.jpg
Notes:
47055781852 I'm pretty sure this is a transparent maggot - the larva of a fly (Diptera) - hurrying along under the same piece of oak bark where I found the bristletail insects in this photo yesterday. It's moving at a pretty good clip, leaving a slime trail behind, fast enough to be hard to photograph - not bad for a critter with no legs! After looking at lots of photos of maggots on the web, I think this one might be the the larva of a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae - see the photos at BugGuide. I find the attractive adult flies later in the year, see this photo. I'm not sure that's what this maggot will become, but it's possible. By rights, I suppose I could add this photo to the Fly Day Friday group, but that feels too weird - but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2019)

Another day, another 7/10 inch of rain! We had light but steady rain all day long, and I never did get outside. Tomorrow might be our one day to dry out with only a 20% chance of rain. Then rain becomes likely again on Sunday - with the twist that it might be cold enough to snow. Our next chance of rain comes on Wednesday/Thursday.

File.Name: j215b_j214-039.jpg
Notes: 47107924801 Here is a group of uncommon native Chaparral Orchids aka Denseflower Rein Orchids (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family coming up in a mulch of oak leaves in the woods. It's always a surprise to find these plants in bloom come midsummer - see this photo from the end of last July. The basal leaves completely shrivel up by the time they flower with a bare asparagus-like stalk a foot or three tall. You can see a couple of the old stalks on the plant to the left. The new leaves are pretty surprising too! I've been finding them in this particular spot in the mountains for a few years now. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)

File.Name: j215c_j209-021.jpg
Notes: 32165951497 I found this small Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) in my truck last weekend and got this photo on the hood after I moved it outside. (Hah - my truck is cleaner now after all the rain we've been having!) I don't believe the fly is a proper Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family because it doesn't have the long palps that those flies have - see this photo for an example. I think it might be a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae. It has pretty patterned wings like some of those flies have, compare this photo. Insects have been scarce this week, especially flies, so I has happy to find this one - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2019)
CommonNames: Crane Fly; Large Crane Fly; Limoniid Crane Fly

File.Name: j215d_j214-022.jpg
Notes: 46194061335 Here are two more of the odd Jumping Bristletail (Machilidae) insects in the order Microcoryphia that I show in my [Previous] photo. I found them all under the same piece of oak bark in our woodpile. The one I showed yesterday seemed noticeably larger than these two. The more I look at these creatures, the odder they seem. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2019)

File.Name: j216a_j216-004.jpg
Notes:
47118586221 This is an odd mushroom, but it's not so much the mushroom that's odd as where it's growing! It's coming from a past-ripe fruit of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. I believe that it's a Split-Gill fungus in the genus Schizophyllum, but I'm not sure that it's the most common Schizophyllum commune. I usually find that species on dead wood, like in this photo, so finding it on a cactus fruit is quite a shift. If you look close, you can see that the gills are really folds that can open or close like pages in a book. They are open at the moment after our rain yesterday, but they will close again when it dries out. I've read that specimens sealed in a tube in 1911 and then moistened 50 years later unrolled their gills and began shedding spores! This little mushroom is quite small, only about 1/2 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2019)

It was sunny today for a change, but it was also cold and windy with 30+ mph gusts. Our next storm is expected late tonight and tomorrow. This won't be a big rain-producer, but it will be cold enough that it may even bring us some snow. That could be a problem as our oak trees are already weakened by years of drought, and real snow would cause many of them to break. Snow is nice for a photo, but I hope it doesn't happen.

File.Name: j216b_j216-019.jpg
Notes: 40153680143 This is some sort of infection erupting like a boil from this stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). This one shrub has a number of these infections, and I've also seen them on Jim Brush (Ceanothus oliganthus var. sorediatus. I guess that it's the work of a fungus, but I have no idea what kind. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2019)
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus oliganthus; sorediatus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus oliganthus; sorediatus

File.Name: j216c_j214-004.jpg
Notes:
33242726418 These gelatinous blobs look for all the world like frog eggs with embryos inside! I believe they are Myxarium nucleatum, a jelly fungus. what's most striking is the presence of whitish nodules inside the bits of jelly, which apparently are mineral inclusions made of calcium oxalate - see Wikipedia. I found these under a piece of oak bark in our woodpile. Hah - there's a tiny Globular Springtail (Symphypleona, Collembola) moving along across the bottom-center of this photo that I didn't notice at the time. I'll make a note. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2019)

(This fungus looks quite similar to the middle fungus in this triptych of different Witch's Butter fungi. It may be that that one is Exidia thuretiana which is similar but lacks the mineral inclusions.)
ScientificName: Myxarium nucleatum; Symphypleona; Collembola; Exidia thuretiana
associatedTaxa: Myxarium nucleatum; Symphypleona; Collembola; Exidia thuretiana

File.Name: j216d_j212-027.jpg
Notes: 40153312163 Here are more red-capped Russula mushrooms growing in deep mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They are surely in the "Russula emetica" group, maybe Russula silvicola with that white stem or maybe just a red form of the normally cream-colored Russula cremoricolor - see the discussion at MushroomExpert. Whatever they are, they have an "acrid" taste - hot and spicy, like slightly "off" wasabi, not quite pleasant. They are probably poisonous, but they sure are handsome. We also have other Russula's in the woods with caps that aren't so red, see this photo. That makes sense if these are just different color forms of one species. This is when I usually find them, towards the end of our mushroom season. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2019)
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula silvicola; Russula cremoricolor
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula silvicola; Russula cremoricolor

File.Name: j217a_j217-001.jpg
Notes:
47129843551 This little moth has found shelter from the storm today under a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I don't know anything about it, but it might be one of the little brown moths that I call "rain moths" because they are so noticeable after a rain - they collect on our windows at night and keep flying out of my way when I go hiking. I remember them on the windows by the 100s not too many years ago, but there seem to be fewer now. *Sigh* Is this part of the coming insect apocalypse? (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2019)

Another day, another 2/10 inch of rain! We had light but steady rain for much of the day, though I did manage to get outside for a few photos. It is cold, but the rain never turned to snow - a good thing because I don't think our drought-stressed oaks could bear the extra weight of much snow right now. We're still under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory tonight, it's predicted to stay cold all week, and our next chance of rain and snow comes on Thursday.

File.Name: j217b_j217-003.jpg
Notes:
47077341462 Here is a little Mycena mushroom growing in the oak mulch above our retaining wall in light rain this morning. I like that spider web on the left with raindrops - or is it one of my beard hairs?? I never have seen masses of these mushrooms this year like in this photo from a few years ago, just scattered individuals like this. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2019)

(I checked back - it's a hair. Interesting how those things show up everywhere, no wonder hair analysis is so important in forensic toxicology - see Wikipedia.)
Locality: I checked back - it's a hair.  Interesting how those things show up everywhere

File.Name: j217c_j217-009.jpg
Notes: 47077275172 This is the handsome foliage of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) in the rain today. Like my [Previous] photo, I got this shot from from the shelter our covered porch when it was raining pretty hard. We rescued this plant from a slide along Paradise Road many years ago, and it's done well here. We never water it. The leaves have a strong camphor smell. They make good smudge sticks and have a reputation for quieting the dead. Maybe so! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2019)

File.Name: j217d_j217-010.jpg
Notes: 46215354725 Here is a vine of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) climbing over a small Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree, with the dark woodland of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees behind. I got this photo from our covered porch in the rain today - maybe you can see some falling raindrops in the dark area on the left. I've shown this scene before when it was too wet to go out into the rain - see my photos here, here, and here. The last of these photos shows the scene at the end of the growing season. The little tree somehow puts up with being smothered. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2019)
CommonNames: Chaparral Clematis; Madrone; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j218a_j218-003.jpg
Notes:
46227209095 Here are two shots of brand new flower catkins on native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today, showing the separate male pollen-bearing staminate catkins on the left and female seed-bearing pistillate catkins on the right. These small trees are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and the ladies look quite different from the gentlemen. Both are the same species, and they need each other. These catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many insects. This is the latest that I've found the first flowers in the last five years, and I'm not surprised - we've had more weather this year! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2019)

It was sunny today, but cold and windy with gusts over 25 mph for much of the day. It was really too windy for new photos, but I managed to get a few. I'm still not seeing many insects, but I'm not surprised with the icy morning. I reckon insects are sheltering in place until it warms up. It's forecast to stay cold all week, and our next chance of rain (or snow) comes Wednesday night and Thursday.

File.Name: j218b_j218-017.jpg
Notes: 47141233101 Here are the sexual sporophytes of two different kinds of Liverwort (Marchantiophyta ) plants growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. I believe the one on the right is California Liverwort (Asterella californica, which is by far the most common. I believe the one on the left is Palmer's Asterella (Asterella palmeri), which is not so common. It seems to grow in sunnier places, the creeping "leaves" are smaller, and the sporophytes are taller and smoother and less divided - more like helmets than little palm trees. We have more different kinds of liverworts in our woods than I previously thought, see this photo for three more! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2019)
ScientificName: sporophytes; Marchantiophyta ; Asterella californica; Asterella palmeri
associatedTaxa: sporophytes; Marchantiophyta ; Asterella californica; Asterella palmeri

File.Name: j218c_j218-011.jpg
Notes: 47140955501 Here are two very different galls on opposite stems of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. Both are the work of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). I believe the large gall on the right is the gall of the Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens). This one is still smooth, but in time it will develop fissures, and the gall capsules inside will somehow rise to the surface, burst through, and drop to the forest floor - see this photo. This gall is polythalamous - containing many wasp larvae. It is also an integral stem gall that cannot be removed. The small gall on the left might be the gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae), though see this photo. The gall in my present photo looks like the one on the right in that photo - less hairy and with more clasping bracts at the base. It's smaller than a BB. This tiny gall is monothalamous - containing just a single wasp larva. It is also a detachable gall that can be removed - in fact this gall will soon drop away when it is ripe. Like I said, these are two very different galls. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2019)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp; Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Callirhytis perdens; polythalamous; integral; Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; monothalamous; detachable
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Callirhytis perdens; polythalamous; integral; Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; monothalamous; detachable

File.Name: j218d_j218-020.jpg
Notes: 47140848851 Here are raindrops on a new leaf of perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). We had light rain yesterday, but it's been windy since then - not so good for the palmate leaves to hold drops, though that large drop in the center is holding on. Compare this photo from a few years ago. Lupines catch raindrops better than any other plant I know, though it's a mystery to me why some plants hold rain drops so well, while others shed them so fast. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2019)

File.Name: j220a_j220-005.jpg
Notes:
47161439381 I believe this is a tiny Barklouse (Psocoptera) on the curled leaf of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. It's tiny, about 760 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.0 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It seems to be interested in the "leaf roller gall" (my name) on the leaf - see this photo. The rolled leaf looks like there might be a caterpillar inside, but I've opened a few and found aphids instead - see this photo for more information. Also see my [Previous] photo for what may be the start of a gall. I don't notice these barklice very often, though I suspect the woods are full of them. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2019)

I didn't post any photos yesterday because I've been sick. I'm feeling better now, at least not so listless, but still tired enough that I'll only post two photos today. I even got out on a short walk for a few new photos like this one. It was cold and windy, a sign of our newest storm approaching. This won't be a big rain producer, but it's cold enough to snow tonight and tomorrow. We're under a Winter Weather Advisory again tonight.

File.Name: j220b_j216-028.jpg
Notes: 47109277462 This is webbing inside curled leaf of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree - but other than the obvious, I don't know anything else about this. The leaf might be the "leaf roller gall" (my name) that is just forming - see this photo. The rolled leaves look like there might be a caterpillar inside, but I've opened a few and found aphids inside - see this photo. I assume the webbing is made by something else, probably a spider but maybe not. Little mysteries! Webs have been scarce this past week because of cold and rain and wind, so I was happy to find this one for Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2019)

File.Name: j221a_j221-013.jpg
Notes:
47172200881 I found this little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) huddled on a branch of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) when clouds were blocking the sun today. A few minutes later, the sun came out, and the bee took off. At first the bee just looks dark, but if you look closer it becomes deep blue. I don't think it's genus Osmia because it does seem to have pollen-collecting hairs on its rear legs. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, and I welcome suggestions. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2019)

We ended up with just 1/10 inch of rain from our storm today, and no snow - but it was cold and windy, and the radar map still shows some action out in the channel heading away from us. Now the weather looks pretty quiet for the next week, and I can't say I'm sorry. The best news is that Santa Barbara County is no longer in drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor! This is the first time that we've been out of drought - sometimes category "D4 (Exceptional Drought)" - since seven years ago in 2012. Seven years of drought is downright biblical!
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Osmia
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Osmia

File.Name: j221b_j221-020.jpg
Notes: 33296757758 Here are up and down shots of a tiny mosquito-like Dance Fly in the family Empididae, probably genus Empis, working female pistillate flowers on a catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. The willows are just starting to flower. They are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, see this photo. These flies love these flowers, but they are also predators - see this photo for both behaviors. I'll have more photos tomorrow for Fly Day Friday. At least half of this photo counts as a proper "butt shot" - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2019)

File.Name: j221c_j221-004.jpg
Notes: 47119969792 This another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) trying to hide in a leaf cluster of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I reckon it's trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it. (Of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers!) I've been seeing quite a few of these crickets lately, see this photo. I assume they're nocturnal, as I mostly see their rear ends in the day time when they're hiding. Notice that the leaf is getting a curl on the right, probably a developing gall, see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2019)
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Gammarotettix genitalis; Raphidophoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: j221d_j221-007.jpg
Notes: 46448302224 Here is a neat little scene with a plant seedling on the left and liverworts with sporophytes on the right - along with assorted lichens and mosses. I believe the seedling is native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in our canyon, see this photo for the flowers. It's a remarkable plant that flowers all summer. I believe the liverwort is Palmer's Asterella (Asterella palmeri, Marchantiophyta) with smooth sporophytes like little helmets, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2019)
ScientificName: Allophyllum glutinosum; Polemoniaceae; Asterella palmeri; Marchantiophyta
associatedTaxa: Allophyllum glutinosum; Polemoniaceae; Asterella palmeri; Marchantiophyta

File.Name: j222a_j222-007.jpg
Notes:
47129534112 Here is a mating pair of really tiny Midge Flies (Ditera) on a partially decomposed fallen oak leaf. Hah, these two flies together are only about 780 pixels across in this 1:1 macros shot, which comes to 1.5 mm each, less than 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.). They ae probably true Non-biting Midges in the family Chironomidae, but they might be something else too. It's interesting to find a mating pair, and I can make a few observations. Males and females look different. I believe that's the male on the left with the longer, more plumose antennae. The female on the right also seems to have minutely plumose antennae, but they are much shorter. The male is black, but the female is brown. These might be the tiny flies I see dancing in the sunbeams, see this photo and this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2019)

I got this photo today because the highway is still closed near Lake Cachuma. Let me explain. Highway 154 over the mountains has been closed by a blocked culvert since our big rains on February 2, and it's turned out to be a major job to reopen it. I'm not complaining too much. Traffic over San Marcos Pass has gotten steadily worse as more and more people use it as a shortcut to Highway 101 (guided by GPS and the internet?? ), and it's a pleasure to drive with no through traffic. But I had to got to Solvang today for my usual errand, and it's a long drive around. I didn't get home until 2:30 and went looking for a photo. I was happy to find these involved flies - in our shaded-out north slope woods. The highway might reopen in two weeks, weather permitting. If I had to drive the highway daily to get to work like I used to, I'd be celebrating.
Locality: guided by GPS and the internet?? )

File.Name: j222b_j221-024.jpg
Notes: 32239534017 This is another tiny mosquito-like Dance Fly in the family Empididae, probably genus Empis, working female pistillate flowers on a catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. The fly looks metallic is this photo, but I think that's just how the light is hitting it - compare my [Previous] photo. My usual field marks for recognizing these flies is their perfectly round head, tiny neck, and long paddle-like proboscis that is flattened side to side. The willows are just starting to flower. They are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, see this photo. These flies love these flowers, but they are also predators - see this photo for both behaviors. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2019)

File.Name: j222c_j220-008.jpg
Notes: 40216481663 Here is a tiny mosquito-like Dance Fly in the family Empididae, probably genus Empis, working female pistillate flowers on a catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. The willows are just starting to flower. They are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, see this photo. These flies love these flowers, but they are also predators - see this photo for both behaviors. I already have my [Next] two photos for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2019)

File.Name: j222d_j218-001.jpg
Notes: 47129362932 This is a small Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) hanging on to a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I don't believe the fly is a proper Large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family because it doesn't have the long palps that those flies have - see this photo for an example. I think it might be a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae. It has pretty patterned wings like some of those flies have, compare this photo from last week. I've been finding a few of these gangly flies lately, but it's still too early for the proper Large Crane Flies. I already have two other photos for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2019)

File.Name: j223a_j223-016.jpg
Notes:
40227340153 Here are two mating Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae), probably Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes, in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). When I first saw them head on, I thought maybe the bottom spider was eating the top one - see my [Previous] photo! (It happens - see this photo.) They have adopted an interesting position that I don't think I've seen with spiders before. That's the male spider above using his swollen dark palps to transfer sperm to the epigynum of the female below. The female spider almost seems to be embracing the male's head with its palps and chelicerae. It's a sensual moment. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2019)

Today was sunny but still cold, with enough of a breeze to get in the way. It sounds like southern Oregon and northern California are heading into a wet period, but we're going to miss most of it. Southern Oregon needs rain too, so I hope it dumps! Our next chance of rain comes on Wednesday and again with rain likely next weekend - not big storms, but something.
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spiders; Hammock Spiders; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j223b_j223-022.jpg
Notes: 47191976821 When I first saw these spiders, I seriously thought the bottom one was trying to eat the top one. My [Next] photo taken more from the side shows that they are really mating - and there is something downright sensual about what they are doing! They have adopted an interesting position that I don't think I've seen in spiders before. I'm sure they are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae), probably Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes, see this photo. They are in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2019)
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spiders; Hammock Spiders; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j223c_j223-001.jpg
Notes: 33316462658 This is a handsome little moth (Lepidoptera) trying to look like an oak branch. It's actually pretty successful! Its color is actually built up of many color swatches, not at all the uniform gray that you first notice. I don't know what kind of moth it is. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2019)

File.Name: j223d_j223-010.jpg
Notes: 47191761641 Here are the first new flowers of (I believe) our native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in our canyon. These are the shaggy male staminate flowers, and the more delicate female pistillate flowers are barely visible lower down on the stems. I rarely find seeds. It's a remarkably tough grass-like plant that grows in the shaded woods, but also in glaring sun. It's not at all like a typical wet-land sedge, except for the flowers. I really admire the toughness of this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2019)

File.Name: j224a_j224-025.jpg
Notes:
47203458401 Here's another shot of the female Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae), probably a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, that I show in my [Previous] photo. She was the horizontal one of the mating pair that I showed yesterday in this photo. If you look close in today's photo, you can her chelicerae and fangs - and you can still see her slender palps and dingus-like epigynum. I like the symmetry of this shot. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2019)

Today was sunny and even a few degrees warmer - up to 60 for the first time in a while - but there was still enough of a breeze to be a bother. It sounds like southern Oregon and northern California are in a wet period now, but we're going to miss most of it. Southern Oregon is still in drought, so I hope it pours up there! Our next chance of rain comes on Wednesday and again next weekend. The second storm sounds more substantial, but it's still a week away.
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Pityohyphantes; chelicerae; fangs; palps; epigynum
