File.Name: j224b_j224-020.jpg
Notes: 33327815798 I went back today to check on the mating spiders that I showed yesterday in this photo. Both male and female were still there, and though the male spider stayed back in the shadows, the female rushed right out on her web . It's a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae), probably a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This is the female - you can see her slender palps and dingus-like epigynum on the forward-end of her abdomen. They might be going at it again as I write this! I'll check back on them again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2019)
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spider; Hammock Spider; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Pityohyphantes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; palps; epigynum
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; Pityohyphantes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; palps; epigynum

File.Name: j224c_j224-003.jpg
Notes: 47151287542 This is a tiny Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on leaves of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). This is the first of these plant-feeding bugs that I've seen this year, and the plants themselves are just starting to look alive at the start of the growing season. I got this photo today in a place where the sun still never shines at this time of year, and white balance is what it is. We'll finally start seeing some sun in the woods near home in another month, around the time of the equinox. In the meantime, it's been dark and cold - but there are a few critters to be found out there. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2019)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: j224d_j224-006.jpg
Notes: 47151158772 Here are two mushrooms of "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) coming up in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. You can see some of the blushing color on the cap and stem. These aren't really typical Amanitas as they don't have any kind of volval cup on the base, see this photo from January. I've found few mushrooms since the rains let up last week, but I guess this photo shows where they are - hiding deep under the mulch where there is some trapped humidity. They are hard to find - they show their presence only by making the slightest bump in the mulch. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2019)
ScientificName: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: j225a_j225-025.jpg
Notes:
33338676148 This is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on it's new web. Its web is so new that it hasn't accumulated any "trash" yet - mostly the bodies of past prey, look close at this photo. It's small, about 1470 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.8 mm, less than 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This is a male spider with swollen dark palps, compare this photo of a mating pair. They have remarkable bodies - like finely cracked "crazed" porcelain. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2019)

It was sunny and almost warm today - up into 60s for the first time in a while - but there was still enough of a breeze to be a bother. Northern California and Oregon are in a wet period now, but we're going to miss most of it. We have rain likely on Tuesday night and Wednesday from the tail end of that storm. We have another chance of rain next weekend that sounds more substantial.

File.Name: j225b_j225-007.jpg
Notes: 33338554768 Here are new pollen-bearing staminate catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek - with many tiny mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera), probably genus Empis. See this recent photo for a closer shot of a fly, but it's interesting how many of them there are on the flowers here. I count 17 flies, and there are no doubt more on the back sides of the stamens. These flies do love the flowers, but they are also predators - see this photo for both behaviors. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2019)
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Empididae; Diptera; Empis
associatedTaxa: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Empididae; Diptera; Empis

File.Name: j225c_j225-005.jpg
Notes: 47213910871 I found this pair of legs sticking out of a rolled oak leaf today - like the wicked witch after Dorothy's house fell on her in Oz! I'm not sure whose legs these are - but they are insect legs, and they might well be the legs of another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) . They do like to hide in dark corners of the oak leaves, see this photo. I think I can see one of the typical bumps on its rear end back in the shadows. On the other hand, I can't recall seeing a cricket with such striking black and white stripes - like long socks! I got this photo just as the light was fading in this place. It's remarkable how fast the light changes as the sun moves through the north-slope trees. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2019)

File.Name: j225d_j223-004.jpg
Notes: 47213803211 I was standing in front of a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) when I saw this moth land on the other side of a leaf. It's tail was sticking out, so I got this quick shot. It was gone a moment later, and I never really got a look at it. It looks like a woven rug. I'm sure it counts as a "micro-moth", and I'm hoping someone here can identify it from this fragment alone. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2019)

File.Name: j226a_j226-049.jpg
Notes:
46500838754 Look close! Here are new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family - with a little Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) bridging the gap between the flowers. It's growing in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year - and maybe never shines at any time of year. It seems there should be more of these than there are with the decent rains we've been having. Maybe the recent cold weather is slowing them down? They are beautiful flowers, more elegant than our usual yellow "wild mustards" that are mostly non-native. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2019)

Clouds moved in today, pushed along by winds from the south. I went to a place along Stagecoach Road that is somewhat sheltered and got a few photos. The signs looks good for a rain - the frogs are even chorusing tonight! This first storm tomorrow does not sound like much, but a more potent storm is behind it for this weekend.
ScientificName: Cardamine californica; Brassicaceae; Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Cardamine californica; Brassicaceae; Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: j226b_j226-021.jpg
Notes: 46500708914 Here are lichens, mosses, ferns, and new wildflowers, oh my! (There were also mushrooms growing near the base of shrub on the right.) The flowers are Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. The red color comes mostly from leafy bracts, but there are tubular flowers in there too. The ferns are California Polypody (Polypodium californicum, Polypodiaceae), and the moss and lichens are what they are. The plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, no doubt Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) which is visible to the right. I also find the plants under Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to the roots of their host species. They come up mushroom-like, all ready to flower, see this photo of the same scene taken a month ago. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2019)
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; California Polypody; Bigberry Manzanita; Chamise
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Polypodium californicum; Polypodiaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Polypodium californicum; Polypodiaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; haustoria

File.Name: j226c_j226-006.jpg
Notes: 46500635254 Here are the first new flowers I've seen his year on a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. The flowers look a lot like the manzanita flowers in my [Previous] photo - both are in the same plant family, though these are puffier and more oblate. This might be my favorite tree in our mountains. Hah, see my Naked lady Madrone photo of a tree that looks like a lady. It's one of my most viewed photos, but probably just because its name pops up in so many silly searches! (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2019)

File.Name: j226d_j226-033.jpg
Notes: 47171452712 I found this Amanita mushroom growing in loose soil under a small oak. It does have warts on the cap which are easily rubbed away, and it doesn't have much of a ring or volva. At first I thought it might be our white Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata), but the lack of a volva pretty much rules that out. It seems to be too white for The Blusher (A. novinupta), and it doesn't stain with handling. This one might be Amanita sponsus, see this photo - though that mushroom has a more distinct partial veil ring. There's some info at the Amanitaceae web site. This might also the the one I've called Amanita silvicola in the past, compare this photo and Michelle's comment. By the way, this is our other live oak - probably Interior Live Oak (Quercus wislizeni var. frutescens) or Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei). One difference that's visible in this photo is that the leaves are flat, not cupped like Q. agrifolia. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2019)
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita ocreata; A. novinupta; Amanita sponsus; Amanita silvicola; Quercus wislizeni var. frutescens; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: j227a_j227-004.jpg
Notes:
46509915884 Here are raindrops on new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica, Brassicaceae) surrounded by leaves of perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). It was too wet to take a real walk today, but I found these plants above our retaining wall - and got this photo in the drizzle. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2019)

We've had almost 1/4 inch of drizzle today - not a lot of measurable rain, but there was drizzle all day, and its still going as I write. We should have a couple of days to dry out, and then the next storm comes on Saturday. And our next storm after that comes in about a week. I like the way this is going! All this rain activity is wonderful after so many recent drought years.

File.Name: j227b_j226-004.jpg
Notes: 46509819234 I found this tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). The flower was whipping around in the wind yesterday, and this photo came out better than I expected. It's a pretty little bee with a metallic bronze color. Maybe it's genus Lasioglossum - but maybe not. Any help with the ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2019)

File.Name: j227c_j225-010.jpg
Notes: 32291145157 This is the web of the female Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) that I show mating in my [Previous] photo. You can actually see the spider in the upper-left, I'll make a note. I believe this is a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The web is slightly dome-shaped, held up in the middle with extensive scaffolding that is not visible in my photo. It looks kind of haphazard at first but imagine trying to make one. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2019)
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spider; Hammock Spider; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j227d_j223-015.jpg
Notes: 47181073612 Here is another shot of the two mating Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) that I showed a couple of days ago in this photo. I believe these are Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes, in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They have adopted an interesting position that I don't think I've seen with spiders before. That's the male spider above using his swollen dark palps to transfer sperm to the epigynum of the female below. (Maybe you can even see a drop of sperm on the palp?) The female spider almost seems to be embracing the male's head with its palps and chelicerae. It's a sensual moment. Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2019)

File.Name: j228a_j228-016.jpg
Notes:
40277987853 This is another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) finding some shelter from the weather on (and under) a narrow leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Phrymaceae plant family. I've been finding quite a few of these crickets this year, and this one seems smaller than most - definitely less than 1/4 inch long. I had the thought that it might be something else - but I don't think so. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2019)

I was surprised when I checked the rain gauge this morning and found 9/10 inch - most of it from last night! A whole day and night of drizzle adds up. We were in a cloud today, and it was surprisingly calm. Our next 100% chance of rain comes on Saturday, which is expected to bring more rain. Then there is another storm next week on Wednesday that might tap into an "atmospheric river" and deliver some real rain! Or maybe not - it's still a week away.
CommonNames: Chaparral Camel Cricket; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: j228b_j228-001.jpg
Notes: 46519226134 I found this tiny true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a cactus fruit in the garden this morning. What pretty colors it has! The pale green colors suggest that it is teneral - recently emerged from its final molt from nymph to adult - but that's just a guess. With those four-segmented antennae, it might a real Plant Bug in the family Miridae. It's small, about 950 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.7 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2019)

File.Name: j228c_j225-002.jpg
Notes: 47242844041 Here is the rear end of a medium-size spider that has its head buried in its retreat under a dead oak leaf. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is. It has a web like a Sheet Web Weaver, though the spider seems too large and dark to be one of those, and I haven't yet got a look at its face to check its eye arrangement. Whatever it is, here is its rear end for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2019)

File.Name: j228d_j221-005.jpg
Notes: 32300575187 This is the rear end of another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) trying to hide under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Hah - it seems to have radio antennae on its butt! This is the same cricket I showed a week ago in this photo. I added that one to the Beautiful Bug Butt group, and I'll do the same with this one - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2019)

File.Name: j301a_j301-056.jpg
Notes:
46338162085 This is a tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see it's mighty proboscis folded under its head and the prominent spines on its front legs and head. They are gnarly-looking predators, though they are shy and retiring in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)

We were in a cloud again today, and we did get a few sprinkles as our next storm approaches. We're expecting heavy rain late tonight and tomorrow - and again next Tuesday and Wednesday. *Sigh* The highway over the mountains reopened today after being closed by a blocked culvert due to big rains a month ago. Traffic over San Marcos Pass has gotten steadily worse as more and more people use it as a shortcut to Highway 101 (guided by GPS and the internet?? ), and it's been a pleasure to drive with no through traffic. At least it will be much easier to get to the valley.
Locality: guided by GPS and the internet?? )
CommonNames: Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j301b_j301-039.jpg
Notes: 47200475952 Here are two shots of mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera), probably genus Empis, on pistillate catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. Both flies are clutching other smaller flies, which are probably "nuptial gifts" to entice potential mates. It's my impression that the gifts are often wrapped in silk like little balloons, but I've seen no sign of that. (There are photos of this at BugGuide, towards the bottom of the page.) The flies look large in my photos, but they are only about 1/4 inch long, and their prey are tiny. It would be quite a photo to show a large Robber Fly with one of these Dance Flies with an even tinier prey-fly! These flies are usually described as predators, but I find them mostly working these flowers. I guess they are predators too. There are actually four flies in his photo - so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)
ScientificName: Empididae; Diptera; Empis; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Empididae; Diptera; Empis; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: j301c_j301-035.jpg
Notes: 47200339332 This Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) has its web in an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) tree by the creek. It's the same tree where I've been finding the tiny mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera), see my [Next] photo and this photo. It seems the spider has been finding them too! I think I see two flies in the larder, but they are partially digested and hard to make out. I believe this is another Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes - the same kind I've been showing mating, e.g. in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)

File.Name: j301d_j301-011.jpg
Notes: 47252192241 I found this lovely mass of slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) on a pile of old wood chips that are all that's left of a large oak tree that came down across a neighbor's road a few years ago, see this photo. I guess these are the well-named Insect-egg Slime Mold (Leocarpus fragilis) - though I suppose they could also be something else. They seem to be just coming out of their plasmodium stage to form proper sporangia. I've been looking for these, and I was happy to find them today. I hope the rain that's expected tonight doesn't wash them all away. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)

File.Name: j302a_j302-006.jpg
Notes:
40297380323 This is "just a fly" on a spiny cactus in the garden - with raindrops from the drizzle this afternoon. The fly might be bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera), but who knows. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2019)

Most of our rain was done by noon, but there was pretty steady drizzle for the rest of the day. I tried to take a walk but didn't get very far - it was just too wet for my camera. We ended up with 2.2 inches, a bit more than was forecast. That brings our total for the water year (since September) to over 40 inches, about 118% of normal for the year - and more rain is coming next week! I'm smiling after so many recent drought years.

File.Name: j302b_j301-054.jpg
Notes: 47210060002 Here is another tiny Barklouse (Psocoptera) beneath a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The leaf forms a natural light tent for the insect, a nice effect even on a cloudy day. The critter is tiny, only about 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This is the second one I've found lately, compare this photo. I don't notice these barklice very often, though I suspect the woods are full of them. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)

File.Name: j302c_j301-022.jpg
Notes: 46347292125 Here is a medium-size spider in its retreat under a dead oak leaf - the same spider I showed the rear end of in this photo a few days ago. Looking at its eyes and web, I think it might be a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae), possibly genus Tegenaria - though that's just my guess. It has a cupped leaf for a shelter, and it might have even stayed dry during our storm today. I'll check on it tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)

File.Name: j302d_j301-020.jpg
Notes: 32319776787 This is the slime from whence a slime mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) comes, forming on old wood chips. I believe this is the plasmodial slime that will become the sporangia that I showed in this photo yesterday - probably the well-named Insect-egg Slime Mold (Leocarpus fragilis). As I understand it, slime molds are really aggregations of amoeba-like single-celled protists that come together to form the structures known as sporangia to distribute their spores. My photo today shows the plasmodium which is an intermediate stage. It is a multi-nucleate mass where the cell walls separating individual cells dissolve and then reform to produce the spore-bearing sporangia. The spores disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)
ScientificName: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Leocarpus fragilis; sporangia; plasmodium
associatedTaxa: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Leocarpus fragilis; sporangia; plasmodium

File.Name: j303a_j303-030.jpg
Notes:
40309689433 Here is another mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera), probably genus Empis, on a leaf bud of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. The fly is clutching another tiny fly, probably a "nuptial gift" to entice potential mates - see this photo for more info. I was watching another fly with a gift on a nearby flower catkin, when its captive got loose - it's alive! I wonder is that's a condition to attract a mate? The fly looks large in my photo, but it is only about 1/4 inch long, and its prey is tiny. These flies are usually described as predators, but I mostly find them working the willow flowers. I guess they really are predators too. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2019)

It was mostly overcast today, but there were moments of sunshine. Hah, there were also moments of drizzle! It was a nice day with little breeze and my favorite kind of light. It sounds like we have one more nice day tomorrow before our next storm moves in on Tuesday and Wednesday. The forecast is that this will be a real storm with an "atmospheric river" aka "pineapple express" subtropical connection. Maybe up to five inches total?
ScientificName: Empididae; Diptera; Empis; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: j303b_j303-049.jpg
Notes: 46359600975 My, what large "humps" this little spider has! I found it on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It's surely an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the genus Araneus, but I'm not sure what kind. It's tiny, only about 840 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's small size suggests Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, but those over-size humps don't seem right. Maybe it's a juvenile of our large Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides spiders? Whatever it is, it's a cute spider. In the shot on the right, you can see it touching the line that runs to its web - like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing, ready to strike. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2019)
ScientificName: Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus bispinosus; A. montereyensis; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: j303c_j303-012.jpg
Notes: 47273940891 This tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider has found shelter from the storm under a group of spines on a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. These spiders are quite distinctive with that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. This particular spider is actually hiding under a clump of glochid spines - those short hair-like prickles that are barbed and and very difficult to remove, there's more info at Wikipedia. That little patch of green above the spider is a lichen. Our wet weather seems to be encouraging the lichens to reproduce. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2019)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Prickly Pear; lichen

File.Name: j303d_j303-015.jpg
Notes: 47273827271 I went back to the pile of old wood chips looking for slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) today to update my photos here and here. I found this group today, but I'm not sure it's the same thing. That seems to be a problem studying these slime molds - they develop so fast that if you miss a day because of rain, you might miss them completely. I'll see if I can find this one again tomorrow to see what it becomes. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2019)

File.Name: j304a_j304-035.jpg
Notes:
47284763711 My first thought was that these are the mushrooms formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila aka Gymnopus dryophilus) - though the gills don't seem right, adnate or slightly decurrent rather than adnexed. Maybe they are the Fried Chicken Mushroom (Lyophyllum decastes)? They do have copious white mycelium with root-like rhizomorphs at the base, visible at the base of the upturned mushrooms on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2019)

It was mostly cloudy today with moments of sun, and mostly calm with moments of breeze. Now we're getting ready for our new storm which sounds like a whopper. The forecast is for 3-5 inches in the mountains, though it's an "atmospheric river" event with some uncertainty about the exact path. Rain is expected to start Tuesday afternoon, and I hope I'll have a chance to get a few photos in the morning. We're under a NWS Flash Flood Watch on Tuesday night and evacuation orders have gone out for areas below the burn areas. Hang on!
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Lyophyllum decastes
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Lyophyllum decastes

File.Name: j304b_j304-011.jpg
Notes: 40320056843 These are new sporangia of a slime mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) rising from their plasmodial slime on an old wood chip in the same pile as my [Previous] photo. It's obviously a different slime mold, stalked instead of sessile, but is you look close you can see that the sporangia are also developing a lime (calcium carbonate) crust which indicates the order Physarales. Maybe they are the same genus Didymium that I've shown before - see this photo and this photo from last December. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2019)

File.Name: j304c_j304-008.jpg
Notes: 33408933708 Here are two shots of slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) taken yesterday and today - how quickly they change! Both photos are taken of the same fallen leaf on wood chips of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), though I moved the leaf so the backgrounds are different. If you look close, you can actually match the sporangia on the left and right. The pretty ones from yesterday look like the ones I called the Insect-egg Slime Mold (Leocarpus fragilis) in this photo, but now I'm not so sure. You can see today's sporangia developing a lime (calcium carbonate) crust which indicates the order Physarales. I won't try to guess beyond that. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2019)
CommonNames: slime molds; Coast Live Oak; Insect-egg Slime Mold
ScientificName: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Leocarpus fragilis; Physarales
associatedTaxa: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Leocarpus fragilis; Physarales

File.Name: j304d_j304-005.jpg
Notes: 33408861038 Here are a pair of Cup Fungi (Pezizaceae or kin) growing on old wood chips that are all that's left of a large oak tree that came down across a neighbor's road a few years ago, see this photo. Maybe this odd mushroom is the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda or kin) which is said to grow on wood and wood chips. They are a couple of inches across. They've been here for a while, I show a young one in this photo from early January. The cups expand and become more ragged as they age. (Don't we all.) There is no stalk and no ribs at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2019)
CommonNames: Cup Fungi; Spreading Brown Cup Fungus

File.Name: j305a_j305-008.jpg
Notes:
40329937073 This strange scene is looking close at the new sporangia of the slime mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) that I showed yesterday in this photo. I got this photo today, but they look about the same as they did yesterday. They are on a fallen oak leaf in a pile of old wood chips, less than a millimeter across, and appear to have a lime (calcium carbonate) crust. They contain the spores of the single-celled protists that form them, and they will disperse to fly "mother nature's silver seed to a new home in the sun" - as Neil Young sings. See my comments to this photo for more details. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2019)

I started to do my usual walk today, but I was caught short by early rain at noon. It was curious that the first rain felt downright tropical with large drops and a hint of sunlight behind the clouds. That soon changed to small drops and dark skies. We've had about 1/4 inch of rain so far. The big rains are expected later tonight and tomorrow. We're under a NWS Flash Flood Watch tonight and evacuation orders have gone out for areas below the burn areas. Hang on!

(Yipes - thunder and lightning at 7:00 pm . . . )

File.Name: j305b_j305-005.jpg
Notes: 47242141882 Here are two more shots of slime molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) taken yesterday and today - how quickly they change! I found these on an old wood chip in a pile. They are different from the ones I showed yesterday in this photo, though I suppose they might be the same kind. The photo on the left taken yesterday shows the streaming slime-like plasmodium - if you look close, you can see a network of connections through which it is moving. In the photo on the right taken today, the plasmodium has all been allocated to the developing sporangia. It's interesting that there is not an exact match between the two photos, though I can see a correspondance - as though some of the new sporangia on the left were absorbed by others. I'll try to find them again tomorrow to see what they become, though heavy rain is expected later tonight, and I might not be able to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2019)

File.Name: j305c_j304-026.jpg
Notes: 47242047492 I was trying to get a photo of these new Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) flowers when I suddenly noticed this mating pair of tough-looking Spiny Assassin Bugs (Sinea, Reduviidae) looking right at me. I did a quick shift of focus and got the photo before they vanished behind the flowers. I've been seeing a few of these predators lately, see this photo, but I've never caught them in the act before. Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2019)

File.Name: j305d_j304-020.jpg
Notes: 46379398335 This is a little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) pretending to be a stick between two leaves of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). That's its tail on top with two pairs of prolegs, and its head down below with three pairs of true legs. Pretty good camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2019)

File.Name: j306a_j306-009.jpg
Notes:
32363051737 This is a small Harvestman (Opiliones) spider on the partial-veil ring of The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom in my [Previous] photo. I found it on the mushroom after I picked it for my photo. These harvestmen are built differently from true spiders with just one body part and only one pair of eyes. This is actually a "butt shot" of the little spider, but it seems pretty obscure for the BBBT group. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2019)

Our storm last night started with a bang - thunder and lightning and hail and rain! I started to add a comment to my first photo last night, when the power finally went out and cut me off. There were apparently 1000s of lightning strikes over the Santa Barbara area - pretty exciting with the lights out! There are some photos at local news sites Edhat and Noozhawk. We ended up with just over 2-1/2 inches of rain. That's less than the 3-5 inches that was forecast for the mountains, but I'm not complaining. We had off-and-on showers today, and they might continue for another day or two.

File.Name: j306b_j306-005.jpg
Notes: 33428962248 Here are two shots of "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) coming up in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. You can see the blushing color that these mushrooms develop - from handling but also from age. This mushroom has a prominent ring, but it doesn't have any kind of volval cup on the swollen base. Mushrooms still seem scarce after all the rain we've been having lately, but I hope they're getting ready to appear. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2019)
ScientificName: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: j306c_j303-025.jpg
Notes: 47304714791 Here are raindrops on a spider web after a storm last weekend. It's probably the web of another Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider in a different small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) from my [Previous] photo. If you look close, you can see the woods reflected in the drops. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2019)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j306d_j228-014.jpg
Notes: 47252127882 This is looking up into the tangled web of a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I got this photo last week after a night of drizzle, and there are still a few drops in the web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2019)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j307a_j307-020.jpg
Notes:
32372011777 I believe from the web and eye arrangement that this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider. It's sitting on its web under a shrub of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Phrymaceae plant family. I tried to get closer and *whoop* the spider vanished back into the shadows. I have another recent shot of a similar spider in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2019)

The big rains are done for now, but there's still moisture in the air, and we had some showers today. I could feel it getting colder. The sun is expected to out for the next couple of days, but wind might be an issue. We have another chance of a small rain on Sunday.
CommonNames: Funnel Web Weaver; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: j307b_j307-026.jpg
Notes: 46590471394 Here is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) grooming its wings on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - with its butt up in the air. These wasps have no sign of a "wasp waist" - but sawflies are like that, see this photo from last year. This is the first of these insects I've managed to photograph this year, but I'm sure they will become increasingly common as we move into spring. The caterpillar-like larvae become real pests of our oak trees in some years, see this photo. Because of the way I got this photo, I had to rotate the image almost 180∞. It's not the sharpest photo, but it shows the wasp's rear end - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2019)

File.Name: j307c_j301-060.jpg
Notes: 46590381974 This another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) trying to hide under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - and exposing its rear end in the process. I've been finding a lot of these tiny crickets lately, usually in a similar pose, They are perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but I've been showing too many of them lately, e.g. this one last week and this one the week before. I won't add this one to the group, but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)

File.Name: j307d_j301-048.jpg
Notes: 46590316734 Here are a couple of mosquito-like Dance Flies (Empididae, Diptera), probably genus Empis, on a pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. Most of the fly in front is not in focus except for its striped rear end - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2019)

File.Name: j308a_j308-023.jpg
Notes:
33447494458 Here is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). See my [Previous] photo for the other side of this butterfly. I saw dozens of these butterflies today flying steadily north, mostly in groups of two or three, but this was the only one I saw that was standing still. I reckon this is the start of a migration moving north from Mexico - and sure enough, there are news stories at Google News with titles like "Why are there so many butterflies in Southern California right now?" in the Pasadena Star-News and "Butterflies are taking over Palm Springs, Coachella Valley thanks to winter rains" in the Desert Sun. (There must be a joke here about immigration, but I refuse!) I remember years ago we were coming back from Joshua Tree and encountered a mass migration of these butterflies - many 1000s of them - slowing down traffic on the I-10 highway and piling up on car grills and windshields. I don't know if this migration is so massive, but its underway. It's a good thing too, as I didn't see a single Painted Lady in all of 2018 - the last photo I got was on November 11, 2017. Welcome back ladies! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2019)

It was sunny today, but chilly and quite windy with 20-30 mph gusts for most of the day. That's really too windy for my kind of photos, so I has happy to get these nice butterfly shots. I hope the winds die down tonight.

It has five small eyespots, with the second and fifth being larger than the other three. (Counting from the front, the first eyespot is smallest.) The American Lady butterfly looks somewhat similar, but only has two larger eyespots below, compare this photo. I saw dozens of these butterflies today flying steadily north, but this was the only one I saw that was standing still. I reckon this is the start of a migration moving north from Mexico. See my [Next] photo for the other side of this butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2019)

Notes: 40358148043 Here is another mosquito-like Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera), probably genus Empis, perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I usually find these flies in the nearby willows, but I guess this one is waiting out a rain shower before getting back to work. If you look close, you can see that the fly is actually standing in water that has pooled up along the edge of the leaf. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2019)

File.Name: j308d_j306-016.jpg
Notes: 32381161837 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) on a pollen-bearing staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. Well, maybe it's not just a fly - after all, it's working a native flower well away from any houses and not looking for garbage. It's quite bristly. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2019)

File.Name: j309a_j309-042.jpg
Notes:
46418351335 Here is another shot of two mating Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) - different spiders from the ones I showed a couple of weeks ago in this photo. I believe these are also Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes, in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's interesting to compare this arrangement with the one in my previous photo because in a way their positions are reversed. That's the male spider on the right using his swollen dark palps to transfer sperm to the dingus-like epigynum of the larger female on the left. (Is that a drop of spider sperm on his palp??) The female spider seems to be embracing the male's head with her palps and chelicerae. It's a sensual moment. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2019)

Clouds were moving in today, and it was chilly and breezy - though not as windy as yesterday. I guess this is our newest storm moving in, though not much rain is expected. I'll admit that I'm ready for some warm and dry weather!

File.Name: j309b_j309-016.jpg
Notes: 46418224705 This is some spider's exuviae on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - the shed skin after the molt. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is - maybe the Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider in this photo? That word "exuviae" sounds plural, but it is the preferred word even in the singular. It occurs to me that the word "remains" as in "someone's remains" works the same way. There's some discussion of this at BugGuide here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2019)

File.Name: j309c_j309-038.jpg
Notes: 46418045715 Here is a different Dance Fly (Empididae, Diptera), on a pollen-bearing staminate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. It's black and only about half the size of the the Dance Flies I've been showing, e.g. in this photo. The larger one is about 1500 pixels long in a 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.9 mm, almost 1/4 inch. This small one is about 850 pixels long, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. Despite the difference in size, this one is still instantly recognizably with its round head and paddle-like proboscis. Maybe it's genus Rhamphomyia as opposed to genus Empis? (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2019)

File.Name: j309d_j309-024.jpg
Notes: 33456985598 My first thought was that this is another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) stretched out on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) - but it seems too different. It seems flatter and less chubby, it has patterned eyes, and the two little "horns" on its rear end seem more pointed, compare this photo taken on the next day. It's might be the same kind of cricket, but maybe not - at least it is a Long-horned Orthopteran in the suborder Ensifera. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2019)

File.Name: j310a_j310-043.jpg
Notes:
47291476892 I found this little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) huddled on a new leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) on a dark day with rain threatening. The photo came out better than I expected. The fly is tiny, only about 1200 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.7 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I've seen these little flies early in the season before, see this photo and this photo. I believe this fly is tribe Phthiriini and maybe genus Acreophthiria - compare this photo at BugGuide. Nice eyes and great whiskers! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2019)

Clouds have been piling up and threatening rain today, but so far its been dry. There's still a 30% chance of rain tonight, and we're under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory. There was one of those annoying breezes today that keeps everything in motion and makes getting new photos difficult.

File.Name: j310b_j310-007.jpg
Notes: 46429066265 Here is another tiny Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis, Raphidophoridae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). If you compare the photo I posted yesterday, you can see that that one is clearly different - look especially at the shape and the eyes. I'm not even sure if that one is a cricket - it may be some other kind of long-horned Orthopteran. I was happy to get this photo today as a reminder of how different the other one is. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2019)

File.Name: j310c_j310-017.jpg
Notes: 47343882971 Here are a couple of Fluted Black Elfin Saddle mushrooms, distinctive with a fluted stem and dark cap. I've always called identified this mushroom as Helvella lacunosa, but that species has now been split up. This one might be Helvella dryophila which is associated with hardwoods like the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees I found them under today. I find something very similar in Oregon under conifers, see this photo on the left. I call these "brain mushrooms" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. Sometimes when you first touch the cap, you can see a little puff of spores being released. They are just starting to fruit in the canyon, though I've seen a few elsewhere, see this photo. This is their usual time, towards the end of the mushroom season. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2019)

File.Name: j310d_j310-012.jpg
Notes: 47291125182 This odd mushroom has cinnamon-colored gills and a long stalk that seems to comes from some kind of branch at the bottom. The stalk is longer than the section I got. I put my four inch long pocket knife (and digging tool) in the photo for scale. I guess that this is some kind of genus Phaeocollybia, though I'm not sure what kind. They are apparently most common in the Pacific Northwest under conifers, but I found this one in sandy soil near Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm just guessing about its identity. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2019)

File.Name: j311a_j311-018.jpg
Notes:
47354561111 I found this handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) hiding on a branch of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) today. (It was hiding under a leaf, but I bent the stem over to get this photo.) Maybe it's genus Platypedia? The green colors suggest that it is teneral - recently emerged from its final molt from nymph to adult. I'm not sure if these early cicadas sing at all, they might just make the clicking noise that I've been hearing up in the oaks. I believe we have different cicadas later in summer that do sing, but I have a hard time hearing cicadas. They sing at the the same frequency as my tinnitus, and I just can't pick them out. But it's not so bad to have a personal background chorus of cicadas! I don't find these insects very often, but sometimes I'm lucky - see my photos here and here. I left this one alone for a while while I got photos of the butterfly in my [Previous] shot, and it was gone when I came back. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2019)

We had moments of sun today, and also moments of clouds. There was even a time when it felt like it might rain, but it passed. It was also noticeably warmer today, up to 60∞ for the first time in quite a while.
ScientificName: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Platypedia; teneral

File.Name: j311b_j311-024.jpg
Notes: 47354463751 I found this somewhat tattered California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly getting water from the rock today - a sandstone outcrop in this case. It's a kind of vertical puddling, as the rock holds a lot of rain water for a surprisingly long time. These butterflies overwinter as adults and are active on warm winter days like we had today, with temps up to at least 60 degrees. This specimen looks like its been around. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2019)

File.Name: j311c_j311-046.jpg
Notes: 46439420735 I believe these odd mushrooms are the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum) growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see the ribs on the outside of the cap, which extend almost to the cap margin. As author David Arora says, "this species looks like a cross between a cup fungus and an elfin saddle." I have recent photos of an elfin saddle in this photo and a Peziza cup fungus in this photo, and you can see the similarities. But the ribs of this elfin cup are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2019)

File.Name: j311d_j311-037.jpg
Notes: 47354250531 Here are two shots of what I believe is the beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom. I put my four inch long pocket knife (and digging tool) in the photo for scale. I found several of these yesterday growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees, but there were also Madrone and Ceanothus nearby. I thought at first that it might be something else because the volva is not well-developed, but maybe I left part of it in the ground when I picked it. They are beautiful, but deadly poison. They are easy to recognize: pure white cap and gills, like a styrofoam cup in the woods; free gills; membranous ring or "skirt" on the stem; and the characteristic "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. Don't eat them! That goes for dogs too - a neighbor lost a beloved dog to one of these mushrooms. However, it's not dangerous to touch or admire them. They are probably the most elegant mushroom in our woods, very beautiful. I haven't found one of these mushrooms in the woods since 2015, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2019)

File.Name: j312a_j312-006.jpg
Notes:
32422328597 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with an even tinier fly (Diptera) for prey in the throat of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). I believe the spider is another genus Mecaphesa spider with hairy legs and abdomen, and the fly is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae). It's been god-awful windy today, so I went to a place that is somewhat sheltered, and I still had to take fifty shots to get this keeper. This was one of those days when I'm really grateful for digital cameras. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2019)

Like I said, it was really windy today with 40 mph gusts. That's really too windy for my kind of close photos - when you magnify the subject, you also magnify any movement, so everything gets blurred. I did my best, and I hope the wind dies down tonight.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; fly; Prickly-Phlox; Bee Fly

File.Name: j312b_j312-018.jpg
Notes:
46640872584 The mass migration of Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterflies is still happening, and I had to drive to town today on an errand, and . . . splat! This is all that's left of an unfortunate butterfly on my windshield, and it's not just this one. See this photo from a few days ago for what the butterfly should look like. I remember years ago we were coming back from a school trip to Joshua Tree and encountered a mass migration of these butterflies - many 1000s of them - slowing down traffic on the I-10 highway and piling up on car grills and windshields, their bright yellow remains looking quite striking. There's more information about this migration at Google News. I suspect this is why a few butterfly collectors with a net and killing jar really don't do significant damage to the populations. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2019)

Update from VC Star on March 16, 2019:

"The migrating painted ladies are fueled by a packet of yellow abdominal fat, stored from teir caterpillar days, that enables them to fly hundreds of miles without stopping. (That fat is responsible for the yellow streaks on your car windshield if you happen to run into one.) When the fat reserve runs out, they will stop and breed. The next generation will continue the trek north.
Locality: That fat is responsible for the yellow streaks on your car windshield if you happen to run into one.) When the fat reserve runs out

File.Name: j312c_j309-044.jpg
Notes: 47364069971 Here is another shot of two mating Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) from a couple of days ago, see this photo. I believe these are Hammock Spiders in the genus Pityohyphantes, in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). That's the male spider on the right using his swollen palps to transfer sperm to the dingus-like epigynum of the larger female on the left - see my earlier photo for more info. Is that (relatively) large drop on the male's palp really spider sperm? (Why so much??) The female spider seems to be embracing the male's head with her palps and chelicerae. It's a sensual moment. Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2019)
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spiders; Hammock Spiders; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Pityohyphantes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; palps; epigynum

File.Name: j312d_j311-007.jpg
Notes: 32422067537 This is another shot of the Cicada (family Cicadidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) that I show in my [Previous] photo. This is as I found it, hiding under leaves of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I tried nudging it out, and it didn't want to move, so I bent the stem over to get the other photo. Maybe it's genus Platypedia? I suspect that it's teneral - recently emerged from its final molt from nymph to adult - though you can't really tell why from this photo. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2019)

File.Name: j313a_j313-016.jpg
Notes:
46458303935 I found this Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly today where it was puddling on damp sandstone. This butterfly has no strong markings below, but you can see a bit of the blue upper wings where its wing is torn. These butterflies were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from back east. This is the first new butterfly I've managed to photograph this year that isn't overwintering from last year. Butterflies and wildflowers both seem late this year, no doubt because of all the weather we've been having. I hope they both make up for their late start. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2019)

It was still windy this morning, but then the winds started to subside. It was curious that the winds also changed direction, coming largely from the south rather than the north like yesterday. I don't know what that means, but tomorrow might be a very nice day.
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; Spring Azure

File.Name: j313b_j313-010.jpg
Notes: 47320410182 Here is another California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly getting water from the rock - a sandstone outcrop in this case. It's a kind of vertical puddling, as the rock holds a lot of rain water for a surprisingly long time. These butterflies overwinter as adults and are active on warm winter days like we had today - but it's odd that this specimen looks so pristine. It even has the blue spots on the trailing edge of its hindwings which usually disappear pretty soon. Could it be newly emerged? (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2019)

File.Name: j313c_j312-017.jpg
Notes: 33496760288 The pointy forewings of this butterfly indicate that it is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) - a female. It was an interesting find yesterday because it's the first one I've seen for some time, and because it's working new flowers of Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. I've seen this combination before, see this photo from 2012, but it's not commonplace. I believe these butterflies overwinter as adults, but I don't see them flying around in winter very often - much less than the California Tortoiseshell in my [Next] photo. It was windy yesterday, and this isn't the best photo - but it's a record shot. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2019)

File.Name: j313d_j311-043.jpg
Notes: 46457938875 This is the web of a Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) - a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's actually the web of the female spider I show mating in this photo a couple of weeks ago, and it's the same web I show in this photo. You can see that this web is a broad dome held up by extensive rigging lines. These lines probably do double duty - holding up the web, but also knocking down flying insects. I got this photo two days ago, but the web was pretty much destroyed by the big winds yesterday. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2019)
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spiders; Hammock Spider; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: j314a_j314-014.jpg
Notes:
47329041452 This is another tiny nymph of a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It looks like it's wearing carefully-fitted armor that lets it move - and I suppose that's just what an exoskeleton is! This critter is tiny, about 1160 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.5 mm, not quite 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This might be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring, but I think they usually have jet-like hairs coming out of their rear ends like this. Maybe they only develop their tails as adolescents - like growing a beard? It's interesting that I have another photo of a similar nymph on the same shrub from earlier this year. I guess they like it here. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2019)

It was a very nice day with temps in the mid-60s. We may even get up to the 70s this weekend, and I suppose that's proper because spring officially starts next week. (There's also a chance of rain for the equinox next Wednesday!) There was a breeze today - with gusty moments - mostly from the south. Maybe these were "eddy winds" wrapping around Point Conception or spill-over from the Santa Ana winds that are happening down south?
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: j314b_j314-007.jpg
Notes: 32440215457 I found this small true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) today. Maybe it's a Plant Bug in the family Miridae , but maybe not. Once I noticed this one, I saw a few more on the same shrub. I don't know what kind of bug it is, but it has a rich color with a hint of red mixed in with the black. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2019)
CommonNames: true bug; Greenbark Ceanothus; Plant Bug

File.Name: j314c_j314-037.jpg
Notes: 46658655124 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) hanging its rear end off of a leaf of a white-flowered Ceanothus shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm confused about what kind of Ceanothus it is - either Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) or Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) or maybe a hybrid? I don't know what kind of fly it is either, but it was visiting the native flowers well away from any houses and not looking for garbage. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2019)

File.Name: j314d_j309-001.jpg
Notes: 46658606554 This is the strongly humped rear end of the tiny spider that I showed in this photo a couple of weeks ago. I found it on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It's surely an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the genus Araneus, but I'm not sure what kind - see my other photo for some discussion. It hung around for some time, but it just disappeared this week. I hope it comes back. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2019)

File.Name: j315a_j315-007.jpg
Notes:
46666849354 This little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) has captured a small Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) on a plant of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe from the markings that the spider is a female Pelegrina aeneola, compare this photo. The crane fly looks like another Limoniid Crane Fly (Limoniidae) with short palps and clear wings, see my first photo today, here. All my photos today feature flies, though this one has seen happier days. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2019)

Today was very nice with temps in the upper-60s. We may even get into the 70s this weekend, and I suppose that's proper because spring officially starts next week. (There's also a chance of rain for the equinox next Wednesday!) There was a breeze today, but not too bad. I had my usual errand in the Santa Ynez Valley, and it's the first time I've been happy that the highway is open again.
Locality: There's also a chance of rain for the equinox next Wednesday!)  There was a breeze today

File.Name: j315b_j310-006.jpg
Notes: 47390086831 This is a bristly fly (Diptera) on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I've seen a number of these dark flies this week, and I don't know what kind they are. My first impression is that these flies have a greasy look. I suppose it's bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. I already have my [Previous] two photos for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2019)
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: j315c_j309-014.jpg
Notes: 33513904908 I'm not sure what kind of fly (Diptera) this is, though I don't think it's "just a fly". For one thing, it's dusted with yellow pollen from the willow trees by the creek about 20 feet away, so it's got interesting tastes for a fly. It's perched in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), probably grounded when clouds blocked the early sun. Any help with identification will be appreciated. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2019)
